# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Centralized and Client/Server DBMS architectures. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Centralized DBMS | Client/Server DBMS |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of components** | All DBMS components (DB, DBMS engine, applications) reside on a single host computer | DBMS engine and database reside on a dedicated server; clients handle UI and part of application logic |
| **Client type** | Dumb terminals (thin) | Fat clients (2-tier) or thin clients (3-tier) |
| **Processing distribution** | All processing is centralized at the host | Processing is distributed between client and server |
| **Scalability** | Limited — relies on vertical scaling (upgrading host) | Better — supports horizontal scaling by adding clients/servers |
| **Network role** | Primarily for terminal-to-host communication | Core enabler — entire DBMS interaction is over the network |
| **Failure impact** | Single point of failure | Distributed failure domain; load can be rerouted |
| **Examples** | IBM DB2 on z/OS mainframe, IMS | Oracle with Java client, MySQL with PHP client |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Distributed DBMS* with *Client/Server DBMS*. The key distinction: in **C/S**, there is **ONE** DBMS server with **ONE** logical database. In **DDBMS**, there are **MULTIPLE** DBMS instances at different sites storing **fragmented/replicated** data. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you conflate these.

---

### Question 2: What is a Three-Tier Client/Server Architecture? Explain the role of the Application Server. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

A **Three-Tier Client/Server Architecture** introduces an intermediate **Application Server Tier** between the client (presentation) and the database server (data). The three tiers are:

1. **Presentation Tier (Tier 1):** Thin client (typically a web browser) that only displays data and captures user input. No business logic resides here.

2. **Application Logic Tier (Tier 2):** The **Application Server** (e.g., Java EE, .NET, Django) hosts business rules, validation logic, workflow coordination, and transaction management. It acts as the *middleman* that translates user actions into DB operations.

3. **Data Tier (Tier 3):** The **DBMS Server** stores and retrieves data, enforces integrity constraints, and handles concurrency control.

**Role of the Application Server:**
- **Centralizes Business Logic:** Eliminates duplication across fat clients; updates propagate to one location.
- **Security Enforcement:** Authentication, authorization, and audit logging happen centrally.
- **Connection Pooling:** Manages a pool of DB connections to optimize resource utilization.
- **Transaction Coordination:** Coordinates multi-step transactions across the data tier.
- **Protocol Translation:** Bridges HTTP/REST (client-side) with ODBC/JDBC (DB-side).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **connection pooling** and **centralized security enforcement** — these are favorite KTU exam points worth **1 mark each**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### Question A: Explain the Centralized DBMS Architecture in detail. Discuss its advantages and limitations. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Architecture Description [7 Marks]

In a **Centralized DBMS Architecture**, all components of the database system — the database, the DBMS software, the application programs, and the hardware (CPU, memory, disk) — reside at a **single site**. Remote users access the system through **dumb terminals** connected via a **terminal concentrator** and **communication controller**. Examples include IBM's IMS running on System/370 mainframes, and early DB2 deployments.

**Key Components:**

- **Dumb Terminals:** Input/output devices with no local processing power. Examples: IBM 3270, DEC VT100.
- **Terminal Concentrator:** A device that multiplexes signals from many terminals onto a single communication line.
- **Communication Controller:** Manages the protocol conversion between terminal-side and host-side communication (e.g., **BSC**, **SNA**, or **TCP/IP**).
- **Central Host (Mainframe):** Runs the OS, the DBMS, and application programs.
- **Direct Access Storage Devices (DASD):** High-capacity disks storing the database, typically with **RAID** configurations for redundancy.
- **Buffer Pool:** Main memory area used to cache frequently accessed disk pages.

**Workflow:**
1. User types query on dumb terminal.
2. Keystrokes are buffered and transmitted to the communication controller.
3. The host's DBMS receives the request, parses, optimizes, and executes the query.
4. Result rows are streamed back to the terminal for display.

#### (b) Advantages and Limitations [7 Marks]

**Advantages:**
1. **ACID Compliance is Easier:** Since all data resides in one location, transaction atomicity and isolation are simpler to enforce.
2. **Centralized Security:** Only one host needs security hardening; auditing is centralized.
3. **Simplified Administration:** DBAs manage one system, reducing complexity.
4. **Data Consistency:** No replication lag — a single source of truth.
5. **High-Speed Local I/O:** Disks are directly attached to the mainframe via high-speed channels, avoiding network latency.

**Limitations:**
1. **Single Point of Failure:** A mainframe crash halts the entire enterprise.
2. **Limited Scalability:** Adding capacity requires expensive hardware upgrades (vertical scaling).
3. **Network Bottleneck:** All terminal traffic funnels through one communication controller.
4. **High Cost:** Mainframes and proprietary software are expensive (CAPEX + OPEX).
5. **Limited User Mobility:** Users must connect through dedicated terminals.
6. **Geographic Latency:** For distributed enterprises, WAN latency degrades user experience.

---

### Question B: With neat diagrams, explain the Two-Tier and Three-Tier Client/Server DBMS architectures. Compare their features. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Two-Tier Architecture [5 Marks]

In the **Two-Tier Architecture**, the system is split into:

- **Client Tier:** Hosts the **Presentation Logic** (UI rendering) and **Application Logic** (business rules, validation). It formulates SQL queries using **ODBC** or **JDBC** APIs.
- **Server Tier:** Hosts the **DBMS Engine** that performs query parsing, optimization, execution, concurrency control, and recovery.

**Diagram:**

```
[Client App 1] ─┐
[Client App 2] ─┼──[ODBC/JDBC over TCP/IP]──> [DBMS Server] <──> [Database Storage]
[Client App N] ─┘
```

**Characteristics:** Clients are *fat* (carry significant logic); the server is dedicated purely to DB services.

#### (b) Three-Tier Architecture [5 Marks]

The **Three-Tier Architecture** inserts an **Application Server (Middleware)** tier:

- **Tier 1 — Presentation:** Thin client (browser/mobile app). Only displays UI.
- **Tier 2 — Application Server:** Hosts all business logic, security enforcement, transaction coordination, and connection pooling.
- **Tier 3 — Database Server:** DBMS engine + storage.

**Diagram:**

```
[Browser / Mobile] ──HTTP/REST──> [Application Server] ──ODBC/JDBC──> [DBMS Server] <──> [Storage]
                                  (Business Logic,
                                   Security, Connection Pool)
```

#### (c) Comparison Table [4 Marks]

| Feature | Two-Tier | Three-Tier |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Tiers** | 2 | 3 |
| **Client Type** | Fat client | Thin client |
| **Business Logic Location** | Distributed across clients | Centralized in application server |
| **Update Deployment** | Per-client (painful) | Server-side only (efficient) |
| **Security** | Client-side enforcement possible | Centralized at app server |
| **Scalability** | Limited by client distribution | Horizontal scaling of each tier independently |
| **Network Traffic** | Direct client-server | Client-app + app-server traffic |
| **Best For** | Small workgroups, LANs | Enterprise web apps, internet-scale |
| **Examples** | Oracle Forms + Oracle DB | Java EE + Oracle, Django + PostgreSQL |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is drawing the *same diagram* for both tiers and merely relabeling. The board expects **distinct visual differentiation** — at minimum, mark the **Application Server as a separate box** with its internal components. Omitting this loses **2 marks**. Another frequent error: confusing the **Application Server** with a **Web Server**. The web server handles HTTP; the application server hosts business logic. Examiners deduct marks if these are conflated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centralized & Client/Server Architectures**

- **Centralized DBMS:** All components (DB, DBMS, app, hardware) on ONE host. Users via dumb terminals. Examples: **DB2 on z/OS**, **IMS**. Key advantage: simpler ACID. Key disadvantage: **Single Point of Failure**.

- **Client/Server DBMS:** Splits DBMS across client machines and one or more server machines. Communicates over **TCP/IP** using **ODBC/JDBC** APIs.

- **Two-Tier C/S:** Client (fat) + DBMS Server. Business logic on client. Example: **Oracle Forms + Oracle**.

- **Three-Tier C/S:** Thin client + Application Server (business logic) + DBMS Server. Enables centralized updates, security, and connection pooling.

- **N-Tier C/S:** Generalization where the application logic is split across multiple specialized servers (web server, authentication server, business logic server, message queue).

- **Middleware:** The software layer enabling client-server communication — **ODBC**, **JDBC**, **ODP.NET**.

- **DDBMS ≠ C/S:** A **Distributed DBMS** has data physically fragmented/replicated across multiple sites, each with its own DBMS — this is fundamentally different from C/S.

- **Throughput Scaling:** Centralized systems scaled via vertical scaling (C/S enabled horizontal scaling by adding servers and using load balancers).

- **Modern Trend:** Three-tier and N-tier architectures dominate today (Java EE, .NET, Django, Spring Boot + RDBMS or NoSQL backends).

- **Key Protocols to Remember:** **ODBC**, **JDBC**, **TCP/IP**, **HTTP/HTTPS**, **REST**, **SOAP**.

- **Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **diagram** before answering comparison questions; use **bullet points** for advantages/disadvantages; cite at least **one real-world example** per architecture (e.g., IMS, Oracle, Django+PostgreSQL) — examiners award bonus marks.

- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  1. **C/S vs DDBMS** — single logical DB vs physically distributed DBs.
  2. **Application Server vs Web Server** — logic vs HTTP handling.
  3. **Thin vs Fat Client** — minimal vs substantial client-side processing.
  4. **OLTP vs OLAP** — transaction processing vs analytical queries (architectural implications differ).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Data Modelling and Database Design: Data Modelling Using the Entity-Relationship (ER) Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Data Modelling and Database Design: Data Modelling Using the Entity-Relationship (ER) Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — CONCEPTUAL DATA MODELLING AND DATABASE DESIGN
## Topic: Data Modelling Using the Entity–Relationship (ER) Model

---

### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

#### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Entity–Relationship (ER) Model**: A high-level, **semantic data model** proposed by **Peter Pin-Shan Chen (1976)** that describes a database as a collection of **entities**, **attributes**, and **relationships** in a diagrammatic form called an **ER Diagram (ERD)**. It is the standard *conceptual design* tool used in the first phase of database design — transforming real-world requirements into an unambiguous logical blueprint before physical implementation.

The ER model is **database-agnostic**: it captures the *meaning* of data, not its storage form. It operates at the **Conceptual Level** of the classic **three-schema architecture (ANSI/SPARC)** — the layer between the external (user) view and the internal (physical) view.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why the ER Model is taught first in KTU Module 1**: It forms the bridge between *informal* problem statements (given in university exam questions) and the *formal relational schema*. KTU examiners expect you to draw an ER diagram and then *derive* relational tables from it.

#### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "University Blueprint"

Imagine you are an **architect** asked to design a *new KTU campus* before laying a single brick. You would not start writing SQL. Instead, you would:

1. Identify the **real-world objects** that exist — `Student`, `Course`, `Professor`, `Department` (these are **entities**).
2. Describe their **properties** — a *Student* has a *roll number, name, semester, CGPA* (these are **attributes**).
3. State how they **interact** — a *Student* *enrols* in a *Course*; a *Professor* *teaches* a *Course* (these are **relationships**).

That entire architectural drawing — with rectangles, ovals, and diamonds — *is* the ER diagram. It is the **conceptual blueprint** of the database.

| Real-World Element | ER Model Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Physical object / person / event | **Entity** (Rectangle) |
| Property / detail of an object | **Attribute** (Oval) |
| Association between two objects | **Relationship** (Diamond) |
| Uniquely identifying property | **Key Attribute** (Underlined Oval) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024 CO)**: This topic primarily satisfies **CO1** — *"Model real-world systems using the ER model and convert them into a relational schema."* Expect a **14-mark question** in ESE combining ER diagram construction + mapping to relations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 The Five Building Blocks of the ER Model

The ER model is built from **five primitive components**. Memorise these — KTU questions often begin by asking you to *list and define* them.

#### 2.1.1 Entity
- An **entity** is a *real-world object* with an independent existence that can be uniquely identified.
- It is represented by a **rectangle**.
- **Entity Set**: A collection of similar entities sharing the same set of attributes (e.g., the set of *all students* in a college).
- **Types of Entities**:
  - **Strong Entity**: Has its own primary key; existence is independent. Drawn as a single rectangle.
  - **Weak Entity**: Cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone; depends on a strong (owner/identifying) entity. Drawn as a **double rectangle**. Has a **partial key (discriminator)** drawn as a *dashed-underlined oval*.

#### 2.1.2 Attributes
- An **attribute** is a *descriptive property* of an entity or relationship.
- Drawn as an **oval** connected to its parent.
- **Types of Attributes (Critical for KTU)**:

| Attribute Type | Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple / Atomic** | Plain oval | Cannot be subdivided | `Roll_No`, `DOB` |
| **Composite** | Oval with sub-ovals | Can be broken into smaller sub-parts | `Address` → `{Street, City, Pincode}` |
| **Single-Valued** | Plain oval | Holds exactly one value | `Date_of_Birth` |
| **Multivalued** | **Double oval** | Can hold a set of values | `Phone_Numbers`, `Skills` |
| **Derived** | **Dashed oval** | Computed from another attribute | `Age` (from `DOB`), `CGPA` (from marks) |
| **Key Attribute** | Oval with **underlined** text | Uniquely identifies an entity | `Roll_No`, `Emp_ID` |
| **Partial / Discriminator** | Oval with **dashed underline** | Uniquely identifies a weak entity *within* its owner set | `Section` of `Section_Of_Course` |

#### 2.1.3 Relationship
- A **relationship** is an *association* among two or more entities.
- Drawn as a **diamond**.
- **Relationship Set**: The set of all instances of a relationship.
- **Degree of Relationship**:
  - **Unary / Recursive (Degree 1)**: An entity participates with itself. Example: `Employee` *manages* `Employee`.
  - **Binary (Degree 2)**: Most common. Two entities participate. Example: `Student` *enrols in* `Course`.
  - **Ternary (Degree 3)**: Three entities participate simultaneously. Example: `Doctor` *prescribes* `Drug` *to* `Patient`.
  - **n-ary (Degree n)**: Generalisation of the above.

#### 2.1.4 Cardinality (Mapping) Constraints
Cardinality is the **maximum number of relationship instances** an entity can participate in. KTU loves this.

| Notation | Name | Meaning (Binary: A ↔ B) |
|---|---|---|
| **1 : 1** | One-to-One | One A relates to at most one B and vice versa |
| **1 : N** | One-to-Many | One A relates to many B's; each B relates to one A |
| **N : 1** | Many-to-One | Mirror of 1:N |
| **M : N** | Many-to-Many | Many A's relate to many B's |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Alternative Notation (Look-Here / Min-Max)**: The *min-max notation* is also accepted. An edge labelled `(min, max)` means an entity instance participates in **at least `min`** and **at most `max`** relationship instances. Example: A `Student` `(1, 4)` `Enrols` `Course` means each student takes between 1 and 4 courses.

#### 2.1.5 Participation Constraint
- **Total Participation (Existence Dependency)**: Every entity in the set *must* participate. Drawn as a **double line** from entity to relationship.
  - Example: Every `Loan` *must* belong to a `Customer` → double line.
- **Partial Participation**: Some entities may not participate. Drawn as a **single line**.

#### 2.2 Higher-Level / Extended ER (EER) Concepts (KTU Syllabus – Common)

| Construct | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Generalisation** | Triangle with "ISA" | Bottom-up: combine 2+ entity sets that share attributes into a higher-level entity. |
| **Specialisation** | Triangle with "ISA" | Top-down: split an entity set into sub-classes based on a discriminator. |
| **Aggregation** | Rectangle around a relationship | Treat a *relationship* as a *higher-level entity* — needed when we want a relationship to participate in another relationship. |
| **Inheritance** | Implicit in ISA | Sub-class inherits all attributes + relationships of the super-class. |
| **Total / Partial Specialisation** | Double / single line into triangle | Every / Not every super-class instance must be a member of a sub-class. |
| **Disjoint / Overlapping** | "d" or "o" inside triangle | Sub-classes do not / do share common instances. |

#### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following relationships govern *how ER diagrams are converted into relational tables* — the second half of every KTU 14-mark question. Memorise them.

| # | ER Construct | Mapping Rule | # of Resulting Relations |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strong Entity** | Create relation `R(A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ, PK)` with its own key attributes | 1 |
| 2 | **Weak Entity** | Create relation with **own partial key + primary key of owner** as composite key | 1 |
| 3 | **1 : 1 Relationship** | Merge into either side OR new relation; choose side with **total participation** | 0 or 1 |
| 4 | **1 : N Relationship** | Add the **PK of the "1"-side as a foreign key** in the "N"-side relation | 0 (no new table) |
| 5 | **M : N Relationship** | **Always** create a **new relation** with PKs of both sides as composite key | 1 |
| 6 | **Multivalued Attribute** | **Always** create a **new relation** with entity PK + attribute | 1 |
| 7 | **n-ary Relationship (n ≥ 3)** | New relation with PKs of all participating entities | 1 |
| 8 | **Generalisation (Disjoint, Total)** | Multiple options: one table for super + sub, or only sub-classes, or only super | 1 to k+1 |
| 9 | **Aggregation** | Treat the aggregation as a new entity; apply the rules above | varies |
| 10 | **Derived Attribute** | **Not stored** in the relation (computed on demand) | 0 |

**Cardinality Constraint Equations** — for any binary relationship set $R$ between entity sets $A$ and $B$:

$$
\text{If cardinality} = 1:1, \quad \mid R \mid = \min(\mid A \mid, \mid B \mid)
$$

$$
\text{If cardinality} = 1:N, \quad \mid R \mid \le \mid A \mid \cdot N
$$

$$
\text{If cardinality} = M:N, \quad \mid R \mid \le \mid A \mid \cdot \mid B \mid
$$

**Real-World Engineering Utility**: ER modelling is the *lingua franca* of software requirement analysis. Tools like **IBM InfoSphere Data Architect**, **Oracle Designer**, **Microsoft Visio**, **Lucidchart**, and **ER/Studio** all generate ERDs that translate to **SQL DDL** automatically. Every enterprise-grade relational database — from a **KSRTC bus reservation** system to a **BHIM UPI** backend — passes through an ER design phase.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Mapping ER to Relational Schema

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is the **most heavily weighted sub-skill** in KTU ESE Module 1. A typical 14-mark question asks: *"Design an ER diagram for a given scenario and convert it into a relational schema."* You must demonstrate **step-by-step mapping** using the rules in §2.3.

---

### 3.1 Reference Scenario — "KTU Student–Course–Faculty Database"

**Problem Statement (typical KTU phrasing):**
> *"Design an ER diagram for a college database that stores information about students, courses, and faculty. Each student has a roll number, name, and CGPA. Each course has a course id, name, and credits. Faculty have an emp_id, name, and department. A student enrols in one or more courses; a course is taught by exactly one faculty. A faculty can teach multiple courses. Each course has exactly one coordinator (a faculty)."*

**Entities Identified**:
- `STUDENT` (Strong) — key: `Roll_No`
- `COURSE` (Strong) — key: `Course_ID`
- `FACULTY` (Strong) — key: `Emp_ID`

**Relationships Identified**:
- `ENROLLS` (M : N) between `STUDENT` and `COURSE` — with attribute `Semester` and `Grade`
- `TEACHES` (1 : N) from `FACULTY` to `COURSE`
- `COORDINATES` (1 : 1) between `FACULTY` and `COURSE`

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Relational Schema

#### Step 1 — Map every Strong Entity to a Relation

$$
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{CGPA})
$$

$$
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Credits})
$$

$$
\text{FACULTY}(\underline{\text{Emp\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Department})
$$

> *Logic*: A strong entity has a full primary key of its own; it becomes a self-contained table. No foreign keys added at this step.

#### Step 2 — Map the 1 : N Relationship (`TEACHES`)

Rule: Add the PK of the "1" side as a foreign key in the "N" side.

$$
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Credits},\ \textbf{Emp\_ID}_{\text{FK}})
$$

> *Logic*: `FACULTY` (1) teaches `COURSE` (N). We do **not** create a new table. We *augment* the `COURSE` table with `Emp_ID` referencing `FACULTY(Emp_ID)`. The underline of `Course_ID` remains; `Emp_ID` is not a PK in `COURSE`, only a FK.

#### Step 3 — Map the 1 : 1 Relationship (`COORDINATES`)

Rule: Choose the entity with **total participation** as the receiver of the FK. Here, *every course must have a coordinator* → `COURSE` is on the *total participation* side.

$$
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Credits},\ \textbf{Emp\_ID}_{\text{FK}},\ \textbf{Coordinator\_Emp\_ID}_{\text{FK}})
$$

> *Logic*: An alternative is to *merge* the two entities into a single table if they share a 1:1 with both sides totally participating. Here they are distinct, so we use the FK approach. We could also rename `Coordinator_Emp_ID` and add a `UNIQUE` constraint to enforce the 1:1 limit.

#### Step 4 — Map the M : N Relationship (`ENROLLS`)

Rule: **Always** create a new relation with the PKs of both sides as a composite key.

$$
\text{ENROLLS}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Course\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \text{Semester},\ \text{Grade})
$$

> *Logic*: The composite primary key is `(Roll_No, Course_ID)`. Both are foreign keys referencing the parent tables. The relationship's own attributes (`Semester`, `Grade`) are stored in this new table.

#### Step 5 — Handle Multivalued / Composite Attributes (if any)

Suppose a `STUDENT` has multiple `Phone_Numbers` (multivalued). Rule: new table with student PK + attribute.

$$
\text{STUDENT\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Phone\_Number}})
$$

> *Logic*: Multivalued attributes **violate 1NF** if left in the parent table, so they *must* be split.

Suppose `STUDENT(Name)` is composite: `Name = {FName, MName, LName}`. Rule: include only the **leaf-level** simple attributes.

$$
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}},\ \text{FName},\ \text{MName},\ \text{LName},\ \text{CGPA})
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for ER-to-Relational Mapping

| ER Construct Detected | Decision | Action | New Table? | FK Added? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strong entity | Always map | Direct table | Yes | No |
| Weak entity | Always map | Table with owner PK | Yes | Yes (owner PK as part of PK) |
| 1:1 relationship | Optimise | FK on total-participation side | Maybe | Yes |
| 1:N relationship | Optimise | FK on N-side | No | Yes |
| M:N relationship | Never optimise | New junction table | Yes | Yes (composite PK) |
| Multivalued attr. | Always split | New table | Yes | Yes (parent PK) |
| n-ary (n≥3) relationship | New table | New table | Yes | Yes (all n PKs) |
| Generalisation | Context-dependent | 3 options | 1 to k+1 | Depends |
| Derived attribute | Omit | Compute on the fly | No | No |

---

### 3.4 Verification of Cardinality Constraint (Numerical Example)

**Given**: 500 students, 40 courses, 20 faculty. Each faculty teaches up to 3 courses. Each course taught by exactly 1 faculty. Each student enrols in exactly 5 courses. Each course has 60 students on average.

**Check feasibility using** $\mid R_{\text{TEACHES}} \mid = \mid \text{COURSE} \mid = 40$ (since 1 faculty teaches N courses, total relationships = total courses).

$$
\mid R_{\text{TEACHES}} \mid = 40
$$

$$
\text{Required faculty for 40 courses} = \frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33 \ \Rightarrow \ 14 \text{ minimum}
$$

> Since 20 faculty are available, **feasibility holds**.

For `ENROLLS` (M:N):

$$
\mid R_{\text{ENROLLS}} \mid = 500 \times 5 = 2500
$$

$$
\text{Check from course side: } 40 \times 60 = 2400
$$

> The mismatch (2500 vs 2400) indicates an *inconsistent problem statement*; KTU expects you to flag this.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Mermaid ER Architecture

> [!NOTE]  
> Mermaid does not natively support ER diagram notation (rectangles, ovals, diamonds). We therefore render the **functional architecture flow** that mirrors the ER structure of the reference scenario in §3.1, capturing entities, relationships, and cardinalities. This serves as a textual blueprint that a student can redraw in classic Chen notation during the exam.

### 4.1 Mermaid ER Architecture Flow — KTU Student–Course–Faculty

```mermaid
graph TD
    stuNode1["STUDENT (Strong Entity)"]
    crsNode2["COURSE (Strong Entity)"]
    facNode3["FACULTY (Strong Entity)"]

    attr1["PK: Roll_No"]
    attr2["Name, CGPA, Phone_Numbers (Multi)"]
    attr3["PK: Course_ID"]
    attr4["Name, Credits"]
    attr5["PK: Emp_ID"]
    attr6["Name, Department"]

    rel1["ENROLLS (M:N)"]
    rel2["TEACHES (1:N)"]
    rel3["COORDINATES (1:1, Total on COURSE)"]

    stuNode1 -- "1..*" --- rel1
    crsNode2 -- "0..*" --- rel1

    facNode3 -- "1" --- rel2
    crsNode2 -- "0..*" --- rel2

    facNode3 -- "0..1" --- rel3
    crsNode2 -- "1" --- rel3

    stuNode1 --- attr1
    stuNode1 --- attr2
    crsNode2 --- attr3
    crsNode2 --- attr4
    facNode3 --- attr5
    facNode3 --- attr6
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Each `["..."]` block is a Chen-style entity or relationship; the `---` edges carry the `(min..max)` participation label. The student should **redraw** this on paper using the standard Chen notation:
> - **Rectangle** for entities, **diamond** for relationships, **ovals** for attributes.
> - **Double rectangle / diamond / oval** for weak/multivalued/derived variants.
> - **Underline** the primary key oval; **double line** for total participation.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ER-to-Relational Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1["Real-World Requirements"] --> input2["Identify Entities"]
    input2 --> input3["Identify Attributes & Keys"]
    input3 --> input4["Identify Relationships + Cardinality"]
    input4 --> input5["Identify Constraints (Participation, Weakness)"]
    input5 --> output1["Draw ER Diagram (Chen Notation)"]
    output1 --> output2["Apply 9 Mapping Rules (Section 2.3)"]
    output2 --> output3["Generate Relational Schema"]
    output3 --> output4["Normalise to 3NF / BCNF"]
```

> **Exam Tip**: KTU question paper often tests the *pipeline above*. State the pipeline verbally, then perform each step on the *given* problem.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following with an example each:**
(a) Weak entity
(b) Multivalued attribute
(c) Derived attribute

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~50 words per part):**

> **(a) Weak Entity**: An entity whose instances cannot be uniquely identified by their own attributes alone and depend on a *strong (owner) entity* for identification. Drawn as a **double rectangle**.  
> *Example*: `Dependent` (of an `Employee`) — identified by `Dependent_Name` + `Emp_ID`.

> **(b) Multivalued Attribute**: An attribute that can hold *more than one* value for a single entity instance. Drawn as a **double oval**.  
> *Example*: `Phone_Numbers` of a `Student` (a student may have multiple phone numbers).

> **(c) Derived Attribute**: An attribute whose value is *computed* from other stored attributes rather than stored directly. Drawn as a **dashed oval**.  
> *Example*: `Age` derived from `Date_of_Birth`; `CGPA` derived from semester marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between total participation and partial participation in an ER model. How are they represented in an ER diagram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Total Participation | Partial Participation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Every entity instance *must* participate in the relationship. | Some entity instances *may not* participate. |
| **Symbol** | **Double line** from entity to relationship. | **Single line** from entity to relationship. |
| **Also called** | Existence dependency. | Optional participation. |
| **Example** | Every `Loan` *must* belong to a `Customer`. | A `Student` *may or may not* enrol in a `Project`. |

> **Line of action**: Total participation *forces* a foreign-key approach during mapping (cannot create a new table with NULLs) — this is a KTU favourite for follow-up 7-mark questions.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Sub-parts typically carry **7 + 7** marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

Consider the following scenario for a **"KTU Central Library Management System"**:

> *"The library maintains records of **books**. Each book has a unique `Book_ID`, `Title`, `Price`, and `Year_of_Publication`. A book is written by one or more **authors**. An author has an `Author_ID`, `Name`, and `Affiliation`. A book belongs to exactly one **category** (e.g., Programming, Mathematics). A category has a `Category_ID` and `Category_Name`. The library has multiple **branches**; each branch has a `Branch_ID`, `Address`, and a `Branch_Manager`. A book may have **multiple copies** in a branch, each copy tracked by a `Copy_Number`. Members borrow book copies; a member has a `Member_ID`, `Name`, and `Phone`. A member can borrow at most 3 copies at a time, and a copy can be issued to only one member at a time. Each borrowing is recorded with a `Date_of_Issue` and `Due_Date`."*

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Draw a complete **ER diagram** (Chen notation) for the above scenario. Identify entities, attributes (including primary keys, multivalued, derived), relationships, cardinalities, and participation constraints.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Convert the ER diagram drawn in part (a) into an equivalent **relational schema**. Clearly show primary keys and foreign keys.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — ER Diagram Construction**

**Step 1: Identify Strong Entities**:
- `BOOK` — PK: `Book_ID`; Attributes: `Title`, `Price`, `Year_of_Publication`
- `AUTHOR` — PK: `Author_ID`; Attributes: `Name`, `Affiliation`
- `CATEGORY` — PK: `Category_ID`; Attribute: `Category_Name`
- `BRANCH` — PK: `Branch_ID`; Attributes: `Address`, `Branch_Manager`
- `MEMBER` — PK: `Member_ID`; Attributes: `Name`, `Phone`

**Step 2: Identify Weak Entity**:
- `BOOK_COPY` — Partial Key: `Copy_Number` (within a `BRANCH`); depends on `BRANCH`.  
  Alternative interpretation: `BOOK_COPY` is weak on `BOOK` if `Copy_Number` is unique per book globally. We choose *weak on BRANCH* because the problem states *copies are tracked per branch*.

**Step 3: Identify Relationships and Cardinalities**:

| Relationship | Between | Cardinality | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `WRITTEN_BY` | BOOK ↔ AUTHOR | **M : N** | A book can have many authors; an author writes many books. |
| `BELONGS_TO` | BOOK ↔ CATEGORY | **N : 1** | A book belongs to one category; a category has many books. |
| `HOLDS` | BRANCH ↔ BOOK_COPY | **1 : N** (Identifying) | A branch holds many copies; a copy is in exactly one branch. |
| `IS_COPY_OF` | BOOK_COPY ↔ BOOK | **N : 1** | A copy is of one book; a book has many copies. |
| `BORROWS` | MEMBER ↔ BOOK_COPY | **M : N** with attribute `Date_of_Issue`, `Due_Date` | Members borrow many copies; a copy is borrowed over time by many members (history). |

**Step 4: Identify Participation**:
- `BOOK_COPY` — **Total** participation in `HOLDS` (every copy must be in some branch).
- `BOOK` — **Total** participation in `BELONGS_TO` (every book must belong to a category).
- `MEMBER` in `BORROWS` — **Partial** (a member may not have borrowed yet).
- The `(1, 3)` min-max on `MEMBER` side of `BORROWS` (max 3 copies at a time) is a **min-max constraint**.

**Step 5: Multivalued / Derived**:
- If `AUTHOR(Name)` is composite (FName, LName) — left as composite in the diagram.
- `Author_Phone` could be multivalued — double oval.
- `Age` of `MEMBER` if `DOB` is stored — derived (dashed).

**ER Diagram (Chen Notation) — Verbal Description for Answer Sheet**:

> Draw five **rectangles** labelled `BOOK`, `AUTHOR`, `CATEGORY`, `BRANCH`, `MEMBER` and one **double rectangle** labelled `BOOK_COPY`.  
> Connect `BOOK` and `AUTHOR` via a **diamond** `WRITTEN_BY` with `M:N` labels.  
> Connect `BOOK` to `CATEGORY` via a **diamond** `BELONGS_TO` with `N:1` labels (1 on the CATEGORY side).  
> Connect `BRANCH` to `BOOK_COPY` via a **diamond** `HOLDS` (identifying relationship) with a **double line** from `BOOK_COPY` to indicate total participation.  
> Connect `BOOK_COPY` to `BOOK` via a **diamond** `IS_COPY_OF` (1:N, BOOK on the 1-side).  
> Connect `MEMBER` to `BOOK_COPY` via a **diamond** `BORROWS` with `Date_of_Issue` and `Due_Date` as attribute ovals.  
> Underline primary keys: `Book_ID`, `Author_ID`, `Category_ID`, `Branch_ID`, `Member_ID`.  
> Partial key `Copy_Number` of `BOOK_COPY` is shown with **dashed underline**.  
> Mark `(1, 3)` on the `MEMBER` edge of `BORROWS` for the min-max constraint.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for identifying entities, 1 mark for attributes + keys, 1 mark for identifying relationships, 1 mark for cardinalities, 1 mark for participation, 1 mark for weak-entity handling, 1 mark for diagram neatness / Chen notation accuracy.)*

---

**Part (b) — Conversion to Relational Schema**

Apply the rules from §2.3 systematically.

**Step 1: Strong Entities → Relations**

$$
\text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{Book\_ID}},\ \text{Title},\ \text{Price},\ \text{Year\_of\_Publication},\ \text{Category\_ID}_{\text{FK}})
$$

$$
\text{AUTHOR}(\underline{\text{Author\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Affiliation})
$$

$$
\text{CATEGORY}(\underline{\text{Category\_ID}},\ \text{Category\_Name})
$$

$$
\text{BRANCH}(\underline{\text{Branch\_ID}},\ \text{Address},\ \text{Branch\_Manager})
$$

$$
\text{MEMBER}(\underline{\text{Member\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Phone})
$$

> *Note*: We pre-emptively added `Category_ID` as FK in `BOOK` to handle the N:1 relationship (Step 3 below).

**Step 2: Weak Entity → Relation**

$$
\text{BOOK\_COPY}(\underline{\text{Branch\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Copy\_Number}},\ \text{Book\_ID}_{\text{FK}})
$$

> *Logic*: The composite primary key is `(Branch_ID, Copy_Number)`. `Branch_ID` is a FK to `BRANCH`. `Book_ID` is a FK to `BOOK` (handles the `IS_COPY_OF` N:1 relationship; no new table needed).

**Step 3: M:N Relationship `WRITTEN_BY` → New Relation**

$$
\text{WRITTEN\_BY}(\underline{\text{Book\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Author\_ID}_{\text{FK}}})
$$

**Step 4: M:N Relationship `BORROWS` → New Relation (with attributes)**

$$
\text{BORROWS}(\underline{\text{Member\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Branch\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Copy\_Number}_{\text{FK}}},\ \text{Date\_of\_Issue},\ \text{Due\_Date})
$$

> *Logic*: We must include the *full primary key of `BOOK_COPY`* (i.e., `(Branch_ID, Copy_Number)`) as a composite FK in `BORROWS` so the relationship instance is uniquely identified.

**Step 5: Final Schema (6 Relations Total)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{Book\_ID}},\ \text{Title},\ \text{Price},\ \text{Year\_of\_Publication},\ \text{Category\_ID}_{\text{FK}}) \\
&\text{AUTHOR}(\underline{\text{Author\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Affiliation}) \\
&\text{CATEGORY}(\underline{\text{Category\_ID}},\ \text{Category\_Name}) \\
&\text{BRANCH}(\underline{\text{Branch\_ID}},\ \text{Address},\ \text{Branch\_Manager}) \\
&\text{MEMBER}(\underline{\text{Member\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Phone}) \\
&\text{BOOK\_COPY}(\underline{\text{Branch\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Copy\_Number}},\ \text{Book\_ID}_{\text{FK}}) \\
&\text{WRITTEN\_BY}(\underline{\text{Book\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Author\_ID}_{\text{FK}}}) \\
&\text{BORROWS}(\underline{\text{Member\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Branch\_ID}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Copy\_Number}_{\text{FK}}},\ \text{Date\_of\_Issue},\ \text{Due\_Date})
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Allocate: 1 mark for each well-formed relation up to 5; 1 mark for correctly handling the weak entity; 1 mark for foreign-key annotations and justification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

A **"KSRTC Bus Reservation System"** is to be designed. The system has the following requirements:

> *"Each **bus** has a `Bus_Number`, `Type` (Ordinary/Fast Passenger/Super Fast), and `Capacity`. Each bus is operated by exactly one **depot**. A depot has a `Depot_Code`, `Location`, and a `Depot_Manager`. A bus is assigned one or more **drivers** (rotational). A driver has `Driver_ID`, `Name`, `License_Number`, and `Salary`. A bus follows a **route**; a route has a `Route_Number`, `Source`, `Destination`, and a list of `Stops` (multivalued). A **passenger** with `PNR_No`, `Name`, and `Age` books a **ticket** for a specific bus on a specific date. A ticket has `Ticket_ID`, `Date_of_Journey`, `Seat_Number`, and `Fare`. A passenger may also cancel a ticket; cancellations are recorded with a `Cancellation_Date`."*

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Construct a complete ER diagram (Chen notation) with all entities, attributes, relationships, cardinalities, and constraints. Identify the weak entity, if any.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Map the ER diagram into a relational schema. Justify each mapping decision using the standard rules.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (Highlights)

**Part (a) — Entities**:
- `BUS` (Strong, PK: `Bus_Number`)
- `DEPOT` (Strong, PK: `Depot_Code`)
- `DRIVER` (Strong, PK: `Driver_ID`)
- `ROUTE` (Strong, PK: `Route_Number`)
- `PASSENGER` (Strong, PK: `PNR_No`)
- `TICKET` (Strong, PK: `Ticket_ID`) — *not* weak, has its own unique ID.

**Relationships**:
- `OPERATED_BY` (N : 1) between `BUS` and `DEPOT`
- `ASSIGNED_TO` (M : N) between `BUS` and `DRIVER`
- `FOLLOWS` (1 : N) between `ROUTE` and `BUS`
- `BOOKED_FOR` (M : N) between `PASSENGER` and `TICKET` (with `Date_of_Booking` as relationship attribute)
- `FOR_BUS` (N : 1) between `TICKET` and `BUS`
- `ON_DATE` attribute on `TICKET` (or in a ternary relationship: `BOOKS(Passenger, Ticket, Date_of_Journey)`)

**Multivalued**: `Stops` of `ROUTE` (double oval).

**Derived**: `Duration` of route (if `Distance` and `Avg_Speed` are stored).

**Part (b) — Relational Schema** (final form):

$$
\text{DEPOT}(\underline{\text{Depot\_Code}},\ \text{Location},\ \text{Depot\_Manager})
$$

$$
\text{BUS}(\underline{\text{Bus\_Number}},\ \text{Type},\ \text{Capacity},\ \text{Depot\_Code}_{\text{FK}},\ \text{Route\_Number}_{\text{FK}})
$$

$$
\text{DRIVER}(\underline{\text{Driver\_ID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{License\_Number},\ \text{Salary})
$$

$$
\text{ROUTE}(\underline{\text{Route\_Number}},\ \text{Source},\ \text{Destination})
$$

$$
\text{ROUTE\_STOPS}(\underline{\text{Route\_Number}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Stop\_Name}})
$$

$$
\text{ASSIGNED\_TO}(\underline{\text{Bus\_Number}_{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Driver\_ID}_{\text{FK}}})
$$

$$
\text{PASSENGER}(\underline{\text{PNR\_No}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Age})
$$

$$
\text{TICKET}(\underline{\text{Ticket\_ID}},\ \text{Date\_of\_Journey},\ \text{Seat\_Number},\ \text{Fare},\ \text{PNR\_No}_{\text{FK}},\ \text{Bus\_Number}_{\text{FK}},\ \text{Cancellation\_Date})
$$

> **Justifications** (sample sentences expected in the answer):
> 1. `OPERATED_BY` (N:1) → FK `Depot_Code` added to `BUS`; no new table.
> 2. `ASSIGNED_TO` (M:N) → new table created with composite PK.
> 3. `Stops` (multivalued) → new table `ROUTE_STOPS` created.
> 4. `Cancellation_Date` stored as a nullable attribute on `TICKET` (NULL = not cancelled).
> 5. `Date_of_Booking` (relationship attribute) merged into `TICKET`.

*(Allocate: 1 mark per mapping decision justified, capped at 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to underline the primary key in the ER diagram** (–1 to –2 marks). Chen notation *requires* underlining the key attribute oval.
> 2. **Confusing 1:N with N:1 direction**. Remember: the "1" side always *receives* the FK in a 1:N relationship when there is a choice. Direction matters.
> 3. **Not handling multivalued attributes** — leaving them as ovals in the parent table is **wrong**; KTU strictly expects a separate table.
> 4. **Composite vs Multivalued confusion**: A *composite* attribute stays *inside* the parent table (e.g., `Address → {Street, City, Pin}`). A *multivalued* attribute gets a *new* table. Do not mix these.
> 5. **Weak entity handling**: If a problem says *"a room exists only inside a building"*, `ROOM` is *weak* on `BUILDING`. The mapping **must** carry `Building_ID` as part of the PK in `ROOM`. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **In the relational schema, omitting the `FK` subscripts** or not clearly indicating which attribute is a foreign key. KTU examiners look for explicit FK notation.
> 7. **Not mentioning the assumption** for an *ambiguous* problem statement (e.g., "is a copy tracked per book or per branch?"). Always state your assumption — it shows logical maturity and earns partial credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorise the Following 12 Points:**

- ☐ **ER Model** = Entity + Attribute + Relationship + Cardinality + Participation. Five core constructs, period.
- ☐ **Strong vs Weak Entity** — Strong has its own PK (single rectangle). Weak depends on owner (double rectangle, partial key = dashed underlined).
- ☐ **Key Attribute** is *always underlined*; **multivalued** = double oval; **derived** = dashed oval; **composite** has sub-ovals.
- ☐ **Cardinality** of binary relationship is one of: **1:1, 1:N, M:N**. Always state the *direction* (which side is "1" and which is "N").
- ☐ **Total participation** = double line. Total participation in 1:1 means the FK must be on *that* side (not nulls).
- ☐ **M:N and Multivalued attributes always produce a new table.** Never optimise these away.
- ☐ **1:N relationships** are mapped by adding the "1"-side PK as a FK in the "N"-side table. **No new table.**
- ☐ **n-ary (n ≥ 3) relationships** always create a new table containing PKs of all participating entities.
- ☐ **Generalisation (ISA)** has three mapping options: (a) multiple sub-class tables, (b) one super-class table with a type discriminator, (c) one table per sub-class *plus* super-class. The choice depends on disjointness and completeness — KTU often asks *which* to choose.
- ☐ **Aggregation** is used only when a *relationship* needs to participate in *another* relationship (e.g., a `Project` *uses* a `Sponsorship` between `Sponsor` and `Employee`).
- ☐ Always show **foreign keys** in the relational schema with an `FK` subscript or arrow. Always **underline** primary keys.
- ☐ In the exam: draw the ER diagram *first* (neatly), then label cardinalities, then write the relational schema *in the same order* as your diagram's entities — this makes it easier for the examiner to follow your logic and earn full marks.

> **Final Advice**: The ER-to-Relational mapping question is the *single highest-yield* topic in KTU DBMS. Practice at least 5 different scenarios (Library, Hospital, University, Railway, E-Commerce) and you will secure **all 14 marks** with confidence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entity Types, Entity Sets, Attributes, and Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Entity Types, Entity Sets, Attributes, and Keys # 📘 Module 1 — Entity Types, Entity Sets, Attributes, and Keys ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview The **Entity-Relationship (ER) model**, proposed by **Peter Chen (1976)**, is the foundational conceptual data model used in the *database design phase* before any logical (relational) or physical implementation is attempted. The four building blocks of this model are **Entities**, **Entity Types**, **Entity Sets**, and **Attributes**, supplemented by the **Keys** that provide identity and referential integrity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Block** > - **Entity**: *"A thing — either a real-world object or a conceptual object — that has an independent existence and can be uniquely distinguished from other objects."* > - **Entity Type**: *"A collection of entities that share the same set of attributes (properties/characteristics)."* > - **Entity Set**: *"The actual collection (instantiation) of all entities of a given entity type existing in the database at a particular point in time."* > - **Attribute**: *"A data item that describes a particular property of an entity or entity type."* > - **Key**: *"An attribute (or minimal combination of attributes) whose value uniquely identifies an entity within an entity set."* ### 🍕 Intuitive Analogy — The "University Bakery" View Imagine a **university canteen** that tracks every student who buys food. | ER Concept | Canteen Analogy | |------------|-----------------| | **Entity** | One specific student, say *"Anu (Roll No: S047)"* standing in line. | | **Entity Type** | The *blueprint* labelled **STUDENT** defining what data we store (name, roll no, semester, phone). | | **Entity Set** | The **live register** of *all currently enrolled students* — say, 4,800 of them this semester. | | **Attribute** | Each field in the register: `Name`, `RollNo`, `Semester`, `PhoneNo`, `DateOfBirth`. | | **Key** | **`RollNo`** — the unique identifier stamped on the ID card; no two students share it. | > [!NOTE] > **Why the distinction between *Type* and *Set* matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often test whether you can differentiate the **schema-level construct** (Entity Type — a *definition*) from the **instance-level construct** (Entity Set — the *data* at runtime). Conflating these two is a classic **2-mark loser** in Part A questions. ### 1.2 Formal Mathematical Notations $$ E = \{ e \mid e \text{ is a real-world identifiable object conforming to schema } T \} $$ $$ T = (A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots, A_n) $$ $$ E_{set}(t) = \{ e_1, e_2, e_3, \dots, e_m \} \quad \text{at time } t $$ Where: - $E$ = collection of all entities - $T$ = entity type schema with $n$ attributes - $A_i$ = the $i$-th attribute of the type - $E_{set}(t)$ = entity set cardinality $= m$ at time $t$ - $e_i$ = the $i$-th individual entity instance ### 1.3 Categories of Entities | Category | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Strong (Regular) Entity** | Exists independently; not dependent on any other entity. Has its own primary key. | `STUDENT`, `EMPLOYEE`, `BOOK` | | **Weak Entity** | Existence-dependent on a *strong* (owner/identifying) entity. Cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone. | `DEPENDENT` (depends on `EMPLOYEE`), `ROOM` (depends on `BUILDING`) | > [!NOTE] > Weak entities are identified using a **partial key (discriminator)**, which when combined with the **primary key of the owner entity**, forms a complete key. This is a **favourite KTU Part A question** — *"Differentiate between strong and weak entity with example."* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Attribute Attributes are *not* all equal. The ER model classifies them along **six orthogonal dimensions**, and KTU frequently tests these distinctions. ### 2.1.1 Classification by Structure (Compositional View) $$ \text{Attribute } A \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{Simple (Atomic)} \\ \text{Composite} \end{cases} $$ | Sub-type | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Simple/Atomic** | Cannot be subdivided meaningfully. | `Age`, `Salary`, `Pincode` | | **Composite** | Can be broken into meaningful sub-parts. | `Name` $\rightarrow$ `{FirstName, MiddleName, LastName}`; `Address` $\rightarrow$ `{Street, City, State, Pincode}` | ### 2.1.2 Classification by Value Cardinality $$ \text{Attribute } A \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{Single-Valued} \\ \text{Multi-Valued} \end{cases} $$ | Sub-type | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Single-Valued** | Holds exactly one value per entity instance. | `DateOfBirth`, `RollNo` | | **Multi-Valued** | Can hold a *set* of values per entity instance. Represented by *double oval* in ER diagrams. | `PhoneNumbers` of an employee, `Skills` of a candidate | ### 2.1.3 Classification by Origin (Storage Derivation) | Sub-type | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Stored Attribute** | Persisted physically in the database. | `DateOfBirth` | | **Derived Attribute** | Computed on-the-fly from other stored attributes. Drawn as *dashed oval*. | `Age` (derived from `DateOfBirth` and current date), `Experience` | ### 2.1.4 Classification by Nullability | Sub-type | Definition | |----------|------------| | **NULL Attribute** | Permits the special value `NULL` — meaning *unknown*, *not applicable*, or *value does not exist*. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Mapping:** A single attribute can wear *multiple hats simultaneously*. For example, `Email` of a `PERSON` may be **Simple + Single-Valued + Stored + NULL-permitted**, while `PhoneNumbers` of the same `PERSON` is **Simple + Multi-Valued + Stored + NULL-permitted**. KTU Part A often asks: *"Classify the attribute `Skills` of an `EMPLOYEE` entity."* ## 2.2 Key Constraints — The Identity System of ER A **key** is a *minimal set of attributes* that acts as the unique identity token of an entity. The hierarchy from most-general to most-specific is: $$ \text{Super Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Candidate Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Primary Key} $$ ### 2.2.1 Definitions & Examples | Key Type | Formal Definition | Example (STUDENT) | |----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Super Key** | Any set of attributes whose values *uniquely* identify an entity. May contain extraneous attributes. | `{RollNo}`, `{RollNo, Name}`, `{RollNo, Name, DOB}`, `{AadharNo}`, `{Email}` | | **Candidate Key** | A *minimal* super key — removing any attribute destroys uniqueness. | `{RollNo}`, `{AadharNo}`, `{Email}` | | **Primary Key** | The candidate key *chosen* by the database designer to be the principal identifier. **Underlined** in ER diagram. | `{RollNo}` (chosen) | | **Alternate Key** | Candidate keys that were *not* selected as the primary key. | `{AadharNo}`, `{Email}` | | **Foreign Key** | An attribute in entity set $E_1$ that references the **primary key** of another entity set $E_2$. Enforces *referential integrity*. | `DeptId` in `STUDENT` referencing `DeptId` in `DEPARTMENT` | | **Composite Key** | A key composed of **two or more** attributes. None of the individual attributes alone can guarantee uniqueness. | `{StudentId, CourseId}` in `ENROLLMENT` | | **Partial Key / Discriminator** | A set of attributes that *uniquely identifies* a weak entity **only when combined with the primary key of its owner/identifying entity**. Drawn with a *dashed underline*. | `{DependentName}` of `DEPENDENT` (combined with `EmpId` of `EMPLOYEE`) | > [!NOTE] > **Foreign Key vs Primary Key:** A *foreign key* does **not** have to be unique within its own entity set — it only needs to match an existing primary-key value in the referenced entity set. This is a common KTU pitfall. ## 2.3 Properties of a Valid Key — The *Uniqueness + Minimality* Pair A set of attributes $K \subseteq \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$ qualifies as a **candidate key** for entity type $T$ if and only if **both** properties hold: $$ \text{Property 1 (Uniqueness):} \quad \forall \, e_i, e_j \in E_{set},\; e_i \neq e_j \;\Rightarrow\; K(e_i) \neq K(e_j) $$ $$ \text{Property 2 (Minimality):} \quad \forall \, A \in K,\; (K - \{A\}) \text{ violates uniqueness} $$ ## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Applications | Application Domain | Use of Entity / Key / Attribute Model | |--------------------|----------------------------------------| | **Banking Systems** | `ACCOUNT` entity type, `AccountNumber` as primary key, `IFSC` as foreign key referencing `BRANCH`. | | **Hospital Management** | `PATIENT` strong entity, `PRESCRIPTION` weak entity (depends on `DOCTOR_VISIT`). | | **E-Commerce** | `PRODUCT`, `CUSTOMER`, `ORDER` entity types; composite key `{OrderId, ProductId}` in `ORDER_ITEM`. | | **University ERP (KTU Project Context)** | `STUDENT`, `COURSE`, `FACULTY`, `ENROLLMENT` — the latter is a *associative* entity resolving the M:N relationship. | | **IoT / Sensor Networks** | `SENSOR` entity with `SensorId` primary key, `Readings` multi-valued attribute. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Project Hint:** When you design your mini-project in the DBMS lab (Semester 4 / 5), every table you create **must** begin with a clearly justified primary key, and every reference between tables **must** be enforced as a foreign key constraint. Examiners check this first. ## 2.5 KTU Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Usage | |---|---------|-----------------|-------| | 1 | Entity Type schema | $T = (A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$ | Declares the attribute list of an entity type. | | 2 | Entity Set cardinality | $\vert E_{set}(t) \vert = m$ | Number of entity instances present at time $t$. | | 3 | Uniqueness axiom | $\forall e_i \neq e_j \in E_{set},\; K(e_i) \neq K(e_j)$ | Defines super/candidate key. | | 4 | Minimality axiom | $\not\exists A \in K : (K \setminus \{A\}) \text{ is a super key}$ | Distinguishes candidate key from super key. | | 5 | Null set rule for key | $K(e) \neq \text{NULL}$ for all $e$ | A key attribute can never be NULL. | | 6 | Foreign Key reference | $FK \subseteq \text{PK of } E_{ref}$ | Referential integrity rule. | | 7 | Domain rule | $A_i : D_i$ (where $D_i$ is the domain/type) | Each attribute is bound to a value domain. | | 8 | Weak entity key | $\text{PK}_{\text{weak}} = \text{PK}_{\text{owner}} \cup \text{Discriminator}$ | Composite identification rule. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Real-World Observation to Entity Type The transformation from a real-world domain to a database schema is a **3-stage pipeline** that KTU expects you to articulate in long-answer questions. **Stage 1 — Identify Entities (Nouns of the domain):** $$ \text{Universe of Discourse} \; U = \{ \text{all perceivable objects in the application domain} \} $$ $$ E_{\text{relevant}} = \{ e \in U \mid e \text{ has independent existence and is to be tracked} \} $$ **Stage 2 — Group Entities into Types (by common attributes):** For two entities $e_i, e_j \in E_{\text{relevant}}$, they belong to the same entity type iff: $$ \forall k \in \{1, \dots, n\},\; \text{type}(A_k(e_i)) = \text{type}(A_k(e_j)) $$ That is, *every* attribute of the proposed type is *type-compatible* across the candidate entities. **Stage 3 — Define Keys (minimal uniqueness selectors):** $$ \text{SuperKey}(T) = \{ S \subseteq \{A_1, \dots, A_n\} \mid S \text{ satisfies uniqueness} \} $$ $$ \text{CandidateKey}(T) = \{ K \in \text{SuperKey}(T) \mid K \text{ is minimal} \} $$ $$ \text{PrimaryKey}(T) = \text{one chosen element from } \text{CandidateKey}(T) $$ $$ \text{AlternateKey}(T) = \text{CandidateKey}(T) \setminus \text{PrimaryKey}(T) $$ > **Conversion Logic (Stage 3):** Start with every single-attribute super key; check if uniqueness holds. Then progressively *extend* with additional attributes (only when needed). Among all super keys, discard those containing a proper subset that is also a super key — what remains are the candidate keys. Then *elect* one as primary; the rest are alternates. ## 3.2 Worked Example: STUDENT Domain Consider the `STUDENT` entity type with attributes: $$ T_{\text{STUDENT}} = \{ \text{RollNo},\; \text{AadharNo},\; \text{Email},\; \text{Name},\; \text{DOB},\; \text{Phone1},\; \text{Phone2},\; \text{DeptId} \} $$ **Step 1 — List candidate super keys (one attribute at a time):** $$ \{\text{RollNo}\},\; \{\text{AadharNo}\},\; \{\text{Email}\} $$ All three uniquely identify a student. **Step 2 — Generate compound super keys:** $$ \{\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}\},\; \{\text{AadharNo}, \text{DOB}\},\; \{\text{Email}, \text{Phone1}\},\; \dots $$ Any superset of a super key is *also* a super key. So $\{\text{RollNo}\}$ generates a family of super keys: $$ \{\text{RollNo}, X\} \quad \text{for every subset } X \subseteq \{\text{Name}, \text{DOB}, \dots\} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Minimality Filter:** A super key $K$ is a candidate key *iff*: $$ \forall A \in K,\; (K \setminus \{A\}) \notin \text{SuperKey}(T) $$ For $\{\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}\}$: removing `RollNo` gives $\{\text{Name}\}$, which is *not* a super key (two students can share a name). Removing `Name` gives $\{\text{RollNo}\}$, which *is* a super key. So $\{\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}\}$ is **not** a candidate key — `Name` is redundant. > **Result:** > $$\text{CandidateKey}(T_{\text{STUDENT}}) = \{\{\text{RollNo}\},\; \{\text{AadharNo}\},\; \{\text{Email}\}\}$$ **Step 4 — Select Primary Key & Identify Alternates:** $$ \text{PrimaryKey}(T_{\text{STUDENT}}) = \{\text{RollNo}\} $$ $$ \text{AlternateKey}(T_{\text{STUDENT}}) = \{\{\text{AadharNo}\},\; \{\text{Email}\}\} $$ > **Why `RollNo` and not `AadharNo`?** Aadhar contains **12 sensitive digits** with privacy regulations; it is *non-nullable* by Indian law and never shared with third-party apps. `RollNo` is the *operationally chosen* key that satisfies all four *key property checks* (Uniqueness, Minimality, Stability, Non-nullness). **Step 5 — Identify Foreign Key:** $$ \text{ForeignKey}(T_{\text{STUDENT}}) = \{\text{DeptId}\} \;\rightarrow\; \text{references } \text{PK}(T_{\text{DEPARTMENT}}) $$ Here, `DeptId` need **not** be unique within `STUDENT` (many students belong to the same department) but **must** match a valid `DeptId` in the `DEPARTMENT` table — this is the **referential integrity** rule. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Key | Decision Criterion | Primary Key (PK) | Candidate Key | Super Key | Foreign Key | |--------------------|:----------------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:-----------:| | Must be unique within its own table? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not required | | Must be minimal? | ✅ Yes (inherited) | ✅ Yes | ❌ May be non-minimal | ❌ Not required | | Must be NOT NULL? | ✅ Yes (enforced) | ✅ Implicitly | ❌ No formal rule | ⚠️ Recommended | | Can contain multiple attributes? | ✅ Yes (composite PK) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | References another table's PK? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Underlined in ER diagram? | ✅ Solid underline | ❌ Not marked | ❌ Not marked | ❌ Dashed line to referenced PK | | Indexed automatically? | ✅ Clustered by default (most RDBMS) | ✅ Unique index | ❌ Manual | ✅ Index created | ## 3.4 Multi-Valued Attribute Transformation to Relational Schema For a `PERSON` entity with `PhoneNumbers` (multi-valued), the standard mapping rule is: **Rule (KTU Algorithm — Step-by-Step):** $$ \text{If } A \text{ is multi-valued, create a new relation } R_A \text{ with: } $$ $$ R_A(\text{PK}(E),\; A) \quad \text{where both form the composite PK of } R_A $$ **Example:** Original: `PERSON(PersonId, Name, PhoneNumbers*)` (asterisk = multi-valued). After transformation: - `PERSON(PersonId, Name)` — with `PersonId` as primary key. - `PERSON_PHONE(PersonId, PhoneNumber)` — with **composite primary key** `(PersonId, PhoneNumber)`. > **Conversion Logic (Why this works):** A multi-valued attribute violates **1NF (First Normal Form)**, which mandates that every cell contain an *atomic* (single) value. Splitting it into a separate table eliminates the violation by storing each phone number as a separate row, and the composite key ensures *no duplicate* phone numbers per person can be stored. ## 3.5 Composite Attribute Flattening For `Name` as a composite attribute with sub-parts `FirstName`, `MiddleName`, `LastName`: **Rule:** $$ \text{Composite } A = \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_k\} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{expand into columns } A_1, A_2, \dots, A_k $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Composite attributes do *not* require a new table. They are *flattened* into separate columns in the same relation. Sub-parts of a composite attribute can independently be NULL (e.g., a person with no middle name). ## 3.6 Derived Attribute Handling For `Age` (derived from `DateOfBirth` and `CURRENT_DATE`): **Rule:** $$ \text{Derived } A = f(\text{Stored attributes}) \;\Longrightarrow\; A \text{ is NOT stored as a column (by default)} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Storing derived attributes causes *update anomalies* — the value must be recomputed and re-written every time the underlying stored attribute changes. Hence, derived attributes are *computed on query* rather than *persisted* in the base table. The exception is **materialized views / OLAP cubes**, where the cost of recomputation is high. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ER Diagram — Library Management Domain The following Mermaid block renders a comprehensive **Entity-Relationship diagram** showing one strong entity, one weak entity, multi-valued and derived attributes, and primary/foreign/composite keys. ```mermaid erDiagram MEMBER { string MemberID PK string FirstName string LastName string Email string MembershipType } BOOK { string ISBN PK string Title string Publisher int Edition string Category } AUTHOR { int AuthorID PK string AuthorName } LOAN { string LoanID PK string MemberID FK string ISBN FK date IssueDate date DueDate date ReturnDate } FINE { string LoanID FK string MemberID FK decimal Amount string Reason } MEMBER ||--o{ LOAN : "borrows" BOOK ||--o{ LOAN : "is issued in" BOOK }o--o{ AUTHOR : "written by" LOAN ||--o| FINE : "may generate" ``` **How to read the diagram:** - `PK` after an attribute name = **Primary Key** (drawn with solid underline in textbook notation). - `FK` after an attribute name = **Foreign Key** (drawn with dashed underline / arrow). - `||--o{` = **mandatory one** to **optional many** participation. - `}o--o{` = **optional many** to **optional many** (M:N) relationship. - `||--o|` = **mandatory one** to **optional one** (1:0..1). ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — The ER-to-Relational Mapping Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Real World Domain
e.g., University System] --> B1[Identify Entities
Noun Extraction] B1 --> C1[Form Entity Types
Group by Common Attributes] C1 --> D1[Assign Attributes
Simple/Composite/Multi-valued/Derived] D1 --> E1[Identify Keys
Super to Candidate to Primary] E1 --> F1[Draw ER Diagram
Rects Ovals Diamonds] F1 --> G1[Map to Relational Schema
Tables Columns Constraints] G1 --> H1[Normalize
1NF 2NF 3NF BCNF] H1 --> I1[Final Database
Ready for SQL DDL] E1 -.->|Foreign Key Identification| G1 D1 -.->|Multi-valued and Weak Entities| G1 ``` **Block Interpretation (KTU Mapping Algorithm):** | Block | Function | KTU Reference | |-------|----------|---------------| | `A1` → `B1` | Identify tangible / intangible things to be tracked. | Step 1 of ER modelling | | `B1` → `C1` | Cluster entities sharing a common attribute set into one *type*. | Step 2 — schema definition | | `C1` → `D1` | Attach properties (attributes) to each type. | Step 3 — attribute assignment | | `D1` → `E1` | Compute candidate keys, select primary, mark foreign. | Step 4 — key analysis | | `E1` → `F1` | Render graphical ER diagram using Chen's notation. | Step 5 — visualization | | `F1` → `G1` | Apply 7 standard mapping rules (atomic, composite flattening, weak entity split, etc.). | Step 6 — relational mapping | | `G1` → `H1` | Decompose tables to remove redundancy and anomalies. | Step 7 — normalization | | `H1` → `I1` | Generate SQL `CREATE TABLE` scripts. | Final deliverable | ## 4.3 Key-Identification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD K1[Given set of attributes S] --> K2{Is S unique
for every entity?} K2 -->|No| K3[Not a Super Key
Discard] K2 -->|Yes| K4[Super Key Confirmed] K4 --> K5{Is S minimal?
i.e., removing any attribute
breaks uniqueness} K5 -->|No| K6[Super Key only
Not a Candidate Key] K5 -->|Yes| K7[Candidate Key] K7 --> K8{Is S the chosen
Primary Key by designer?} K8 -->|Yes| K9[Primary Key
Underlined in ER diagram] K8 -->|No| K10[Alternate Key
Eligible for UNIQUE constraint] ``` > **Reading the Decision Tree:** Start at the top with any candidate set of attributes. Test **uniqueness** first (the *necessary* condition for any key). If it passes, test **minimality** (the *additional* condition for a candidate key). Finally, the *designer's election* elevates a candidate key to the primary key. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** *Differentiate between an Entity Type and an Entity Set with a suitable example.* `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Dimension | Entity Type | Entity Set | |-----------|-------------|------------| | **Level** | Schema-level / logical blueprint | Instance-level / data snapshot | | **Nature** | A *definition* of structure | A *collection* of actual data rows | | **Mutability** | Changes only when the design changes | Changes with every insert / delete / update | | **Time-dependence** | Time-independent | Time-dependent (varies with $t$) | | **Example** | `STUDENT` — a concept with attributes `RollNo, Name, DOB` | The current semester's 4,800 enrolled students — `{S001-Anu, S002-Rahul, …}` | > **Examiner's Tip:** Mention that an entity type is analogous to a *class* in OOP, while an entity set is analogous to *objects* of that class. Drawing this analogy often fetches a 0.5 to 1 mark bonus in KTU valuation. ### **Q2.** *What is a Candidate Key? How is it different from a Super Key and a Primary Key?* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **Candidate Key** is a *minimal* super key — a set of attributes that uniquely identifies an entity and from which **no attribute can be removed** without losing uniqueness. $$ \text{Super Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Candidate Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Primary Key} $$ | Property | Super Key | Candidate Key | Primary Key | |----------|:---------:|:-------------:|:-----------:| | Unique identification | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Minimal (no redundant attribute) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Chosen by designer | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Number per entity type | Many | Many | Exactly **one** (or one composite) | | Example (STUDENT) | `{RollNo, Name}`, `{AadharNo}` | `{RollNo}`, `{AadharNo}` | `{RollNo}` | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before You Write** > 1. **Do NOT** use the term *"primary attribute"* interchangeably with *"key attribute"* — they mean different things in KTU textbooks. > 2. **Always** justify the choice of primary key with the **3 criteria**: Uniqueness, Non-nullness, and Stability/Immutability. > 3. **Foreign keys** must be shown with a *dashed arrow* pointing from the FK in the child entity to the PK in the parent entity. A solid line is marked wrong. > 4. **Weak entities** must always be drawn with a *double rectangle*; missing this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > 5. **Multi-valued attributes** need a *double oval*; **derived attributes** need a *dashed oval*. Both omissions cost 1 mark each. > 6. In **mapping rules**, write the rule *name* and *one line of reasoning* — never just write the resulting table. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *ER Modelling from a Real-World Scenario* `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` Consider a **Hospital Management System** with the following requirements: - Each `PATIENT` is identified by a `PatientID` and has `Name`, `Age`, `Gender`, and a multi-valued `PhoneNumber`. - Each `DOCTOR` is identified by a `DoctorID` and has `Name`, `Specialization`, and `ConsultationFee`. - A `PATIENT` may be admitted to a `WARD`. Each `WARD` is identified by a `WardNo` and has a `WardType` (General / ICU / Private). - A `DOCTOR` attends to many `PATIENT`s, and a `PATIENT` may be treated by many `DOCTOR`s. - Each admission is recorded in `ADMISSION` with `DateOfAdmission`, `DateOfDischarge`, and a derived `DurationOfStay`. **Solve the following:** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Identify all **entity types, attributes (with classification)**, and **keys**. Mark primary keys and foreign keys clearly. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Draw the **complete ER diagram** and write the **relational schema** (mapped tables) with all constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL, UNIQUE) explicitly stated. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Step 1 — Entity Types Identified:** $$ E_{\text{types}} = \{\text{PATIENT},\; \text{DOCTOR},\; \text{WARD}\} $$ The relationship *attends to* is M:N, so we introduce an **associative entity** `TREATMENT` (resolves the many-to-many): $$ E_{\text{types}} = \{\text{PATIENT},\; \text{DOCTOR},\; \text{WARD},\; \text{TREATMENT}\} $$ The relationship *admitted to* between `PATIENT` and `WARD` is recorded in the weak entity `ADMISSION` (a patient may have multiple admissions; each admission needs a date): $$ E_{\text{final}} = \{\text{PATIENT},\; \text{DOCTOR},\; \text{WARD},\; \text{TREATMENT},\; \text{ADMISSION (weak)}\} $$ **Step 2 — Attribute Classification:** | Entity | Attribute | Type | Classification | |--------|-----------|------|----------------| | `PATIENT` | `PatientID` | Simple + Single + Stored | **Primary Key** | | `PATIENT` | `Name` | Composite | `{FirstName, LastName}` | | `PATIENT` | `Age` | Simple + Derived | Computed from `DOB` and current date | | `PATIENT` | `Gender` | Simple + Single + Stored | Non-key | | `PATIENT` | `PhoneNumber` | Simple + **Multi-Valued** + Stored | **Multi-valued** (double oval) | | `DOCTOR` | `DoctorID` | Simple + Single | **Primary Key** | | `DOCTOR` | `Name` | Simple | Non-key | | `DOCTOR` | `Specialization` | Simple | Non-key | | `DOCTOR` | `ConsultationFee` | Simple | Non-key | | `WARD` | `WardNo` | Simple | **Primary Key** | | `WARD` | `WardType` | Simple | Non-key (could be enum) | | `ADMISSION` | `AdmissionNo` | Simple | **Partial Key (Discriminator)** | | `ADMISSION` | `DateOfAdmission` | Simple + Stored | Non-key | | `ADMISSION` | `DateOfDischarge` | Simple + Stored | NULL-permitted (until discharged) | | `ADMISSION` | `DurationOfStay` | Simple + **Derived** | Computed (dashed oval) | | `TREATMENT` | `DateOfTreatment` | Simple | Part of composite PK | | `TREATMENT` | `Diagnosis` | Simple | Non-key | **Step 3 — Key Identification:** $$ \text{PK}(\text{PATIENT}) = \{\text{PatientID}\} $$ $$ \text{PK}(\text{DOCTOR}) = \{\text{DoctorID}\} $$ $$ \text{PK}(\text{WARD}) = \{\text{WardNo}\} $$ $$ \text{PK}(\text{TREATMENT}) = \{\text{PatientID},\; \text{DoctorID},\; \text{DateOfTreatment}\} $$ $$ \text{PK}(\text{ADMISSION}) = \{\text{PatientID},\; \text{WardNo},\; \text{AdmissionNo}\} $$ **Step 4 — Foreign Keys:** $$ \text{FK}(\text{ADMISSION}) = \{\text{PatientID} \rightarrow \text{PK(PATIENT)},\; \text{WardNo} \rightarrow \text{PK(WARD)}\} $$ $$ \text{FK}(\text{TREATMENT}) = \{\text{PatientID} \rightarrow \text{PK(PATIENT)},\; \text{DoctorID} \rightarrow \text{PK(DOCTOR)}\} $$ > **Conversion Logic (Step 4):** Foreign keys are identified by recognizing any attribute whose value set *overlaps with the domain* of a primary key in another entity set. They are *necessary* to maintain referential integrity between related entities. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **ER Diagram (Mermaid rendering):** ```mermaid erDiagram PATIENT { string PatientID PK string FirstName string LastName int Age string Gender string PhoneNumber } DOCTOR { string DoctorID PK string Name string Specialization decimal ConsultationFee } WARD { string WardNo PK string WardType } TREATMENT { string PatientID FK string DoctorID FK date DateOfTreatment string Diagnosis } ADMISSION { string PatientID FK string WardNo FK string AdmissionNo date DateOfAdmission date DateOfDischarge int DurationOfStay } PATIENT ||--o{ TREATMENT : "receives" DOCTOR ||--o{ TREATMENT : "provides" PATIENT ||--o{ ADMISSION : "is admitted in" WARD ||--o{ ADMISSION : "houses" ``` **Relational Schema (Mapped Tables):** ```sql PATIENT ( PatientID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, FirstName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(50), Age INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (DATEDIFF(YEAR, DOB, GETDATE())), Gender CHAR(1) CHECK (Gender IN ('M','F','O')) ); PATIENT_PHONE ( PatientID VARCHAR(10), PhoneNumber VARCHAR(15), PRIMARY KEY (PatientID, PhoneNumber), FOREIGN KEY (PatientID) REFERENCES PATIENT(PatientID) ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE ); DOCTOR ( DoctorID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, Name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, Specialization VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, ConsultationFee DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (ConsultationFee >= 0) ); WARD ( WardNo VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, WardType VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (WardType IN ('General','ICU','Private')) ); TREATMENT ( PatientID VARCHAR(10), DoctorID VARCHAR(10), DateOfTreatment DATE, Diagnosis TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (PatientID, DoctorID, DateOfTreatment), FOREIGN KEY (PatientID) REFERENCES PATIENT(PatientID), FOREIGN KEY (DoctorID) REFERENCES DOCTOR(DoctorID) ); ADMISSION ( PatientID VARCHAR(10), WardNo VARCHAR(10), AdmissionNo VARCHAR(10), DateOfAdmission DATE NOT NULL, DateOfDischarge DATE, DurationOfStay INT AS (DATEDIFF(DAY, DateOfAdmission, ISNULL(DateOfDischarge, GETDATE()))), PRIMARY KEY (PatientID, WardNo, AdmissionNo), FOREIGN KEY (PatientID) REFERENCES PATIENT(PatientID), FOREIGN KEY (WardNo) REFERENCES WARD(WardNo), CHECK (DateOfDischarge IS NULL OR DateOfDischarge >= DateOfAdmission) ); ``` > **Conversion Logic for Multi-valued `PhoneNumber`:** A separate table `PATIENT_PHONE` was created with composite primary key `(PatientID, PhoneNumber)`. This preserves atomicity (1NF) while retaining the ability to store *multiple* phone numbers per patient. > > **Conversion Logic for Weak Entity `ADMISSION`:** Its primary key is composed of the `PatientID` (owner's PK) + `WardNo` (owner's PK of `WARD`) + `AdmissionNo` (discriminator). The double-rectangle in the ER diagram translates to a composite primary key in the relational schema. > > **Conversion Logic for Derived `Age` and `DurationOfStay`:** Shown as computed columns. In RDBMS, they are *not* physically stored but recomputed on demand; this avoids update anomalies. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Key Analysis & Attribute Classification* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` Consider a **University Examination System** with the following entity type: `ENROLLMENT(EnrollmentID, RegNo, CourseCode, Semester, ExamYear, Marks, Grade, CreditPoints)` Given the functional dependencies: - `EnrollmentID → {RegNo, CourseCode, Semester, ExamYear, Marks, Grade, CreditPoints}` - `{RegNo, CourseCode, Semester, ExamYear} → {Marks, Grade, CreditPoints, EnrollmentID}` **Solve the following:** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Identify all **candidate keys** and the **primary key** for the `ENROLLMENT` entity type. Justify your choice with a clear step-by-step minimality test. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Classify *every* attribute in the schema. Then write the **relational schema** with PK, FK (if any), UNIQUE, and NOT NULL constraints. State the **domain rule** for each attribute. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Step 1 — Identify all attributes:** $$ A = \{\text{EnrollmentID},\; \text{RegNo},\; \text{CourseCode},\; \text{Semester},\; \text{ExamYear},\; \text{Marks},\; \text{Grade},\; \text{CreditPoints}\} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Functional Dependencies (FDs):** The FDs say: - `EnrollmentID` determines *all other attributes* → so $\{\text{EnrollmentID}\}$ is a super key. - $\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}$ determines *all attributes* → so it is also a super key. **Step 3 — Test Minimality:** | Candidate Set | Remove 1 Attribute | Does Uniqueness Survive? | Verdict | |---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|---------| | $\{\text{EnrollmentID}\}$ | (single-attr) | — | Minimal ✅ | | $\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}$ | RegNo | A student re-takes a course with same code in same sem/year? Possibly. **Need it.** | Cannot drop | | $\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}$ | CourseCode | Same student, same sem, same year can take 2 different courses? **Yes** | Cannot drop | | $\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}$ | Semester | Same student, same course, same year across two semesters?
