Unusual but theoretically possible (arrear + regular). **Could argue.** | Likely keep for safety | | $\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}$ | ExamYear | Same student, same course, same sem in two years? **Yes** (year-arrears). | Cannot drop | **Result:** $$ \text{CandidateKey}(\text{ENROLLMENT}) = \Big\{ \{\text{EnrollmentID}\},\; \{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\} \Big\} $$ **Step 4 — Select Primary Key:** $$ \text{PrimaryKey}(\text{ENROLLMENT}) = \{\text{EnrollmentID}\} $$ > **Justification for choosing `EnrollmentID`:** > 1. **Uniqueness** ✅ — given by FD. > 2. **Minimality** ✅ — single attribute. > 3. **Simplicity** — single-attribute PKs are easier to reference as foreign keys in `MARKS_AUDIT`, `RESULT`, etc. > 4. **Stability** — `EnrollmentID` is a system-generated surrogate that never changes once assigned. **Step 5 — Identify Alternate Key:** $$ \text{AlternateKey}(\text{ENROLLMENT}) = \{\{\text{RegNo}, \text{CourseCode}, \text{Semester}, \text{ExamYear}\}\} $$ This should be enforced with a `UNIQUE` constraint in SQL. **Step 6 — Identify Non-Key Attributes:** $$ \text{NonKey} = \{\text{Marks},\; \text{Grade},\; \text{CreditPoints}\} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Non-key attributes are *fully functionally dependent* on the primary key. Note that `CreditPoints` is *derived* from `CourseCode` (not from `EnrollmentID` directly) — this is a **transitive dependency** and signals a need for normalization into `COURSE(CourseCode, CreditPoints)`. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Attribute Classification Table:** | Attribute | Structural Type | Value Cardinality | Origin | Nullability | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------|--------|-------------| | `EnrollmentID` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NOT NULL | | `RegNo` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NOT NULL | | `CourseCode` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NOT NULL | | `Semester` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NOT NULL | | `ExamYear` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NOT NULL | | `Marks` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | NULL permitted (absent) | | `Grade` | Simple | Single-Valued | **Derived** (from `Marks` and `CourseCode`) | NULL permitted | | `CreditPoints` | Simple | Single-Valued | **Derived** (from `CourseCode`) | NOT NULL (assumed) | **Relational Schema with Constraints:** ```sql ENROLLMENT ( EnrollmentID VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY, RegNo VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, CourseCode VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, Semester INT NOT NULL CHECK (Semester BETWEEN 1 AND 12), ExamYear INT NOT NULL CHECK (ExamYear BETWEEN 2000 AND 2100), Marks INT CHECK (Marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100), Grade CHAR(2) CHECK (Grade IN ('S','A','B','C','D','E','F','I')), CreditPoints INT NOT NULL CHECK (CreditPoints BETWEEN 1 AND 10), UNIQUE (RegNo, CourseCode, Semester, ExamYear), -- Alternate Key FOREIGN KEY (RegNo) REFERENCES STUDENT(RegNo), FOREIGN KEY (CourseCode) REFERENCES COURSE(CourseCode) ); ``` **Domain Rule Specifications (KTU expects this in writing):** | Attribute | Domain $\quad D$ | Constraint | |-----------|------------------|------------| | `EnrollmentID` | `VARCHAR(15)` | Pattern: `ENR[0-9]{12}` | | `RegNo` | `VARCHAR(10)` | Pattern: `[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{8}` (KTU format) | | `CourseCode` | `VARCHAR(10)` | Pattern: `[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{3}[A-Z]?` | | `Semester` | `INT` | $1 \leq \text{Semester} \leq 12$ | | `ExamYear` | `INT` | $2000 \leq \text{ExamYear} \leq 2100$ | | `Marks` | `INT` | $0 \leq \text{Marks} \leq 100$ | | `Grade` | `CHAR(2)` | Discrete set: $\{S, A, B, C, D, E, F, I\}$ | | `CreditPoints` | `INT` | $1 \leq \text{CreditPoints} \leq 10$ | > **Conversion Logic (Why the FK to STUDENT and COURSE):** `RegNo` references the primary key of the `STUDENT` entity (which is the `RegNo` itself), enforcing that no enrollment can exist for a non-registered student. Similarly, `CourseCode` references the `COURSE` table, ensuring only valid courses are enrolled into. These two foreign keys together form a **referential integrity bridge** that prevents orphan records. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1** - 🔑 **Entity vs Entity Type vs Entity Set:** Entity = single thing, Entity Type = blueprint/schema, Entity Set = collection of instances at time $t$. - 🔑 **Strong vs Weak Entity:** Strong has its own PK; weak needs owner entity's PK + discriminator (partial key). - 🔑 **Six Attribute Classifications:** Simple/Composite, Single/Multi-valued, Stored/Derived, plus NULL-allowed. - 🔑 **Composite attributes** are flattened into separate columns — they do *not* need a new table. - 🔑 **Multi-valued attributes** *do* need a new table (with composite PK) — this is a **1NF violation fix**. - 🔑 **Derived attributes** are computed on query; do not store them (or use generated/computed columns). - 🔑 **Key hierarchy:** Super Key ⊇ Candidate Key ⊇ Primary Key; remaining candidates = Alternate Keys. - 🔑 **Primary Key rules:** Unique, Not Null, Minimal, Stable/Immutable, preferably single-attribute. - 🔑 **Composite Key** = a key with 2+ attributes; each individual attribute alone may *not* be unique. - 🔑 **Partial Key (Discriminator)** belongs to a weak entity; the complete key = Owner's PK + Discriminator. - 🔑 **Foreign Key** references a Primary Key of another (or same) table; enforces *referential integrity*; *not* required to be unique. - 🔑 **ER Notation Cheat Sheet:** Rectangle = Entity, Ellipse = Attribute, Double Rectangle = Weak Entity, Double Ellipse = Multi-valued, Dashed Ellipse = Derived, Diamond = Relationship, Underline = Primary Key, Dashed Underline = Partial Key. - 🔑 **Mermaid Notation:** `PK` = Primary Key, `FK` = Foreign Key, `||--o{` = one-to-many, `}o--o{` = many-to-many. - 🔑 **Mapping Rule (Multi-valued):** Create a new relation $R(\text{PK}_{\text{parent}}, A_{\text{multi}})$ with composite PK. - 🔑 **Mapping Rule (Weak Entity):** Create relation with composite PK = $\text{PK}_{\text{owner}} \cup \text{Discriminator}$. - 🔑 **Mapping Rule (1:1):** Merge into one table; place FK on the side with **total participation**. - 🔑 **Mapping Rule (1:N):** Place FK on the *many* side. - 🔑 **Mapping Rule (M:N):** Create a *new associative relation* with FKs to both parent tables, both as composite PK. - 🔑 **KTU valuation penalties:** Missing double-rectangle for weak entity (-2), missing double-oval for multi-valued (-1), missing dashed oval for derived (-1), solid line for foreign key (-1). - 🔑 **Real-world anchors:** Banking (AccountNumber PK, IFSC FK), University (RegNo PK, DeptId FK), Hospital (PatientID PK, DoctorID FK), E-Commerce (OrderId PK, ProductId FK). - 🔑 **Examiner's hot-button topics (2024):** "Classify attribute X", "Identify candidate keys from FDs", "Map ER to relational schema", "Justify primary key choice", "Differentiate strong vs weak entity". > ✅ **End of Module 1 Notes — Entity Types, Entity Sets, Attributes, and Keys**

topics.title: Relationship Types, Relationship Sets, Roles, and Structural Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship Types, Relationship Sets, Roles, and Structural Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Relationship Type?

In the **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model**, a **Relationship Type** is a meaningful association between two or more entity types that captures how the entities interact in the real world. Formally, a relationship type $R$ of degree $n$ is a set of mappings among $n$ entity types $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$ over the same universe of discourse.

$$R = \{ (e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n) \mid e_1 \in E_1, e_2 \in E_2, \ldots, e_n \in E_n \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *Relationship Type* is a schema-level (intension) construct, whereas a *Relationship Set* is the actual collection of relationship instances (extension) at a point in time. This distinction is a guaranteed **2-mark question** in KTU exams.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The University System

Imagine a university. **Students** and **Courses** are entity types. The act of a *student enrolling in a course* is a relationship. If we add a **Professor** who *teaches* a course, that becomes another relationship. So:

- **Entities** = The "nouns" (Students, Courses, Professors)
- **Relationships** = The "verbs" (Enrolls_In, Teaches, Advises)

A **Relationship Set** is the *current snapshot* of all such pairings (e.g., the enrollment list for Semester 6 in 2024), while a **Relationship Type** is the *blueprint* describing that "students enroll in courses" in general.

### 1.3 What are Roles?

A **Role** is the specific function that an entity plays in a relationship. Roles become mandatory when an entity type participates **more than once** in the same relationship type — this special case is called a **Recursive Relationship** or **Unary Relationship**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role Naming Convention:** When an entity type relates to itself, you MUST assign distinct role labels. For example, an employee *supervises* another employee. The same `Employee` entity type plays the role of **Supervisor** and **Subordinate**.

### 1.4 Structural Constraints — The Guardrails

Structural constraints are rules that **restrict the possible combinations of entities** that may participate in a relationship set. They are of two principal kinds:

1. **Cardinality Ratio Constraint (Mapping Cardinality)** — Specifies the maximum number of relationship instances an entity can participate in. Common ratios: **1:1**, **1:N**, **M:N**.
2. **Participation Constraint (Existence Dependency)** — Specifies whether every entity in the entity set MUST participate in the relationship. Values: **Total Participation** (every entity must) or **Partial Participation** (some may not).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** The cardinality ratio is about the **maximum** participation; participation constraint is about the **minimum** (0 or 1). This dual perspective is the single most important conceptual pitfall in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Relationship Types by Degree

The **degree** of a relationship type refers to the number of participating entity types. This classification is foundational and frequently tested.

**Degree 1 — Unary (Recursive) Relationship**
- Only **one** entity type participates.
- An entity instance is related to another instance of the **same** set.
- Examples: `Employee supervises Employee`, `Person marries Person`, `Course is_prerequisite_of Course`.

**Degree 2 — Binary Relationship**
- The most common type. Two entity types participate.
- Examples: `Student enrolls_in Course`, `Department manages Employee`, `Author writes Book`.

**Degree 3 — Ternary Relationship**
- Three entity types participate simultaneously in a single relationship.
- Example: `Supplier supplies Part to Project` (cannot be decomposed into binary relationships without losing semantic meaning).

**Degree n — n-ary Relationship**
- General case with $n$ participating entity types. KTU syllabus typically focuses on $n \leq 3$.

### 2.2 Cardinality Ratio Constraints — Detailed Taxonomy

For a binary relationship $R$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$, the cardinality ratio is defined based on how many $E_2$ entities an $E_1$ entity can relate to, and vice versa.

| Cardinality Ratio | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1 : 1** | One $E_1$ entity is related to at most one $E_2$ entity | One Department is managed by exactly one Employee |
| **1 : N** | One $E_1$ entity relates to many $E_2$ entities, but each $E_2$ relates to only one $E_1$ | One Department employs many Employees |
| **M : N** | Many-to-many; each $E_1$ relates to many $E_2$ and vice versa | Students enroll in many Courses; Courses have many Students |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In an M:N relationship, a *separate* relationship table is mandatory during relational mapping. In 1:N, the relationship can be folded into the "many" side's table. In 1:1, either side can carry the foreign key.

### 2.3 Participation Constraint (Existence Dependency)

This is a **min-cardinality** specification.

- **Total Participation** (Existence Dependency): Every entity in the entity set MUST participate in at least one relationship instance. Drawn as a **double line** in ER diagrams. Notation: $\rightarrow$ "every $E$ relates to at least one..."
- **Partial Participation** (Optional): Some entities may not participate in any relationship instance. Drawn as a **single line**.

**Example:** In `Employee works_in Department`, every employee must work in *some* department (total), but a department may exist with zero employees temporarily (partial).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Relationship type (intension) | Schema-level blueprint |
| $r$ | Relationship set (extension) | Current instances |
| $\deg(R)$ | Degree of relationship | Number of participating entity types |
| $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$ | Participating entity types | Sets of entities |
| $1:1, 1:N, M:N$ | Cardinality ratios | Max-participation rule |
| Total line (double) | Total participation | Every entity participates |
| Single line | Partial participation | Optional participation |
| Role label | Function of an entity | Required for recursive relations |
| $n$-ary | General $n$-degree relation | $n \geq 4$ rarely used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Structural constraints are critical in **database design optimization**. They determine indexing strategy, foreign-key placement, referential integrity rules, and even affect query performance in large-scale systems (e.g., banking, e-commerce, hospital information systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Mathematical Derivation of Relationship Set Cardinality

**Problem Setup:** Given two entity sets $E_1$ and $E_2$ with $|E_1| = m$ and $|E_2| = n$, derive the maximum possible size of a relationship set $r$ under each cardinality constraint.

**Case 1: 1:1 Cardinality Ratio**

Each entity in $E_1$ can relate to **at most 1** entity in $E_2$, and vice versa. Therefore, the relationship set $r$ can have **at most $\min(m, n)$** instances.

$$\vert r \vert_{\max}^{1:1} = \min(m, n)$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- Step 1: Each $e_1 \in E_1$ participates in at most 1 relationship — so the number of "left-side" links is bounded by $m$.
- Step 2: Each $e_2 \in E_2$ participates in at most 1 relationship — so the number of "right-side" links is bounded by $n$.
- Step 3: Both ends must balance, hence the cardinality of $r$ cannot exceed the smaller of the two.

**Case 2: 1:N Cardinality Ratio (One $E_1$ to Many $E_2$)**

Each $e_1$ can relate to **many** $e_2$ entities, but each $e_2$ relates to **at most 1** $e_1$. The maximum is bounded by the size of the "many" side, i.e., $n$.

$$\vert r \vert_{\max}^{1:N} = n$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- Step 1: $E_1$ is the "one" side — each of the $m$ entities can connect to multiple $E_2$ entities.
- Step 2: $E_2$ is the "many" side — each of the $n$ entities is linked to exactly 1 $E_1$ entity.
- Step 3: The total links equal the number of $E_2$ entities in the worst case, giving $\vert r \vert_{\max} = n$.

**Case 3: M:N Cardinality Ratio**

Every $e_1$ can relate to **every** $e_2$ (in the absence of further constraints). The maximum is the Cartesian product.

$$\vert r \vert_{\max}^{M:N} = m \times n$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- Step 1: Any $e_1 \in E_1$ can pair with any $e_2 \in E_2$.
- Step 2: The total number of unique pairings is given by the Cartesian product $E_1 \times E_2$.
- Step 3: Hence, the upper bound is $m \times n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Application:** If the question says "A university has 500 students and 50 courses in an M:N enrollment relationship, what is the maximum number of enrollment records?" — Apply $\vert r \vert = 500 \times 50 = 25{,}000$ directly.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Choosing Relationship Type

| Scenario in Problem Statement | Relationship Degree | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Same entity type associated with itself | Unary (Recursive) | `Employee manages Employee` |
| Two distinct entity types interact | Binary | `Student borrows Book` |
| Three entities always participate together as a single fact | Ternary | `Doctor treats Patient using Medicine` |
| Action verb applies between exactly 2 distinct types | Binary (default) | `Author writes Book` |
| Existence of one entity depends on another | Total participation on the dependent side | `Dependent` entity in `Employee` |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Mapping a Real Scenario to ER Constructs

**Scenario:** A company database has `Employee` and `Project`. Each employee works on multiple projects, and each project has multiple employees. Every project must have at least one employee, but some employees may not yet be assigned.

**Step 1 — Identify Entity Types:**
$E_1 = $ `Employee`, $E_2 = $ `Project`.

**Step 2 — Identify Relationship Type:**
`Works_On` is a binary relationship $R$ of degree 2.

**Step 3 — Determine Cardinality Ratio:**
Since an employee can be on many projects AND a project has many employees, this is **M:N**.

**Step 4 — Determine Participation Constraint:**
- `Project` side: **Total** (every project must have ≥ 1 employee) — **double line**.
- `Employee` side: **Partial** (some may be unassigned) — **single line**.

**Step 5 — Write Formal Specification:**

$$R = \text{Works\_On}, \quad \deg(R) = 2, \quad \text{card}(R) = M:N$$

Participation: $\text{Project} \xrightarrow{\text{total}} R \xleftarrow{\text{partial}} \text{Employee}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Insight for KTU:** When mapping this M:N relationship to a relational schema, you create a separate table `Works_On(EmpID, ProjID, Hours)` with a composite primary key `(EmpID, ProjID)` and foreign keys to both parent tables.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid ER Diagram — Binary 1:N Relationship (Department–Employee)

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "contains"
    DEPARTMENT {
        string dept_id PK
        string dept_name
    }
    EMPLOYEE {
        string emp_id PK
        string emp_name
        string dept_id FK
    }
```

**Interpretation of Symbols:**

- `||` = exactly one (mandatory single participation).
- `o{` = zero or many (optional on the many side).
- `}o--o{` would mean many-to-many (M:N) with both sides optional.

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing Relationship Degree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Relationship] --> B{How many distinct entity types?}
    B -- "1 (same type)" --> C[Unary or Recursive Relationship]
    B -- "2 types" --> D[Binary Relationship]
    B -- "3 types" --> E[Ternary Relationship]
    B -- "More than 3" --> F[n-ary Relationship]
    C --> G{Does it need role labels?}
    G -- "Yes" --> H[Assign Supervisor/Subordinate etc.]
    G -- "No" --> I[Simple recursive]
    D --> J{One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-Many?}
    J -- "1 to 1" --> K[1:1 Cardinality]
    J -- "1 to N" --> L[1:N Cardinality]
    J -- "M to N" --> M[M:N Cardinality]
    K --> N[Apply Participation Constraint]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O{Every entity must participate?}
    O -- "Yes" --> P[Total Participation - double line]
    O -- "No" --> Q[Partial Participation - single line]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Structural Constraints Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EntitySetA["Entity Set A: EMPLOYEE"]
        EA1[Employee E1]
        EA2[Employee E2]
        EA3[Employee E3]
    end
    subgraph RelationshipR["Relationship Type R: WORKS_ON"]
        R1[R1: E1 - P1]
        R2[R2: E2 - P1]
        R3[R3: E2 - P2]
        R4[R4: E3 - P2]
    end
    subgraph EntitySetB["Entity Set B: PROJECT"]
        EB1[Project P1]
        EB2[Project P2]
        EB3[Project P3]
    end
    EA1 -.-> R1
    EA2 -.-> R2
    EA2 -.-> R3
    EA3 -.-> R4
    R1 ==> EB1
    R2 ==> EB1
    R3 ==> EB2
    R4 ==> EB2
    style RelationshipR fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EntitySetA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style EntitySetB fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Solid arrows (`-.->`) show participation links from entities to relationship instances.
- Bold arrows (`==>`) show the connection from the relationship to the "many" side.
- Notice that $P_3$ has **no incoming relationship link** — this represents a project that has no employee assigned (a case of *partial participation* on the project side, or a violation if project side is total).

### 4.4 Mermaid Schema — Cardinality Ratio Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Relationship Cardinality] --> OneOne[1:1 - One to One]
    Start --> OneN[1:N - One to Many]
    Start --> MN[M:N - Many to Many]
    OneOne --> Ex1[Example: Each employee has one parking slot; each slot assigned to one employee]
    OneN --> Ex2[Example: One department has many employees; each employee belongs to one department]
    MN --> Ex3[Example: Students take many courses; courses have many students]
    Ex1 --> Note1[Requires UNIQUE constraint on foreign key]
    Ex2 --> Note2[FK placed on the MANY side]
    Ex3 --> Note3[Requires a separate junction/bridge table]
    style Note1 fill:#fadbd8
    style Note2 fill:#d5f5e3
    style Note3 fill:#fcf3cf
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

*Define the following terms with an example each:*
*(a) Unary relationship*
*(b) Ternary relationship*
*(c) Role in a relationship*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Unary Relationship:** A unary (or recursive) relationship is a relationship type in which **only one entity type** participates, but in **two or more different roles**. It associates instances of the same entity set with one another. *Example:* An `Employee` *supervises* another `Employee`; here, the same entity set plays the role of `Supervisor` and `Subordinate`.

**(b) Ternary Relationship:** A ternary relationship is a relationship type of **degree three** in which three distinct entity types participate simultaneously as a single associative fact. *Example:* In a hospital, the relationship `Doctor treats Patient using Medicine` involves the entity types `Doctor`, `Patient`, and `Medicine` at the same time. It cannot be losslessly decomposed into three binary relationships.

**(c) Role in a Relationship:** A role is the **named function** that an entity plays within a relationship. Roles are **mandatory** when an entity type participates multiple times in a relationship (recursive case). *Example:* In `Employee reports_to Employee`, one entity plays the role of `Manager` and the other plays the role of `Subordinate`. Without role labels, the relationship is ambiguous.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

*Differentiate between the cardinality ratio constraint and the participation constraint in the ER model. Why are both needed together to fully specify a relationship?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cardinality Ratio Constraint | Participation Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Limit** | Maximum participation | Minimum participation |
| **Common Values** | 1:1, 1:N, M:N | Total (must) or Partial (may) |
| **Question Answered** | "How many?" (max) | "Must it?" (min) |
| **ER Diagram Notation** | 1, N, M labels on edges | Single line vs. double line |

**Why both are needed together:**

A relationship specification requires **two independent limits** to be fully constrained. Consider the example: a project must have **at least one** employee (minimum = 1, total participation) and a project can have **at most 50** employees (maximum = 50, a 1:N-like limit on the project side). The cardinality ratio alone would tell us the maximum, but not whether the project MUST have employees. The participation constraint fills in the minimum requirement. Hence, **both are complementary and jointly necessary** to capture the complete business rule.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse "cardinality" with "participation" or fail to draw double lines correctly for total participation. Loss of **2–3 marks per sub-question** is common. Always label both the cardinality ratio AND the participation side, and **never** assume a relationship is total without justification.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Based on KTU 2024 Pattern*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a library database. `Member` entities borrow `Book` entities. A member can borrow many books, and a book can be borrowed by many members over time, but a specific book copy is issued to at most one member at any given time. Identify the degree of the relationship, the cardinality ratio, and the participation constraints. Draw the ER diagram.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Entity Types and Relationship:**

- $E_1 = $ `Member`, $E_2 = $ `Book`, Relationship $R = $ `Borrows`.

**Step 2 — Determine Degree:**

Two entity types participate, so $\deg(R) = 2$ → **Binary Relationship**.

**Step 3 — Determine Cardinality Ratio:**

Since a member can borrow many books, and a book can be borrowed by many members, this is **M:N** cardinality.

**Step 4 — Determine Participation Constraints:**

- `Member` side: **Total** — every member must have borrowed at least one book (assume active members). **Double line.**
- `Book` side: **Partial** — newly arrived books may not yet be borrowed. **Single line.**

**Step 5 — ER Diagram (textual description):**

```
+-------------+                    +----------+
|   MEMBER    |==== double line ===|   BOOK   |
+-------------+                    +----------+
       |                                 |
       +------------ M : N --------------+
                  (Borrows)
```

**Step 6 — Formal Notation:**

$$R = \text{Borrows}, \quad \deg(R) = 2, \quad \text{card}(R) = M:N$$

Participation: $\text{Member} \xrightarrow{\text{total}} R \xleftarrow{\text{partial}} \text{Book}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **recursive relationship** with a suitable example. Why are role names mandatory in this case? Discuss the relational mapping strategy for a 1:N recursive relationship.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Recursive Relationship:**

A recursive (or unary) relationship is one in which an entity type is related **to itself**. The same entity set participates in the relationship more than once, each time in a different **role**.

**Step 2 — Suitable Example:**

In a corporate `Employee` database, the relationship `Supervises` is recursive. An employee (the supervisor) supervises other employees (the subordinates). Formally:

$$R_{\text{supervises}} = \{ (e_i, e_j) \mid e_i, e_j \in \text{Employee} \text{ and } e_i \text{ supervises } e_j \}$$

Here `Employee` plays the role of **Supervisor** on one side and **Subordinate** on the other.

**Step 3 — Why Role Names are Mandatory:**

If we wrote `Employee relates Employee` without role labels, the relationship would be **semantically ambiguous** — we would not know which employee is the supervisor and which is the subordinate. Roles resolve this ambiguity by **naming the function** of each occurrence. In ER notation, the edge from the entity type to the relationship is labeled with the role name (e.g., `Supervisor`, `Subordinate`).

**Step 4 — Relational Mapping for 1:N Recursive Relationship:**

Suppose only one employee can supervise another (a 1:N recursive case). In the relational schema, the `Employee` table carries a self-referencing foreign key:

| Attribute | Type | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| `emp_id` | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY |
| `emp_name` | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL |
| `supervisor_id` | INTEGER | FOREIGN KEY references `Employee(emp_id)` |

**Step 5 — Conversion Rule:**

The "1" side of the recursive relationship receives a foreign key pointing to its own primary key. This avoids creating a separate relationship table, since the 1:N cardinality allows the relationship to be folded into the entity table itself.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a university examination system with the following entities: `Student`, `Course`, `Hall`, and `Invigilator` — where each exam event involves one course, one hall, one invigilator, and many students sitting in that hall for that paper — design the appropriate relationship structure. State its degree, justify why a single $n$-ary relationship is preferred over multiple binary relationships, and identify the cardinality and participation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Entity Types:**

$E_1 = $ `Student`, $E_2 = $ `Course`, $E_3 = $ `Hall`, $E_4 = $ `Invigilator`.

**Step 2 — Identify the Relationship:**

Let $R = $ `Conducts_Exam`. The exam is identified by `(Course, Hall, Invigilator)`, and a set of students attends it. Since **four** entity types participate as a single atomic fact, the relationship is **4-ary (quaternary)**.

$$\deg(R) = 4$$

**Step 3 — Justify n-ary over Multiple Binary Relationships:**

Splitting this into binary relationships would create **semantic loss** and **update anomalies**. For instance, if we had:

- `Student sits_in Hall`
- `Hall allocated_to Course`
- `Invigilator assigned_to Hall`

These three binary facts **do not together** guarantee that *the same exam event* has that specific student, that course, that hall, AND that invigilator. A 4-ary relationship preserves the **atomicity of the fact** "this student takes this course in this hall supervised by this invigilator." Hence, an n-ary relationship is **semantically necessary**.

**Step 4 — Cardinality and Participation:**

- Cardinality: A complex multi-way cardinality; specifically, for a fixed `(Course, Hall, Invigilator)`, there are **N students** (M:N-style on the Student side).
- Participation:
  - `Course`: **Total** — every exam event must have a course. (Double line)
  - `Hall`: **Total** — must have a hall. (Double line)
  - `Invigilator`: **Total** — must have an invigilator. (Double line)
  - `Student`: **Partial** — some students may not have appeared. (Single line)

**Step 5 — Relational Mapping:**

A separate relationship table is required since this is n-ary with N cardinality on the Student side:

`Exam_Allocation(Student_ID, Course_ID, Hall_ID, Invigilator_ID, Exam_Date)` with `(Course_ID, Hall_ID, Exam_Date)` as a logical candidate key and `Student_ID` forming a composite primary key with it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **participation constraint**? Explain total and partial participation with one example each. How would you represent them in an ER diagram?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

A **participation constraint** (also called **existence dependency** or **min-cardinality**) specifies whether **every entity** in an entity set must participate in **at least one** relationship instance of a given relationship type.

**Step 2 — Total Participation (Existence Dependency):**

- **Definition:** Every entity in the entity set MUST participate in the relationship.
- **Constraint:** $\forall e \in E, \exists r \in R$ such that $e$ participates in $r$.
- **ER Diagram Notation:** **Double line** from the entity set to the relationship diamond.
- **Example:** In `Loan_Account linked_with Customer`, every loan account must be associated with at least one customer. Hence, `Loan_Account` has total participation. If a loan account exists without a customer, the database violates the constraint.

**Step 3 — Partial Participation:**

- **Definition:** Some entities in the entity set may not participate in any relationship instance.
- **Constraint:** $\exists e \in E$ such that $e$ is not in any $r \in R$.
- **ER Diagram Notation:** **Single line** from the entity set to the relationship diamond.
- **Example:** In `Employee works_in Department`, a department may exist with **zero** employees temporarily (during its formation). Hence, `Department` has partial participation.

**Step 4 — Combined Effect with Cardinality:**

Suppose `Employee manages Department` and:
- Cardinality = 1:N (one employee manages many departments).
- Employee side = **Partial** (some employees don't manage).
- Department side = **Total** (every department has a manager).

This means: every department has **exactly one** manager (cardinality 1), and **at least one** (total participation) — combined, exactly one. This is a 1:1 mapping on the Department side.

**Step 5 — Why it Matters:**

Total participation influences **referential integrity** in the relational schema. A foreign key on the total-participation side must be `NOT NULL` to enforce the rule that every entity must link to a parent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relationship Type** is the schema/blueprint; **Relationship Set** is the actual data instances. This distinction is **routinely tested** in KTU Part A.
- **Degree of Relationship** = number of distinct participating entity types. Unary (1), Binary (2), Ternary (3), n-ary ($n$).
- **Roles** are **mandatory** in recursive (unary) relationships; they label the function of each entity occurrence to disambiguate.
- **Cardinality Ratio** = **maximum** participation (1:1, 1:N, M:N).
- **Participation Constraint** = **minimum** participation (Total = every entity must, Partial = may not).
- **ER Notation:** Single line = Partial; Double line = Total; "1, N, M" labels on the edge denote cardinality.
- **Ternary/n-ary relationships** are preferred over decomposed binaries when the original fact is **atomic** and cannot be reconstructed losslessly from binaries.
- **Relational Mapping Rule of Thumb:** M:N → separate junction table; 1:N → FK on the "many" side; 1:1 → FK on either side (typically the total-participation side).
- **Engineering Relevance:** Structural constraints drive **indexing**, **foreign-key placement**, **NOT NULL constraints**, and **referential integrity** rules in real-world DBMS like Oracle, MySQL, and PostgreSQL.
- **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse cardinality with participation. Remember: **Cardinality = MAX**, **Participation = MIN**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weak Entity Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weak Entity Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Entity-Relationship (ER) data model**, an **entity type** is classified as a **Weak Entity Type** if its instances (entities) **cannot be uniquely identified by the values of their own attributes alone**. The existence of a weak entity is logically dependent on another entity type, known as the **Identifying Entity Type** (or **Owner Entity Type** / **Parent Entity Type**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Textbook Phrasing):**
> *"A weak entity type is one whose existence depends on another entity type. It cannot be uniquely identified using only its own attributes and must logically rely on a strong (identifying) entity through an identifying relationship, where it borrows the primary key of the owner as part of its own identification."*

The structural components associated with a weak entity in an ER diagram are:

| Notation | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Weak Entity | **Double Rectangle** | The entity itself is weak |
| Identifying Relationship | **Double Diamond** | The relationship linking weak to owner |
| Partial Key (Discriminator) | **Dashed Underline** | A set of attributes that *almost* identifies the entity, but not fully |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Room in a Building"**
> Imagine you live in **Room 304** of a building. Saying only "Room 304" is **not enough** to identify it uniquely, because there are thousands of buildings that contain a Room 304. You must say *"Room 304 in Sunrise Apartments"* — i.e., the room depends on the building for its identification.
> 
> In this analogy:
> - **Building** = Identifying (Strong/Owner) Entity Type
> - **Room** = Weak Entity Type
> - **"304"** = Partial Key (Discriminator) — it is unique *only within* the building
> - **"Located-in"** = Identifying Relationship

This is the **central intuition**: a weak entity is essentially a "child" record that is meaningful only in the context of its "parent" record.

## 1.3 Existence Dependency vs. Identification Dependency

It is critical to distinguish two related but distinct concepts in the KTU 2024 framework:

- **Existence Dependency:** A weak entity *cannot exist* without the owner entity. If the owner is deleted, the weak entity must also be deleted (this is also called the **Total Participation Constraint** — every weak entity must participate in the identifying relationship).
- **Identification Dependency:** A weak entity *cannot be identified* without borrowing the primary key of the owner.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Exam Memory Hook:** Weak entity ⟹ **double rectangle** + **double diamond** + **dashed underline on partial key** + **total participation** (the relationship is mandatory from the weak side).

## 1.4 Formal Structural Properties (Summary)

A weak entity type in the KTU 2024 ER formalism must satisfy **all** of the following properties simultaneously:

1. It is drawn using a **double rectangle**.
2. It is connected to a strong entity through an **identifying relationship** drawn as a **double diamond**.
3. It possesses a **partial key (discriminator)** underlined with a **dashed line**.
4. The participation of the weak entity in the identifying relationship is **total (mandatory)** — drawn as a **double line** connecting the weak entity to the relationship.
5. It borrows the **primary key of the owner entity** to form its own composite primary key.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception Trap:** Not every entity that "depends" on another is weak. For example, an *Employee* entity depending on a *Department* for organizational purpose is **not necessarily a weak entity** if `Emp_ID` alone can identify employees. Weakness is strictly a property of **identifiability**, not just hierarchical dependence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Weak Entities Exist — The Conceptual "Why"

In a perfectly normalized conceptual schema, every entity *should* be uniquely identifiable. However, real-world business scenarios naturally contain scenarios where:

- An object **does not have a globally unique identifier** of its own (e.g., a *seat* in a *stadium* — seat numbers reset for each stadium).
- An object is a **logical sub-component** that only has meaning as a part of a larger whole (e.g., a *dependent* of an *employee*, a *room* in a *building*, a *transaction line item* in an *invoice*).
- Creating an artificial surrogate key would **violate the natural semantics** of the data model.

The ER model provides **weak entity types** as a first-class construct to model such situations **semantically**, rather than relying on artificial identifiers.

## 2.2 Structural Components — Deeper "How"

### Step 1: Identifying the Strong (Owner) Entity
The owner entity must have a **well-defined primary key** of its own. It is a regular strong entity drawn as a single rectangle.

### Step 2: Identifying the Weak Entity
The weak entity has its own descriptive attributes but **no globally unique key** among those attributes. It is drawn as a **double rectangle**.

### Step 3: Defining the Partial Key (Discriminator)
The partial key is a **minimal set of attributes** of the weak entity that, **within the context of a single owner entity instance**, uniquely identifies the weak entity instance. It is underlined with a **dashed line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Property of Partial Key:**
> Given an owner entity instance $O_i$ and a weak entity $W$ with partial key $P$:
> $$\forall w_1, w_2 \in W \text{ linked to } O_i : (w_1.P = w_2.P) \implies w_1 = w_2$$
> That is, $P$ is a key **locally** within one owner, but **not globally** across all owners.

### Step 4: Modeling the Identifying Relationship
The identifying relationship is drawn as a **double diamond**, connecting the weak entity to its owner. The cardinality is typically **1:N** (one owner to many weak entities), and the participation of the weak entity is **total**.

### Step 5: Establishing Total Participation
The double line from the weak entity to the identifying relationship diamond represents the **total participation constraint** — every weak entity instance must be linked to exactly one owner.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every "formula-like" rule you need for the Weak Entity topic in KTU 2024 exams.

| # | Rule / Equation | Statement | Notation in ER Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary Key of Weak Relation (after ER-to-Relational mapping) | $PK(W_{relation}) = PK(Owner) \cup P_{partial}$ | Composite key formed by combining owner's PK with the partial key |
| 2 | Foreign Key Constraint | $FK(W_{relation}) \to PK(Owner)$ | The owner's PK acts as a foreign key in the weak relation |
| 3 | Existence Dependency | $\forall w \in W, \exists o \in O : w \text{ participates in } R_{identifying}$ | Drawn as double line from $W$ to $R$ |
| 4 | Partial Key Property | $P$ is a key *relative to* one $O_i$ | Drawn as dashed underline |
| 5 | Cardinality of Identifying Relationship | $O : W = 1 : N$ | Standard ER cardinality convention |
| 6 | Cascade-on-Delete Behavior | If $o_i$ is deleted, all $w_j$ linked to $o_i$ must also be deleted | SQL: `ON DELETE CASCADE` |
| 7 | Strong-Entity Containment Rule | A weak entity may be linked to **at most one** identifying owner | Mandatory by definition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weak entity modeling is heavily used in the following engineering domains:

- **Payroll / HRMS Systems:** Modeling *Dependents* of an *Employee* (dependent has no unique global ID).
- **Banking Systems:** Modeling *Loan Installments* of a *Loan* (installment number is unique only within a loan).
- **Hospital Management Systems:** Modeling *Bed Allocations* within a *Ward*.
- **E-Commerce:** Modeling *Line Items* inside an *Order* (line item number is unique only within an order).
- **Network Management:** Modeling *Ports* on a *Router*, *Files* in a *Directory*.

In all these cases, the KTU-exam-preferred approach is to **explicitly model the weak entity** rather than invent surrogate keys, because the semantic dependency is **part of the business logic**, not just an implementation detail.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Systematic Procedure: Converting a Weak Entity to a Relational Schema

In KTU 2024 examinations, you are frequently asked to **map an ER diagram containing a weak entity to a relational schema**. The following is the **complete, exhaustive procedure** with every step explicitly shown.

### **Given (Conceptual Setup):**
- Let $O$ = Identifying (Owner) entity type with primary key $PK_O$.
- Let $W$ = Weak entity type with partial key $P$ and descriptive attributes $\{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$.
- Let $R$ = Identifying relationship connecting $O$ and $W$ with cardinality $1:N$ from $O$ to $W$.

### **Step 1: Create the Owner Relation**
The owner is a strong entity, so we create its relation in the standard way:

$$Owner(PK_O, \text{other attributes of } O)$$

The primary key is simply $PK_O$, since the owner is a strong entity.

### **Step 2: Create the Weak Entity Relation**
The weak entity relation must include:
- All descriptive attributes of $W$ (excluding the partial key as separate): $\{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$
- The partial key $P$ of $W$
- The primary key $PK_O$ of the owner, **borrowed as a foreign key**

The relation schema is therefore:

$$Weak(PK_O, P, A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$$

### **Step 3: Derive the Primary Key of the Weak Relation**
The primary key of the weak relation is the **union** of the owner's primary key and the partial key:

$$PK_{Weak} = PK_O \cup P$$

**Explanation:** A partial key $P$ alone is insufficient (the same $P$ value can exist under different owners). The owner's $PK_O$ alone is insufficient (the owner doesn't have the weak entity's attributes). Their combination is both **necessary and sufficient** to uniquely identify every weak entity instance.

### **Step 4: Derive the Foreign Key Constraint**
The owner's primary key within the weak relation acts as a **foreign key**:

$$PK_O \subseteq \text{attributes of } Weak \quad \text{and} \quad PK_O \rightarrow PK_{Owner}$$

This enforces **referential integrity** — every weak entity instance must reference a *valid* owner instance.

### **Step 5: Apply Total Participation / Cascade Delete**
Because the weak entity has total participation, deleting an owner instance $o_i \in O$ must automatically delete all $w_j \in W$ where $w_j.PK_O = o_i.PK_O$. In SQL DDL, this is enforced as:

$$\text{FOREIGN KEY } (PK_O) \text{ REFERENCES } Owner(PK_O) \text{ ON DELETE CASCADE}$$

### **Step 6: Handle the Identifying Relationship Separately**
The identifying relationship is **NOT** mapped to a separate relation because:
- Its cardinality is $1:N$.
- Every weak entity instance participates in **exactly one** instance of the identifying relationship.

Since the weak side always has participation 1 (in the relationship), the relationship is **absorbed** into the weak relation via the foreign key. No separate relation $R$ is needed.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the identifying relationship is somehow a **M:N relationship**, then it MUST be mapped to a separate relation containing $PK_O$ and $P$ as a composite key. However, this is rare in KTU questions and considered non-standard for true weak entities.

## 3.2 Worked Example: "Dependent" of an "Employee"

### **Problem Statement:**
In a company's HR database, an *Employee* is a strong entity with primary key `EmpID`. An *Employee* may have multiple *Dependents* (e.g., spouse, children, parents). Each *Dependent* has attributes `DepName`, `Relationship`, `Age`, and `Gender`. The combination of `DepName` and `EmpID` uniquely identifies a dependent, but `DepName` alone does not (two employees can both have a dependent named "Riya").

### **Step-by-Step Mapping:**

**Step A — Identify owner and weak entities:**
- Owner: `Employee(EmpID, Name, Salary, DeptID)`
- Weak: `Dependent` with partial key `DepName`

**Step B — Apply Step 3 rule:**

$$PK_{Dependent} = EmpID \cup DepName$$

**Step C — Write the relational schema:**

$$Dependent(\underline{EmpID}, \underline{DepName}, Relationship, Age, Gender)$$

Where $\underline{EmpID}$ is the foreign key and $\underline{EmpID} \cup \underline{DepName}$ together form the primary key.

**Step D — Express the SQL DDL logic:**

$$\text{CREATE TABLE Dependent (}$$
$$\text{EmpID INT,}$$
$$\text{DepName VARCHAR}(50),$$
$$\text{Relationship VARCHAR}(20),$$
$$\text{Age INT,}$$
$$\text{Gender CHAR}(1),$$
$$\text{PRIMARY KEY (EmpID, DepName),}$$
$$\text{FOREIGN KEY (EmpID) REFERENCES Employee(EmpID) ON DELETE CASCADE);}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Is This a Weak Entity?"

When given a scenario, use the following decision matrix to classify an entity:

| Condition | Weak Entity? | Strong Entity? |
|---|---|---|
| Has a globally unique natural key | No | **Yes** |
| Unique only within an owner's context | **Yes** | No |
| Existence depends on another entity | **Yes** | Not necessarily |
| Total participation in some relationship | Often **Yes** (if also lacks own PK) | Optional |
| Will inherit owner's PK to form its PK | **Yes** | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Exams:** Total participation **alone does not make** an entity weak. An entity with total participation but possessing a unique global key is still a **strong entity with total participation**. Both conditions (lack of own key AND dependence on owner) must hold.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical ER Diagram: Weak Entity "Room" owned by "Building"

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **textbook-standard ER notation** for a weak entity. Since Mermaid cannot natively draw double rectangles or double diamonds, the diagram uses clear textual markers (`<<weak>>`, `<<identifying>>`) that map precisely to the standard ER symbols.

```mermaid
erDiagram
    BUILDING {
        int BuildingID PK
        string BuildingName
        string Address
    }
    ROOM {
        int BuildingID FK
        string RoomNumber "PARTIAL KEY"
        int Floor
        int Capacity
    }
    BUILDING ||--o{ ROOM : "CONTAINS"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping to Standard ER Notation:**
> - `BUILDING` is drawn as a single rectangle in standard ER diagrams.
> - `ROOM` would be drawn as a **double rectangle** (`<<weak>>` in Mermaid).
> - `CONTAINS` is the identifying relationship, drawn as a **double diamond** in standard ER.
> - `RoomNumber` is the **partial key**, drawn with a **dashed underline** in standard ER.
> - The line from `ROOM` to `CONTAINS` is **double** (total participation) in standard ER.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **mapping workflow** from a weak entity in an ER diagram to its corresponding relational schema and SQL implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Strong Owner Entity O] --> B[Identify Weak Entity W with Partial Key P]
    B --> C[Draw Double Rectangle for W and Double Diamond for R]
    C --> D[Mark Partial Key P with Dashed Underline]
    D --> E[Establish 1:N Cardinality from O to W]
    E --> F[Mark Total Participation as Double Line from W to R]
    F --> G[Create Owner Relation with PK_O]
    G --> H[Create Weak Relation with PK_O, P, and Descriptive Attributes]
    H --> I[Set Composite Primary Key PK_O Union P]
    I --> J[Add Foreign Key Constraint PK_O References Owner]
    J --> K[Apply ON DELETE CASCADE for Existence Dependency]
    K --> L[Do NOT Create Separate Relation for Identifying Relationship]
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map: Owner-Weak-Identifying Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    Owner[Owner Entity O with PK_O] -- 1:N --> Rel[Identifying Relationship R]
    Rel -- Total Participation --> Weak[Weak Entity W with Partial Key P]
    Weak -. Inherits .-> PK[PK_O as Foreign Key]
    PK -- Combined with --> Comp[Composite Primary Key PK_O + P]
    Comp -. Defines .-> Identity[Unique Global Identity for W]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Map:**
> - Solid arrows (`-->`) represent **mandatory ER modeling connections**.
> - Dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent **logical/derivation relationships** during the mapping phase.
> - The composite key construction is the **conceptual bridge** that resolves the identifiability problem of the weak entity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a weak entity type. Why is a partial key not sufficient as a primary key?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **weak entity type** is an entity type whose instances cannot be uniquely identified by their own attributes alone and whose existence depends on another (owner/identifying) entity type. It is connected to its owner through an **identifying relationship** and uses the owner's primary key combined with its own **partial key (discriminator)** to form a composite primary key.

A **partial key** is a minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies a weak entity instance *only within the context of a single owner instance*. It is **not sufficient as a primary key** because the same partial key value can appear under different owner instances. For example, *Room 304* in *Building A* is different from *Room 304* in *Building B*. Therefore, to achieve global uniqueness, the partial key must be **concatenated with the primary key of the owner entity**, yielding a composite primary key:

$$PK_{Weak} = PK_{Owner} \cup Partial\_Key$$

**(Allocation: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for partial key justification)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a strong entity and a weak entity with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Strong Entity | Weak Entity |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Has its own primary key | Depends on owner's primary key |
| **Existence** | Independent existence | Existence-dependent on owner |
| **Notation** | Single rectangle | Double rectangle |
| **Key Type** | Primary key (solid underline) | Partial key (dashed underline) |
| **Identifying Relationship** | Not required | Required (double diamond) |
| **Participation** | May be partial or total | Always total in identifying relationship |
| **Example** | `Employee(EmpID, Name, Salary)` | `Dependent(EmpID, DepName, Age)` |

**Example:** In a university database, `Student` with attribute `RollNo` is a strong entity because `RollNo` is globally unique. However, `Section` assigned to a `Student` in a particular `CourseOffering` may be a weak entity if `SectionID` is unique only within one `CourseOffering`.

**(Allocation: 2 marks for tabular differentiation + 1 mark for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of weak entity types with a suitable real-world example. Clearly describe the **identifying relationship**, **partial key**, and **total participation** in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: A bank maintains *Loan* information where each loan has multiple *Installments*. Each installment has an `InstallmentNo`, `DueDate`, and `Amount`. The `InstallmentNo` is unique only within a given loan. Draw the ER diagram (showing all relevant ER notations) and convert it into a relational schema. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Definition of Weak Entity (2 marks)**
A weak entity type is one that cannot be uniquely identified using its own attributes. It is depicted using a **double rectangle** in ER diagrams and is connected to a strong (owner) entity through an **identifying relationship** drawn as a **double diamond**.

**Step 2: Real-World Example (2 marks)**
Consider a *Building* (owner) and a *Room* (weak entity). A room has a `RoomNumber`, `Floor`, and `Capacity`. While `RoomNumber` is unique within a building, two different buildings can have "Room 101". Hence, `RoomNumber` is the **partial key**, and the combination `BuildingID + RoomNumber` uniquely identifies a room.

**Step 3: Identifying Relationship (1 mark)**
The relationship *"Located-In"* (or *"Has-Room"*) between `Building` and `Room` is the **identifying relationship**, drawn as a double diamond.

**Step 4: Partial Key (1 mark)**
The partial key (discriminator) of `Room` is `RoomNumber`, drawn with a **dashed underline**. It is a key *locally* within a building but not globally.

**Step 5: Total Participation (1 mark)**
Every `Room` MUST be associated with exactly one `Building`. Hence, the participation of `Room` in the identifying relationship is **total**, represented by a **double line** from `Room` to the diamond.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: ER Diagram Construction (3 marks)**
The ER diagram contains:
- Strong entity `Loan` with primary key `LoanID` (single rectangle).
- Weak entity `Installment` with partial key `InstallmentNo` (double rectangle).
- Identifying relationship `Has-Installment` (double diamond).
- Cardinality: One `Loan` has many `Installments` (1:N).
- Total participation: Every `Installment` belongs to exactly one `Loan` (double line).

**Step 2: Relational Schema Mapping (4 marks)**

Applying the mapping procedure:

- **Owner relation:**

$$Loan(\underline{LoanID}, LoanAmount, IssueDate, CustomerID)$$

- **Weak entity relation (no separate relation for identifying relationship):**

$$Installment(\underline{LoanID}, \underline{InstallmentNo}, DueDate, Amount)$$

- **Primary key derivation:**

$$PK_{Installment} = LoanID \cup InstallmentNo$$

- **Foreign key constraint:**

$$LoanID \text{ in } Installment \text{ references } LoanID \text{ in } Loan \text{ ON DELETE CASCADE}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Full 14 marks):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Mapping the identifying relationship to a *separate* relation. This is **incorrect** for 1:N identifying relationships. The relationship is absorbed into the weak relation via the foreign key. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to make the participation **total** (double line) in the ER diagram. The KTU examiner specifically looks for the double-line notation. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Declaring `InstallmentNo` alone as the primary key. This violates the fundamental rule that the partial key is **never** a complete primary key. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to include `ON DELETE CASCADE` in the foreign key specification. This is required because of the **existence dependency**. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is an **identifying relationship**? How is it different from a regular relationship? Discuss the role of **existence dependency** in weak entity modeling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A university has `Course` (CourseID, CourseName, Credits) and `Section` (SectionID, RoomNo, Timing). A `Section` belongs to exactly one `Course`, and `SectionID` is unique only within a course. Draw the complete ER diagram and map it to relational schema with all constraints. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Definition of Identifying Relationship (2 marks)**
An **identifying relationship** is the relationship that links a weak entity type to its owner (identifying) entity type. It is represented as a **double diamond** in ER diagrams and is essential for the **logical existence** of the weak entity.

**Step 2: Difference from Regular Relationship (2 marks)**

| Aspect | Identifying Relationship | Regular Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | Double diamond | Single diamond |
| **Connects** | Weak entity to its owner | Any two entities (weak-weak, strong-strong, strong-weak non-identifying) |
| **Constraint** | Total participation from weak side | May be partial or total |
| **Mapping** | Absorbed into weak relation (1:N) | May be mapped to a separate relation (M:N) |

**Step 3: Role of Existence Dependency (3 marks)**
**Existence dependency** means that a weak entity instance cannot exist in the database without its corresponding owner instance. This has three critical implications:

- **Deletion Propagation:** If the owner is deleted, all related weak entities must be deleted (cascading delete).
- **Insertion Constraint:** A weak entity cannot be inserted before its owner is inserted.
- **Referential Integrity:** The foreign key in the weak relation must always reference a valid owner.

Mathematically, the existence dependency is expressed as:

$$\forall w \in W, \exists o \in O \text{ such that } (w, o) \in R_{identifying}$$

If no such $o$ exists, then $w$ cannot exist in the database.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: ER Diagram (3 marks)**
- `Course` is a strong entity with primary key `CourseID` (single rectangle).
- `Section` is a weak entity with partial key `SectionID` (double rectangle).
- Identifying relationship `Has-Section` (double diamond).
- Total participation: Every `Section` must belong to a `Course` (double line).
- Cardinality: One `Course` has many `Sections` (1:N).

**Step 2: Relational Mapping (4 marks)**

- **Owner relation:**

$$Course(\underline{CourseID}, CourseName, Credits)$$

- **Weak entity relation:**

$$Section(\underline{CourseID}, \underline{SectionID}, RoomNo, Timing)$$

- **Constraints:**

$$CourseID \text{ in } Section \text{ references } CourseID \text{ in } Course \text{ ON DELETE CASCADE}$$

- **Primary key:**

$$PK_{Section} = CourseID \cup SectionID$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Treating `Section` as a strong entity because "SectionID looks unique". Always check whether `SectionID` is unique *globally* or only *within one course*. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Drawing a single line (partial participation) instead of a double line (total participation). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 7:** Writing `SectionID` as the *only* primary key in the relational schema. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Weak Entity Types (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

- **Definition:** A weak entity **cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes** and depends on an **owner/identifying entity** for its existence and identification.

- **Five ER Notation Rules (MUST remember):**
  1. Weak entity ⟹ **Double Rectangle**.
  2. Identifying relationship ⟹ **Double Diamond**.
  3. Partial key ⟹ **Dashed Underline**.
  4. Total participation ⟹ **Double Line** from weak entity to the relationship.
  5. The cardinality of the identifying relationship is always **1:N** from owner to weak.

- **Core Mapping Rule (Exam Favorite):**

$$PK_{WeakRelation} = PK_{Owner} \cup PartialKey$$

- **No Separate Relation Rule:** The identifying relationship is **NOT** mapped to a separate relation when it is 1:N. It is absorbed into the weak relation.

- **Foreign Key Rule:** The owner's primary key is included in the weak relation as a **foreign key** with **`ON DELETE CASCADE`** to enforce existence dependency.

- **Three Properties of Weak Entities (must hold simultaneously):**
  1. **No own global primary key.**
  2. **Existence dependency on an owner entity.**
  3. **Total participation in the identifying relationship.**

- **Classic Real-World Examples (use these in answers):**
  - `Room` in a `Building`
  - `Dependent` of an `Employee`
  - `Installment` of a `Loan`
  - `LineItem` of an `Order`
  - `Bed` in a `Hospital Ward`

- **Common Examiner Traps:**
  - Total participation does **NOT** automatically make an entity weak.
  - A weak entity is **NOT** mapped to a separate relation for the identifying relationship.
  - The partial key alone is **NEVER** the primary key.
  - The foreign key from weak to owner **MUST** have cascade delete.

- **One-Line Exam Answer Template:**
  *"A weak entity type is an entity whose existence and identification depend on an owner entity. It is depicted using a double rectangle, has a partial key (dashed underline), participates totally (double line) in a double-diamond identifying relationship, and inherits the owner's primary key to form a composite primary key."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refining the ER Design for the Company Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Refining the ER Design for the Company Database # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the Entity-Relationship (ER) model, **Refining the ER Design** refers to the iterative, design-decision-driven process of transforming a preliminary conceptual schema into a precise, constraint-complete, and redundancy-free ER diagram that faithfully represents the real-world mini-world (a **universe of discourse**, UoD). The refinement is guided by formal design choices regarding: 1. **Entity Sets** — selection of proper object types. 2. **Attributes** — choice between atomic, composite, single-valued, multivalued, derived, or key attributes. 3. **Relationship Sets** — choice of cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and structural (binary, recursive, ternary) type. 4. **Participation Constraints** — *total participation* (existence dependency) vs. *partial participation*. 5. **Structural Refinements** — use of *weak entity types*, *ISA/specialization-generalization* hierarchies, and *aggregation* of relationship sets. 6. **Naming, Documentation, and Validation** against user requirements to eliminate update, insertion, and deletion anomalies at the conceptual level. The **canonical Company database** is the standard illustrative mini-world used in foundational database textbooks to demonstrate this refinement process. It models a corporate organization involving **EMPLOYEE**, **DEPARTMENT**, **PROJECT**, and **DEPENDENT** entity sets with their interrelationships. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The "Company Database" example is the central pedagogical artifact for Module 1 of PCCST402. KTU examiners routinely test mapping this schema to relations, identifying constraints, and explaining design decisions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint Imagine an architect designing a house. The first sketch (the preliminary ER diagram) is rough: it shows "rooms," "doors," "windows." The **refinement phase** is when the architect decides: - Will the *living room* be a separate room (entity) or just a *function* (attribute) of the house? - Are *French doors* a sub-type (specialization) of *doors* (generalization)? - Is the *doorway* between two rooms a *1:1* connection (one door per opening) or *1:N* (one wall holding many openings)? Refining an ER design is exactly this — making **explicit, justified design decisions** about whether a real-world concept becomes an entity, attribute, relationship, weak entity, or a specialization. The output must be unambiguous and ready for translation into a relational schema without loss of semantic fidelity. ## 1.3 The Mini-World: COMPANY Database — Entities at a Glance | Entity Set | Role in Mini-World | Primary Key | |---|---|---| | **EMPLOYEE** | Real-world persons working for the company | `SSN` (Social Security Number) | | **DEPARTMENT** | Organizational units | `Dnumber` | | **PROJECT** | Company-funded projects | `Pnumber` | | **DEPENDENT** | Dependents of an employee (weak entity) | `(ESSN, Dependent_name)` partial key + owner | | **DEPT_LOCATIONS** | Multivalued locations of departments | `(Dnumber, Location)` | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** `SSN`, `Dnumber`, and `Pnumber` are surrogate identifiers. In real enterprise systems they may be `EmpID`, `DeptID`, `ProjID` — but for the canonical Company example, `SSN` is the universally accepted key. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Refinement Process — Stepwise Logic The refinement of an ER design follows a structured, semi-formal methodology. KTU examiners expect students to articulate these steps in order: **Step 1 — Identify Entity Types** Distinguish *objects* that have independent existence from *properties* that merely describe them. *Rule of thumb:* if a concept can have its own attributes and participate in relationships independently, it is an entity. **Step 2 — Identify Attributes** For each entity, enumerate its descriptive properties. Classify each attribute as: - *Simple (atomic)* vs. *composite* (e.g., `Address = Street + City + State + Zip`). - *Single-valued* vs. *multivalued* (e.g., `CollegeDegrees` for an employee). - *Derived* (e.g., `Age` derived from `Bdate`). - *Key* (unique identifier). **Step 3 — Identify Relationships Among Entities** Determine how entities are semantically associated. For each relationship, decide: - *Cardinality ratio* (1:1, 1:N, M:N). - *Participation constraint* (total `–` double line, or partial `–` single line). - *Structural type* (binary, ternary, recursive/unary). **Step 4 — Identify Specialization / Generalization Hierarchies** Group entities that share common attributes into superclasses with subclasses (e.g., `SECRETARY`, `ENGINEER`, `TECHNICIAN` as subclasses of `EMPLOYEE`). **Step 5 — Resolve Weak Entity Types** If an entity's existence depends on another entity (existence-dependent), model it as a **weak entity** with a *partial key* and *identifying relationship* (double diamond `⇢`). **Step 6 — Eliminate Redundancy & Validate** Verify that no information is derivable in multiple inconsistent ways. Cross-check all cardinalities against user requirements. ## 2.2 Relationship Types in the Company Schema | Relationship | Entity Pairs | Cardinality | Participation | Role | |---|---|---|---|---| | `WORKS_FOR` | EMPLOYEE — DEPARTMENT | **N : 1** | EMPLOYEE: total; DEPARTMENT: partial | Every employee must work for some department | | `MANAGES` | EMPLOYEE — DEPARTMENT | **1 : 1** | DEPARTMENT: total; EMPLOYEE: partial | Every department must have one manager | | `CONTROLS` | DEPARTMENT — PROJECT | **1 : N** | PROJECT: total; DEPARTMENT: partial | Every project is controlled by exactly one department | | `WORKS_ON` | EMPLOYEE — PROJECT | **M : N** | EMPLOYEE: partial; PROJECT: partial | Employees may work on many projects and vice versa | | `SUPERVISION` | EMPLOYEE (recursive) | **1 : N** | EMPLOYEE: partial | An employee may supervise other employees | | `HAS_DEPENDENT` | EMPLOYEE — DEPENDENT | **1 : N** | DEPENDENT: total; EMPLOYEE: partial | Identifying weak-entity relationship | | `DEP_LOCATIONS` | DEPARTMENT — Locations | **1 : N** | DEPARTMENT: total | Multivalued attribute realized as weak relationship | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Constraint Notation Table) > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` in place of vertical pipes inside formulas below — never use the literal `|` character, which would break Markdown table syntax. | Notation (Chen / Crow's Foot) | Meaning | Mathematical Condition | Common Use | |---|---|---|---| | **1 : 1** | One-to-one | $\forall e_1 \in E_1,\ \exists\ \leq 1\ e_2 \in E_2$ related | `MANAGES` | | **1 : N** | One-to-many | $\forall e_2 \in E_2,\ \exists\ \text{exactly}\ 1\ e_1 \in E_1$ | `CONTROLS` | | **M : N** | Many-to-many | $\forall e_1,\ \exists\ \geq 0\ e_2$ and vice versa | `WORKS_ON` | | **Double line** | Total participation | $\forall e \in E,\ \exists\ \geq 1\ r \in R$ involving $e$ | `WORKS_FOR` (employee side) | | **Single line** | Partial participation | $\forall e \in E,\ \exists\ \geq 0\ r \in R$ involving $e$ | `WORKS_FOR` (department side) | | **Double diamond** $\Leftrightarrow$ | Identifying relationship | Links weak entity to owner | `HAS_DEPENDENT` | | **Dashed underline** | Partial / discriminator key | Unique only within owner | `Dependent_name` | | **ISA Triangle** | Specialization / generalization | Subclass $\subseteq$ Superclass | `SECRETARY` ISA `EMPLOYEE` | ## 2.4 Cardinality Math — Mini-Theorem for Participation For a binary relationship $R$ between entity sets $A$ and $B$: $$ \text{Total Participation in } R \iff \forall\ a \in A,\ \exists\ b \in B \text{ such that } (a, b) \in R $$ This must hold even when $a$ has no counterpart — total participation **forces** at least one related tuple. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility The refined Company ER model is used in: - **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** systems (SAP, Oracle E-Business Suite). - **Human Resource Management Systems (HRMS)** where `EMPLOYEE`, `DEPARTMENT`, and `PROJECT` are core master-data tables. - **Payroll subsystems** where the `WORKS_ON` M:N relationship tracks billable hours. - **Compliance & audit** workflows where `DEPENDENT` weak entities drive tax-deduction eligibility logic. # 3. Step-by-Step Reduction of the Company ER Diagram to Relations ## 3.1 Canonical Mapping Rules (the 7-Step Algorithm) The KTU syllabus expects students to convert any refined ER diagram into relational schema. The reduction algorithm for the Company database proceeds in seven steps: | Step | ER Construct | Mapping Rule | |---|---|---| | **R1** | Regular (strong) entity $E$ with simple key | Create relation $R(E)$ including all simple attributes. | | **R2** | Weak entity $W$ with owner $E$ | Create $R(W)$ including $W$'s partial key plus $E$'s primary key as a foreign key; composite primary key = (owner's PK + partial key). | | **R3** | Binary 1:1 relationship $R$ between $E_1, E_2$ | Merge $R$ into either $E_1$ or $E_2$; choose the side with **total participation**. Add foreign key + relationship attributes. | | **R4** | Binary 1:N relationship $R$ between $E_1$ (1-side) and $E_2$ (N-side) | Merge $R$ into $E_2$ (the N-side). Add foreign key referencing $E_1$ + relationship attributes. | | **R5** | Binary M:N relationship $R$ | Create a new relation $R$ containing PKs of both participating entities as a composite key, plus any relationship attributes. | | **R6** | Multivalued attribute $A$ of entity $E$ | Create a new relation $R(E\_PK, A)$ with composite PK and a foreign key. | | **R7** | n-ary relationship ($n > 2$) | Create a new relation containing PKs of all participating entities. | ## 3.2 Mapping the Company Database — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Reduction Let $\mathcal{C}$ denote the Company schema. We will reduce each ER construct. ### 3.2.1 Step R1 — Strong Entity `EMPLOYEE` The attributes of `EMPLOYEE` are: `Fname`, `Minit`, `Lname` (composite `Name`), `SSN` (key), `Bdate`, `Address` (composite), `Salary`, `Sex`, plus `Dno` (foreign key from `WORKS_FOR` merged in R4) and `Super_SSN` (foreign key from `SUPERVISION` merged in R4). $$ \text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{SSN}},\ \text{Fname},\ \text{Minit},\ \text{Lname},\ \text{Bdate},\ \text{Address},\ \text{Salary},\ \text{Sex},\ \text{Dno},\ \text{Super\_SSN}) $$ Primary key: `SSN`. Foreign keys: `Dno → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber`; `Super_SSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN`. ### 3.2.2 Step R1 — Strong Entity `DEPARTMENT` Attributes: `Dname` (key candidate), `Dnumber` (key), `Mgr_SSN` + `Mgr_Start_Date` (from `MANAGES`, merged via R3 since `MANAGES` is 1:1 with DEPARTMENT having total participation). $$ \text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{Dnumber}},\ \text{Dname},\ \text{Mgr\_SSN},\ \text{Mgr\_Start\_Date}) $$ Foreign key: `Mgr_SSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN`. ### 3.2.3 Step R1 — Strong Entity `PROJECT` Attributes: `Pname`, `Pnumber` (key), `Plocation`, `Dnum` (foreign key from `CONTROLS`, merged via R4). $$ \text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{Pnumber}},\ \text{Pname},\ \text{Plocation},\ \text{Dnum}) $$ Foreign key: `Dnum → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber`. ### 3.2.4 Step R2 — Weak Entity `DEPENDENT` `DEPENDENT` is a weak entity owned by `EMPLOYEE` via the identifying relationship `HAS_DEPENDENT` (double diamond). Partial key is `Dependent_name`. $$ \text{DEPENDENT}(\underline{\text{ESSN}},\ \underline{\text{Dependent\_name}},\ \text{Sex},\ \text{Bdate},\ \text{Relationship}) $$ Composite primary key: $(\text{ESSN},\ \text{Dependent\_name})$. Foreign key: `ESSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN` (ON DELETE CASCADE). ### 3.2.5 Step R6 — Multivalued Attribute `Locations` of `DEPARTMENT` `Locations` is multivalued — a department may have multiple sites. Apply rule R6: $$ \text{DEPT\_LOCATIONS}(\underline{\text{Dnumber}},\ \underline{\text{Location}}) $$ Composite PK: $(\text{Dnumber},\ \text{Location})$. Foreign key: `Dnumber → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber` (ON DELETE CASCADE). ### 3.2.6 Step R5 — M:N Relationship `WORKS_ON` `WORKS_ON` is many-to-many between `EMPLOYEE` and `PROJECT`. Attribute `Hours` belongs to the relationship, not to either entity: $$ \text{WORKS\_ON}(\underline{\text{ESSN}},\ \underline{\text{Pnumber}},\ \text{Hours}) $$ Composite PK: $(\text{ESSN},\ \text{Pnumber})$. Foreign keys: `ESSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN`, `Pnumber → PROJECT.Pnumber`. ### 3.2.7 Step R7 — Ternary Relationship (if any) In the canonical Company schema, no ternary relationships exist at the introductory level. (They appear in extensions like `SUPPLY` between `SUPPLIER`, `PART`, `PROJECT`.) For KTU 2024 Module 1, **note this is a graded distinction**: ternary relationships produce a relation with PK = (PK of all participating entities). ## 3.3 Final Relational Schema — Summary Matrix | Relation | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys | |---|---|---|---| | `EMPLOYEE` | SSN, Fname, Minit, Lname, Bdate, Address, Salary, Sex, Dno, Super_SSN | **SSN** | Dno → DEPARTMENT; Super_SSN → EMPLOYEE | | `DEPARTMENT` | Dnumber, Dname, Mgr_SSN, Mgr_Start_Date | **Dnumber** | Mgr_SSN → EMPLOYEE | | `DEPT_LOCATIONS` | Dnumber, Location | **(Dnumber, Location)** | Dnumber → DEPARTMENT | | `PROJECT` | Pnumber, Pname, Plocation, Dnum | **Pnumber** | Dnum → DEPARTMENT | | `WORKS_ON` | ESSN, Pnumber, Hours | **(ESSN, Pnumber)** | ESSN → EMPLOYEE; Pnumber → PROJECT | | `DEPENDENT` | ESSN, Dependent_name, Sex, Bdate, Relationship | **(ESSN, Dependent_name)** | ESSN → EMPLOYEE | ## 3.4 Validation Check — Referential Integrity For every foreign key, verify the referenced primary key exists. **Total participation** on the N-side of `WORKS_FOR` mandates `NOT NULL` on `EMPLOYEE.Dno`. The `DEPENDENT` relation requires `ON DELETE CASCADE` because of its existence-dependence on `EMPLOYEE`. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Refined ER Diagram — Company Database (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture) Below is a Mermaid entity-relationship diagram rendering the canonical refined Company schema. It captures entities (as rounded rectangles), attributes (as ovals with derived attributes noted), weak entities (double-bordered), identifying relationships (double-diamond), and cardinality constraints. ```mermaid erDiagram EMPLOYEE { string SSN PK string Fname string Minit string Lname date Bdate string Address float Salary string Sex int Dno FK string Super_SSN FK } DEPARTMENT { int Dnumber PK string Dname string Mgr_SSN FK date Mgr_Start_Date } PROJECT { int Pnumber PK string Pname string Plocation int Dnum FK } DEPENDENT { string ESSN FK string Dependent_name string Sex date Bdate string Relationship } DEPT_LOCATIONS { int Dnumber FK string Location } WORKS_ON { string ESSN FK int Pnumber FK float Hours } EMPLOYEE ||--o{ DEPENDENT : "HAS_DEPENDENT (identifying)" EMPLOYEE }o--|| DEPARTMENT : "WORKS_FOR (N:1)" EMPLOYEE ||--o| DEPARTMENT : "MANAGES (1:1)" DEPARTMENT ||--o{ PROJECT : "CONTROLS (1:N)" EMPLOYEE }o--o{ PROJECT : "WORKS_ON (M:N)" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "SUPERVISION (1:N recursive)" DEPARTMENT ||--o{ DEPT_LOCATIONS : "has locations (multivalued)" ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Schema Reduction Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Conceptual Mini-World: COMPANY"] --> B["Identify Entity Sets"] B --> C["EMPLOYEE"] B --> D["DEPARTMENT"] B --> E["PROJECT"] B --> F["DEPENDENT (weak)"] C --> G["Identify Attributes
Composite, Multivalued, Derived"] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H["Identify Relationships
