+ Cardinality + Participation"] H --> I["Apply 7-Step Mapping Rules R1 through R7"] I --> J["EMPLOYEE relation"] I --> K["DEPARTMENT relation"] I --> L["PROJECT relation"] I --> M["DEPENDENT relation (weak)"] I --> N["WORKS_ON relation (M:N)"] I --> O["DEPT_LOCATIONS relation (multivalued)"] J --> P["Validated Relational Schema"] K --> P L --> P M --> P N --> P O --> P ``` ## 4.3 Notation Legend (Chen-Style) | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | Rectangle | Entity set | | Oval | Attribute | | Diamond | Relationship set | | Double-bordered rectangle | Weak entity | | Double-diamond | Identifying relationship | | Dashed underline | Partial key | | Double line connecting entity to relationship | Total participation | | ISA triangle | Specialization / generalization | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define a weak entity type. Why is it necessary in the Company database, and how is it mapped to a relation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **weak entity type** is an entity whose existence is logically dependent on another entity (the *owner* or *identifying entity*); it cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone and requires the primary key of the owner entity plus a *partial key* (discriminator) for unique identification. In the Company database, `DEPENDENT` is a weak entity because a dependent (e.g., a spouse or child) has meaning only in the context of the `EMPLOYEE` they belong to — `Dependent_name` alone may not be unique across the company, but `(SSN, Dependent_name)` is unique. **Mapping rule (R2):** create relation `DEPENDENT(ESSN, Dependent_name, Sex, Bdate, Relationship)` with composite primary key `(ESSN, Dependent_name)` and foreign key `ESSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN` with `ON DELETE CASCADE` to enforce existence dependency. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark example, 1 mark mapping)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-2] **Differentiate between total participation and partial participation constraints with an example from the Company database.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Total participation** (also called *existence dependency*) means every entity in the entity set must participate in at least one relationship of the given type — drawn as a **double line** from the entity to the relationship diamond. **Partial participation** allows some entities to participate in zero relationships — drawn as a **single line**. In the Company database, every `EMPLOYEE` must `WORKS_FOR` a `DEPARTMENT` (total participation on the employee side, double line), but a `DEPARTMENT` may exist without any `EMPLOYEE` working for it temporarily (partial participation on the department side, single line). Equivalently: $\forall e \in \text{EMPLOYEE},\ \exists\ d \in \text{DEPARTMENT}$ such that $(e, d) \in \text{WORKS\_FOR}$, while for departments, $\exists\ d \in \text{DEPARTMENT}$ with no employee in `WORKS_FOR`. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks definition, 1.5 marks example)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **seven-step methodology** for reducing an ER schema to a relational schema, with reference to the Company database. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Construct the **complete ER diagram** for the Company database with all entities, attributes, weak-entity constructs, identifying relationships, recursive relationships, and cardinality constraints clearly labeled. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) — Seven-Step Reduction Algorithm:** The reduction proceeds in seven rules (R1–R7). For the Company database: **R1 — Strong entities:** `EMPLOYEE`, `DEPARTMENT`, `PROJECT` each become a relation with their simple attributes as columns and their declared key as primary key. `EMPLOYEE` keeps all simple attributes (Fname, Minit, Lname, Bdate, Address, Salary, Sex); the composite `Name` attribute is decomposed into Fname/Minit/Lname. **R2 — Weak entity `DEPENDENT`:** Create relation with `ESSN` (FK to `EMPLOYEE.SSN`) + `Dependent_name` (partial key) + other attributes; composite PK = `(ESSN, Dependent_name)`. **R3 — 1:1 relationship `MANAGES`:** Since `DEPARTMENT` has total participation, merge `MANAGES` into `DEPARTMENT`: add `Mgr_SSN` (FK) and `Mgr_Start_Date`. **R4 — 1:N relationships `WORKS_FOR` and `CONTROLS`:** Merge into the N-side: `EMPLOYEE.Dno` (FK → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber) for `WORKS_FOR`; `PROJECT.Dnum` (FK → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber) for `CONTROLS`. Recursive `SUPERVISION` (1:N on EMPLOYEE) merges as `EMPLOYEE.Super_SSN`. **R5 — M:N relationship `WORKS_ON`:** Create new relation `WORKS_ON(ESSN, Pnumber, Hours)` with composite PK and both as FKs. **R6 — Multivalued attribute `Locations`:** Create `DEPT_LOCATIONS(Dnumber, Location)` with composite PK. **R7 — Ternary relationships:** None present in the basic Company schema. *(7 marks: 1 mark per rule with correct application)* **(b) — ER Diagram Construction:** (See SECTION 4.1 above for the rendered Mermaid diagram. The student must reproduce all entities, attributes including the composite `Address`, the multivalued `Locations`, the derived `Age`, the weak entity `DEPENDENT`, the identifying relationship `HAS_DEPENDENT`, the recursive `SUPERVISION` on `EMPLOYEE`, and label all cardinalities: WORKS_FOR(N:1), MANAGES(1:1), CONTROLS(1:N), WORKS_ON(M:N), SUPERVISION(1:N), HAS_DEPENDENT(1:N).) *(7 marks — 1 mark per major ER construct, 2 marks for cardinalities)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Discuss the design decisions and refinement choices required when modeling the **Company database**, focusing on (i) entity vs. attribute distinction, (ii) entity vs. relationship distinction, and (iii) binary vs. n-ary relationships. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Reduce the **complete Company ER diagram** to relational schema. For every relation, specify the primary key and all foreign keys with their referential targets. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) — Refinement Design Decisions:** **(i) Entity vs. Attribute:** If a concept has its own descriptive properties and participates in relationships independently, it should be an entity — not an attribute. For example, `PROJECT` is an entity (it has Pname, Pnumber, Plocation, participates in CONTROLS, WORKS_ON), not a mere attribute of `DEPARTMENT`. Conversely, `Sex` is an attribute of `EMPLOYEE` (no further description needed and does not participate in relationships independently). **(ii) Entity vs. Relationship:** `DEPENDENT` is an entity (has attributes Name, Sex, Bdate, Relationship) rather than a relationship, because dependents can have multiple attributes and may be queried independently. A relationship is preferred when the concept is purely an association without independent attributes other than descriptive ones — e.g., `WORKS_ON` is a relationship with attribute `Hours` only because it has no other descriptive property. **(iii) Binary vs. N-ary:** When in doubt, prefer binary relationships because they map cleanly to relational foreign keys. Ternary (n-ary, $n>2$) relationships are reserved for situations that **cannot be decomposed** into binary ones without losing semantic information. For example, in the standard Company schema all relationships are binary. However, if we needed to model `SUPPLY(SUPPLIER, PART, PROJECT)` — recording which supplier supplies which part to which project — this is irreducible to three binary relationships because the quantity/contract terms depend jointly on all three. The ternary version produces one relation `SUPPLY(SID, PID, JID, Qty)` with composite PK `(SID, PID, JID)`. *(7 marks: 2 marks each for (i), (ii), and (iii))* **(b) — Relational Schema Reduction:** (See SECTION 3.3 for the complete mapping. Briefly:) - `EMPLOYEE(SSN, Fname, Minit, Lname, Bdate, Address, Salary, Sex, Dno, Super_SSN)` — PK = **SSN**; FKs: Dno → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber, Super_SSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN. - `DEPARTMENT(Dnumber, Dname, Mgr_SSN, Mgr_Start_Date)` — PK = **Dnumber**; FK: Mgr_SSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN. - `DEPT_LOCATIONS(Dnumber, Location)` — PK = **(Dnumber, Location)**; FK: Dnumber → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber. - `PROJECT(Pnumber, Pname, Plocation, Dnum)` — PK = **Pnumber**; FK: Dnum → DEPARTMENT.Dnumber. - `WORKS_ON(ESSN, Pnumber, Hours)` — PK = **(ESSN, Pnumber)**; FKs: ESSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN, Pnumber → PROJECT.Pnumber. - `DEPENDENT(ESSN, Dependent_name, Sex, Bdate, Relationship)` — PK = **(ESSN, Dependent_name)**; FK: ESSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN. *(7 marks: 1 mark per relation with correct PK/FK specification, 1 mark for referential-integrity reasoning)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Treating `DEPENDENT` as a strong entity.** This is the single most frequent error. It must be marked with a double-bordered rectangle and linked to `EMPLOYEE` via a *double-diamond* `HAS_DEPENDENT` relationship with `Dependent_name` as the *partial key* (dashed underline). > 2. **Wrong primary key for `WORKS_ON`.** Students often write `Hours` as part of the PK. The PK is `(ESSN, Pnumber)` only; `Hours` is a non-key attribute. > 3. **Forgetting to merge `MANAGES` into `DEPARTMENT`.** Many students create a separate `MANAGES` relation, losing the 1:1 semantics. Since `MANAGES` is 1:1 with total participation on the DEPARTMENT side, it merges into `DEPARTMENT`. > 4. **Missing the recursive `SUPERVISION` arrow on `EMPLOYEE`.** Recursive relationships are often overlooked. > 5. **Confusing cardinality notation.** In Chen notation, `WORKS_FOR` is `N:1` (employee:department). In Crow's Foot, the "many" symbol is on the EMPLOYEE side. > 6. **Multivalued attribute treatment.** `Locations` of `DEPARTMENT` must become a *separate relation* `DEPT_LOCATIONS`, not a single column — or first-normal-form (1NF) is violated when implemented. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **Company database** models a corporate enterprise with `EMPLOYEE`, `DEPARTMENT`, `PROJECT`, and `DEPENDENT` (weak entity). - `SSN` is the universal primary key for `EMPLOYEE`; `Dnumber` for `DEPARTMENT`; `Pnumber` for `PROJECT`. - `DEPENDENT` is a **weak entity** with partial key `Dependent_name` and identifying relationship `HAS_DEPENDENT`. - Cardinalities: `WORKS_FOR` (N:1), `MANAGES` (1:1), `CONTROLS` (1:N), `WORKS_ON` (M:N), `SUPERVISION` (recursive, 1:N), `HAS_DEPENDENT` (1:N). - **Total participation** of `EMPLOYEE` in `WORKS_FOR` means every employee must belong to a department (`NOT NULL` on `Dno`). - The **seven mapping rules** (R1–R7) systematically reduce any ER diagram to a relational schema. - Multivalued attributes (`Locations`) require a **separate relation** to satisfy 1NF. - Derived attributes (`Age`) are **not stored** in relations; they are computed when needed. - Always specify **ON DELETE CASCADE** for FKs referencing owners of weak entities. - The order of mapping matters: strong entities first, then weak entities, then relationships, then multivalued attributes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** When KTU asks to "design an ER diagram for the Company database," always include (1) all four entity sets with proper weak-entity notation, (2) the recursive `SUPERVISION` self-loop, (3) the `WORKS_ON` M:N relationship with `Hours` as a relationship attribute, and (4) the multivalued `Locations` realized as a separate relation. These four elements distinguish a full-mark answer from an average one.

topics.title: The Relational Data Model and SQL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Relational Data Model and SQL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Relational Data Model

The **Relational Data Model** is a formal, mathematical framework proposed by **Edgar F. "Ted" Codd** in **1970** (IBM Research) for representing, storing in, and retrieving data from a database. It organises data into **relations** (tables) that are perceived by the user as a collection of **tuples** (rows) sharing common **attributes** (columns). Every relation conforms to a **schema** (structure) and an **instance** (current data), and is governed by a rigorous set of **integrity constraints** derived from first-order predicate logic and set theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The relational model is the *conceptual foundation* of all commercial RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server). You MUST master its formal vocabulary — *relation, tuple, attribute, domain, degree, cardinality* — because the same terms recur in relational algebra, SQL, normalization, and transaction theory.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Excel Sheet on Steroids"

Imagine a school register kept in a thick notebook:

| Real-World Concept | Relational Term | School-Register Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Whole notebook | Database | The principal's almirah |
| Single page | Relation (Table) | A page titled "Class 10A Marks" |
| Column header | Attribute | "Roll No", "Name", "Marks" |
| Row of entries | Tuple | A single student's row |
| Type of values allowed | Domain | Only integers (0-100) for "Marks" |
| Unique roll number | Primary Key | "Roll No 1, 2, 3 …" |
| Reference to another page | Foreign Key | "Class Teacher" linking to the Teacher page |

The relational model simply **codifies** what teachers already do informally — but with **mathematical precision**, no duplicate rows, and rules that *guarantee* correctness. The "steroid" part comes from being able to ask *any* question about the data using a declarative language (SQL) without writing algorithms.

### 1.3 Formal Vocabulary (KTU-Exact Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions (Memorise Verbatim for KTU 3-Mark Questions):**
> - **Domain (D):** A set of atomic, indivisible, permissible values for an attribute. Example: `D_Marks = {0, 1, 2, …, 100}`.
> - **Attribute:** The name of a role played by some domain in a relation. Example: `Marks` plays the role of `D_Marks` in `STUDENT`.
> - **Tuple (t):** An ordered set of values (one per attribute) describing a single entity.
> - **Relation Schema (R):** The structural descriptor, written as `R(A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ)` with associated domains.
> - **Relation (r / R):** A set of tuples conforming to schema R at a given instant — a *mathematical set*, hence **no duplicate tuples**.
> - **Degree of R:** Number of attributes `n` (columns).
> - **Cardinality of r:** Number of tuples `|r|` (rows) — **dynamic**, changes with insertions/deletions.

### 1.4 Structural Properties of a Relation

A valid relation must satisfy:
1. **Each cell contains a single atomic value** — First Normal Form (1NF) is inherent to the model.
2. **All entries in any column are from the same domain.**
3. **Each row is unique** — mathematically guaranteed by being a *set*.
4. **Attribute names are unique** within a relation.
5. **The order of rows and columns is immaterial** (set theory + relational algebra treat the relation as unordered).
6. **NULL is permitted** to represent missing/inapplicable information.

### 1.5 Keys — The "Identity Card" System

Keys are the single most-tested concept on this topic in KTU papers.

| Key Type | Formal Definition | Example (STUDENT relation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Super Key** | Any set of attributes whose values uniquely identify a tuple | {RollNo}, {RollNo, Name}, {Name, DOB, Phone} |
| **Candidate Key** | A *minimal* super key — no proper subset is a super key | {RollNo}, {Email} |
| **Primary Key (PK)** | The candidate key chosen by the DBA to be the main identifier | {RollNo} |
| **Alternate Key** | Candidate keys *not* selected as primary | {Email} |
| **Foreign Key (FK)** | Attribute(s) in R₁ referencing the PK of another relation R₂, enforcing referential integrity | `DeptID` in `EMPLOYEE` → `DeptID` in `DEPARTMENT` |
| **Composite Key** | A key made of 2+ attributes | {RollNo, SubjectCode} in `MARKS` |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "S-CAP-FA":** **S**uper → **C**andidate → **A**lternate (and **P**rimary) → **F**oreign → **A**ll keys.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Integrity Constraints (The "Traffic Rules" of the Database)

Integrity constraints are predicates that *every legal instance* of a database must satisfy. They protect data from corruption.

### 2.1.1 Inherent / Model Constraints
- No duplicate tuples (set property).
- No null for any attribute that is part of the primary key.
- Atomic values in each cell (1NF).

### 2.1.2 Schema-Based Constraints
- **Key Constraint:** PK value must be unique and NOT NULL.
- **Entity Integrity:** The primary key cannot be NULL (because NULL = unknown → cannot identify).
- **Referential Integrity:** Every FK value must either (i) match an existing PK in the parent table, OR (ii) be NULL (if the FK is not part of its own PK and is allowed to be null). Violations are governed by:
  - `RESTRICT` (default — reject the change),
  - `CASCADE` (propagate),
  - `SET NULL` (orphan → NULL),
  - `SET DEFAULT`.

### 2.1.3 Application / Semantic Constraints
- Business rules written into triggers or `CHECK` clauses. Example: `CHECK (Marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** Distinguish **Entity Integrity** (no NULL PK) from **Referential Integrity** (FK must match PK or be NULL). They are often confused in 3-mark questions.

## 2.2 Relational Algebra — The Theoretical Backbone of SQL

Relational algebra is a **procedural query language** consisting of operators that take relations as input and produce new relations (closure property). Every SQL query can be translated to a relational algebra expression.

### 2.2.1 Fundamental (Primitive) Operations

Let `R` and `S` be two relations.

| # | Operation | Symbolic Notation | Set-Theoretic / Logic Definition | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SELECT** (restriction) | $\sigma_{condition}(R)$ | Returns tuples of R satisfying the predicate | Unary |
| 2 | **PROJECT** | $\pi_{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_k}(R)$ | Returns specified columns, removing duplicates | Unary |
| 3 | **CARTESIAN PRODUCT** | $R \times S$ | Concatenation of every tuple of R with every tuple of S | Binary |
| 4 | **UNION** | $R \cup S$ | Set of tuples in R **or** S (duplicates removed); R & S must be **union-compatible** | Binary |
| 5 | **SET DIFFERENCE** | $R - S$ | Tuples in R **but not** in S; union-compatible | Binary |
| 6 | **RENAME** | $\rho_{NewName \leftarrow OldName}(R)$ | Renames a relation or its attributes | Unary |

### 2.2.2 Derived (Additional) Operations

| Operation | Derived Form | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **NATURAL JOIN** (⋈) | $\pi_{*}(\sigma_{R.A=S.A}(R \times S))$ | Combines related tuples across relations on common attributes |
| **EQUI-JOIN** | $\sigma_{R.A=S.A}(R \times S)$ | Theta-join where the condition is `=` |
| **THETA-JOIN** (⋈θ) | $\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S)$ | Join with any comparison operator |
| **INTERSECTION** | $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$ | Common tuples |
| **DIVISION** (÷) | $R \div S$ | Tuples of R associated with **all** tuples of S |
| **OUTER JOIN** (LEFT / RIGHT / FULL) | $R \bowtie S$ with null padding | Preserves unmatched tuples |

### 2.2.3 Closure Property (Why It Matters)
Relational algebra is **closed**: the output of every operation is itself a relation that can be fed into further operations. This permits the building of arbitrarily complex queries by composition.

## 2.3 SQL (Structured Query Language) — The Practical Engine

SQL is the **declarative, non-procedural** standard language for relational databases (ISO/IEC 9075). It is a *superset of relational algebra* plus tuple calculus features.

### 2.3.1 Sub-languages of SQL

| Sub-language | Full Form | Purpose | Example Commands |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines schema | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Modifies data | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Retrieves data | `SELECT` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Authorisation | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manages transactions | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` |

### 2.3.2 Canonical SQL Query Block

```sql
SELECT  [DISTINCT]  column_list
FROM    table_list
[WHERE   row_filter_condition]
[GROUP BY grouping_columns]
[HAVING  group_filter_condition]
[ORDER BY sort_columns [ASC | DESC]];
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Processing Order (NOT textual order!):**
> `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY`. This sequence is the single most-tested fact in KTU 14-mark SQL questions. Misordering causes the classic "aggregate in WHERE" mistake.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cardinality after SELECT** | $\vert \sigma_{C}(R) \vert \leq \vert R \vert$ | Restriction can only decrease rows |
| 2 | **Cardinality after PROJECT** | $\vert \pi_{A}(R) \vert \leq \vert R \vert$ | Eliminates duplicates |
| 3 | **Cardinality of Cartesian Product** | $\vert R \times S \vert = \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ | Multiplicative |
| 4 | **Cardinality of Natural Join** | $\vert R \bowtie S \vert \leq \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ | Often less due to matching |
| 5 | **Division** | $R \div S = \pi_{A}(R) - \pi_{A}((\pi_{A}(R) \times S) - R)$ | "For all" semantics |
| 6 | **Degree after PROJECT** | $deg(\pi_{A}(R)) = \vert A \vert$ | Number of projected attributes |
| 7 | **Degree of JOIN** | $deg(R \bowtie S) = deg(R) + deg(S) - deg(common\_attrs)$ | Subtract common columns once |
| 8 | **Union-Compatibility** | $deg(R) = deg(S)$ AND $\forall i:\ dom(R.A_i) = dom(S.A_i)$ | Required for ∪, −, ∩ |
| 9 | **Full Outer Join Size** | $\vert R \cup_{F} S \vert = \vert R \cup S \vert$ (conceptually) | Pads non-matches with NULL |
| 10 | **NULL Logic** | `NULL = NULL` → UNKNOWN; `NOT(UNKNOWN)` → UNKNOWN | Three-Valued Logic (3VL) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | ACID transactions over customer-account relations |
| **E-Commerce** | JOINs between `Order`, `Customer`, `Product`, `Inventory` relations |
| **Healthcare** | Referential integrity ensures `Prescription.PatientID` always links to a real patient |
| **Aviation / Railways** | Composite keys (TrainNo + CoachNo + SeatNo) as natural super keys |
| **Data Warehousing** | Division operator simulates "all products bought by all customers" |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Designing a Mini-Schema and Querying It

Consider the following **library** database:

```sql
BOOK(BookID PK, Title, Price, PubYear)
AUTHOR(AuthorID PK, AuthorName, Country)
WROTE(BookID FK, AuthorID FK, PRIMARY KEY(BookID, AuthorID))
```

**Sample Instance:**

**BOOK**

| BookID | Title | Price | PubYear |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | DBMS Concepts | 550 | 2020 |
| B2 | Operating Systems | 480 | 2019 |
| B3 | Compilers | 600 | 2021 |
| B4 | Networks | 450 | 2018 |

**AUTHOR**

| AuthorID | AuthorName | Country |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | Korth | USA |
| A2 | Silberschatz | USA |
| A3 | Tanenbaum | Netherlands |
| A4 | Aho | USA |

**WROTE**

| BookID | AuthorID |
|---|---|
| B1 | A1 |
| B1 | A2 |
| B2 | A3 |
| B3 | A4 |
| B3 | A1 |
| B4 | A3 |

### 3.1.1 Derivation 1 — Natural Join `BOOK ⋈ WROTE ⋈ AUTHOR`

We compute step-by-step. First, take the Cartesian product of `BOOK` and `WROTE` (cardinality = 4 × 5 = 20 tuples). Then apply the **selection predicate** equating common `BookID`. Then JOIN with `AUTHOR` on `AuthorID`.

Mathematical expression:

$$\text{Result} = \pi_{Title, AuthorName, Country}(\sigma_{B.BookID = W.BookID \;\land\; W.AuthorID = A.AuthorID}(BOOK \times WROTE \times AUTHOR))$$

Step-by-step filtering — only matching rows survive:

1. `BOOK.B1` matches `WROTE.B1` (twice — for A1, A2) → 2 rows.
2. `BOOK.B2` matches `WROTE.B2` (A3) → 1 row.
3. `BOOK.B3` matches `WROTE.B3` (A4, A1) → 2 rows.
4. `BOOK.B4` matches `WROTE.B4` (A3) → 1 row.

**Cardinality of join = 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 6 tuples.**

Equivalent SQL:

```sql
SELECT B.Title, A.AuthorName, A.Country
FROM   BOOK B
NATURAL JOIN WROTE W
NATURAL JOIN AUTHOR A;
```

### 3.1.2 Derivation 2 — Relational Division: "Books written by ALL American authors"

Goal: List books for which **every** author from the USA appears in the WROTE table.

Let:
- `R = π_{BookID, AuthorID}(WROTE)` (a 2-column relation)
- `S = π_{AuthorID}(σ_{Country = 'USA'}(AUTHOR))` = `{A1, A2, A4}`

The division $R \div S$ returns the BookIDs that are associated with **all** authors in S.

Apply the algebraic identity:

$$R \div S = \pi_{BookID}(R) - \pi_{BookID}\big((\pi_{BookID}(R) \times S) - R\big)$$

**Step 1:** $\pi_{BookID}(R) = \{B1, B2, B3, B4\}$

**Step 2:** Compute $\pi_{BookID}(R) \times S$ — all possible (BookID, AuthorID) pairs where AuthorID ∈ {A1, A2, A4}:

| BookID | AuthorID |
|---|---|
| B1 | A1 |
| B1 | A2 |
| B1 | A4 |
| B2 | A1 |
| B2 | A2 |
| B2 | A4 |
| B3 | A1 |
| B3 | A2 |
| B3 | A4 |
| B4 | A1 |
| B4 | A2 |
| B4 | A4 |

**Step 3:** Subtract `R` (the actual WROTE table restricted to USA authors):

- B1 actually has A1, A2 → missing A4 in R → so (B1, A4) **stays** in the difference.
- B2 actually has A3 only → missing A1, A2, A4 → **all three pairs stay**.
- B3 actually has A4, A1 → missing A2 → (B3, A2) **stays**.
- B4 actually has A3 → missing A1, A2, A4 → **all three pairs stay**.

Difference (`(π(R)×S) − R`):

| BookID | AuthorID |
|---|---|
| B1 | A4 |
| B2 | A1 |
| B2 | A2 |
| B2 | A4 |
| B3 | A2 |
| B4 | A1 |
| B4 | A2 |
| B4 | A4 |

**Step 4:** Project the BookIDs of the difference: `{B1, B2, B3, B4}`.

**Step 5:** Subtract from $\pi_{BookID}(R)$:

$$\{B1, B2, B3, B4\} - \{B1, B2, B3, B4\} = \emptyset$$

**Conclusion:** No book is written by **all** American authors. (B1 is closest — written by 2 of 3 USA authors.)

Equivalent SQL (using the "double NOT EXISTS" trick):

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT W1.BookID
FROM   WROTE W1
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT A.AuthorID
    FROM   AUTHOR A
    WHERE  A.Country = 'USA'
    AND    NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT *
        FROM   WROTE W2
        WHERE  W2.BookID  = W1.BookID
        AND    W2.AuthorID = A.AuthorID
    )
);
```

### 3.1.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Query: "Total price of books per author, only authors earning > 1000"

```sql
SELECT A.AuthorName, SUM(B.Price) AS TotalRevenue
FROM   AUTHOR A
JOIN   WROTE W ON A.AuthorID = W.AuthorID
JOIN   BOOK   B ON W.BookID  = B.BookID
GROUP BY A.AuthorID, A.AuthorName
HAVING SUM(B.Price) > 1000;
```

**Trace (using logical processing order):**
1. `FROM`: Cartesian product of AUTHOR × WROTE × BOOK = 4 × 5 × 4 = 80 rows.
2. `JOIN ON`: Filters to 6 matching rows (as derived in §3.1.1).
3. `GROUP BY AuthorID`: Groups by each author. Each group is then collapsed.
4. Aggregates calculated: `SUM(Price)` per group.
5. `HAVING`: Filters groups where `SUM(Price) > 1000`.

**Per-author computation:**

| AuthorID | AuthorName | Books Written | SUM(Price) | Passes HAVING? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Korth | B1 (550), B3 (600) | **1150** | ✅ Yes |
| A2 | Silberschatz | B1 (550) | 550 | ❌ No |
| A3 | Tanenbaum | B2 (480), B4 (450) | 930 | ❌ No |
| A4 | Aho | B3 (600) | 600 | ❌ No |

**Final output:** `Korth, 1150`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `WHERE` cannot reference aggregate functions. `HAVING` *can*. Students often write `WHERE SUM(Price) > 1000` and lose 3 marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Translating English to Relational Algebra + SQL

| English Phrasing Pattern | Relational Algebra | SQL Clause |
|---|---|---|
| "Find all …" | $\pi_{...}(\sigma_{...}(R))$ | `SELECT … FROM … WHERE …` |
| "For every / All" | Division `R ÷ S` | `NOT EXISTS` (double negation) |
| "Only / Just / Exclusively" | Difference `R − S` | `EXCEPT` / `NOT IN` |
| "At least one" | Join (⋈) | `EXISTS` / `IN` |
| "More than half" | Aggregation + HAVING | `GROUP BY` + `HAVING COUNT(*) > n/2` |
| "Highest / Lowest" | $\gamma_{MAX/MIN}(R)$ | `MAX()` / `MIN()` or `ORDER BY … LIMIT 1` |
| "Number of distinct" | $\gamma_{COUNT(DISTINCT ...)}$ | `COUNT(DISTINCT col)` |
| "Common to both" | $R \cap S$ | `INTERSECT` |
| "Combine vertically" | $R \cup S$ | `UNION` (or `UNION ALL` to keep dupes) |
| "Combine horizontally" | $R \bowtie S$ | `JOIN ... ON` |

> [!TIP]
> This translation matrix is your **14-mark answer skeleton**. In KTU paper valuations, a clean English→RA→SQL chain is the gold standard — examiners give full marks when both notations are present.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Relational Model Conceptual Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Level User Views] --> B[Conceptual Level Relational Schema]
    B --> C[Internal Level Physical Storage]
    B --> D[Relational Algebra Engine]
    D --> E[SQL Parser Optimiser]
    E --> F[Query Execution Engine]
    F --> G[Disk Based Relation Storage]
    C --> G
    G --> F
    E --> H[Transaction Manager]
    H --> I[Concurrency Control Lock Manager]
    H --> J[Recovery Manager Log based]
    I --> K[Buffer Manager]
    J --> K
    K --> G
    B --> L[Integrity Constraint Checker]
    L --> F
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Relation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Relation STUDENT] --> S[Schema R name Attributes Domains Keys]
    R --> I[Instance Set of Tuples at time t]
    S --> S1[Attributes: RollNo Name DOB CGPA]
    S --> S2[Domains: int string date float]
    S --> S3[Primary Key: RollNo]
    S --> S4[Foreign Key: DeptID references DEPARTMENT]
    I --> T1[Tuples t1 t2 tn]
    I --> P1[Properties: No dups Atomic 1NF Unordered]
    S4 --> RI[Referential Integrity Enforcer]
    S3 --> EI[Entity Integrity PK NOT NULL]
    P1 --> NM[Normal Form 1NF 2NF 3NF BCNF]
```

## 4.3 Relational Algebra Operator Tree — Sample Query

**Question:** "Show names of authors who have written books priced above 500."

$$\pi_{AuthorName}\big( \sigma_{Price > 500}( AUTHOR \bowtie WROTE \bowtie BOOK )\big)$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Project AuthorName] --> J2[Natural Join AUTHOR with WROTE_BOOK]
    J2 --> S[Select Price greater than 500]
    S --> J1[Natural Join WROTE with BOOK]
    J1 --> A[AUTHOR relation]
    J1 --> B[BOOK relation]
    J1 --> W[WROTE relation]
    A --> J2
    J2 --> S
    S --> P
```

## 4.4 SQL Query Execution Pipeline (Logical Order)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[FROM clause Cartesian product of tables] --> W[WHERE clause row by row filter]
    W --> G[GROUP BY clause partition into groups]
    G --> H[HAVING clause group level filter]
    H --> S[SELECT clause compute expressions and aggregates]
    S --> D[DISTINCT remove duplicate output rows]
    D --> O[ORDER BY sort the final result set]
    O --> L[LIMIT OFFSET optional pagination]
```

## 4.5 Referential Integrity Action Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parent row change attempt] --> Q{Action Specified on FK}
    Q -->|RESTRICT| R1[Reject parent change Error]
    Q -->|CASCADE| R2[Propagate change to child rows]
    Q -->|SET NULL| R3[Set child FK to NULL]
    Q -->|SET DEFAULT| R4[Set child FK to default value]
    Q -->|NO ACTION deferrable| R5[Check at transaction end]
    R1 --> END1[Constraint preserved]
    R2 --> END2[Constraint preserved]
    R3 --> END3[Constraint preserved]
    R4 --> END4[Constraint preserved]
    R5 --> END5[Constraint preserved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The block diagrams are constructed so each *node* corresponds to a sub-expression of the relational algebra or a SQL clause. The KTU examiner's expectation is that you draw similar trees for any query carrying 7+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Primary Key and a Candidate Key. Can a relation have more than one primary key?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Candidate Key** is any minimal super key — a set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple and from which no attribute can be removed without losing uniqueness (1 mark). The **Primary Key** is the candidate key *selected* by the database designer to serve as the main unique identifier for tuples in a relation (1 mark). A relation **cannot** have more than one primary key, but it can have multiple candidate keys (the unselected ones are then called *Alternate Keys*) (1 mark).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain Entity Integrity and Referential Integrity constraints with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Entity Integrity** states that no attribute participating in the primary key of a relation can have a NULL value, because a NULL would make the tuple unidentifiable (1 mark). Example: in `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Dept)`, `RollNo` cannot be NULL (1 mark). **Referential Integrity** states that every value of a foreign key must either match an existing primary-key value in the parent relation or be NULL (1 mark). Example: `STUDENT.DeptID` (FK) must equal some existing `DEPARTMENT.DeptID` or be NULL.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. A(a)** **[7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Consider the following schema:
`STUDENT(SID, SName, Age, DeptID)`
`COURSE(CID, CName, Credits)`
`ENROLLMENT(SID, CID, Grade)` — with FK to both STUDENT and COURSE.

Write relational algebra expressions and equivalent SQL queries for the following:

**(i)** Find the names of all students who have enrolled for the course "DBMS".  
**(ii)** Find the names of students who have enrolled in *every* course offered.  
**(iii)** Find the names of students who have *not* enrolled in *any* course.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) "Students enrolled in DBMS" (3 marks for RA + SQL)**

Relational Algebra:

$$\pi_{SName}\big( STUDENT \bowtie ENROLLMENT \bowtie \sigma_{CName = \text{''DBMS''}}(COURSE) \big)$$

SQL:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT S.SName
FROM   STUDENT S
JOIN   ENROLLMENT E ON S.SID = E.SID
JOIN   COURSE    C ON E.CID = C.CID
WHERE  C.CName = 'DBMS';
```

**Valuation note (1 mark for RA, 1 mark for SQL, 1 mark for DISTINCT / duplicates removal).**

**(ii) "Students enrolled in EVERY course" — Relational Division (3 marks)**

This is the classic *division* pattern. Let:
- $R = \pi_{SID, CID}(ENROLLMENT)$
- $S = \pi_{CID}(COURSE)$

$$Result = \pi_{SName}\big( STUDENT \bowtie (R \div S) \big)$$

SQL (using double NOT EXISTS):

```sql
SELECT S.SName
FROM   STUDENT S
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT C.CID
    FROM   COURSE C
    WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT *
        FROM   ENROLLMENT E
        WHERE  E.SID = S.SID
        AND    E.CID = C.CID
    )
);
```

**(iii) "Students not enrolled in any course" (1 mark for set difference)**

$$\pi_{SName}(STUDENT) - \pi_{SName}(STUDENT \bowtie ENROLLMENT)$$

```sql
SELECT SName
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  SID NOT IN (SELECT SID FROM ENROLLMENT);
```

---

**Q. A(b)** **[7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Discuss the different types of JOIN operations in SQL with suitable examples and the conditions under which each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) INNER JOIN (1 mark):** Returns only the rows that have matching values in both tables based on the join predicate. It is the default `JOIN` in SQL.

```sql
SELECT S.SName, C.CName, E.Grade
FROM   STUDENT S
INNER JOIN ENROLLMENT E ON S.SID = E.SID
INNER JOIN COURSE    C ON E.CID = C.CID;
```

Use when you want *only* matched pairs — e.g., students who are actively enrolled.

**(2) LEFT (OUTER) JOIN (1.5 marks):** Returns *all* rows from the left table plus matched rows from the right (or NULLs for unmatched right columns). Preferred when the left table represents the "primary" set whose full information must be preserved.

```sql
SELECT S.SName, C.CName
FROM   STUDENT S
LEFT JOIN ENROLLMENT E ON S.SID = E.SID
LEFT JOIN COURSE    C ON E.CID = C.CID;
```
Here, students with no enrollment will appear with `NULL` for `CName`.

**(3) RIGHT (OUTER) JOIN (1 mark):** Mirror of LEFT JOIN — preserves all rows of the right table.

**(4) FULL (OUTER) JOIN (1.5 marks):** Preserves *all* rows of *both* tables; unmatched cells are padded with NULLs. Useful for symmetric merge scenarios.

```sql
SELECT S.SName, C.CName
FROM   STUDENT S
FULL OUTER JOIN ENROLLMENT E ON S.SID = E.SID;
```

**(5) CROSS JOIN (1 mark):** Cartesian product — no condition. Useful for generating combinatorial pairs.

**(6) SELF JOIN (1 mark):** A table joined to itself with aliases. Used for hierarchical data (employee → manager).

```sql
SELECT E.Ename AS Employee, M.Ename AS Manager
FROM   EMP E
JOIN   EMP M ON E.MgrID = M.EmpID;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Confusing `ON` vs `WHERE`:** With OUTER JOINs, predicates in `ON` are evaluated *during* the join (preserving outer rows), while `WHERE` filters them *after* (often turning the outer join back into an inner join). Placing the condition in the wrong place is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> - **Forgetting `DISTINCT` in division queries** causes duplicate rows in the output.
> - **Using `WHERE` with aggregates** (`WHERE COUNT(*) > 5`) is a syntax error — must use `HAVING`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. B(a)** **[7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]**
**Explain the Relational Data Model in detail. Discuss domain, tuple, attribute, schema, and instance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Relational Data Model** is a formal data model based on first-order predicate logic and set theory, introduced by E. F. Codd (1970), in which data is perceived as a collection of **relations** (1 mark).

- **Domain:** A set of atomic, permissible values. `D_Grade = {A, B, C, D, F}` (1 mark).
- **Attribute:** The role played by a domain within a relation. `Grade` of domain `D_Grade` (0.5 marks).
- **Tuple:** An ordered collection of attribute values, e.g., `(S101, 'Anu', 'A')` (1 mark).
- **Schema:** The structural blueprint: `STUDENT(SID, SName, Grade)` (1 mark).
- **Instance:** The actual set of tuples at a particular moment — **dynamic** (1 mark).

**Example illustrating closure (RA):** Renaming and projection of `STUDENT`:

$$\pi_{SName}(\sigma_{Grade = \text{''A''}}(STUDENT))$$

yields a new relation containing only the names of students with grade A. (1.5 marks for the worked example.)

---

**Q. B(b)** **[7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
**Consider the schema:**
`EMPLOYEE(EID, Ename, Salary, DeptID)`  
`DEPARTMENT(DID, Dname, Location)`  
`PROJECT(PID, Pname, Budget, DID)`  
`WORKS_ON(EID, PID, Hours)`

**Write SQL queries for the following:**

**(i)** Retrieve the names of employees working in the "Research" department. (2 marks)  
**(ii)** For each project, retrieve the project name and the total hours worked on it. (2 marks)  
**(iii)** Retrieve the names of employees who work on *all* projects of department "Research". (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i)**
```sql
SELECT E.Ename
FROM   EMPLOYEE E
JOIN   DEPARTMENT D ON E.DeptID = D.DID
WHERE  D.Dname = 'Research';
```

**(ii)**
```sql
SELECT P.Pname, SUM(W.Hours) AS TotalHours
FROM   PROJECT P
LEFT JOIN WORKS_ON W ON P.PID = W.PID
GROUP BY P.PID, P.Pname;
```

**(iii) — Division Pattern (NOT EXISTS / NOT EXISTS)**
```sql
SELECT E.Ename
FROM   EMPLOYEE E
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT P.PID
    FROM   PROJECT P
    JOIN   DEPARTMENT D ON P.DID = D.DID
    WHERE  D.Dname = 'Research'
    AND    NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT *
        FROM   WORKS_ON W
        WHERE  W.EID = E.EID
        AND    W.PID = P.PID
    )
);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall on Q. B(b)(iii):** Many students write a single `NOT IN` query, which fails when *any* subquery returns NULL. Using correlated `NOT EXISTS` is NULL-safe and the KTU-preferred solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin on Wall):**
> - [ ] **Relation ≠ Table:** A relation is a *mathematical set*; tables in SQL may have duplicates and order (theoretically).
> - [ ] **Degree** = number of attributes (columns); **Cardinality** = number of tuples (rows).
> - [ ] **Keys ladder:** Super → Candidate → (Primary + Alternates). Foreign Key may be NULL only if it is *not* part of its own PK.
> - [ ] **Entity Integrity:** PK cannot be NULL. **Referential Integrity:** FK must match existing PK or be NULL.
> - [ ] **Closure property** of relational algebra: every operator returns a relation → enables nesting.
> - [ ] **Cardinality rules:** $|R \times S| = |R|\cdot|S|$, $|R \bowtie S| \le |R|\cdot|S|$, $|R \cup S| \le |R|+|S|$.
> - [ ] **Division = "for all" / "every":** implement as double `NOT EXISTS`.
> - [ ] **Logical (not textual) SQL order:** FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY.
> - [ ] **`WHERE` filters rows, `HAVING` filters groups** — never mix them.
> - [ ] **Three-Valued Logic:** `NULL = NULL` is `UNKNOWN`, not `TRUE`; use `IS NULL`.
> - [ ] **OUTER JOIN's `ON` clause** preserves unmatched rows; moving the predicate to `WHERE` silently turns it into an inner join.
> - [ ] **Set operations** (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`) require *union-compatible* relations (same degree, compatible domains).
> - [ ] **DDL vs DML vs TCL vs DCL** — classify each command correctly in 3-mark definitions.
> - [ ] **Always draw the operator tree** in 14-mark algebra questions — examiners reward visual clarity.
> - [ ] **Composite keys** appear in M:N junction tables like `WROTE(BookID, AuthorID)` and `ENROLLMENT(SID, CID)`.
> - [ ] **NULL ≠ 0 ≠ empty string** — it denotes *unknown* or *not applicable*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Relational Data Model — Formal Definition

The **Relational Data Model** is a formal, mathematically grounded framework proposed by **Edgar F. Codd (1970)** in his landmark paper *"A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks"*. It represents the database as a collection of **relations**, which are perceived by the user as **tables** consisting of rows and columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Adopted by Board of Studies):**
> A **relation** $R$ over a set of domains $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$ is a subset of the Cartesian product $D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$. Each element of this subset is called a **tuple**, and the relation is logically structured as a table with $n$ **attributes** (columns).

### 1.2 Core Terminology

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|------|----------------|
| **Domain $D$** | A set of atomic (indivisible) permissible values for an attribute. |
| **Attribute $A_i$** | The name of the role played by a domain $D_i$ in a relation schema. |
| **Tuple $t$** | An ordered set of values $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n \rangle$ where $v_i \in D_i$. |
| **Relation Schema $R$** | A relation name $R$ together with a list of attributes $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$. |
| **Relation Instance $r(R)$** | A finite set of tuples conforming to schema $R$ at a specific point in time. |
| **Degree of Relation** | Number of attributes $n$ in the relation schema. |
| **Cardinality** | Number of tuples in the relation instance at a given moment. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Filing Cabinet**
> Think of the **relational model** as a **spreadsheet or ledger book** in a bank. Each *sheet* is a **relation** (table). Each *column header* (Account No, Name, Balance) is an **attribute**. Each *row* (one customer's record) is a **tuple**. The *set of allowed values* for Balance (positive numbers with 2 decimal places) is the **domain**. The "blueprint" of the sheet (its column structure) is the **schema**, while the actual filled-in rows represent the **instance** at that moment in time.

**Why tables?** Because users intuitively understand grids. Codd's genius was to hide the underlying complexity (linked lists, hierarchical trees) behind a simple tabular abstraction, freeing the application programmer from worrying about physical storage paths.

## 1.4 Properties of Relations (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every relation in the relational model must satisfy these six structural properties:
> 1. **Atomic Values**: Every value in a tuple is an atomic (single) value — no repeating groups or arrays (this is the **First Normal Form** property).
> 2. **Unique Tuples**: Every tuple in a relation is unique (no two rows are identical).
> 3. **Unordered Tuples**: The order of rows is insignificant.
> 4. **Unordered Attributes**: The order of columns is insignificant (attributes are referenced by name, not position).
> 5. **Homogeneous Columns**: All values in a single column belong to the same domain.
> 6. **Tuple-by-Tuple Interpretation**: Each tuple can be uniquely interpreted on its own (guaranteed by the primary key).

## 1.5 Schema vs. Instance — Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Relation Schema (intension)** = The logical structure / design of the relation. It is **static** and changes rarely.
>
> **Relation Instance (extension)** = The actual data stored at a particular time. It is **dynamic** and changes with every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
>
> Example: `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, DOB, Branch)` is the **schema**. The set of 5,000 student records on `15-March-2025` is the **instance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Mathematical Representation

A **relation schema** $R$ of degree $n$ is denoted as:

$$R(A_1 : D_1, A_2 : D_2, \dots, A_n : D_n)$$

Where each $A_i$ is an attribute name drawn from the union of underlying domains, and $D_i$ is its associated domain.

The corresponding **relation instance** $r(R)$ is a finite subset of the Cartesian product of the domains:

$$r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$$

A tuple $t$ in $r(R)$ is a function mapping attribute names to domain values:

$$t : \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\} \rightarrow D_1 \cup D_2 \cup \dots \cup D_n$$

A tuple $t$ on a specific attribute $A_i$ is written as $t[A_i]$ or $t.A_i$.

## 2.2 Keys in the Relational Model (Deep Dive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keys are the heart of integrity enforcement.** KTU 2024 examiners love asking "distinguish between X and Y" or "explain Z with example" — memorise every type below.

### 2.2.1 Super Key
- A set of attributes $SK \subseteq R$ such that for any two distinct tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in $r(R)$: $t_1[SK] \neq t_2[SK]$.
- **Uniqueness** is guaranteed, but **minimality is not required**.
- Every relation has at least one super key (the set of all attributes).

### 2.2.2 Candidate Key
- A **minimal** super key — no proper subset is a super key.
- A candidate key satisfies both **uniqueness** and **irreducibility**.
- A relation may have **multiple candidate keys**.

### 2.2.3 Primary Key
- The candidate key **chosen by the database designer** to uniquely identify tuples.
- Properties enforced: NOT NULL + UNIQUE.

### 2.2.4 Alternate Key
- Candidate keys that were **not chosen** as the primary key.
- These are still unique and non-redundant.

### 2.2.5 Unique Key (Secondary Key)
- A key whose values must be **unique** across the relation, but is **not used as the primary identifier**.
- In SQL, may allow a single NULL (DB-dependent).

### 2.2.6 Foreign Key
- An attribute (or set of attributes) $FK$ in a relation $R_2$ that **references the primary key** $PK$ of another relation $R_1$.
- The value of $FK$ in $R_2$ must either match a value of $PK$ in $R_1$ or be NULL.
- Establishes **referential integrity** between relations.

## 2.3 Relational Integrity Constraints (Four Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGH-YIELD TOPIC** — These four constraints are guaranteed by the relational model itself and form the basis for almost every Part B question in Module 2.

### 2.3.1 Domain Constraint
- **Rule**: Every value of an attribute $A_i$ must be an atomic value from the specified domain $D_i$.
- **Example**: In `STUDENT(Marks)`, the domain is integers from 0 to 100. The value `105` violates the constraint.

### 2.3.2 Key Constraint (Uniqueness Constraint)
- **Rule**: The value of any **candidate key** must be **unique** across all tuples of the relation.
- **Example**: Two students cannot have the same `RollNo`.

### 2.3.3 Entity Integrity Constraint
- **Rule**: The **primary key** attribute(s) cannot have a `NULL` value.
- **Reason**: The primary key identifies a real-world entity — without it, the entity is unidentifiable.
- **Example**: `RollNo` in `STUDENT` cannot be NULL.

### 2.3.4 Referential Integrity Constraint
- **Rule**: A foreign key value in relation $R_2$ must either:
  1. Match an existing primary key value in the referenced relation $R_1$, **OR**
  2. Be `NULL` (meaning the reference is unknown or not applicable).
- **Example**: `DeptID` in `STUDENT` must match a valid `DeptID` in `DEPARTMENT` table.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the consolidated 14-mark answer framework for Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formal Condition | Symbol | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------------------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | Relation | $r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$ | $r(R)$ | Logical table representation |
| 2 | Tuple | $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n \rangle$ | $t$ | One row / record |
| 3 | Super Key | $t_1[SK] \neq t_2[SK] \ \forall t_1 \neq t_2$ | $SK$ | Uniqueness guarantee |
| 4 | Candidate Key | Minimal $SK$ such that no subset works | $CK$ | Primary identifier candidate |
| 5 | Primary Key | Chosen $CK$, no NULLs | $PK$ | Tuple identifier |
| 6 | Foreign Key | $FK \subseteq R_2$, $FK \subseteq PK$ of $R_1$ | $FK$ | Inter-table linking |
| 7 | Domain Constraint | $t[A_i] \in dom(A_i)$ | $\in$ | Type & range check |
| 8 | Key Constraint | $t_1[CK] \neq t_2[CK]$ | $\neq$ | Uniqueness enforcement |
| 9 | Entity Integrity | $t[PK] \neq \text{NULL}$ | $\neq \phi$ | No missing identifier |
| 10 | Referential Integrity | $t_2[FK] \in \pi_{PK}(r(R_1)) \cup \{\text{NULL}\}$ | $\in$ | Valid cross-reference |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: `Account` and `Customer` relations linked by foreign keys ensure every transaction references a valid account.
- **Hospital Management**: `Patient(SSN, Name, ...)` enforces entity integrity — no patient can exist without a unique SSN.
- **E-Commerce**: `Order` and `Product` relations joined by `ProductID` maintain referential integrity even at millions of transactions per second.
- **IoT & Sensor Data**: Domain constraints (e.g., `Temperature` ∈ [-50°C, 150°C]) reject spurious sensor readings at ingestion time.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Formal Relation Construction (Foundational)

**Problem:** Construct a relation $R$ with schema $R(A, B, C)$ where:
- $dom(A) = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- $dom(B) = \{x, y\}$
- $dom(C) = \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$

**Step 1**: Form the Cartesian product of all three domains.

$$D_A \times D_B \times D_C = \{(v_1, v_2, v_3) \mid v_1 \in D_A, v_2 \in D_B, v_3 \in D_C\}$$

**Step 2**: Enumerate the total number of possible tuples.

$$|D_A \times D_B \times D_C| = \vert D_A \vert \times \vert D_B \vert \times \vert D_C \vert = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12$$

**Step 3**: A relation $R$ is *any subset* of these 12 tuples. Suppose we define $R$ as the set of tuples where $C$ is True.

$$r(R) = \{(1, x, T), (1, y, T), (2, x, T), (2, y, T), (3, x, T), (3, y, T)\}$$

**Step 4**: Cardinality $= 6$, Degree $= 3$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Finding Candidate Keys via Attribute Closure

**Problem:** Given a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with the set of Functional Dependencies (FDs):

$$F = \{A \rightarrow B, \quad B \rightarrow C, \quad C \rightarrow D, \quad D \rightarrow E, \quad E \rightarrow A\}$$

**Find all candidate keys of $R$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Attribute Closure $X^+$** is the set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$ using the given FDs. $X$ is a candidate key if and only if $X^+ = R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Verify that no single attribute is a key (each single attribute derives only its forward chain).

- $A^+ = \{A\}$ (using $A \rightarrow B$, we add $B$)

$$A^+ = \{A, B\}$$

Continue: add $C$ via $B \rightarrow C$, add $D$ via $C \rightarrow D$, add $E$ via $D \rightarrow E$, and add back $A$ (already present).

$$A^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R \implies A \text{ is a candidate key}$$

**Step 2**: Since the FDs form a single cyclic chain $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow A$, every single attribute acts as a key.

- $B^+ = \{B, C, D, E, A\} = R$ ✓
- $C^+ = \{C, D, E, A, B\} = R$ ✓
- $D^+ = \{D, E, A, B, C\} = R$ ✓
- $E^+ = \{E, A, B, C, D\} = R$ ✓

**Step 3**: All **five** single attributes are candidate keys. No composite key is needed.

**Conclusion**: The set of all candidate keys is:

$$\mathcal{CK} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$

The designer may choose any one as the **primary key**; the remaining four become **alternate keys**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Attribute Key Discovery

**Problem:** Given $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with FDs:

$$F = \{AB \rightarrow C, \quad C \rightarrow D, \quad D \rightarrow B\}$$

**Find all candidate keys.**

### Derivation

**Step 1**: Check single attributes.

- $A^+ = \{A\}$ (no FD starts with $A$ alone)
- $B^+ = \{B\}$
- $C^+ = \{C, D, B\}$ (still missing $A, E$)
- $D^+ = \{D, B\}$ (still missing $A, C, E$)
- $E^+ = \{E\}$

**No single attribute is a key.**

**Step 2**: Check two-attribute combinations systematically.

Compute $(AE)^+$:

$$\{A, E\} \xrightarrow{\text{no FD}} \text{stop} \implies (AE)^+ = \{A, E\}$$

Not a key. Compute $(AB)^+$:

$$\{A, B\} \xrightarrow{AB \rightarrow C} \{A, B, C\} \xrightarrow{C \rightarrow D} \{A, B, C, D\}$$

No more FDs applicable (D→B gives nothing new, E unreachable).

$$(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D\} \neq R \quad \text{(Missing } E\text{)}$$

**Not a key.** Compute $(AD)^+$:

$$\{A, D\} \xrightarrow{D \rightarrow B} \{A, B, D\} \xrightarrow{AB \rightarrow C} \{A, B, C, D\}$$

$$(AD)^+ = \{A, B, C, D\} \neq R$$

**Step 3**: Note that $E$ appears in **no FD on the right-hand side** in a useful way and is **not present on the left of any FD**. This means $E$ **must** be part of every candidate key.

**Step 4**: Compute closures with $E$ included.

- $(AE)^+ = \{A, E\}$ ✗
- $(BE)^+ = \{B, E\}$ ✗
- $(CE)^+ = \{C, D, B, E\} = \{B, C, D, E\}$ ✗
- $(DE)^+ = \{D, B, E\}$ ✗
- $(AB, E)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R$ ✓
- $(AC, E)^+ = \{A, C, D, B, E\} = R$ ✓
- $(AD, E)^+ = \{A, D, B, C, E\} = R$ ✓
- $(BC, E)^+ = \{B, C, D, E\}$ ✗
- $(BD, E)^+ = \{B, D, E\}$ ✗
- $(CD, E)^+ = \{C, D, B, E\}$ ✗

**Step 5**: Confirm minimality — no proper subset of $\{A,B,E\}$, $\{A,C,E\}$, or $\{A,D,E\}$ is a key (already verified above).

**Conclusion**: The set of candidate keys is:

$$\mathcal{CK} = \{ABE, ACE, ADE\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: $A$ and $E$ are both **essential** — $A$ because no other attribute can derive it, and $E$ because it never appears on the RHS of any FD. Such attributes are called **essential / prime attributes**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this — examiners expect you to produce a clean comparison.**

| Constraint | Scope | Applies To | NULL Allowed? | Violation Example |
|------------|-------|------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Domain** | Attribute-level | Single column | Yes | Marks = 150 (out of 0–100 range) |
| **Key** | Relation-level | Candidate key attributes | Only for non-PK unique keys | Two tuples with same RollNo |
| **Entity Integrity** | Relation-level | Primary key only | **No (strict)** | RollNo = NULL |
| **Referential Integrity** | Inter-relation | Foreign key attributes | Yes (if not part of PK) | DeptID = 99 (not in DEPARTMENT) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Architecture of the Relational Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RELATIONAL MODEL] --> B[STRUCTURAL PART]
    A --> C[INTEGRITY PART]
    A --> D[MANIPULATIVE PART]
    B --> B1[Relation]
    B --> B2[Relation Schema]
    B --> B3[Relation Instance]
    B --> B4[Attributes]
    B --> B5[Tuples]
    B --> B6[Domains]
    C --> C1[Domain Constraint]
    C --> C2[Key Constraint]
    C --> C3[Entity Integrity]
    C --> C4[Referential Integrity]
    D --> D1[Retrieval]
    D --> D2[Insert]
    D --> D3[Update]
    D --> D4[Delete]
    C1 --> X1[Atomic Values Only]
    C2 --> X2[Unique Candidate Keys]
    C3 --> X3[PK Not Null]
    C4 --> X4[FK Matches PK or Null]
    B1 --> Y1[Table]
    B2 --> Y2[Table Header Design]
    B3 --> Y3[Table Data Snapshot]
    B4 --> Y4[Column Names]
    B5 --> Y5[Row Records]
    B6 --> Y6[Value Pools]
```

## 4.2 Key Hierarchy — Types of Keys in RDBMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[KEYS IN RELATIONAL MODEL] --> SK[SUPER KEY]
    K --> CK[NON-SUPER ATTRIBUTES]
    SK --> CK2[CANDIDATE KEY]
    CK2 --> PK[PRIMARY KEY]
    CK2 --> AK[ALTERNATE KEY]
    K --> UK[UNIQUE KEY]
    K --> FK[FOREIGN KEY]
    SK --> S1[Unique but not minimal]
    CK2 --> C1[Unique AND minimal]
    PK --> P1[Chosen by designer, no NULL]
    AK --> A1[Other candidate keys not chosen]
    UK --> U1[Unique value, may allow single NULL]
    FK --> F1[References PK of another table]
    FK --> R1[Establishes Referential Integrity]
    PK -.Rejects.-> N1[NULL VALUES]
    FK -.Permits.-> N2[NULL VALUES if not PK of own table]
```

## 4.3 Constraint Enforcement Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[USER QUERY] --> V[SCHEMA VALIDATOR]
    V --> W{DOMAIN CHECK}
    W -- Pass --> X{KEY UNIQUENESS}
    W -- Fail --> E1[REJECT ERROR 1]
    X -- Pass --> Y{ENTITY INTEGRITY}
    X -- Fail --> E2[REJECT ERROR 2]
    Y -- Pass --> Z{REFERENTIAL CHECK}
    Y -- Fail --> E3[REJECT ERROR 3]
    Z -- Pass --> EX[EXECUTE TRANSACTION]
    Z -- Fail --> E4[REJECT ERROR 4]
    E1 --> ERR[ERROR REPORT TO CLIENT]
    E2 --> ERR
    E3 --> ERR
    E4 --> ERR
    EX --> COMM[COMMIT TO DATABASE]
    style V fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style EX fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style ERR fill:#f66,stroke:#333
    style COMM fill:#6f6,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Relational Schema Example — STUDENT / DEPARTMENT / COURSE

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ STUDENT : "has many"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ COURSE : "offers"
    STUDENT }o--o{ COURSE : "enrolls in"
    DEPARTMENT {
        string DeptID PK
        string DeptName
        string HOD
    }
    STUDENT {
        string RollNo PK
        string Name
        string DOB
        string DeptID FK
    }
    COURSE {
        string CourseID PK
        string CourseName
        int Credits
        string DeptID FK
    }
    ENROLLMENT {
        string RollNo FK
        string CourseID FK
        string Grade
    }
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "appears in"
    COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "appears in"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: `||--o{` means "exactly one to zero-or-many". `}o--o{` means "many-to-many (requires a junction table — here `ENROLLMENT` resolves the M:N relationship)". `PK` denotes primary keys; `FK` denotes foreign keys. The arrows show where **referential integrity** is enforced.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define the following with one example each:**
(a) Relation Schema
(b) Domain
(c) Attribute

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**(a) Relation Schema (1 mark):**
A **relation schema** is the logical design / structure of a relation. It is denoted by $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$ where $R$ is the relation name and $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ are the attribute names. It describes the **intension** of the relation.
*Example:* `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, DOB, Branch)` — this is the schema.

**(b) Domain (1 mark):**
A **domain** is a set of atomic, permissible values from which the values of an attribute are drawn.
*Example:* $dom(\text{Marks}) = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 100\}$; $dom(\text{Branch}) = \{\text{CS, EC, ME, CE}\}$.

**(c) Attribute (1 mark):**
An **attribute** is the name of a role played by a domain in a relation schema. Each attribute is associated with a specific domain.
*Example:* In `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, DOB, Branch)`, `RollNo`, `Name`, `DOB`, `Branch` are four attributes.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Distinguish between a *candidate key* and a *super key*. Give one example of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | Super Key | Candidate Key |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Definition** | A set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple | A **minimal** super key |
| **Minimality** | Not required | Required (no proper subset is a super key) |
| **Count** | Many per relation | One or more per relation |
| **Role** | Used in analysis | Used to choose primary key |

*Example:* In relation $R(\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}, \text{Email}, \text{Phone})$:
- **Super keys:** $\{\text{RollNo}\}$, $\{\text{Email}\}$, $\{\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}\}$, $\{\text{Phone}, \text{Email}\}$, etc.
- **Candidate keys:** $\{\text{RollNo}\}$, $\{\text{Email}\}$ (minimal super keys). $\{\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}\}$ is **not** a candidate key because its proper subset $\{\text{RollNo}\}$ is already a super key.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the following relational integrity constraints in detail with one example each: **(7 marks)**
  (i) Domain Constraint
  (ii) Key Constraint
  (iii) Entity Integrity Constraint
  (iv) Referential Integrity Constraint

**(b)** Consider the relation $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with the set of Functional Dependencies:

$$F = \{AB \rightarrow C, \quad C \rightarrow D, \quad D \rightarrow B, \quad D \rightarrow E\}$$

Find all **candidate keys** of $R$. Show the step-by-step closure calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Relational Integrity Constraints (7 Marks)**

**(i) Domain Constraint (1.75 marks):**
The **domain constraint** specifies that every value of an attribute $A_i$ must be an **atomic value belonging to the predefined domain** $dom(A_i)$. It enforces data type, format, range, and allowed values.
*Example:* In `STUDENT(Marks)`, $dom(\text{Marks}) = [0, 100]$. A value of `150` violates the domain constraint.

**(ii) Key Constraint (1.75 marks):**
The **key constraint** (uniqueness constraint) states that the value of any **candidate key** must be **unique** across all tuples in the relation — no two tuples can have the same value for the candidate key attributes.
*Example:* In `STUDENT(RollNo, Name)`, two students cannot have the same `RollNo`. The value `CS2101` can appear in only one tuple.

**(iii) Entity Integrity Constraint (1.75 marks):**
The **entity integrity constraint** states that the **primary key** attribute(s) of a relation **cannot be NULL**. This is because the primary key is the unique identifier of a real-world entity — without it, the entity cannot be identified.
*Example:* `RollNo` in `STUDENT` is the primary key. Inserting `(NULL, 'Anu', 'CSE')` violates entity integrity.

**(iv) Referential Integrity Constraint (1.75 marks):**
The **referential integrity constraint** applies to foreign keys. It states that a foreign key value in relation $R_2$ must either **match an existing primary key value in the referenced relation $R_1$** or be **NULL**.
*Example:* Consider:
- `DEPARTMENT(DeptID PK, DeptName)`
- `STUDENT(RollNo PK, Name, DeptID FK references DEPARTMENT)`

If `STUDENT` has a tuple with `DeptID = 'D99'` and no such `DeptID` exists in `DEPARTMENT`, referential integrity is violated. The action may be **Rejected**, **Cascade**, or **Set NULL** (depending on the policy).

---

**Part (b) — Finding Candidate Keys (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify attributes that **never appear on the RHS** of any FD (essential prime attribute candidates).
- $A$ appears only on LHS → must be in every key.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Check single-attribute closures.
- $A^+ = \{A\}$ — not a key.
- $B^+ = \{B\}$ — not a key.
- $C^+ = \{C, D, B, E\}$ — not a key (missing $A$).
- $D^+ = \{D, B, E\}$ — not a key (missing $A, C$).
- $E^+ = \{E\}$ — not a key.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Check two-attribute combinations (focus on those containing $A$).
- $(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ — start with $A, B$; apply $AB \rightarrow C$ to get $C$; apply $C \rightarrow D$ to get $D$; apply $D \rightarrow E$ to get $E$. **Reached $R$ ✓**
- $(AC)^+ = \{A, C, D, B, E\}$ — apply $C \rightarrow D$, $D \rightarrow B$ and $D \rightarrow E$. **Reached $R$ ✓**
- $(AD)^+ = \{A, D, B, C, E\}$ — apply $D \rightarrow B$, then $AB \rightarrow C$, then $C \rightarrow D$ (already have), and $D \rightarrow E$. **Reached $R$ ✓**
- $(AE)^+ = \{A, E\}$ — not a key.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Verify minimality.
- $ABE$: drop $A$? $\{B,E\}^+ = \{B,E\}$ — not a key. So $A$ is essential. Drop $B$? $\{A,E\}^+ = \{A,E\}$ — not a key. So $AB$ is essential. $\{A,B\}^+ = R$ already. ✓
- Similarly confirm $ACE$ and $ADE$ are minimal.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** State the final result.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{CK} = \{ABE, ACE, ADE\}}$$

All three are candidate keys. The designer may select any one as the **primary key**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Exam 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the components of the **Relational Data Model**. Define the formal terms: *domain*, *tuple*, *attribute*, *relation*, *relation schema*, and *relation instance* with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the different **types of keys** in a relational database with a suitable example relation. Show how **referential integrity** is maintained using foreign keys. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Components of the Relational Data Model (7 Marks)**

The **Relational Data Model** has three logical components:

**1. Structural Part (2 marks):**
Defines the database as a collection of **relations** (tables). Each relation has a unique name and consists of rows and columns.

**2. Integrity Part (2 marks):**
Defines rules (constraints) that the data must obey: domain, key, entity integrity, and referential integrity.

**3. Manipulative Part (1.5 marks):**
Defines operations to query and update the database: retrieval (SELECT), insertion (INSERT), update (UPDATE), and deletion (DELETE).

**Formal Definitions (1.5 marks):**

- **Domain $D$:** A set of atomic, permissible values. *Example:* $dom(\text{Branch}) = \{\text{CS, EC, ME, CE}\}$.
- **Attribute $A$:** Name of a role played by a domain. *Example:* `Branch` is an attribute of `STUDENT`.
- **Tuple $t$:** An ordered set of values representing one record. *Example:* $t = \langle \text{"CS2101"}, \text{"Anu"}, \text{"CSE"} \rangle$.
- **Relation $r(R)$:** A subset of $D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$ — a set of tuples conforming to schema $R$.
- **Relation Schema $R$:** The structure of the relation. *Example:* `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Branch)`.
- **Relation Instance:** The actual data at a point in time — the **state** of the relation.

**Example Relation Instance of `STUDENT`:**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|--------|------|--------|
| CS2101 | Anu | CSE |
| EC2102 | Balu | ECE |
| ME2103 | Chitra | MECH |

---

**Part (b) — Types of Keys & Referential Integrity (7 Marks)**

**1. Super Key (1 mark):** A set of attributes that **uniquely identifies** tuples. Minimality not required.
*Example:* $\{\text{RollNo}\}$, $\{\text{Email}\}$, $\{\text{RollNo, Name}\}$.

**2. Candidate Key (1 mark):** A **minimal** super key.
*Example:* $\{\text{RollNo}\}$, $\{\text{Email}\}$.

**3. Primary Key (1 mark):** The candidate key **chosen by the designer** to identify tuples; never NULL.
*Example:* `RollNo` chosen as PK (Email becomes an alternate key).

**4. Alternate Key (1 mark):** Unchosen candidate keys.
*Example:* `Email` is the alternate key.

**5. Unique Key (1 mark):** A key that enforces uniqueness but may allow one NULL.
*Example:* `Aadhaar` in `CITIZEN` table — unique, but can be NULL for non-Indians.

**6. Foreign Key (1 mark):** An attribute referencing the primary key of another (or same) relation.
*Example:* `DeptID` in `STUDENT` references `DeptID` in `DEPARTMENT`.

**Referential Integrity Maintenance (1 mark):**
When inserting into `STUDENT` with `DeptID = 'D05'`, the system checks that `D05` exists in `DEPARTMENT.DeptID`. If not, the insertion is **rejected** (or cascaded/set NULL, depending on the policy defined in the `REFERENCES` clause).

```sql
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    RollNo   VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
    Name     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    DeptID   VARCHAR(5),
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DeptID)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating "alternate key" with "unique key".** Alternate keys are *candidate keys not chosen as primary*. Unique keys are *additional non-PK columns that must be unique* — they are not necessarily candidate keys.
> 2. **Forgetting NULL rule in Entity Integrity.** Many students write "primary key cannot be empty or zero." The precise KTU phrasing is: **"The primary key must NOT be NULL."** Zero is a valid integer — only NULL is forbidden.
> 3. **Confusing domain and key constraint.** Domain constraint checks *value range*; key constraint checks *uniqueness* of an entire set of attributes. Do not mix them up in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **In closure problems, skipping minimality check.** Computing $X^+$ is only half the answer. You MUST verify that **no proper subset of $X$** is a key — otherwise $X$ is just a super key, not a candidate key.
> 5. **Writing "foreign key cannot be NULL."** This is wrong! A foreign key **can** be NULL (signifying "no link") — the only restriction is that if non-NULL, it must match an existing PK in the parent table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — tick off each item before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Relational Model** is the foundation of RDBMS; proposed by **Edgar F. Codd (1970)**.
- [ ] **Relation** = Table; **Tuple** = Row; **Attribute** = Column; **Domain** = Set of allowed atomic values.
- [ ] **Schema (intension)** = Structure; **Instance (extension)** = Current data.
- [ ] Six properties of relations: atomic values, unique tuples, unordered rows/cols, homogeneous columns, tuple-by-tuple interpretation.
- [ ] **Super Key** = Unique identifier (may have redundant attrs).
- [ ] **Candidate Key** = Minimal super key.
- [ ] **Primary Key** = Chosen candidate key, **NOT NULL**.
- [ ] **Alternate Key** = Unchosen candidate keys.
- [ ] **Foreign Key** = Attribute referencing PK of another table; value must match or be NULL.
- [ ] **Domain Constraint** → Atomic values from declared domain.
- [ ] **Key Constraint** → Candidate key values must be unique.
- [ ] **Entity Integrity** → PK cannot be NULL.
- [ ] **Referential Integrity** → FK must match a parent PK or be NULL.
- [ ] **Closure $X^+$** = Set of all attributes derivable from $X$ via FDs. $X$ is a key iff $X^+ = R$.
- [ ] **Essential prime attributes** (those never on the RHS of any FD) must be part of **every** candidate key.
- [ ] Remember to use `\text{NULL}` or `\phi` in LaTeX, **never** write `|NULL|` (breaks markdown table).
- [ ] Practice 3–4 closure problems before the exam — they appear in **almost every KTU paper**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relational Algebra and Relational Calculus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relational Algebra and Relational Calculus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Relational Algebra (RA)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Relational Algebra is a **procedural query language** consisting of a set of operations that take one or two relations as input and produce a new relation as their output. It is the theoretical foundation of the **Structured Query Language (SQL)** and forms the mathematical backbone of query optimization in relational database management systems (RDBMS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property — Closure:** Every operation in Relational Algebra takes relations as input and produces a **relation** as output. This is known as the **closure property**, and it is the single most important concept behind composing complex queries from simple operations.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of Relational Algebra as a **"recipe for a data chef."** When you walk into a kitchen (the database), the chef (query engine) follows your exact step-by-step instructions. You don't just say "give me something tasty" — you say "first, take the vegetables (input relation), then chop them (SELECT), then boil them (PROJECT), and finally mix them (JOIN) to make the soup (output relation)." The chef must follow *your* procedure exactly. Each intermediate dish is itself a complete dish (relation) that can be served or used as an ingredient for the next step.

The **6 fundamental (primitive) operations** of Relational Algebra are:
- **Selection (σ)** — picks rows (tuples)
- **Projection (π)** — picks columns (attributes)
- **Union (∪)** — combines rows from two relations
- **Set Difference (−)** — subtracts rows
- **Cartesian Product (×)** — combines every row of one with every row of another
- **Rename (ρ)** — renames the output schema

## 1.2 Relational Calculus (RC)

**Formal Definition:**
> Relational Calculus is a **non-procedural (declarative) query language** that describes *what* data to retrieve without specifying *how* to retrieve it. The query is expressed as a **declaration of properties** that the result tuples must satisfy. There are two mathematically equivalent forms: **Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)** and **Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If Relational Algebra is a recipe, then Relational Calculus is a **"grocery list with conditions."** You write down a description of the dish you want — *"I want ingredients that are red, round, and weigh more than 100 grams"* — and the kitchen (query engine) figures out how to find them. You don't care whether they come from shelf A or shelf B; you only describe the properties. The system uses a calculus-based optimizer to translate your description into an efficient execution plan.

**Two Variants of Relational Calculus:**

| Variant | Variable Type | Output Looks Like |
|---|---|---|
| **Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)** | Ranges over **tuples** (entire rows) | A set of tuples $t$ such that some predicate holds |
| **Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)** | Ranges over **domain values** (single cells/columns) | A set of values drawn from attribute domains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Codd's Theorem (1972):** *Relational Algebra, Tuple Relational Calculus, and Domain Relational Calculus are **logically equivalent** in expressive power.* Anything you can express in one, you can express in the others. This is why SQL (which is calculus-like in spirit) can always be translated to relational algebra for optimization.

**Standard Metrics (used in KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations):**
- The set of primitive RA operations = **6** (Select, Project, Union, Set Difference, Cartesian Product, Rename)
- Tuple variables in TRC are typically written as $t$, $s$, $u$, …
- Domain variables in DRC are typically written as $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$
- A relation of degree $n$ has $n$ attributes (also called **arity**)
- A relation of cardinality $m$ has $m$ tuples (also called **cardinality**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Fundamental Operations of Relational Algebra

### A. Selection (σ) — Horizontal Filter
The **SELECT** operation picks tuples (rows) that satisfy a given predicate. It is a **horizontal** partition.

$$\sigma_{P}(R)$$

where $P$ is a selection predicate built from attribute names, comparison operators ($=, \neq, \lt, \le, \gt, \ge$), and logical connectives ($\land, \lor, \neg$).

- **Input:** A single relation $R$
- **Output:** A relation containing only those tuples of $R$ that satisfy $P$
- **Degree:** Same as $R$
- **Cardinality:** Less than or equal to that of $R$

### B. Projection (π) — Vertical Filter
The **PROJECT** operation picks specified columns (attributes) from a relation, eliminating duplicates automatically.

$$\pi_{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n}(R)$$

- **Input:** A single relation $R$
- **Output:** A relation containing only the listed attributes
- **Degree:** Equal to the number of listed attributes
- **Cardinality:** Less than or equal to that of $R$ (duplicates are removed)

### C. Union (∪) — Row Combination
The **UNION** operation merges two relations that are **union-compatible** (same degree, same domains, but possibly different names).

$$R \cup S = \{ t \mid t \in R \lor t \in S \}$$

- **Input:** Two union-compatible relations $R, S$
- **Output:** A relation containing all tuples appearing in either $R$ or $S$ (duplicates removed)
- **Condition:** $R$ and $S$ must have the same number of attributes with matching domains

### D. Set Difference (−) — Row Subtraction
$$R - S = \{ t \mid t \in R \land t \notin S \}$$

- **Input:** Two union-compatible relations
- **Output:** Tuples in $R$ but not in $S$
- **Important:** $R - S \neq S - R$ (non-commutative)

### E. Cartesian Product (×) — All-Pair Combination
$$R \times S = \{ t \circ s \mid t \in R \land s \in S \}$$

where $t \circ s$ is the concatenation of tuple $t$ and tuple $s$.

- **Degree:** $\text{degree}(R) + \text{degree}(S)$
- **Cardinality:** $\vert R \vert \times \vert S \vert$
- **Almost always followed by a SELECT** to form a meaningful JOIN

### F. Rename (ρ) — Schema Relabelling
$$\rho_{X(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)}(E)$$

Renames the output of expression $E$ to a new relation $X$ with optional attribute renaming. Crucial for **self-joins** and **Cartesian product** disambiguation.

## 2.2 Derived (Extended) Operations

These operations can be **derived** from the 6 primitives but are useful shorthand:

- **Intersection:** $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$
- **Natural Join:** $R \bowtie S = \pi_{A}(\sigma_{R.A = S.A}(R \times S))$
- **Theta Join:** $R \bowtie_{\theta} S = \sigma_{\theta}(R \times S)$
- **Division:** $R \div S$ — used for "for all" queries
- **Left/Right/Full Outer Join:** preserves unmatched tuples
- **Assignment (←):** stores intermediate results in a temporary variable

## 2.3 Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)

A TRC query has the form:
$$\{ t \mid P(t) \}$$
where $t$ is a **tuple variable** and $P(t)$ is a **formula** built from:
- **Atoms:** $t \in R$, $s \in R$, $t.A \; \theta \; s.B$, $t.A \; \theta \; c$
- **Connectives:** $\land, \lor, \neg$
- **Quantifiers:** $\exists, \forall$

> [!IMPORTANT]
