- **Standard Data Types:** `INT`, `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `CHAR(n)`, `DATE`, `TIMESTAMP`, `BOOLEAN`.
- **Viva Tip:** When asked *"Why use VARCHAR instead of CHAR?"* — Answer: *VARCHAR allocates only the required storage, while CHAR pads with spaces to fixed length, wasting memory for short strings.*
- **Golden Rule for KTU:** Always specify the data type size explicitly (e.g., `VARCHAR(50)`, not just `VARCHAR`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Manipulation Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Manipulation Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Data Manipulation Language (DML)** is a sub-language of SQL (Structured Query Language) that is used by application programs and end-users to **retrieve, insert, update, and delete data** stored in the database objects (primarily tables and views). In the relational model formulated by E.F. Codd and adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCST402 - Database Management Systems*, DML operations manipulate the *tuples* (rows) of a *relation* (table) without altering the schema definition (which is the responsibility of DDL - Data Definition Language).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The official KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 mandates the study of DML commands: `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, along with set operations (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`), nested subqueries, and the `WHERE` clause filtering mechanism. DML is the *practical engine* of every CRUD application.

### 1.2 Classification of DML

KTU examiners expect students to explicitly classify DML into two types:

1. **Procedural DML** (also called *Low-Level DML* or *Record-at-a-Time DML*): Requires the user to specify **what** data is needed *and* **how** to obtain it. Requires knowledge of access paths, indexes, and physical storage. *Example: Relational Algebra extensions in older systems, embedded SQL loops.*
2. **Non-Procedural DML** (also called *High-Level DML* or *Set-at-a-Time DML*): Requires the user to specify only **what** data is needed. The DBMS optimizer determines the most efficient access path. *Example: Standard SQL used in MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and KTU lab experiments.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examination Tip:** KTU paper setters frequently ask: *"Differentiate between Procedural and Non-Procedural DML."* Always write a 3-row comparison: *Definition, User specifies, Efficiency.*

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Warehouse Manager

Imagine a **massive library warehouse** (the database) filled with **filing cabinets** (tables) containing thousands of **folders** (rows/tuples). The warehouse manager cannot let customers just walk in and rearrange the shelves (that would corrupt the system, like DDL altering schema). Instead, the manager provides a **standard request form** (DML statements) with four distinct actions:

- **SEARCH** the cabinets for a specific folder → `SELECT`
- **ADD** a new folder to the cabinet → `INSERT`
- **MODIFY** an existing folder's contents → `UPDATE`
- **REMOVE** a folder from the cabinet → `DELETE`

The customer only writes *what* they want, not *how* to walk through the aisles. The manager (the SQL engine/optimizer) figures out the fastest path. This is the essence of **Non-Procedural DML**.

### 1.4 The Four Core DML Operations (CRUD)

| Acronym | SQL Verb | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **C**reate | `INSERT` | Adding a new library card |
| **R**ead | `SELECT` | Searching for a book |
| **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | Changing a phone number on a card |
| **D**elete | `DELETE` | Cancelling a library card |

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **CRUD** is universally used in software engineering. KTU expects students to know the mapping. **Standard benchmark metric** is that DML operations must be **ACID compliant** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) — a critical KTU Module 3 topic that links directly to Module 2 transactions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a DML Statement

A DML statement is composed of **logical clauses** that follow a strict syntactic grammar defined by the ISO/IEC 9075 SQL standard. The KTU lab manual and theory examinations test these clauses individually.

### 2.2 The SELECT Statement: The Heart of DML

The `SELECT` statement is the most powerful and frequently examined DML command. It is structured as follows:

- **`SELECT` clause** — Specifies the attributes (columns) to retrieve. `*` denotes *all attributes*.
- **`FROM` clause** — Specifies the source relation(s).
- **`WHERE` clause** — Filters rows based on a boolean predicate (selection in relational algebra).
- **`GROUP BY` clause** — Partitions rows into groups for aggregation.
- **`HAVING` clause** — Filters groups (post-aggregation predicate).
- **`ORDER BY` clause** — Sorts the result set (ASC by default, DESC for descending).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Processing Order** (as defined by the SQL standard — *highly tested*): `FROM` → `WHERE` → `GROUP BY` → `HAVING` → `SELECT` → `ORDER BY`. Students often mistakenly think `SELECT` executes first — this is a common board-exam trap.

### 2.3 DML vs DDL: The Boundary

| Feature | DML (Data Manipulation) | DDL (Data Definition) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Manipulate *data* (rows) | Manipulate *structure* (schema) |
| Commands | `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` |
| Rollback | Can be rolled back (transactional) | Auto-committed in many DBMSs |
| Effect | Modifies tuples in existing tables | Modifies the database catalog/metadata |
| Trigger | Fires DML triggers | Fires DDL triggers |

### 2.4 Set Operations in SQL (Module 2 KTU Mandate)

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 explicitly requires the following set-theoretic operations that combine results of multiple `SELECT` queries:

- **`UNION`** — Returns all distinct rows from both queries. Equivalent to $\text{Result}_A \cup \text{Result}_B$.
- **`UNION ALL`** — Returns all rows including duplicates. (Faster, no de-duplication).
- **`INTERSECT`** — Returns rows present in both. Equivalent to $\text{Result}_A \cap \text{Result}_B$.
- **`EXCEPT`** (or `MINUS` in Oracle) — Returns rows in A but not in B. Equivalent to $\text{Result}_A - \text{Result}_B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compatibility Rule:** For set operations, both `SELECT` statements **must** have the same number of columns and compatible data types in the same order. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Template | Logical Operation |
|---|---|---|
| Insertion | `INSERT INTO Table (cols) VALUES (vals);` | Adds a tuple |
| Selective Update | `UPDATE Table SET col=val WHERE condition;` | Modifies rows |
| Deletion | `DELETE FROM Table WHERE condition;` | Removes rows |
| Projection | `SELECT col1, col2 FROM Table;` | $\pi_{col1, col2}(Table)$ |
| Selection | `SELECT * FROM Table WHERE cond;` | $\sigma_{cond}(Table)$ |
| Cartesian Product | `SELECT * FROM A, B;` | $A \times B$ |
| Natural Join | `SELECT * FROM A NATURAL JOIN B;` | $A \bowtie B$ |
| Set Union | `SELECT ... UNION SELECT ...;` | $A \cup B$ |
| Set Intersection | `SELECT ... INTERSECT SELECT ...;` | $A \cap B$ |
| Set Difference | `SELECT ... EXCEPT SELECT ...;` | $A - B$ |
| Aggregate | `SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(salary) FROM Emp;` | Group functions |
| Nested Query | `SELECT * FROM E WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM D);` | Subquery logic |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DML forms the foundation of:
- **Banking Systems** — Daily transactional updates to account balances (`UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE acc_id = 101`).
- **E-Commerce Platforms** — Order placement, inventory updates, and customer review retrieval.
- **Healthcare Information Systems** — Inserting patient records, retrieving lab results, modifying prescriptions.
- **Internet of Things (IoT)** — Continuous `INSERT` of sensor telemetry into time-series tables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reference Schema for Analytical Walkthrough

To make derivations concrete, we use a canonical KTU-style schema that mirrors the lab manual:

- **STUDENT**(`roll_no`, `name`, `dob`, `dept`, `cgpa`, `city`)
- **COURSE**(`course_id`, `course_name`, `credits`, `dept`)
- **ENROLLMENT**(`roll_no`, `course_id`, `grade`)

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 1: The INSERT Command (Derivation of Tuple Insertion)

The relational insertion operator $r \leftarrow r \cup \{t\}$ is implemented in SQL as follows. The evaluation is **row-by-row** and **auto-committed** only inside a transaction block.

**Step 1 — Identify the target relation and its arity:**

$$\text{Target} = \text{STUDENT}, \quad \text{Arity}(R) = 6$$

**Step 2 — Construct the tuple $t$ from values or subquery:**

$$t = (101, \text{`Anu'}, \text{`2004-05-12'}, \text{`CSE'}, 8.9, \text{`Kochi'})$$

**Step 3 — Invoke the SQL insertion operator:**

$$
\text{INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll\_no, name, dob, dept, cgpa, city) VALUES} \\
(101, \text{`Anu'}, \text{`2004-05-12'}, \text{`CSE'}, 8.9, \text{`Kochi'});
$$

**Step 4 — Verify constraint satisfaction:** The DBMS checks **Entity Integrity** (Primary Key `roll_no` not NULL and unique), **Referential Integrity** (any foreign key must exist in parent table), and **Domain Integrity** (e.g., `cgpa` between 0 and 10).

> [!NOTE]
> The insertion of multiple tuples can be performed using a subquery:
> `INSERT INTO TopStudents (roll_no, name) SELECT roll_no, name FROM STUDENT WHERE cgpa > 9.0;`
> This executes a **set-at-a-time** insertion — a Non-Procedural DML characteristic.

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 2: The UPDATE Command (Set-At-A-Time Modification)

The relational update operator $r \leftarrow \sigma_{P}(r) \cup \{(t_1.a_1, \ldots, t_1.a_n \text{ with modified values})\} \cup \sigma_{\neg P}(r)$ conceptually means: *replace values in rows matching the predicate, leave non-matching rows unchanged*.

**Step 1 — Predicate $P$ specification (in `WHERE`):**

$$P : (\text{cgpa} \geq 8.5) \land (\text{dept} = \text{`CSE'})$$

**Step 2 — Specify the new attribute value expression:**

$$\text{New CGPA} = \text{cgpa} + 0.1$$

**Step 3 — Compose the SQL statement:**

$$
\text{UPDATE STUDENT SET cgpa = cgpa + 0.1} \\
\text{WHERE cgpa} \geq 8.5 \text{ AND dept = `CSE'};
$$

**Step 4 — Set evaluation result:** The DBMS scans `STUDENT`, applies $P$ to each tuple, and for matching tuples applies the assignment. This is **set-at-a-time** — a single statement modifies *all* matching rows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Forgetting the `WHERE` clause updates *every row* in the table. This is a classic board-exam scenario where examiners deduct marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 3: The DELETE Command

The deletion operator removes tuples matching predicate $Q$ from relation $r$, equivalent to $r \leftarrow r - \sigma_{Q}(r)$.

**Step 1 — Predicate $Q$ for deletion:**

$$Q : (\text{city} = \text{`Delhi'}) \lor (\text{cgpa} < 5.0)$$

**Step 2 — Compose SQL:**

$$
\text{DELETE FROM STUDENT WHERE city = `Delhi' OR cgpa} < 5.0;
$$

**Step 3 — Referential action:** If a deleted `roll_no` is referenced in `ENROLLMENT`, the DBMS either rejects deletion (default `RESTRICT`) or cascades the deletion (`CASCADE`).

### 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough 4: Nested Subquery (Quantified Selection)

Retrieve the names of students enrolled in *every* course offered by the CSE department. This requires a **correlated subquery with `NOT EXISTS`**.

**Step 1 — Outer query:** Iterate over each student in `STUDENT`.

**Step 2 — Correlated subquery (per student):** Check if there exists a CSE course in which the student is *not* enrolled.

**Step 3 — Negation of existence:** Use `NOT EXISTS` — student is enrolled in all CSE courses if there is *no* CSE course they are missing.

**Step 4 — SQL composition:**

$$
\text{SELECT S.name} \\
\text{FROM STUDENT S} \\
\text{WHERE NOT EXISTS (} \\
\quad \text{SELECT C.course\_id FROM COURSE C} \\
\quad \text{WHERE C.dept = `CSE'} \\
\quad \text{AND NOT EXISTS (} \\
\qquad \text{SELECT E.roll\_no FROM ENROLLMENT E} \\
\qquad \text{WHERE E.roll\_no = S.roll\_no AND E.course\_id = C.course\_id} \\
\quad \text{)} \\
\text{);}
$$

This double `NOT EXISTS` pattern is the **universal quantification** $\forall$ expressed in SQL.

### 3.6 Analytical Walkthrough 5: Set Operation Derivation

Find the `roll_no` of students who are enrolled in either DBMS **or** DSA, but not both.

**Step 1 — Set of students in DBMS:**

$$S_1 = \pi_{\text{roll\_no}}(\text{ENROLLMENT} \bowtie \sigma_{\text{course\_name = `DBMS'}}(\text{COURSE}))$$

**Step 2 — Set of students in DSA:**

$$S_2 = \pi_{\text{roll\_no}}(\text{ENROLLMENT} \bowtie \sigma_{\text{course\_name = `DSA'}}(\text{COURSE}))$$

**Step 3 — Symmetric difference using set operations:**

$$\text{Result} = (S_1 \cup S_2) - (S_1 \cap S_2) = (S_1 - S_2) \cup (S_2 - S_1)$$

**Step 4 — SQL implementation using `EXCEPT`:**

$$
\text{SELECT roll\_no FROM ENROLLMENT E, COURSE C WHERE E.course\_id = C.course\_id AND C.course\_name = `DBMS'} \\
\text{EXCEPT} \\
\text{SELECT roll\_no FROM ENROLLMENT E, COURSE C WHERE E.course\_id = C.course\_id AND C.course\_name = `DSA'};
$$

### 3.7 Comparative DML Statement Matrix

| Operation | Input | Output | Atomicity | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `INSERT` | Tuple values | New tuple in $r$ | Yes (all-or-nothing) | Via `DELETE` |
| `UPDATE` | Predicate + new values | Modified tuples in $r$ | Yes (all-or-nothing) | Via reverse `UPDATE` |
| `DELETE` | Predicate | Tuples removed from $r$ | Yes (all-or-nothing) | Only if backed up |
| `SELECT` | Query spec | Result-set (virtual) | Read-only | Always |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DML Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SQL Language] --> B[DML Data Manipulation Language]
    A --> C[DDL Data Definition Language]
    A --> D[DCL Data Control Language]
    A --> E[TCL Transaction Control Language]
    B --> F[Procedural DML]
    B --> G[Non Procedural DML]
    G --> H[SELECT Read]
    G --> I[INSERT Create]
    G --> J[UPDATE Modify]
    G --> K[DELETE Remove]
    H --> L[Joins]
    H --> M[Subqueries]
    H --> N[Set Operations]
    N --> O[UNION]
    N --> P[INTERSECT]
    N --> Q[EXCEPT]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#17becf,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 SELECT Statement Logical Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Raw Tables in FROM Clause] --> from[FROM Cartesian Product]
    from --> where[WHERE Filter Predicate]
    where --> group[GROUP BY Partitioning]
    group --> having[HAVING Group Filter]
    having --> select[SELECT Projection]
    select --> order[ORDER BY Sorting]
    order --> result[Final Result Set]
    style start fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style result fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style from fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style where fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style group fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style having fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style select fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style order fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 CRUD-to-DML Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user[End User or Application] -->|issues DML| engine[SQL Engine Optimizer]
    engine -->|parses| parser[Query Parser]
    parser -->|validates syntax| validator[Syntax and Semantic Validator]
    validator -->|optimizes| optimizer[Query Optimizer]
    optimizer -->|executes plan| executor[Execution Engine]
    executor -->|reads or writes| storage[Disk and Buffer Pool]
    storage -->|result set| executor
    executor -->|returns| user
    style user fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
    style engine fill:#50e3c2,color:#000000
    style storage fill:#d0021b,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Set Operation Truth Table for Binary DML

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Query A Result Set] --> union[UNION]
    A --> intersect[INTERSECT]
    A --> except[EXCEPT]
    B[Query B Result Set] --> union
    B --> intersect
    B --> except
    union --> U[A union B all distinct]
    intersect --> I[A intersect B common]
    except --> E[A minus B in A only]
    style A fill:#bd10e0,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#bd10e0,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style E fill:#d0021b,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Differentiate between Procedural and Non-Procedural DML. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**
Procedural DML requires the user to specify both *what* data is required and *how* to retrieve it, often involving step-by-step record navigation and access path specification. *Example: Cursor-based embedded SQL in older COBOL systems, or Relational Calculus extended with procedural loops.*

Non-Procedural DML requires the user to specify only *what* data is required; the DBMS query optimizer automatically determines the most efficient access path. *Example: A standard `SELECT` statement in MySQL or PostgreSQL.*

The key advantage of Non-Procedural DML is that applications become **data-independent** and benefit from automatic optimization. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition procedural, 1 mark definition non-procedural, 1 mark example.)*

---

**Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*What is a nested subquery? Explain with an example using the `IN` operator.*

**Model Answer:**
A nested subquery (also called an inner query) is a `SELECT` statement embedded within another `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` statement. The inner query executes first and its result set is used by the outer query as a comparison value or set of values.

*Example:* Retrieve the names of students enrolled in the course 'DBMS':

$$
\text{SELECT name FROM STUDENT} \\
\text{WHERE roll\_no IN (} \\
\quad \text{SELECT roll\_no FROM ENROLLMENT} \\
\quad \text{WHERE course\_id = (SELECT course\_id FROM COURSE WHERE course\_name = `DBMS')} \\
\text{);}
$$

Here the innermost subquery finds the `course_id` of DBMS, the next subquery finds all `roll_no` of enrolled students, and the outer query retrieves their names. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks example with logical explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Internal Choice Instruction (KTU Standard):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider the following schema:
- **EMP**(`empno`, `ename`, `job`, `salary`, `deptno`)
- **DEPT**(`deptno`, `dname`, `location`)

Write SQL DML statements for the following: *(7 marks)*

**(i)** Insert a new employee with `empno=1001`, `ename='Rahul'`, `job='Developer'`, `salary=50000`, `deptno=20`.

**(ii)** Increase the salary of all employees in department 20 by 10%.

**(iii)** Delete all employees whose salary is less than 30000.

**(iv)** Display the names of employees and their department names who earn more than the average salary of their department.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Single-row INSERT:**
$$
\text{INSERT INTO EMP (empno, ename, job, salary, deptno)} \\
\text{VALUES (1001, `Rahul', `Developer', 50000, 20);}
$$

**(ii) Bulk UPDATE with arithmetic expression:**
$$
\text{UPDATE EMP SET salary = salary} \times 1.10 \text{ WHERE deptno = 20;}
$$

**(iii) Conditional DELETE:**
$$
\text{DELETE FROM EMP WHERE salary} < 30000;
$$

**(iv) Correlated subquery with join (compares each employee to the average of their own department):**
$$
\text{SELECT E.ename, D.dname} \\
\text{FROM EMP E, DEPT D} \\
\text{WHERE E.deptno = D.deptno} \\
\text{AND E.salary} > \text{(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM EMP WHERE deptno = E.deptno);}
$$

**Logical explanation:** For every employee tuple in the outer query, the correlated subquery computes the average salary of *that specific department* (referencing `E.deptno` from the outer query). The outer query then retains only employees whose salary exceeds this average. *(7 marks: 2 marks for parts i-iii correct, 3 marks for part iv with explanation, 2 marks for syntax accuracy.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the following set operations in SQL with examples: `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`. State the compatibility rule that must be satisfied. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Compatibility Rule:** Both `SELECT` statements involved in a set operation must have the same number of columns, and the data types of corresponding columns must be compatible (or implicitly convertible). The `ORDER BY` clause can appear only once, at the very end of the combined query.

**`UNION`:** Combines the result sets of two queries and returns **all distinct** rows (duplicates are eliminated automatically).

$$
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE deptno = 10} \\
\text{UNION} \\
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE job = `Manager'};
$$

This returns the `empno` of employees in department 10 *and* the `empno` of all managers, with duplicates removed.

**`INTERSECT`:** Returns only those rows that appear in **both** result sets.

$$
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE deptno = 10} \\
\text{INTERSECT} \\
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE job = `Manager'};
$$

This returns the `empno` of employees who are *both* in department 10 *and* managers.

**`EXCEPT`:** Returns rows that appear in the **first** result set but **not** in the second. (Note: Oracle uses `MINUS` instead of `EXCEPT`.)

$$
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE deptno = 10} \\
\text{EXCEPT} \\
\text{SELECT empno FROM EMP WHERE job = `Manager'};
$$

This returns `empno` of employees in department 10 who are *not* managers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is using `UNION` when `UNION ALL` is desired (the former performs an expensive sort-deduplicate; the latter is faster and keeps duplicates). Another is writing `EXCEPT` as `EXCEPTION` (a PL/SQL keyword, not SQL set operation). Always verify DBMS-specific syntax — KTU lab uses **MySQL 8.0+** which supports `UNION` and `INTERSECT` natively, but **`EXCEPT` is NOT supported in MySQL** — use `NOT IN` or `NOT EXISTS` instead. Deduct 1 mark if student does not mention this.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per operation, 1 mark compatibility rule, 1 mark example accuracy, 1 mark MySQL caveat.)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the following DML concepts: *(7 marks)*

**(i)** `INSERT` with a subquery (set-at-a-time insertion).

**(ii)** The difference between `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses.

**(iii)** `UPDATE` with multiple column assignments.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Set-at-a-time INSERT using subquery:**
Suppose we want to archive all employees from department 10 into a backup table `EMP_BACKUP`:

$$
\text{INSERT INTO EMP\_BACKUP (empno, ename, salary)} \\
\text{SELECT empno, ename, salary FROM EMP WHERE deptno = 10;}
$$

The subquery `SELECT ... FROM EMP WHERE deptno = 10` produces a result set that is *inserted directly* into `EMP_BACKUP` without manually listing values. This is a powerful **set-oriented** feature of Non-Procedural DML.

**(ii) `WHERE` vs `HAVING`:**
- The `WHERE` clause filters **individual rows** *before* any grouping occurs. It cannot use aggregate functions like `COUNT`, `AVG`, `SUM`.
- The `HAVING` clause filters **groups** *after* the `GROUP BY` operation. It is the only place where aggregate functions can legally appear as predicates.

*Example:*
$$
\text{SELECT deptno, COUNT(*), AVG(salary)} \\
\text{FROM EMP} \\
\text{WHERE salary} > 20000 \text{ --- filters individual rows first} \\
\text{GROUP BY deptno} \\
\text{HAVING AVG(salary)} > 40000; \text{--- filters aggregated groups second}
$$

**(iii) Multiple column UPDATE:**
$$
\text{UPDATE EMP SET salary = salary} \times 1.15, \text{job = `Senior `} \parallel \text{job} \\
\text{WHERE deptno = 30;}
$$

This simultaneously modifies the `salary` (15% raise) and prepends "Senior " to the `job` field for all department 30 employees. The `||` operator is SQL string concatenation in PostgreSQL/Oracle; in MySQL, use `CONCAT(job, 'Senior ')` or string functions.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each sub-part, 1 mark for overall clarity and syntax.)*

---

**(b)** Consider the schema: `STUDENT(roll_no, name, dept, cgpa, city)`. Write SQL queries for the following: *(7 marks)*

**(i)** List the names of all students whose `cgpa` is higher than the `cgpa` of every student in the 'CSE' department.

**(ii)** List the names of students from 'Kerala' cities (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) who have the maximum CGPA in their respective department.

**(iii)** Use `EXCEPT` (or alternative in MySQL) to find students who are from Kochi *but not* from CSE department.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Universal quantification using `> ALL`:**
$$
\text{SELECT name FROM STUDENT} \\
\text{WHERE cgpa} > \text{ALL (SELECT cgpa FROM STUDENT WHERE dept = `CSE');}
$$

The inner subquery returns all CGPA values of CSE students. The outer query selects students whose CGPA exceeds *every* one of these — equivalent to the universal quantifier $\forall x$ in relational calculus.

**(ii) Subquery with `MAX` per department (correlated):**
$$
\text{SELECT name FROM STUDENT S1} \\
\text{WHERE city IN (`Kochi', `Trivandrum', `Calicut')} \\
\text{AND cgpa = (SELECT MAX(cgpa) FROM STUDENT S2 WHERE S2.dept = S1.dept);}
$$

The correlated subquery computes the maximum CGPA within the *current row's* department. The outer query keeps only students matching that maximum.

**(iii) Set difference using `NOT IN` (MySQL-compatible alternative to `EXCEPT`):**
$$
\text{SELECT roll\_no FROM STUDENT WHERE city = `Kochi' AND roll\_no NOT IN (SELECT roll\_no FROM STUDENT WHERE dept = `CSE');}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Deduction Alert:** Many students write `MAX(cgpa) IN (subquery)` instead of `cgpa = (subquery)`, which is logically incorrect and forfeits 2 marks. Another common error is forgetting the alias `S1` and `S2` in the correlated subquery, causing a Cartesian-product ambiguity — KTU deducts 1 mark. The `IN` operator does *not* need parentheses around comma-separated values in some DBMS, but for clarity always use them.

*(7 marks: 2 marks query (i), 2.5 marks query (ii) with correlation logic, 1.5 marks query (iii) with set-difference explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DML**

- DML = **Data Manipulation Language**; it manipulates **rows (tuples)**, not schema.
- Two classifications: **Procedural** (specifies *how*) and **Non-Procedural** (specifies *only what*).
- The **four CRUD verbs**: `SELECT` (Read), `INSERT` (Create), `UPDATE` (Modify), `DELETE` (Remove).
- `SELECT` logical processing order: **FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY** (board-exam classic).
- `WHERE` filters **rows**; `HAVING` filters **groups**; aggregates (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`) appear in `HAVING` or `SELECT`, *never* in `WHERE`.
- **Set operations** (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`) require **same number of columns and compatible data types** in both queries.
- `UNION` removes duplicates (slow); `UNION ALL` keeps duplicates (fast).
- `EXCEPT` is the SQL standard for set difference; Oracle uses `MINUS`; **MySQL 8.0 does NOT support `EXCEPT`** — use `NOT IN` or `NOT EXISTS`.
- **Nested subquery**: inner query executes first; correlated subquery re-executes per outer row.
- `> ALL` = universal quantification ($\forall$); `IN` = set membership ($\in$); `EXISTS` = existential quantification ($\exists$).
- **DML is transactional** — operations can be rolled back using TCL commands (`COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`).
- **Always include a `WHERE` clause in `UPDATE` and `DELETE`** — omitting it modifies/deletes *every* row in the table.
- `INSERT` with subquery = set-at-a-time insertion (a powerful Non-Procedural feature).
- **ACID compliance** is the property ensuring DML reliability: **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability**.
- KTU lab environment typically uses **MySQL 8.0+** or **PostgreSQL 14+** — verify syntax compatibility before writing exam answers.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Data Manipulation Language*
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus for PCCST402*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assertions, Triggers, and Views
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assertions, Triggers, and Views

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Active Database Elements

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Database Management Systems, Module 2 transitions from pure data definition (DDL) and data manipulation (DML) into the realm of **Active Database Elements** and **Virtual Relations**. These are advanced SQL constructs that allow a database to enforce complex integrity rules, react automatically to data changes, and present customized data perspectives to different users without altering the underlying base tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2)**
> Students must master three pillars: **Assertions** (declarative integrity), **Triggers** (procedural reactivity), and **Views** (virtual schema customization). These collectively form the backbone of modern RDBMS applications like Oracle, PostgreSQL, and MySQL.

---

## 1.2 Assertions — The Database "Constitution"

### Formal Definition
An **Assertion** is a declarative SQL constraint that specifies a condition that the database must always satisfy. Unlike check constraints (which are tied to a single table), an assertion is a schema-level element that can span **multiple tables**. The DBMS continuously enforces the assertion; if any modification violates the assertion's predicate, the entire transaction is **rolled back**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Think of an **Assertion** as a **"Constitution"** of a country. Just as a constitution imposes a rule that must never be violated (e.g., "No citizen shall own more than 1,000 acres"), an assertion imposes a rule across the entire database (e.g., "The sum of salaries in any department must not exceed the budget"). If anyone tries to break it, the action is **automatically nullified** — it is never allowed to occur.

### Key KTU Terminology
- **Declarative Constraint:** The user declares *what* must be true, not *how* to enforce it.
- **Schema-Level Scope:** Exists independently of any single table.
- **Always-Satisfied Invariant:** Evaluated on every relevant UPDATE, INSERT, or DELETE.

---

## 1.3 Triggers — The Database "Automatic Reflexes"

### Formal Definition
A **Trigger** is a procedural block of code (typically using the SQL/PSM standard with procedural extensions) that is **automatically executed** by the DBMS in response to a specified data-modification event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on a specified table, subject to a specified optional condition.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> A **Trigger** is like a **home smoke alarm system**. You do not manually trigger it; the moment smoke (a data event like an UPDATE) is detected in the kitchen (a specific table), the alarm (the trigger body) automatically fires and performs actions (sounding sirens, calling the fire department — analogous to logging, rolling back, or cascading). It is an **Event-Condition-Action (ECA)** rule.

### The ECA Paradigm (KTU High-Yield)
- **Event:** INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE
- **Condition:** An optional WHEN clause (a Boolean predicate)
- **Action:** The procedural block (BEGIN ... END)

---

## 1.4 Views — The Database "Custom Windows"

### Formal Definition
A **View** is a **virtual relation** defined by a query. It does not store data physically; instead, it dynamically generates its result set by executing the underlying SQL query whenever the view is referenced. Views provide logical data independence, security, and query simplification.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> A **View** is like a **"Customized Window with Curtains"** in a house. The actual room (base table) remains intact, but from outside (the user), you only see the view you want. If the furniture inside the room is rearranged (base table data changes), what you see through the window also updates automatically — but the window itself is not the room; it is a virtual projection.

### Two Critical KTU Sub-Types
1. **Updatable Views:** Simple views (single base table, no aggregates, no DISTINCT) that support INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE.
2. **Materialized Views:** Views whose result is physically stored and periodically refreshed (covered in advanced KTU electives).



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assertion — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Syntax Structure

$$ \text{CREATE ASSERTION } \textit{assertion\_name} \text{ CHECK } (\textit{predicate}) $$

- The `predicate` is a Boolean expression involving one or more tables.
- A **NULL result** of the predicate is treated as **TRUE** (i.e., the assertion is not violated). This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Steps
1. The DBMS parser registers the assertion as a schema object.
2. Every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on any table referenced in the predicate triggers a **constraint check**.
3. If the predicate evaluates to **FALSE**, the modification is rejected.
4. If the predicate evaluates to **TRUE** or **UNKNOWN (NULL)**, the modification proceeds.

### 2.1.3 KTU-Standard Examples (Rigor Required)
- **Budget Constraint:** `CHECK (NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM Dept WHERE budget < (SELECT SUM(salary) FROM Emp WHERE Emp.dno = Dept.dno)))`
- **Domain Limit:** `CHECK (1 >= (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Emp WHERE salary > 1000000))`
- **Comparative Cardinality:** `CHECK ((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Enrolled) <= 2 * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Student))`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:** Assertions are crucial in banking systems (negative balance prevention), inventory systems (stock ≥ 0), and HR systems (no employee exceeds CEO salary by a factor).

---

## 2.2 Trigger — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 General Syntax Template (SQL Standard)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE TRIGGER } \textit{trigger\_name} \\
&\text{ \{BEFORE \mid AFTER \mid INSTEAD OF\}} \quad \textit{event} \quad \text{ON } \textit{table\_name} \\
&\text{[FOR EACH \{ROW \mid STATEMENT\}]} \\
&\text{[WHEN } (\textit{condition})] \\
&\text{BEGIN } \textit{atomic\_sql\_statements} \text{ END;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.2 Granularity — The Crucial Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Row-Level Trigger (FOR EACH ROW):** Executes once per affected row. Provides access to **OLD** and **NEW** transition variables/tables. Essential for auditing.
> 
> **Statement-Level Trigger (FOR EACH STATEMENT):** Executes once per SQL statement, regardless of row count. Useful for transaction-level logging.

### 2.2.3 Timing Semantics (KTU High-Yield)

| Timing | Behavior | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BEFORE** | Fires *prior* to the modification | Data validation, auto-population of derived columns |
| **AFTER** | Fires *after* the modification commits | Auditing, replication, cascading notifications |
| **INSTEAD OF** | Replaces the modification entirely | Makes non-updatable views updatable |

### 2.2.4 Transition Variables
- **OLD.column:** Value of the column *before* the event.
- **NEW.column:** Value of the column *after* the event.
- **OLD is NULL for INSERT; NEW is NULL for DELETE.**

---

## 2.3 View — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Syntax Template

$$ \text{CREATE VIEW } \textit{view\_name} \text{ AS } \textit{subquery} \text{ [WITH CASCADE CHECK OPTION]} $$

### 2.3.2 The Query Modification Engine
When a view is referenced, the DBMS performs **Query Rewriting** (also called *view merging*). The view's subquery is textually substituted into the outer query, transforming the virtual reference into a real query against the base tables.

### 2.3.3 Updatability Criteria (KTU Exam Focus)
A view is **updatable** if and only if **all** of the following hold:
1. Derived from a **single base table** (no joins).
2. No `DISTINCT`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, or aggregate functions.
3. No `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, or `EXCEPT`.
4. All NOT NULL columns of the base table appear in the view.
5. No subqueries in the SELECT list.

### 2.3.4 WITH CHECK OPTION
Ensures that any INSERT or UPDATE through the view does not produce a row that the view's WHERE clause would exclude. Guarantees **view-consistent updates**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Core Syntax | Scope | Granularity | KTU Key Point |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Assertion** | `CREATE ASSERTION name CHECK (pred)` | Schema-wide | Tuple-set level | Evaluates on every modification |
| **Trigger (Row)** | `... FOR EACH ROW ...` | Single table | Per affected row | Access to OLD and NEW |
| **Trigger (Stmt)** | `... FOR EACH STATEMENT ...` | Single table | Per SQL statement | No OLD/NEW row variables |
| **View** | `CREATE VIEW v AS <query>` | Schema object | Query result | Virtual; not stored |
| **Mat. View** | `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ...` | Schema object | Physically stored | Requires REFRESH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Triggers power real-time fraud detection in fintech. Views power multi-tenant SaaS dashboards. Assertions power compliance auditing in regulated industries (HIPAA, GDPR).

---

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Feature | Assertion | Trigger | View |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Declarative | Procedural (ECA) | Virtual relation |
| **Storage** | None (only predicate) | Code in data dictionary | None (only definition) |
| **Reactivity** | Automatic (on violation) | Automatic (on event) | Lazy (on reference) |
| **Multi-Table** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (one table per trigger) | ✅ Yes (via joins) |
| **Performance** | Costly (always checked) | Moderate | Efficient (query rewrite) |



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Assertion for Salary Cap (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** *Write an assertion to ensure that no employee in the Emp table earns more than 5 times the average salary of all employees.*

### Step 1 — Identify the Predicate
The rule states: For every employee `e`, the salary `e.sal` must satisfy:

$$
e.sal \leq 5 \times \text{AVG}(Emp.sal)
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Subquery Form
We rephrase using a universal quantifier (or equivalently, a NOT EXISTS clause):

$$
\text{For all } e \in Emp : e.sal \leq 5 \times \text{AVG}(Emp.sal)
$$

### Step 3 — Formal SQL Translation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE ASSERTION SalaryCap} \\
&\text{CHECK (} \\
&\quad \text{NOT EXISTS (} \\
&\quad\quad \text{SELECT * FROM Emp E} \\
&\quad\quad \text{WHERE E.sal > 5 * (SELECT AVG(sal) FROM Emp)} \\
&\quad \text{)} \\
&\text{);}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Logical Verification
- If every employee satisfies the inequality, the inner SELECT returns an **empty set**.
- `NOT EXISTS (empty set)` evaluates to **TRUE** → assertion is **satisfied**.
- If one employee violates the rule, the inner SELECT returns a non-empty set.
- `NOT EXISTS (non-empty)` evaluates to **FALSE** → assertion is **violated** → **rollback**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Trigger for Automatic Audit Logging

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** *Create a trigger on the Emp table that, after any UPDATE of salary, inserts the old and new salary into an `Emp_Audit` table along with a timestamp.*

### Step 1 — Identify Event, Condition, Action
- **Event:** `AFTER UPDATE OF sal ON Emp`
- **Condition:** Always fires (no WHEN clause)
- **Action:** Insert into `Emp_Audit (eid, old_sal, new_sal, change_time)`

### Step 2 — Choose Granularity
We need to log *each* row whose salary changed, so we choose **`FOR EACH ROW`**.

### Step 3 — Full SQL Construction

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE TRIGGER SalaryAuditTrigger} \\
&\text{AFTER UPDATE OF sal ON Emp} \\
&\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
&\text{BEGIN} \\
&\quad \text{INSERT INTO Emp_Audit (eid, old_sal, new_sal, change_time)} \\
&\quad \text{VALUES (OLD.eid, OLD.sal, NEW.sal, CURRENT\_TIMESTAMP);} \\
&\text{END;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Transition Variables
- `OLD.eid`, `OLD.sal` → the eid and salary **before** the update.
- `NEW.sal` → the salary **after** the update.
- If the trigger were on DELETE, `NEW.sal` would be NULL.
- If the trigger were on INSERT, `OLD.sal` would be NULL.

### Step 5 — Counter-Example: Statement-Level Granularity
If we had used `FOR EACH STATEMENT`, we could **not** access `OLD.eid` directly. We would instead use the **transition tables** `OLD_TABLE` and `NEW_TABLE` (SQL Standard; Oracle uses `REFERENCING OLD AS OLD_TABLE`).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Updatable View with Check Option

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** *Create a view `CS_Students` showing all students enrolled in the 'CS' department. Ensure that no insert through the view can place a non-CS student into the view.*

### Step 1 — Base Schema Assumption
We assume a `Student(sid, name, dept, gpa)` base table.

### Step 2 — Define the View

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE VIEW CS\_Students AS} \\
&\text{SELECT sid, name, gpa} \\
&\text{FROM Student} \\
&\text{WHERE dept = 'CS'} \\
&\text{WITH CHECK OPTION;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Query Rewrite (Proof of Virtual Nature)
Suppose a user runs: `SELECT * FROM CS_Students WHERE gpa > 9.0;`
The DBMS rewrites this transparently to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{SELECT sid, name, gpa} \\
&\text{FROM Student} \\
&\text{WHERE dept = 'CS' AND gpa > 9.0;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Insert Behavior
If a user issues `INSERT INTO CS_Students VALUES (101, 'Anu', 9.2);` (without specifying dept), the DBMS will:
- Reject the insert because `dept` is **NOT NULL** in the base table and not in the view (assumed), OR
- Accept the insert with `dept = NULL`, but the **WITH CHECK OPTION** ensures the resulting row still satisfies the view's WHERE clause (i.e., `dept = 'CS'`). Since `NULL ≠ 'CS'`, the insert is **rejected**.

### Step 5 — Alternative with CASCADE
`WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION` is stricter: it checks the predicate against **all underlying views** in the dependency chain, not just the immediate view.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Construct | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Enforce a **multi-table invariant** (e.g., dept budget) | **Assertion** | Schema-wide declarative power |
| Enforce a **single-table column constraint** | **CHECK** constraint | Lighter overhead than assertion |
| React to a **change** with side effects (log, cascade) | **Trigger** | Procedural, event-driven |
| Provide a **customized projection** to users | **View** | Logical data independence |
| Store **precomputed aggregates** for performance | **Materialized View** | Physical storage; periodic refresh |
| Implement **referential cascade actions** | **Trigger** or `ON DELETE CASCADE` | Trigger for custom logic |



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trigger Execution Flow (ECA Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Issues DML Statement (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)"] --> B{"Does a Trigger Exist for this Table/Event?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Execute DML Normally"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Trigger Timing?"}
    D -- "BEFORE" --> E["Execute Trigger Body"]
    D -- "AFTER" --> G["Execute DML on Base Table"]
    D -- "INSTEAD OF" --> H["Trigger Body Replaces DML"]
    E --> F{"WHEN Condition Satisfied?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G
    F -- "No" --> C
    G --> I{"WHEN Condition Satisfied?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Execute Trigger Body"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Commit DML Only"]
    J --> L["Commit / Rollback Transaction"]
    H --> L
    K --> L
    C --> L
```

## 4.2 View Query Rewrite Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph User["User Query Layer"]
        Q1["SELECT * FROM CS_Students WHERE gpa > 9.0"]
    end
    subgraph Rewriter["Query Rewriter Engine"]
        R1["Detect View Reference: CS_Students"]
        R2["Fetch View Definition from Data Dictionary"]
        R3["Textually Substitute Subquery"]
    end
    subgraph Optimizer["Query Optimizer"]
        O1["Parse Rewritten SQL"]
        O2["Generate Execution Plan"]
    end
    subgraph Storage["Base Tables"]
        T1["Student (Base Table)"]
    end
    Q1 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> T1
    T1 --> RESULT["Result Set Returned to User"]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Assertion vs Trigger vs View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCHEMA["Database Schema Layer"]
        direction TB
        T1["Table: Emp"]
        T2["Table: Dept"]
        T3["Table: Project"]
        A1["Assertion: BudgetCap (spans Emp+Dept)"]
        TG1["Trigger: AuditTrigger (on Emp)"]
        V1["View: HighEarners (projects Emp)"]
    end
    subgraph DMLEVENTS["DML Event Layer"]
        E1["INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on Emp"]
        E2["INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on Dept"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["Outputs / Side Effects"]
        O1["Reject DML (Rollback)"]
        O2["Insert into Audit Table"]
        O3["Virtual Result Set"]
    end
    E1 --> TG1
    TG1 --> O2
    E1 --> A1
    E2 --> A1
    A1 --> O1
    E1 --> V1
    V1 --> O3
    T1 --- A1
    T2 --- A1
    T1 --- TG1
    T1 --- V1
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Construct

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to enforce or model a database rule?"] --> Q1{"Does the rule span multiple tables?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes, declarative, no side effects" --> ASRT["Use ASSERTION"]
    Q1 -- "No, single table only" --> Q2{"Need to perform side effects (log, cascade)?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> TRG["Use TRIGGER"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> CHK["Use CHECK Constraint"]
    Q1 -- "Need to hide schema complexity / show custom projection" --> VW["Use VIEW"]
    Q1 -- "Need precomputed aggregates for performance" --> MVIEW["Use MATERIALIZED VIEW"]
```



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between an assertion and a trigger in SQL. In which scenario would you prefer a trigger over an assertion?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Assertion | Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Declarative — states *what* must hold | Procedural — states *what to do* when an event occurs |
| **Scope** | Schema-wide; can span multiple tables | Confined to a single table (per trigger) |
| **Action on Violation** | Transaction is rolled back automatically | Can perform side effects (logging, cascading, custom rollback) |
| **Side Effects** | None (pure constraint) | Yes (can INSERT into other tables, call procedures) |

**Scenario for Trigger Preference:** When we need to perform **side effects** beyond a simple reject — for example, *automatically logging every salary change to an `Emp_Audit` table* — an assertion cannot do this, but a trigger can.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is an updatable view? List any four conditions under which a view is not updatable.**

**Model Answer:**

An **updatable view** is a view through which INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operations on the base table can be performed. SQL allows updates only on views that are simple enough to unambiguously map each view tuple to a single base table tuple.

**Four Conditions Where a View is NOT Updatable:**

1. The view is defined using `DISTINCT`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, or aggregate functions (`SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`).
2. The view is defined using set operations (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`).
3. The view involves a `JOIN` across two or more base tables.
4. The view contains a subquery in its SELECT list, or omits any NOT NULL column of the base table.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with an internal choice between two questions.

---

### PART B — CHOICE 1: Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

Consider the following schema of a university database:
- `STUDENT(SID, SNAME, DEPT, GPA)`
- `ENROLLED(SID, COURSE_ID, GRADE)`
- `FACULTY(FID, FNAME, DEPT, SALARY)`
- `TEACHES(FID, COURSE_ID)`

#### (a) Write an assertion to ensure that the average GPA of students in the 'CS' department is always greater than 7.0. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE ASSERTION CS\_AvgGPA\_Check} \\
&\text{CHECK (} \\
&\quad ( \\
&\quad\quad \text{SELECT AVG(GPA) FROM STUDENT WHERE DEPT = 'CS'} \\
&\quad ) > 7.0 \\
&\text{);}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation (Step-by-Step for 7 Marks):**

1. **Definition Step:** The assertion is named `CS_AvgGPA_Check` to clearly indicate its semantic intent — checking the CS department's average GPA. *(1 mark)*
2. **Predicate Construction:** The inner subquery computes `AVG(GPA)` filtered by `DEPT = 'CS'`. *(2 marks)*
3. **Comparison Logic:** The outer `CHECK` clause enforces the invariant that this average is strictly greater than 7.0. *(2 marks)*
4. **Enforcement:** Whenever an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on `STUDENT` occurs, the DBMS recomputes the average; if it falls to 7.0 or below, the modification is rolled back. *(1 mark)*
5. **NULL Semantics Note:** If there are no CS students, `AVG` returns NULL, which CHECK treats as TRUE, so the assertion is satisfied (this is the SQL standard behavior, important for KTU). *(1 mark)*

---

#### (b) Create an updatable view `Top_Performers` that lists the SID, SNAME, and GPA of all students with GPA > 8.5. Explain why this view is updatable. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE VIEW Top\_Performers AS} \\
&\text{SELECT SID, SNAME, GPA} \\
&\text{FROM STUDENT} \\
&\text{WHERE GPA > 8.5} \\
&\text{WITH CHECK OPTION;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Updatability Verification (for full 7 marks):**

1. **Single Base Table:** The view is derived from only one table `STUDENT` — satisfying the first upgradability rule. *(2 marks)*
2. **No Aggregates/Group By:** The view uses neither `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `DISTINCT`, nor aggregate functions like `SUM`/`AVG`/`COUNT`. *(2 marks)*
3. **No Set Operations / Joins:** The query does not use `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`, or any `JOIN` clause. *(1 mark)*
4. **Column Coverage:** All NOT NULL columns of `STUDENT` other than those listed need not be considered if the view is used only for read-mostly purposes; however, **WITH CHECK OPTION** ensures any insert/update maintains the GPA > 8.5 invariant. *(1 mark)*
5. **Conclusion:** Hence the view is updatable; an `INSERT INTO Top_Performers VALUES (501, 'Rahul', 9.1)` is valid and propagates to the base `STUDENT` table. *(1 mark)*

---

### PART B — CHOICE 2: Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

Consider the schema:
- `Account(Acc_No, Branch, Balance)`
- `Transaction(TID, Acc_No, T_Type, Amount, T_Date)`

#### (a) Define a trigger that prevents any withdrawal transaction (T_Type = 'W') that would cause the account balance to drop below ₹1,000. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE TRIGGER PreventOverdraw} \\
&\text{BEFORE INSERT ON Transaction} \\
&\text{FOR EACH ROW} \\
&\text{WHEN (NEW.T\_Type = 'W')} \\
&\text{BEGIN} \\
&\quad \text{IF (} \\
&\quad\quad \text{SELECT Balance FROM Account} \\
&\quad\quad \text{WHERE Acc\_No = NEW.Acc\_No} \\
&\quad ) - NEW.Amount < 1000 \\
&\quad \text{THEN} \\
&\quad\quad \text{SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'} \\
&\quad\quad \text{SET MESSAGE\_TEXT = 'Withdrawal would breach minimum balance';} \\
&\quad \text{END IF;} \\
&\text{END;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation for 7 Marks:**

1. **Event & Timing:** `BEFORE INSERT ON Transaction` — chosen because we must validate *before* the transaction is recorded. *(1 mark)*
2. **Granularity:** `FOR EACH ROW` — required since the check is per transaction row. *(1 mark)*
3. **Condition:** `WHEN (NEW.T_Type = 'W')` filters the trigger to fire only for withdrawals. *(1 mark)*
4. **Logic:** The action fetches the current balance, subtracts the new amount, and checks if the result is below 1,000. *(2 marks)*
5. **Violation Handling:** `SIGNAL SQLSTATE` raises an error, which the DBMS translates into a transaction abort. *(1 mark)*
6. **Why BEFORE not AFTER:** If we used AFTER, the bad transaction would already be recorded, requiring a compensating delete — less atomic and clean. *(1 mark)*

---

#### (b) Create a view `Branch_Balances` that shows each branch's total balance (sum of all accounts). Is this view updatable? Justify. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CREATE VIEW Branch\_Balances AS} \\
&\text{SELECT Branch, SUM(Balance) AS Total\_Balance} \\
&\text{FROM Account} \\
&\text{GROUP BY Branch;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Updatability Analysis (for 7 marks):**

1. The view uses `SUM(Balance)`, which is an **aggregate function**. *(2 marks)*
2. It uses `GROUP BY Branch`, which is disallowed in updatable views. *(2 marks)*
3. Even if a user attempts to update the view, there is **no unique mapping** from a view tuple (Branch, Total_Balance) back to a single base table row. *(2 marks)*
4. **Conclusion:** The view is **NOT updatable**. Only `SELECT` operations are allowed on it. *(1 mark)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Missing semicolon after assertion:** Students often forget the `;` after `CHECK (...)`, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing ASSERTION with CHECK constraint:** An assertion is schema-level (no `CONSTRAINT` keyword on a table); a CHECK is column/table-level. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **OLD vs NEW in INSERT/DELETE:** Writing `OLD.sal` inside an INSERT trigger is a syntax error (OLD is NULL). Examiners check this.
> 4. **View updatability omissions:** Forgetting to mention `WITH CHECK OPTION` when the question asks for an updatable view with constraint enforcement — **2 marks lost**.
> 5. **Granularity confusion:** Using `FOR EACH STATEMENT` but referencing `OLD.column` directly — examiners deduct 2 marks and may give 0 for the trigger body.
> 6. **Neglecting to mention NULL semantics in assertions:** If the inner subquery returns NULL, the assertion passes — this is SQL standard behavior and is a favorite KTU trick question.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Assertion** = schema-level, declarative, multi-table constraint that must always be true; uses `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK (...)`.
- ✅ **Trigger** = ECA rule (`Event-Condition-Action`); uses `BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF` and `FOR EACH ROW/STATEMENT`.
- ✅ **OLD** and **NEW** are transition variables available in row-level triggers; OLD is NULL for INSERT, NEW is NULL for DELETE.
- ✅ **View** = virtual table from a stored query; uses `CREATE VIEW ... AS <subquery>`.
- ✅ **WITH CHECK OPTION** prevents inserts/updates that would violate the view's WHERE clause.
- ✅ **Updatable View** = single base table, no aggregates, no DISTINCT/GROUP BY, no joins, no set operations.
- ✅ **BEFORE triggers** validate or modify data *before* it is written; **AFTER triggers** are for side effects (logging).
- ✅ **Assertions** use NULL-as-TRUE semantics — a frequent KTU exam trick.
- ✅ **INSTEAD OF triggers** are used to make non-updatable views updatable.
- ✅ **Materialized views** store data physically and need REFRESH; normal views are always virtual.
- ✅ **Trigger vs Assertion decision rule:** Side effects needed → Trigger; pure constraint → Assertion.
- ✅ **View vs Query decision rule:** Reused frequently across users/applications → View; one-off → inline query.



<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relational Database Design Using ER-to-Relational Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relational Database Design Using ER-to-Relational Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ER-to-Relational Mapping** is the systematic, algorithmic process of converting the conceptual schema expressed in an **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram** into an equivalent **logical schema** consisting of a set of well-formed **relations (tables)** in the **Relational Data Model**. This mapping preserves the structural semantics (entities, attributes, keys) and the integrity constraints (cardinality, participation, keys) of the original ER model.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this conversion is the bridge between **Conceptual Design** (Module 2) and **Logical/Relational Design**, ensuring that the resulting relational schema is in **First Normal Form (1NF)** as a minimum baseline and free from redundancy once further normalization (3NF/BCNF) is applied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint-to-House Conversion"

Imagine an architect draws a detailed **blueprint** (ER Diagram) showing rooms (entities), doors between them (relationships), and furniture (attributes). The builder cannot lay bricks using a blueprint directly — they need a **materials list and assembly instructions** (Relational Schema with tables, keys, and foreign keys).

- The **ER Diagram** is like the *blueprint*: visual, abstract, great for communication.
- The **Relational Schema** is like the *bill of materials*: precise, tabular, ready to be implemented in an RDBMS like MySQL, Oracle, or PostgreSQL.

**ER-to-Relational Mapping is the rulebook that translates every blueprint symbol into a precise row-and-column instruction set.**

## 1.3 Why Is This Step Mandatory in KTU 2024?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Step 1 of the Relational Design Pipeline** in Module 2 of PCCST402.
> - It directly supports **Course Outcome CO2**: *Design relational databases using ER modeling and normalization techniques*.
> - It is tested in **ESE (End Semester Examination)** Part A (3 marks) and **Part B (14 marks)** questions every semester.

## 1.4 Components of an ER Model That Must Be Mapped

| ER Construct | Must Map To | Default Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Strong (Regular) Entity | Relation | One table per entity |
| Weak Entity | Relation | Table with composite PK + FK |
| Attribute | Column | Atomic column |
| Multivalued Attribute | Separate Relation | New table |
| Derived Attribute | Not stored | Computed at runtime |
| 1:1 Relationship | Foreign Key | Add FK to any one side |
| 1:N Relationship | Foreign Key | Add FK to the "N" side |
| M:N Relationship | New Relation | Junction/Bridge table |
| N-ary Relationship | New Relation | Junction table with n FKs |
| Specialization/Generalization | Multiple Strategies | Type-discriminator or split tables |
| Aggregation | Treat as Higher-Order Relationship | M:N style junction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7-Step Standard ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm

The mapping algorithm was formalized by Elmasri & Navathe and is the **gold standard** taught in KTU PCCST402. Mastering these 7 steps guarantees full marks in ESE questions.

### Step 1: Mapping of Regular (Strong) Entities

- **Rule:** For every regular entity type $E$, create a relation $R_E$.
- **Attributes:** Include all **simple, atomic (single-valued)** attributes of $E$.
- **Primary Key:** Select one key attribute $K_E$ of $E$. If $K_E$ is **composite**, include all its constituent attributes.
- **Skip:** Do **NOT** include multivalued or derived attributes here.

$$
R_E(\underline{K_E}, \text{simpleAttrs}(E))
$$

### Step 2: Mapping of Weak Entities

- **Rule:** For every weak entity $W$ with owner entity $E$, create a relation $R_W$.
- **Attributes:** Include all simple atomic attributes of $W$ + the **primary key of $E$ as a foreign key**.
- **Primary Key:** $\text{PK}(R_W) = \text{partialKey}(W) \cup \text{PK}(E)$.

$$
R_W(\underline{\text{partialKey}(W)}, \text{PK}(E), \text{simpleAttrs}(W))
$$

### Step 3: Mapping of Binary 1:1 Relationships

- **Strategy A — Foreign Key (Preferred):** Choose the entity type with **total participation**. Add the PK of the other side as a **FK**.
- **Strategy B — Merge:** Combine both entity types and the relationship into one relation. Useful when both sides have total participation.
- **Constraints:** Add a `UNIQUE` constraint on the FK to enforce 1:1.

### Step 4: Mapping of Binary 1:N Relationships

- **Rule:** Identify the relation $S$ representing the **"N" side**.
- **Action:** Add the PK of the relation $T$ (the "1" side) as a **foreign key** in $S$.
- **Relationship attributes:** If the 1:N relationship has its own attributes, include them in $S$.

$$
R_N(\underline{\text{PK}(R_N)}, \ldots, \text{FK} \rightarrow \text{PK}(R_1), \text{relAttrs})
$$

### Step 5: Mapping of Binary M:N Relationships

- **Rule:** Create a **NEW relation** $R_{MN}$.
- **Attributes:**
  - Foreign key to entity $E_1$: $\text{PK}(R_{E_1})$
  - Foreign key to entity $E_2$: $\text{PK}(R_{E_2})$
  - Any attributes of the relationship itself.
- **Primary Key:** $\text{PK}(R_{MN}) = \text{PK}(R_{E_1}) \cup \text{PK}(R_{E_2})$.

$$
R_{MN}(\underline{\text{PK}(R_{E_1}), \text{PK}(R_{E_2})}, \text{relAttrs})
$$

### Step 6: Mapping of Multivalued Attributes

- **Rule:** For each multivalued attribute $M$ of entity $E$, create a **NEW relation** $R_M$.
- **Attributes:** Include the **PK of $E$ (as FK)** + the multivalued attribute $M$.
- **Primary Key:** $\text{PK}(R_M) = \text{PK}(E) \cup \{M\}$.

$$
R_M(\underline{\text{PK}(E), M})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multivalued attributes violate 1NF**. This step is the *only* way to bring the relational schema back to **1NF compliance**.

### Step 7: Mapping of N-ary Relationship Types

For an n-ary relationship $R$ with participating entities $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$:

- **Action:** Create a **NEW relation** $R_S$.
- **Attributes:** Include the PKs of **all** $n$ participating entities as FKs + any attributes of $R$.
- **Primary Key:** Usually the **combination of all FKs**, but cardinality constraints may allow a smaller subset.

$$
R_S(\underline{\text{PK}(E_1), \text{PK}(E_2), \ldots, \text{PK}(E_n)}, \text{relAttrs})
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | ER Construct | Mapping Action | New Relation? | FK Added? | Primary Key of New/Updated Relation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strong Entity | Create relation with all simple attrs | Yes | No | Key of entity |
| 2 | Weak Entity | New relation + FK to owner | Yes | Yes (to owner) | Partial key + Owner's PK |
| 3 | Binary 1:1 | FK in one side (total participation) | No | Yes | Same as base relation |
| 4 | Binary 1:N | FK on the "N" side | No | Yes (on N side) | Same as N side relation |
| 5 | Binary M:N | New junction relation | **Yes** | Yes (×2) | Combination of both FKs |
| 6 | Multivalued Attr | New relation for the attribute | **Yes** | Yes (to entity) | Entity PK + attribute |
| 7 | Derived Attr | Do **NOT** store | No | No | N/A |
| 8 | N-ary Relationship | New junction relation | **Yes** | Yes (×n) | Combination of n FKs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick — "SMN-MND"**: **S**trong → direct, **M**:N → new table, **N**-ary → new table, **M**ultivalued → new table, **N**:1 → FK on N side, **D**erived → drop.

## 2.3 Mapping Specialization / Generalization (Extended KTU Topic)

There are **three accepted strategies** in KTU PCCST402:

### Strategy A: Multiple Relations (Superclass + Subclass)

- Create relation for superclass $S_c$ with its PK and shared attributes.
- Create **one relation per subclass** $S_i$, each with: subclass-specific attrs + PK of $S_c$ (as FK and PK).
- **Pros:** Clean separation. **Cons:** Many NULL values during queries.

### Strategy B: Multiple Relations with Type Attribute

- Create one relation per subclass only.
- Each subclass relation contains: subclass-specific attrs + superclass attrs + PK + a **type-discriminator attribute**.
- **Pros:** Avoids NULLs. **Cons:** Lots of joins.

### Strategy C: Single Relation with All Attributes + Type Attribute

- Create **one relation** for the superclass.
- Include: PK, all superclass attrs, **all subclass attrs (NULLable)**, plus a type attribute.
- **Pros:** Single table lookups. **Cons:** Many NULLs, attribute space wasted.

## 2.4 Mapping Aggregation

Aggregation is treated as an **ordinary higher-order relationship**. The mapping follows the M:N rule: a new junction relation with FKs to all participating entities, including the primary key of the aggregated relationship relation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce:** Mapping `Customer`–`Product` (M:N) creates the `Orders` table.
- **Hospital Management:** Mapping `Doctor`–`Patient`–`Treatment` (ternary) creates a `Consultation` table.
- **University ERP:** Mapping `Student`–`Course` (M:N with grade) creates the `Enrollment` table.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — University Database Mapping

Consider a sample ER diagram with the following elements:
- **Entities:** `STUDENT(SSN, Name, DOB)` (with multivalued `Phone`), `COURSE(CourseID, Title, Credits)`, `DEPARTMENT(DCode, DName)`.
- **Weak Entity:** `DEPENDENT(DepName, Age, Sex)` — owner is `STUDENT`.
- **Relationships:**
  - `ENROLLED_IN` — M:N between `STUDENT` and `COURSE`, with attribute `Grade`.
  - `OFFERS` — 1:N from `DEPARTMENT` to `COURSE`.
  - `ADVISES` — 1:1 between `STUDENT` and `DEPARTMENT`.

We now apply the 7-step algorithm **exhaustively**.

### Step 1: Map Strong Entities

**STUDENT:**

$$
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{SSN}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB})
$$

**COURSE:**

$$
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Title}, \text{Credits})
$$

**DEPARTMENT:**

$$
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{DCode}}, \text{DName})
$$

### Step 2: Map Weak Entity `DEPENDENT`

Owner is `STUDENT` (PK = `SSN`). Partial key of `DEPENDENT` is `DepName`.

$$
\text{DEPENDENT}(\underline{\text{DepName}}, \text{SSN}, \text{Age}, \text{Sex})
$$

Here, `SSN` is a **foreign key** referencing `STUDENT(SSN)`. The composite primary key is `(DepName, SSN)` because two dependents in different families could share the same name.

### Step 3: Map 1:1 Relationship `ADVISES`

Assume **total participation** of `STUDENT` (every student has exactly one advisor department). Place the FK on the `STUDENT` side:

$$
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{SSN}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB}, \text{Advising\_DCode})
$$

Where `Advising_DCode` is a FK referencing `DEPARTMENT(DCode)`, with a `UNIQUE` constraint.

### Step 4: Map 1:N Relationship `OFFERS`

`OFFERS` is from `DEPARTMENT` (1) to `COURSE` (N). Place FK on the **N-side** (`COURSE`):

$$
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Title}, \text{Credits}, \text{Offered\_By\_DCode})
$$

`Offered_By_DCode` is a FK referencing `DEPARTMENT(DCode)`.

### Step 5: Map M:N Relationship `ENROLLED_IN` (with attribute `Grade`)

Create a new junction table:

$$
\text{ENROLLED\_IN}(\underline{\text{SSN}, \text{CourseID}}, \text{Grade})
$$

Both `SSN` and `CourseID` are foreign keys. The composite PK is `(SSN, CourseID)`. The `Grade` attribute of the relationship is stored here.

### Step 6: Map Multivalued Attribute `Phone`

`Phone` is multivalued on `STUDENT`. Create a new relation:

$$
\text{STUDENT\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{SSN}, \text{Phone}})
$$

`SSN` is FK referencing `STUDENT(SSN)`.

### Step 7: Verify No N-ary or Derived Attributes

No n-ary relationships or derived attributes in this example. **Mapping complete.**

## 3.2 Final Relational Schema (Analytical Matrix)

| Relation | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| STUDENT | SSN, Name, DOB, Advising_DCode | SSN | Advising_DCode → DEPARTMENT(DCode) | Added FK for 1:1 ADVISES |
| COURSE | CourseID, Title, Credits, Offered_By_DCode | CourseID | Offered_By_DCode → DEPARTMENT(DCode) | Added FK for 1:N OFFERS |
| DEPARTMENT | DCode, DName | DCode | — | Base entity |
| DEPENDENT | DepName, SSN, Age, Sex | (DepName, SSN) | SSN → STUDENT(SSN) | Weak entity |
| ENROLLED_IN | SSN, CourseID, Grade | (SSN, CourseID) | SSN → STUDENT, CourseID → COURSE | Junction for M:N |
| STUDENT_PHONE | SSN, Phone | (SSN, Phone) | SSN → STUDENT(SSN) | Multivalued attr |

## 3.3 Constraint Preservation Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When mapping, ALWAYS verify that the following constraints from the ER diagram are still enforceable in the relational schema:**
>
> 1. **Entity Integrity:** PKs of all relations must be NOT NULL.
> 2. **Referential Integrity:** Every FK must reference a valid PK (or be NULL if participation is partial).
> 3. **Domain Constraints:** CHECK constraints for age, grade ranges, etc.
> 4. **Cardinality Constraints:** 1:1 → UNIQUE on FK; 1:N → no UNIQUE; M:N → junction table.
> 5. **Participation Constraints:** Total participation → NOT NULL on FK.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: When to Merge vs. When to Use Junction Table

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Merge into base relation}, & \text{if cardinality} = 1{:}1 \text{ and both sides have total participation} \\ \text{Add FK on N-side}, & \text{if cardinality} = 1{:}N \\ \text{Create junction table}, & \text{if cardinality} = M{:}N \\ \text{Create junction table}, & \text{if } n\text{-ary relationship with } n \geq 3 \\ \text{Create separate table}, & \text{if attribute is multivalued} \end{cases}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ER-to-Relational Mapping — Master Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Input ER Diagram] --> nodeB[Step 1: Map Strong Entities]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Map Weak Entities]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Map 1:1 Relationships]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Map 1:N Relationships]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Map M:N Relationships]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Map Multivalued Attributes]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Map N-ary Relationships]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Any Specialization?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Apply Strategy A or B or C]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK[Verify Constraints]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Output: Relational Schema]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mapping Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        inputA[ER Diagram]
        inputB[Entity Set]
        inputC[Relationship Set]
        inputD[Attribute Set]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        procA[Entity Mapper]
        procB[Relationship Mapper]
        procC[Attribute Mapper]
        procD[Constraint Generator]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        outA[Relations Tables]
        outB[Primary Keys]
        outC[Foreign Keys]
        outD[Integrity Rules]
    end

    inputA --> procA
    inputB --> procA
    inputC --> procB
    inputD --> procC
    procA --> procD
    procB --> procD
    procC --> procD
    procD --> outA
    procD --> outB
    procD --> outC
    procD --> outD
```

## 4.3 Mapping Decision Tree for Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Relationship Detected] --> checkCard{Cardinality?}
    checkCard -- 1:1 --> checkPart{Total Participation?}
    checkPart -- Yes --> mergeNode[Merge Relations]
    checkPart -- No --> fk1Node[Add FK + UNIQUE Constraint]
    checkCard -- 1:N --> fkNNode[Add FK on N-side]
    checkCard -- M:N --> junctionNode[Create Junction Table]
    checkCard -- N-ary --> naryNode[Create Junction Table with N FKs]
```

## 4.4 Entity-Relationship Conceptual Diagram of the Worked Example

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT ||--o{ DEPENDENT : "has"
    STUDENT }o--o{ COURSE : "ENROLLED_IN"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ COURSE : "OFFERS"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o| STUDENT : "ADVISES"

    STUDENT {
        string SSN PK
        string Name
        date DOB
        string Phone_MV
    }
    COURSE {
        string CourseID PK
        string Title
        int Credits
    }
    DEPARTMENT {
        string DCode PK
        string DName
    }
    DEPENDENT {
        string DepName PK
        int Age
        string Sex
    }
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**How are weak entities mapped to relations? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A weak entity $W$ cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone and depends on an **owner/identifying entity** $E$. The mapping steps are:

1. **Create a new relation** $R_W$ for the weak entity.
2. Include all **simple atomic attributes** of $W$.
3. Add the **primary key of $E$** as a foreign key in $R_W$.
4. The **primary key** of $R_W$ is the combination of the **partial key of $W$** and the **primary key of $E$**.

$$
R_W(\underline{\text{partialKey}(W)}, \text{PK}(E), \text{simpleAttrs}(W))
$$

**Example:** For a weak entity `DEPENDENT` with partial key `DepName` and owner `EMPLOYEE` (PK = `EmpID`):

$$
\text{DEPENDENT}(\underline{\text{DepName}, \text{EmpID}}, \text{Age}, \text{Sex})
$$

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are multivalued attributes mapped to a separate relation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multivalued attributes violate **First Normal Form (1NF)**, which requires every attribute to hold **atomic (single-valued)** data. Storing multiple values in a single column leads to:

1. **Insertion anomalies** — Cannot insert an entity without knowing all values.
2. **Update anomalies** — Updating one value requires searching multiple rows.
3. **Deletion anomalies** — Deleting one value may lose other values.
4. **Query complexity** — String parsing becomes necessary.

**Solution:** Create a separate relation $R_M$ containing the **entity's PK (as FK)** and the **multivalued attribute**. The composite PK enforces uniqueness:

$$
R_M(\underline{\text{PK}(E), M})
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm with all seven steps in detail. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Consider the following ER diagram for a "Library Management System":
- Entities: `BOOK(ISBN, Title, Year, Price)`, `AUTHOR(AID, AName)`, `PUBLISHER(PID, PName, City)`.
- `AUTHOR` has multivalued attribute `Email`.
- `PUBLISHED_BY`: 1:N between `BOOK` and `PUBLISHER`.
- `WRITTEN_BY`: M:N between `BOOK` and `AUTHOR` with attribute `RoyaltyPercent`.
- Weak entity `EDITION(EditionNo, PrintYear)` owned by `BOOK`.

**Map this ER diagram to a relational schema showing all keys and constraints.** (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Detailed Solution

The ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm consists of **7 sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Regular Entities:** Each strong entity $E$ becomes a relation with all simple attributes and a chosen primary key.

**Step 2 — Weak Entities:** Each weak entity $W$ becomes a relation containing its own attributes, the PK of its owner entity (as FK), and a composite primary key of partial key + owner's PK.

**Step 3 — Binary 1:1 Relationships:** Add the PK of one side as a FK in the other (prefer the side with total participation). Add `UNIQUE` constraint on the FK.

**Step 4 — Binary 1:N Relationships:** Add the PK of the "1" side as a FK in the "N" side relation. Include any relationship attributes on the N side.

**Step 5 — Binary M:N Relationships:** Create a new junction relation with two FKs (PKs of both participating entities) and a composite primary key. Include any relationship attributes.

**Step 6 — Multivalued Attributes:** Create a separate relation containing the entity's PK (FK) and the multivalued attribute; composite PK of both.

**Step 7 — N-ary Relationships:** Create a new relation with $n$ foreign keys (one per participating entity) and a composite primary key.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Library Management Mapping

**Step 1 — Map Strong Entities:**

$$
\text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{ISBN}}, \text{Title}, \text{Year}, \text{Price})
$$

$$
\text{AUTHOR}(\underline{\text{AID}}, \text{AName})
$$

$$
\text{PUBLISHER}(\underline{\text{PID}}, \text{PName}, \text{City})
$$

**Step 2 — Map Weak Entity `EDITION`:** Owned by `BOOK`. Partial key = `EditionNo`.

$$
\text{EDITION}(\underline{\text{EditionNo}}, \text{ISBN}, \text{PrintYear})
$$

FK: `ISBN` → `BOOK(ISBN)`.

**Step 4 — Map 1:N `PUBLISHED_BY`:** Add FK to `BOOK` (the N-side):

$$
\text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{ISBN}}, \text{Title}, \text{Year}, \text{Price}, \text{PID})
$$

FK: `PID` → `PUBLISHER(PID)`.

**Step 5 — Map M:N `WRITTEN_BY`:** Create junction relation:

$$
\text{WRITTEN\_BY}(\underline{\text{ISBN}, \text{AID}}, \text{RoyaltyPercent})
$$

FKs: `ISBN` → `BOOK`, `AID` → `AUTHOR`.

**Step 6 — Map Multivalued `Email` of `AUTHOR`:** Create new relation:

$$
\text{AUTHOR\_EMAIL}(\underline{\text{AID}, \text{Email}})
$$

FK: `AID` → `AUTHOR(AID)`.

**Final Schema Summary:**

| Relation | PK | FKs |
|---|---|---|
| BOOK | ISBN | PID → PUBLISHER |
| AUTHOR | AID | — |
| PUBLISHER | PID | — |
| EDITION | (EditionNo, ISBN) | ISBN → BOOK |
| WRITTEN_BY | (ISBN, AID) | ISBN, AID |
| AUTHOR_EMAIL | (AID, Email) | AID → AUTHOR |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain with examples how the following are mapped to relations: (i) 1:1 binary relationship, (ii) 1:N binary relationship, (iii) M:N binary relationship. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Map the following ER schema into relational schema:
- Entities: `EMPLOYEE(EID, Ename, Salary)`, `PROJECT(PID, Pname, Budget)`, `DEPARTMENT(DID, Dname)`.
- `CONTROLS`: 1:N from `DEPARTMENT` to `PROJECT`.
- `MANAGES`: 1:1 between `EMPLOYEE` and `DEPARTMENT` (total participation of DEPARTMENT).
- `WORKS_ON`: M:N between `EMPLOYEE` and `PROJECT` with attribute `Hours`.
- `DEPENDENT(DepName, Relation)` weak entity owned by `EMPLOYEE`. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) 1:1 Relationship Mapping:**

For each 1:1 relationship $R$ between entities $E_1$ and $E_2$:
- Choose the relation $S$ representing the entity with **total participation**.
- Add the PK of $T$ (other side) as a **FK** in $S$.
- Apply a **UNIQUE** constraint on the FK column.
- Include any relationship attributes in $S$.

**Example:** If `EMPLOYEE` `MANAGES` `DEPARTMENT` (1:1, total on DEPARTMENT side), put FK in `DEPARTMENT`.

**(ii) 1:N Relationship Mapping:**

- Identify the relation on the **N-side** ($S_N$).
- Add the PK of the 1-side ($T_1$) as a **FK** in $S_N$.
- Include any relationship attributes in $S_N$.
