- No UNIQUE constraint needed (since many N rows reference the same 1 row).

**(iii) M:N Relationship Mapping:**

- Create a **new junction relation** $S_{MN}$.
- Include PKs of both participating entities as **two FKs**.
- Include any attributes of the relationship itself.
- **Composite PK** = combination of both FKs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Detailed Mapping

**Step 1 — Strong Entities:**

$$
\text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{EID}}, \text{Ename}, \text{Salary})
$$

$$
\text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{PID}}, \text{Pname}, \text{Budget})
$$

$$
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{DID}}, \text{Dname})
$$

**Step 2 — Weak Entity `DEPENDENT`:**

$$
\text{DEPENDENT}(\underline{\text{DepName}}, \text{EID}, \text{Relation})
$$

FK: `EID` → `EMPLOYEE(EID)`. PK: `(DepName, EID)`.

**Step 3 — 1:1 `MANAGES` (Total Participation of DEPARTMENT):**

Since `DEPARTMENT` has total participation, place FK on `DEPARTMENT` side:

$$
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{DID}}, \text{Dname}, \text{Manager\_EID})
$$

FK: `Manager_EID` → `EMPLOYEE(EID)`, with `UNIQUE` constraint and `NOT NULL`.

**Step 4 — 1:N `CONTROLS`:**

FK goes on `PROJECT` (N-side):

$$
\text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{PID}}, \text{Pname}, \text{Budget}, \text{Controlling\_DID})
$$

FK: `Controlling_DID` → `DEPARTMENT(DID)`.

**Step 5 — M:N `WORKS_ON` with attribute `Hours`:**

$$
\text{WORKS\_ON}(\underline{\text{EID}, \text{PID}}, \text{Hours})
$$

FKs: `EID` → `EMPLOYEE`, `PID` → `PROJECT`.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make — Leading to Loss of 2-4 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Placing FK on the WRONG side** for 1:N — ALWAYS put FK on the **"N" side**, not the "1" side.
> 2. **Forgetting the UNIQUE constraint** on 1:1 FKs — this allows many-to-many violations.
> 3. **Storing multivalued attributes in the main relation** — guarantees 1NF violation. **Always create a separate table.**
> 4. **Wrong composite PK for weak entities** — must be (partial key + owner's PK), not just the partial key alone.
> 5. **Forgetting to map derived attributes** — they are **NOT** stored; only computed on demand.
> 6. **Missing FK declarations** — Always state the FK reference target explicitly: e.g., "EID references EMPLOYEE(EID)".
> 7. **Confusing M:N junction PK** — composite PK should be **both FKs together**, not a separate ID column.
> 8. **Not including relationship attributes** — In M:N, attributes like `Grade`, `Hours`, `RoyaltyPercent` go into the junction table.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the Exam, Ensure You Can:**
>
> - Recall and apply all **7 steps** of the ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm.
> - Identify **cardinality ratios** (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and choose the correct mapping strategy.
> - Distinguish between **strong and weak entities** and explain composite PK for weak entities.
> - Handle **multivalued attributes** by creating a separate relation (1NF compliance).
> - Explain why **derived attributes are NOT stored** in the relational schema.
> - Map **N-ary relationships** (n ≥ 3) using a junction relation with n FKs.
> - Apply the **three strategies** for mapping Specialization/Generalization.
> - Write proper **FK constraint declarations** including UNIQUE, NOT NULL, and references.
> - Verify the **mapping preserves all ER constraints** (entity, referential, cardinality).
> - Recognize that this step produces a **1NF schema** — further normalization is the next phase.
>
> **Memory Mnemonic:** **"S-S-W-M-N-N-M-N-D"** — Strong, Specialization, Weak, Multivalued, N-ary, 1:N, M:N, 1:1, Derived.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Transactions: Problems and Failures, Desirable Properties of Transactions, Characterizing Schedules Based on Recoverability and Serializability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Transactions: Problems and Failures, Desirable Properties of Transactions, Characterizing Schedules Based on Recoverability and Serializability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Transaction?

A **Transaction** is a logical unit of database processing that includes one or more database access operations (read and write operations) executed as a single, indivisible logical unit of work. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (and the foundational text by Elmasri & Navathe), a transaction is the basic unit of change in a database system, designed to transform the database from one consistent state to another while preserving the integrity of the data.

Formally, a transaction is a sequence of operations of the form $\{$ read_item, write_item, commit, abort, rollback $\}$ grouped together to perform a single logical task.

### Transaction Boundary Markers (SQL Syntax)

In standard SQL, a transaction is demarcated using:
- `BEGIN TRANSACTION` (or `START TRANSACTION`) → Marks the beginning.
- `COMMIT` → Marks the successful end and makes changes permanent.
- `ROLLBACK` (or `ABORT`) → Marks the unsuccessful end and undoes changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A transaction must be **atomic** — either ALL of its operations are applied to the database, or NONE of them are. There is no in-between state from the user's perspective.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Fund Transfer"

Imagine a customer, **Rahul**, transfers **₹5,000** from his **Savings Account (A/c: 1001)** to his friend's **Current Account (A/c: 2002)** at the same bank. This single business action, "Transfer Money," is composed of two inseparable database operations:

| Step | Database Operation | Account | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `UPDATE` (Debit) | 1001 | Balance = Balance - 5000 |
| 2 | `UPDATE` (Credit) | 2002 | Balance = Balance + 5000 |

### What happens if a system crash occurs between Step 1 and Step 2?

If Rahul's account is debited but the friend's account is never credited, the bank effectively **loses ₹5,000** out of thin air. This is unacceptable. Therefore, these two operations must be bundled into a **single transaction** that either completes *entirely* (debit AND credit) or *not at all* (no change to either account). This bundling is the essence of a database transaction.

## 1.3 Operations Within a Transaction

A transaction typically performs two primitive operations on database items:

- **read_item(X):** Reads the value of database item $X$ from the database into a local buffer (or program variable).
- **write_item(X):** Writes the value of database item $X$ from the local buffer back to the database.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Transaction Management?

A database system must handle transactions in the presence of:

1. **Concurrent execution** of multiple transactions (multi-user environment).
2. **System failures** (power outage, software crash, hardware fault).
3. **Distributed execution** (transactions crossing network nodes).

Without proper transaction management, the database could enter an **inconsistent state**, leading to data loss, financial discrepancies, or logical errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Foundational Questions Every KTU Student Must Answer:**
> 1. What is a transaction and what are its operations?
> 2. What can go wrong (problems and failures)?
> 3. What guarantees must a DBMS provide (desirable properties)?
> 4. How do we judge whether a schedule of transactions is "good" (recoverability and serializability)?
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transaction State Diagram (Operations on Transactions)

Every transaction traverses a defined lifecycle. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the state transition model:

> [!NOTE]
> **State Diagram Stages:**
> **Active → Partially Committed → Committed**
> **Active → Failed → Aborted → Terminated**

Detailed state explanations:

- **Active State:** The initial state where the transaction starts executing its read/write operations.
- **Partially Committed State:** Reached after the final statement of the transaction has executed, but **before** the COMMIT operation is finalized (i.e., the log records are flushed to disk but the actual database writes may not be permanent yet).
- **Committed State:** Reached after the COMMIT operation successfully completes. All changes are permanently recorded in the database.
- **Failed State:** Entered when the transaction discovers that normal execution can no longer proceed (due to logical errors, system failures, or explicit abort request).
- **Aborted State:** After the transaction has been rolled back and the database has been restored to its pre-transaction state.
- **Terminated State:** Final state — either the transaction has committed (success) or aborted (failure) and is no longer active in the system.

## 2.2 Problems and Failures in Database Transactions

The KTU 2024 module explicitly lists the following problem categories that motivate the need for recovery and concurrency control:

### 2.2.1 Problems Caused by Concurrent Transactions (Without Proper Control)

1. **The Lost Update Problem (Write-Write Conflict):**
   Two transactions update the same data item, and the update from one is overwritten by the other, effectively "losing" the first update.

   **Example:** $T_1$ reads $X = 100$, computes $X + 10 = 110$ but hasn't written yet. $T_2$ reads $X = 100$, computes $X + 20 = 120$ and writes $X = 120$. Now $T_1$ writes $X = 110$, overwriting $T_2$'s update.

2. **The Temporary Update (Dirty Read) Problem:**
   A transaction reads a value written by another transaction that has not yet committed. If that other transaction aborts, the read becomes invalid.

3. **The Incorrect Summary Problem:**
   One transaction is computing an aggregate (e.g., SUM) while other transactions are updating the data being aggregated, leading to incorrect results.

4. **The Unrepeatable Read Problem:**
   A transaction reads the same data item twice and gets different values because another transaction modified and committed that item in between.

5. **The Phantom Read Problem:**
   A transaction re-executes a query that returns a set of rows and finds that additional rows have been added (or removed) by another committed transaction.

### 2.2.2 Types of System Failures (Recovery Perspective)

| Failure Type | Description | Recovery Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Crash** | Hardware/software failure causing loss of in-memory data | Use log to redo committed / undo uncommitted |
| **Transaction Error** | Logical error (division by zero, constraint violation) | Rollback the transaction |
| **Local Error** | Condition that the transaction itself cannot handle | Rollback the transaction |
| **Concurrency Control Enforcement** | DBMS kills one transaction to preserve serializability | Rollback the victim transaction |
| **Disk Failure** | Physical damage to secondary storage (rare but severe) | Restore from archive + redo log |
| **Catastrophic Failure** | Natural disaster, sabotage | Reconstruct from backup archive |

## 2.3 Desirable Properties of Transactions — The ACID Paradigm

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists ACID as the cornerstone of transaction theory. Every reliable transaction must satisfy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACID Properties (Mnemonic: "ACID" = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)**

### 2.3.1 Atomicity
A transaction is an atomic unit — it must be executed in its entirety or not at all. Partial execution is forbidden. This is the "all-or-nothing" property.

**Who enforces it?** The **Transaction Manager** (using a recovery subsystem / log).

### 2.3.2 Consistency
A transaction must transform the database from one **consistent state** to another **consistent state**. The database should satisfy all integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check constraints, triggers) before and after the transaction.

**Who enforces it?** Both the **programmer** (writing correct transactions) and the **DBMS** (constraint checker).

### 2.3.3 Isolation
Even though multiple transactions may execute concurrently, the result must be as if they executed in isolation — i.e., one after another, without interleaving. Each transaction is unaware of other concurrent transactions.

**Who enforces it?** The **Concurrency Control Subsystem** (using locks, timestamps, etc.).

### 2.3.4 Durability
Once a transaction commits, its effects on the database must **persist permanently**, even in the face of system crashes, power failures, or other disasters.

**Who enforces it?** The **Recovery Subsystem** (using log-based recovery: REDO committed transactions).

## 2.4 Characterizing Schedules Based on Recoverability

A **schedule (or history)** $S$ is a sequence of operations from one or more transactions that preserves the order of operations within each individual transaction. The KTU 2024 module categorizes schedules based on two axes: **Recoverability** and **Serializability**.

### 2.4.1 Recoverability Hierarchy

The recoverability classification is used by the recovery system to determine if it can safely recover from a failure:

1. **Recoverable Schedule:**
   A schedule $S$ is recoverable if, for every transaction $T_i$ that commits in $S$, the commit of $T_i$ must appear **after** the commit of every other transaction $T_j$ from which $T_i$ has read. In other words, no committed transaction should have read data written by an aborted transaction.

2. **Cascadeless Schedule (Avoid Cascading Aborts):**
   A schedule is cascadeless if transactions only read values that were written by **already committed** transactions. In a cascadeless schedule, the abort of one transaction does not force the abort of any other transaction.

3. **Strict Schedule:**
   A schedule is strict if no transaction reads or writes a value written by an uncommitted transaction. This is the strongest recoverability condition — writes are not read by any other transaction until the writing transaction commits or aborts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Relationship:**
> **Strict** $\Rightarrow$ **Cascadeless** $\Rightarrow$ **Recoverable**
> (Every strict schedule is cascadeless; every cascadeless schedule is recoverable.)

## 2.5 Characterizing Schedules Based on Serializability

### 2.5.1 Serial vs. Non-Serial Schedules

- **Serial Schedule:** A schedule in which transactions are executed one after another, with no interleaving of operations from different transactions.
- **Non-Serial Schedule:** A schedule in which operations from multiple transactions are interleaved.

### 2.5.2 The Concept of Serializability

A non-serial schedule is **serializable** if its execution produces the same result and leaves the database in the same state as some serial schedule involving the same transactions.

Two main types of serializability are defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### 2.5.3 Conflict Serializability

Two operations in a schedule **conflict** if they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one of them is a `write_item`. There are three conflict types:

- **Read-Write Conflict:** $T_i$ reads $X$, $T_j$ writes $X$.
- **Write-Read Conflict:** $T_i$ writes $X$, $T_j$ reads $X$.
- **Write-Write Conflict:** $T_i$ writes $X$, $T_j$ writes $X$.

A schedule $S$ is **conflict serializable** if it is conflict equivalent to some serial schedule. This is tested using a **precedence graph** (also called a serialization graph).

### 2.5.4 View Serializability

A schedule $S$ is **view serializable** if it is view equivalent to some serial schedule. Two schedules are view equivalent if they satisfy three conditions:

1. **Initial Read:** For each data item $X$, if $T_i$ reads the initial value of $X$ in one schedule, then $T_i$ must also read the initial value of $X$ in the other schedule.
2. **Read-From Relationship:** If $T_i$ reads $X$ in one schedule, and that value was written by $T_j$, then in the other schedule, $T_i$ must also read the value of $X$ written by $T_j$.
3. **Final Write:** For each data item $X$, the transaction that performs the final write on $X$ in one schedule must also perform the final write on $X$ in the other schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Serializability:**
> **Conflict Serializable** $\subset$ **View Serializable** $\subset$ **All Serializable Schedules**
> (Every conflict-serializable schedule is view-serializable, but not vice versa.)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Key Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transaction States** | Active $\to$ Partially Committed $\to$ Committed / Failed $\to$ Aborted | Failed and Aborted are not the same state |
| **ACID Properties** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | All four are mandatory for a "reliable" DBMS |
| **Recoverability** | Recoverable $\Leftarrow$ Cascadeless $\Leftarrow$ Strict | Strict is the strongest |
| **Serializability Hierarchy** | Conflict $\subset$ View $\subset$ Serializable | Conflict Serializable is a strict subset of View Serializable |
| **Precedence Graph Condition for Conflict Serializability** | A schedule is conflict serializable $\iff$ its precedence graph is **acyclic** (no cycles) | Any cycle in the precedence graph means **NOT** conflict serializable |
| **Number of Possible Serial Schedules (for $n$ transactions)** | $n!$ | For $n = 3$, there are 6 possible serial schedules |
| **Number of Operations in a Schedule** | Sum of operations across all transactions | Used in detecting conflicts |
| **Conflict Pairs** | $3$ types: RW, WR, WW on the same data item | RR is never a conflict |
| **Read-from Edge in View Equivalence** | $T_j \xrightarrow{reads} T_i$ if $T_i$ produces a value read by $T_j$ | Used in view serializability test |
| **Failure Recovery Cost** | Time = (Time to redo committed txns) + (Time to undo uncommitted txns) | Log-based recovery minimizes this |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Transaction management is the **backbone of all mission-critical database systems**:

- **Banking Systems (e.g., HDFC, SBI):** Every transfer, withdrawal, and deposit must be atomic and durable. ACID guarantees prevent financial loss.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Order placement, inventory deduction, and payment processing are bundled into a single transaction.
- **Airline Reservations:** Seat booking and ticket generation are atomic to prevent over-booking.
- **Hospital Management Systems:** Patient record updates must be consistent, isolated, and durable.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm: Testing Conflict Serializability Using a Precedence Graph

The **precedence graph** (serialization graph) is the standard KTU 2024 method to test whether a non-serial schedule is conflict serializable.

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1:** Create a node for each transaction $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ that participates in the schedule $S$.

**Step 2:** For every pair of conflicting operations $O_i$ (in $T_i$) and $O_j$ (in $T_j$), where $O_i$ appears **before** $O_j$ in $S$, draw a directed edge:
$$T_i \longrightarrow T_j$$
This edge indicates that $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ in any equivalent serial schedule.

**Step 3:** Check the resulting directed graph for **cycles**. 

- If **no cycle exists** $\Rightarrow$ the schedule is **conflict serializable**, and any **topological sort** of the graph gives an equivalent serial schedule.
- If **a cycle exists** $\Rightarrow$ the schedule is **NOT conflict serializable**.

### 3.1.1 Detailed Worked Example (KTU Standard)

Consider the following schedule $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S: & \quad r_1(X), \; r_2(X), \; w_1(X), \; r_3(X), \; w_3(X), \; w_2(X), \; c_1, \; c_2, \; c_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $r_i$ means $T_i$ reads, $w_i$ means $T_i$ writes, and $c_i$ means $T_i$ commits.

**Step 1 — Identify all conflicting pairs:**

We scan the schedule for operations on the same data item where at least one is a write:

| Conflict | Order in S | Edge Drawn |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $r_1(X), w_1(X)$ | Same transaction — ignore | None |
| $r_2(X), w_1(X)$ | $T_2$ reads $X$ first, then $T_1$ writes $X$ | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_1$ |
| $w_1(X), w_3(X)$ | $T_1$ writes first, then $T_3$ writes | $T_1 \longrightarrow T_3$ |
| $w_1(X), w_2(X)$ | $T_1$ writes first, then $T_2$ writes | $T_1 \longrightarrow T_2$ |
| $w_3(X), w_2(X)$ | $T_3$ writes first, then $T_2$ writes | $T_3 \longrightarrow T_2$ |
| $r_3(X), w_2(X)$ | $T_3$ reads first, then $T_2$ writes | $T_3 \longrightarrow T_2$ |

**Step 2 — Draw the precedence graph:**

The final set of edges (after eliminating redundancy) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edges:} \quad & T_1 \longrightarrow T_2 \\
& T_1 \longrightarrow T_3 \\
& T_2 \longrightarrow T_1 \\
& T_3 \longrightarrow T_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check for cycles:**

Path: $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_1$ — **CYCLE DETECTED!**

Another path: $T_1 \to T_3 \to T_2 \to T_1$ — also forms a cycle.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since a cycle exists, **schedule $S$ is NOT conflict serializable**.

## 3.2 Algorithm: Topological Sort (When Acyclic)

If the precedence graph is acyclic, a topological sort gives a serial order equivalent to the original schedule. The algorithm:

1. Find a node with in-degree 0 (no incoming edges).
2. Remove that node and all its outgoing edges.
3. Repeat until all nodes are removed.
4. The order of removal is a valid equivalent serial schedule.

### 3.2.1 Worked Topological Sort Example

Consider a schedule with edges: $T_1 \to T_3$, $T_2 \to T_3$, $T_2 \to T_4$.

| Step | Nodes with In-Degree 0 | Selected | Updated Edges |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T_1, T_2$ | $T_1$ (or $T_2$) | $T_1 \to T_3$ removed |
| 2 | $T_2$ | $T_2$ | $T_2 \to T_3$, $T_2 \to T_4$ removed |
| 3 | $T_3$ | $T_3$ | $T_3 \to$ none |
| 4 | $T_4$ | $T_4$ | (empty) |

**Equivalent Serial Schedule:** $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_4$ (or $T_2 \to T_1 \to T_3 \to T_4$, depending on initial choice).

## 3.3 Algorithm: Testing View Serializability

View serializability is more complex than conflict serializability. The KTU 2024 module outlines the following procedure:

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1:** Check if the schedule is conflict serializable. If yes, it is also view serializable — done.

**Step 2:** If not conflict serializable, manually check view equivalence with all possible serial schedules of the participating transactions.

**Step 3:** For each candidate serial schedule, verify the three conditions of view equivalence:

- **Initial Read Condition:** Same set of transactions read the initial values of data items.
- **Read-From Condition:** The same producer-consumer relationships for data values hold.
- **Final Write Condition:** The same transaction performs the final write on each data item.

If any of these conditions is satisfied with at least one serial schedule, the original schedule is **view serializable** but **not conflict serializable** — this is the "blind write" boundary case.

### 3.3.1 Worked Example: Blind Write and View Serializability

Consider:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S: \quad & w_1(X, 5), \; w_2(X, 10), \; w_1(X, 7), \; c_1, \; c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Check conflicts. The pair $(w_1, w_2)$ gives $T_1 \to T_2$; the pair $(w_2, w_1)$ gives $T_2 \to T_1$. The precedence graph has a **cycle**: $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_1$.

**Step 2:** So $S$ is **NOT conflict serializable**. But is it view serializable?

**Step 3:** Check view equivalence with serial schedule $S_{12}: T_1; T_2$ (i.e., $T_1$ all operations first, then $T_2$).

In $S_{12}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12}: \quad & w_1(X, 5), \; w_1(X, 7), \; c_1, \; w_2(X, 10), \; c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Initial Read:** No reads in either schedule — condition trivially satisfied.
- **Read-From:** No reads — condition trivially satisfied.
- **Final Write:** In $S$, the final write on $X$ is by $T_2$ (value 10). In $S_{12}$, the final write on $X$ is also by $T_2$ (value 10). **Condition satisfied!**

**Step 4:** Conclusion — $S$ is **view serializable** (equivalent to $S_{12}$) but **NOT conflict serializable** (due to the cycle). This is the canonical example of the "**blind write**" scenario.

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Possible Serial Schedules

For $n$ transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, the number of distinct serial schedules is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Serial Schedules} & = n! \\
& = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times 2 \times 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Examples:**

- For $n = 2$: Number of serial schedules $= 2! = 2$. They are: $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_1)$.
- For $n = 3$: Number of serial schedules $= 3! = 6$. They are: $(T_1, T_2, T_3)$, $(T_1, T_3, T_2)$, $(T_2, T_1, T_3)$, $(T_2, T_3, T_1)$, $(T_3, T_1, T_2)$, $(T_3, T_2, T_1)$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: ACID Enforcement Mapping

| ACID Property | What It Protects Against | DBMS Component Responsible | Primary Technique |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Atomicity** | Partial execution of transactions | Transaction Manager (Recovery) | Log-based UNDO |
| **Consistency** | Violation of integrity constraints | Constraint Checker + Programmer | Declarative constraints, triggers |
| **Isolation** | Interference from concurrent transactions | Concurrency Control Subsystem | Locking, Timestamps, Optimistic CC |
| **Durability** | Loss of committed data on crash | Recovery Manager (Storage) | Log-based REDO, Checkpointing |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Recoverability Hierarchy

| Schedule Type | Reading Uncommitted Data? | Writing Over Uncommitted Data? | Allows Cascading Aborts? | Recoverable from Failure? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Recoverable** | Allowed (with restriction) | Allowed | Possible, but commits are ordered | Yes |
| **Cascadeless** | **Not allowed** | Allowed | **No** | Yes |
| **Strict** | **Not allowed** | **Not allowed** | **No** (Strongest) | Yes (Easiest) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Transaction State Transition Diagram

This diagram visualizes the complete lifecycle of a database transaction, including the conditions that trigger transitions between states.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active : BEGIN TRANSACTION
    Active --> Active : Read Write operations execute
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : Final statement executed
    Active --> Failed : Logical error or system crash detected
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : Log written to disk and COMMIT succeeds
    PartiallyCommitted --> Failed : System crash before log flush completes
    Committed --> [*] : Transaction terminates successfully
    Failed --> Aborted : ROLLBACK initiated
    Aborted --> [*] : Database restored to pre-transaction state
    note right of Active : The transaction is executing its operations
    note right of PartiallyCommitted : All operations done, awaiting final commit decision
    note right of Committed : Changes are permanent and durable
    note right of Aborted : All changes have been rolled back
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: ACID Properties Architecture

This block diagram shows the DBMS subsystems responsible for enforcing each ACID property and the techniques they employ.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer[Client Application Layer]
        AppLayer[Application Programmer writes Transaction Code]
    end

    subgraph DBMSCore[DBMS Core Engine]
        direction TB
        ConcurrencyCtrl[Concurrency Control Subsystem]
        RecoveryMgr[Recovery Manager Subsystem]
        ConstraintChk[Constraint Checker]
        BufferMgr[Buffer Manager]
    end

    subgraph DiskStorage[Physical Disk Storage]
        Database[(Database Files)]
        LogFiles[(System Log Files)]
    end

    AppLayer --> ConcurrencyCtrl
    AppLayer --> RecoveryMgr
    AppLayer --> ConstraintChk

    ConcurrencyCtrl -. Locking Timestamps .-> DBMSCore
    RecoveryMgr -. REDO UNDO Logging .-> DBMSCore
    ConstraintChk -. Integrity Rules .-> DBMSCore
    BufferMgr -. Caching Paging .-> DBMSCore

    ConcurrencyCtrl --> Database
    RecoveryMgr --> LogFiles
    BufferMgr --> Database
    BufferMgr --> LogFiles

    subgraph ACIDMapping[ACID Property Enforcement]
        direction LR
        AtomicityEnf[Atomicity - Enforced by Recovery Manager using UNDO]
        ConsistencyEnf[Consistency - Enforced by Constraint Checker and Programmer]
        IsolationEnf[Isolation - Enforced by Concurrency Control using Locks]
        DurabilityEnf[Durability - Enforced by Recovery Manager using REDO]
    end

    AtomicityEnf --- RecoveryMgr
    ConsistencyEnf --- ConstraintChk
    IsolationEnf --- ConcurrencyCtrl
    DurabilityEnf --- RecoveryMgr
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Schedule Classification Hierarchy

This diagram illustrates the recoverability and serializability hierarchy, with concrete examples at each level.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AllSchedules[All Possible Schedules]
        direction TB

        subgraph SerialSch[Serial Schedules]
            SS1[Serial S1: T1 then T2]
            SS2[Serial S2: T2 then T1]
        end

        subgraph NonSerialSch[Non-Serial Schedules]
            direction TB

            subgraph RecoverableSch[Recoverable Schedules]
                direction TB
                CascadelessSch[Cascadeless Schedules]
                StrictSch[Strict Schedules - Strongest Recoverable]
            end

            subgraph NonRecoverableSch[Non-Recoverable Schedules]
                NR1[Unrecoverable - Violates Commit Order]
            end
        end

        subgraph SerializableSch[Serializable Schedules]
            direction TB
            ViewSer[View Serializable]
            ConflictSer[Conflict Serializable - Subset of View Serializable]
        end
    end

    SS1 --- SerializableSch
    SS2 --- SerializableSch
    StrictSch --> CascadelessSch
    CascadelessSch --> RecoverableSch
    RecoverableSch --> AllSchedules
    NonRecoverableSch --> AllSchedules
    ConflictSer --> ViewSer
    ViewSer --> SerializableSch
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Precedence Graph Test for Conflict Serializability

This block diagram shows the algorithm flow for testing conflict serializability.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Start with Schedule S]) --> Step1[Step 1: Create one node per transaction]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Scan for conflicting operation pairs]
    Step2 --> Step3{Conflict Found?}
    Step3 -->|Yes| DrawEdge[Draw directed edge Ti to Tj]
    DrawEdge --> ContinueScan[Continue scanning]
    ContinueScan --> Step2
    Step3 -->|No| Step4[Step 3: Check for cycles in the graph]
    Step4 --> CycleCheck{Cycle Detected?}
    CycleCheck -->|Yes| ResultNot[Schedule is NOT conflict serializable]
    CycleCheck -->|No| ResultYes[Schedule IS conflict serializable]
    ResultYes --> TopoSort[Step 4: Apply topological sort to find equivalent serial order]
    TopoSort --> Done([Output: Serial Schedule Equivalent to S])
    ResultNot --> Done2([Output: No equivalent serial schedule exists])
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: System Failure Recovery Architecture

This diagram shows how the recovery subsystem handles different types of failures.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FailureInputs[Input Failure Types]
        F1[System Crash]
        F2[Transaction Error]
        F3[Disk Failure]
        F4[Concurrency Violation]
    end

    subgraph DetectionLayer[Detection and Analysis]
        Detector[Failure Detector Module]
    end

    subgraph RecoveryLayer[Recovery Actions]
        direction TB
        LogReplay[Log Replay Engine]
        UndoOp[UNDO Uncommitted Transactions]
        RedoOp[REDO Committed Transactions]
        CheckpointUtil[Checkpoint Utility]
    end

    subgraph OutputState[Output Database State]
        ConsistentState[Consistent Database State]
    end

    F1 --> Detector
    F2 --> Detector
    F3 --> Detector
    F4 --> Detector

    Detector --> LogReplay
    LogReplay --> UndoOp
    LogReplay --> RedoOp
    CheckpointUtil -. Optimizes recovery time .-> LogReplay

    UndoOp --> ConsistentState
    RedoOp --> ConsistentState
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Transaction. List its four main operations. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A transaction is a logical unit of database processing that consists of one or more database access operations. It transforms the database from one consistent state to another.

The four main operations are:
1. **read_item(X):** Reads the value of data item $X$ into a transaction-local variable.
2. **write_item(X):** Writes the value of $X$ from the local variable back to the database.
3. **commit:** Permanently records all changes made by the transaction.
4. **rollback (or abort):** Undoes all changes and restores the database to its pre-transaction state.

### Question 2: State the ACID properties of a transaction. Briefly explain any two. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The four ACID properties are:

| Property | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution of operations |
| **Consistency** | Database moves from one consistent state to another |
| **Isolation** | Transactions execute as if they are alone in the system |
| **Durability** | Committed changes survive any subsequent failure |

**Atomicity (Detailed):** A transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit. If any operation in the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back. For example, in a fund transfer, both debit and credit must succeed, or neither should happen.

**Isolation (Detailed):** Concurrent transactions must not interfere with each other's intermediate results. Each transaction should see only committed data from other transactions, as if it were running alone. The DBMS achieves this through locking or timestamp-based protocols.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Students often confuse **Cascadeless** and **Strict** schedules. Remember: Strict prevents reading AND writing uncommitted data; Cascadeless only prevents reading.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** In precedence graph problems, students forget to consider **Read-Write conflicts** (not just Write-Write). Every conflict type on the same data item must be examined.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Students mark schedules as "view serializable" without checking all three view equivalence conditions. All three (Initial Read, Read-From, Final Write) must hold.
> - **MISTAKE 4:** Confusing **Recoverable** with **Conflict Serializable**. They are entirely different classifications! Recoverability is about failure handling; Serializability is about consistency.
> - **MISTAKE 5:** Forgetting to **label the edges** with the conflicting data item. This is required for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Test the schedule for conflict serializability and characterize its recoverability. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Consider the following schedule $S$ involving three transactions $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S: \quad & r_2(A), \; r_1(B), \; w_2(A), \; r_3(A), \; w_1(B), \; w_3(A), \; r_1(A), \; w_1(A), \; c_1, \; c_2, \; c_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the precedence graph and determine whether $S$ is conflict serializable. If yes, find an equivalent serial schedule.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the data items involved. Here, the data items are $A$ and $B$.

**Step 2:** Scan the schedule for conflicting operations. A conflict exists when two operations from different transactions access the same data item and at least one is a write.

| Conflict Pair | Order in $S$ | Edge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $r_2(A), w_2(A)$ | Same transaction | None |
| $r_2(A), w_3(A)$ | $T_2$ reads, then $T_3$ writes | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_3$ |
| $w_2(A), r_3(A)$ | $T_2$ writes, then $T_3$ reads | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_3$ |
| $w_2(A), w_3(A)$ | $T_2$ writes, then $T_3$ writes | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_3$ |
| $w_2(A), r_1(A)$ | $T_2$ writes, then $T_1$ reads | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_1$ |
| $w_2(A), w_1(A)$ | $T_2$ writes, then $T_1$ writes | $T_2 \longrightarrow T_1$ |
| $r_1(B), w_1(B)$ | Same transaction | None |
| $w_1(B), w_3(A)$ | Different items, no conflict | None |
| $w_1(B), r_1(A)$ | Different items, no conflict | None |
| $w_3(A), r_1(A)$ | $T_3$ writes, then $T_1$ reads | $T_3 \longrightarrow T_1$ |
| $w_3(A), w_1(A)$ | $T_3$ writes, then $T_1$ writes | $T_3 \longrightarrow T_1$ |

**Step 3:** Unique edges (after removing duplicates):

- $T_2 \longrightarrow T_1$
- $T_2 \longrightarrow T_3$
- $T_3 \longrightarrow T_1$

**Step 4:** Draw the precedence graph.

```
T2 ---> T3
 |        |
 v        v
T1 <-----+
```

**Step 5:** Check for cycles. Path: $T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$. No back-edge. Path: $T_2 \to T_1$. No back-edge. **No cycle detected.**

**Step 6:** Conclusion: $S$ **IS conflict serializable**.

**Step 7:** Topological sort: $T_2$ has in-degree 0. Start with $T_2$. Then $T_3$. Then $T_1$. **Equivalent serial schedule: $T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$.**

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Classify the schedule as Recoverable, Cascadeless, or Strict. Justify.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recoverable Check:**

A schedule is recoverable if no committed transaction has read data written by an uncommitted transaction. In schedule $S$:
- $T_1$ reads $A$ (written by $T_2$) and $T_2$ commits before $T_1$ commits. ✓
- $T_1$ reads $A$ (written by $T_3$) and $T_3$ commits before $T_1$ commits. ✓
- $T_3$ reads $A$ (written by $T_2$) and $T_2$ commits before $T_3$ commits. ✓

The commit order is $c_1, c_2, c_3$. Every read-from relationship is resolved before the reader commits. **Therefore, $S$ is RECOVERABLE.**

**Step 2 — Cascadeless Check:**

A schedule is cascadeless if no transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction. In $S$:
- $T_3$ reads $A$ at a point where $T_2$ has written $A$ but not yet committed (the $c_2$ appears after $r_3(A)$ and $w_3(A)$).
- $T_1$ reads $A$ at a point where $T_2$ has written $A$ but not yet committed.

**Therefore, $S$ is NOT Cascadeless.**

**Step 3 — Strict Check:**

A strict schedule prohibits reading OR writing uncommitted data. Since $T_1$ reads $A$ written by $T_2$ before $T_2$ commits, **$S$ is NOT Strict.**

**Final Classification:** $S$ is **Recoverable** but **not Cascadeless** and **not Strict**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Explain the ACID properties with a real-world example and discuss the types of failures that can occur in a database system. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain each ACID property with the help of a banking transaction example.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Consider a transaction $T$: "Transfer ₹10,000 from Account A to Account B."

**Step 1 — Atomicity:**

The transaction has two operations:
- Debit Account A by ₹10,000.
- Credit Account B by ₹10,000.

Atomicity guarantees that both operations happen together or neither happens. If the system crashes after the debit but before the credit, the DBMS automatically rolls back the debit, restoring Account A to its original balance. **Either both succeed or both fail.**

**Step 2 — Consistency:**

Consistency ensures that the sum of balances in both accounts remains constant. Before the transaction: $\text{Balance}(A) + \text{Balance}(B) = X$. After the transaction: $\text{Balance}(A) - 10000 + \text{Balance}(B) + 10000 = X$. The integrity constraint "total balance is preserved" is maintained. If any constraint (e.g., minimum balance) is violated, the transaction is rejected.

**Step 3 — Isolation:**

If another transaction $T_2$ is running concurrently (e.g., checking the balance of Account A), $T_2$ should not see the intermediate state where A is debited but B is not yet credited. $T_2$ either sees the state before $T$ starts or the state after $T$ completes. **This is achieved via locking or multiversion concurrency control.**

**Step 4 — Durability:**

Once $T$ commits, the new balances (A reduced by 10,000; B increased by 10,000) are **permanently** written to the database. Even if the system crashes 1 second or 1 year later, the changes survive. This is enforced by writing the changes to the log file and forcing them to non-volatile storage before acknowledging the commit.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the various types of failures a DBMS must handle.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Transaction Failure (Local Error):**

Caused by logical errors in the transaction itself (e.g., division by zero, integrity constraint violation, bad input). The DBMS rolls back the transaction.

**Step 2 — System Crash (Soft Failure):**

Caused by power outage, hardware fault, or OS bug. The in-memory data (buffer) is lost, but the disk data survives. The recovery subsystem uses the log to **UNDO uncommitted transactions** and **REDO committed transactions**.

**Step 3 — Disk Failure (Hard Failure):**

Caused by head crash, disk damage, or sector failure. Some disk blocks lose their contents. Recovery involves **restoring from an archival backup** and **re-applying the log** up to the point of failure.

**Step 4 — Catastrophic Failure:**

Fire, flood, earthquake, or sabotage destroys the entire database facility. Recovery requires **off-site backup archives** and a **cold/warm standby site**. This is why mission-critical banks maintain geographically distributed backups.

**Step 5 — Concurrency Control Failure:**

The DBMS detects that the current schedule violates serializability (e.g., through a deadlock detection algorithm) and **aborts one or more transactions** to maintain database consistency.

**Step 6 — Summary Table:**

| Failure Type | Scope | Recovery Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Local Error | Single transaction | ROLLBACK |
| System Crash | Main memory lost | Log-based UNDO/REDO |
| Disk Failure | Disk blocks lost | Archive + Log Replay |
| Catastrophic | Entire system | Off-site backup restore |

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- [ ] **Transaction Definition:** Logical unit of work, all-or-nothing execution.
- [ ] **Four Operations:** read_item, write_item, commit, rollback.
- [ ] **Transaction States:** Active → Partially Committed → Committed OR Active → Failed → Aborted → Terminated.
- [ ] **ACID Properties:** Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. ALL FOUR are required.
- [ ] **Atomicity** is the responsibility of the **Recovery Manager** (UNDO).
- [ ] **Isolation** is the responsibility of the **Concurrency Control Subsystem** (Locks/Timestamps).
- [ ] **Durability** is the responsibility of the **Recovery Manager** (REDO + Log).
- [ ] **Consistency** is the joint responsibility of the **programmer** and the **DBMS constraint checker**.
- [ ] **Failure Types (5):** System Crash, Transaction Error, Local Error, Disk Failure, Catastrophic Failure.
- [ ] **Recoverability Hierarchy:** Strict ⊂ Cascadeless ⊂ Recoverable.
- [ ] **Conflict Types (3):** Read-Write, Write-Read, Write-Write. (Read-Read is NOT a conflict.)
- [ ] **Precedence Graph:** Acyclic ⇒ Conflict Serializable; Cyclic ⇒ NOT Conflict Serializable.
- [ ] **Topological Sort:** Gives equivalent serial order when the graph is acyclic.
- [ ] **View Serializability:** Broader than conflict serializability. Test for view equivalence using 3 conditions: Initial Read, Read-From, Final Write.
- [ ] **Hierarchy:** Conflict Serializable ⊂ View Serializable ⊂ All Serializable.
- [ ] **Blind Writes:** Cause schedules to be view serializable but NOT conflict serializable.
- [ ] **Number of Serial Schedules for $n$ transactions:** $n!$.
- [ ] **Exam Trap:** A schedule can be "Recoverable" but "Not Conflict Serializable" — these classifications are independent.
- [ ] **In SQL:** Use `BEGIN TRANSACTION`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK` to mark transaction boundaries.
- [ ] **Recovery Sub-system** uses a **System Log** stored on non-volatile disk for both UNDO and REDO.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrency Control with Two-Phase Locking Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concurrency Control with Two-Phase Locking Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Concurrency Control** is the set of techniques employed by the Database Management System (DBMS) to coordinate concurrent transactions accessing shared data so that the overall execution preserves the ACID properties — particularly **Atomicity**, **Consistency**, and **Isolation** — while simultaneously exploiting inter-transaction parallelism to maximise system throughput.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a concurrency control protocol that guarantees **conflict-serializability** of transactions. It enforces a rule that every transaction issues all of its lock requests *before* it issues any unlock request, thereby partitioning the transaction's execution into a **Growing Phase** (acquiring locks) and a **Shrinking Phase** (releasing locks).

The protocol was first proposed by Eswaran, Gray, Lorie, and Traiger (1976) and remains the most widely deployed serializability mechanism in commercial database systems (e.g., the lock manager subsystem of DB2, Oracle, and SQL Server).

## 1.2 Lock Granularity Vocabulary (KTU Syllabus Lock-in)

- **Lock** — A synchronisation variable associated with a data item that mediates access.
- **Shared Lock (S / Read Lock)** — Permits concurrent *read* access; multiple transactions may simultaneously hold an S lock on the same item.
- **Exclusive Lock (X / Write Lock)** — Permits only the holding transaction to *read and modify* the item; all other locks are blocked.
- **Lock Manager** — The central arbiter subsystem that maintains the lock table and grants/denies requests.
- **Lock Compatibility Matrix** — A binary truth table dictating which pairs of lock modes may co-exist on the same data item.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Washroom Key System

Imagine a corporate office with **one** washroom and **two kinds of employees**: *Readers* (employees who only need to look at the washroom mirror) and *Editors* (employees who need to re-tile the washroom floor).

- A *Reader* takes a **Shared (S) key** — multiple Readers can be inside at the same time, but no Editor can enter.
- An *Editor* needs an **Exclusive (X) key** — the moment an Editor is inside, the door is bolted; *no one* else (Reader or Editor) may enter.
- The **Two-Phase Locking rule** says: each employee must collect *all* the keys they will ever need **before** they return any key to the rack. Once an employee returns one key, they can no longer collect another.

This single rule magically prevents the chaos of two employees simultaneously re-tiling the same floor tile — the *Lost Update* problem — because the second Editor simply waits in a queue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Two-Phase"?** The phrase captures the bipartite lifecycle: a transaction behaves as if it were composed of two non-overlapping phases. It does **not** mean "two lock operations."

## 1.4 The Problems 2PL Is Designed to Solve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a multi-user DBMS, three classic anomalies arise when transactions interleave without coordination. KTU examiners **love** asking for the demonstration of these:

1. **Lost Update Problem (Write-Write Conflict)** — Two transactions read the same item, both compute a new value based on the old value, and the second write *overwrites* the first update.
2. **Uncommitted Dependency / Dirty Read (Write-Read Conflict)** — A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted concurrent transaction, which then rolls back.
3. **Inconsistent Retrieval / Non-Repeatable Read (Read-Write Conflict)** — A transaction reads the same item twice and gets different values because another transaction modified it in between.

> [!NOTE]
> Two-Phase Locking **eliminates all three** anomalies and produces **conflict-serializable** schedules. The trade-off: it can introduce **deadlock** and does not by itself prevent **starvation**.

## 1.5 Standardised Metrics & Notation

- **$n$** — Number of concurrent transactions.
- **$T_i$** — The $i^{th}$ transaction in the schedule.
- **$D_i$** — A data item accessed by transaction $T_i$.
- **$LS$** — Lock granularity (tuple, page, table, database).
- **Throughput** — Measured in **transactions per second (TPS)**. Typical production OLTP targets are **≥ 1,000 TPS**.
- **Mean time to deadlock detection** — typically in the range **10 ms – 100 ms** for centralised lock managers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Lock Modes Used in 2PL

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **lock compatibility matrix**. The three modes are:

1. **Shared Lock (S)** — Acquired for **READ** operations.
2. **Exclusive Lock (X)** — Acquired for **WRITE** operations.
3. **(Optional, for completeness) Update Lock (U)** — An intermediate mode in some commercial systems (e.g., MS SQL Server) to prevent *lock conversion deadlocks*. Not part of classical 2PL but often asked as a 3-mark question.

## 2.2 Lock Compatibility Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

A lock request by transaction $T_j$ on an item already locked by $T_i$ is granted **iff** the pair is compatible.

| Held by $T_i$ \ Requested by $T_j$ | S | X |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | ✓ Compatible | ✗ Conflict |
| **X** | ✗ Conflict | ✗ Conflict |

> [!NOTE]
> Symbol mapping used in KTU answer scripts: ✓ ⇒ `Yes / True / 1`, ✗ ⇒ `No / False / 0`.

In matrix form, let $C(T_i, T_j) = 1$ denote compatibility:

$$C(T_i, T_j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (L_{T_i}, L_{T_j}) \in \{(\text{NL,NL}), (\text{NL,S}), (\text{NL,X}), (\text{S,NL}), (\text{S,S}), (\text{X,NL})\} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\text{NL}$ denotes *no lock* held.

## 2.3 The Two-Phase Locking Rule — Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Eswaran et al., 1976):** *A schedule produced by a set of transactions each obeying the 2PL rule is **conflict-serializable**.*

A transaction $T$ is in **2PL** if and only if every lock operation issued by $T$ **precedes** the first unlock operation issued by $T$. Equivalently, $T$ executes in two non-overlapping phases:

### Phase 1 — **The Growing Phase**
- $T$ may acquire locks (`lock-S(item)` or `lock-X(item)`).
- $T$ **cannot** release any lock.
- $T$ may upgrade a held S-lock to an X-lock (subject to the upgrade protocol — see §2.6).

### Phase 2 — **The Shrinking Phase**
- $T$ may release locks (`unlock(item)`).
- $T$ **cannot** acquire any *new* lock.
- Once entered, this phase is *monotonic* — a transaction never re-enters the Growing Phase.

Formally, if we let $L_k$ denote the $k^{th}$ lock operation (acquire or release) issued by $T$:

$$\text{Let } L_p = \text{first unlock}, \quad L_q = \text{last lock-acquire}.$$

Then the 2PL condition is:

$$q \leq p \quad \text{(with strict inequality for Basic 2PL)}$$

Equivalently, defining the **lock point** $LP(T)$ as the chronological instant when $T$ acquires its **final lock**:

$$LP(T) = \min\{t \mid \forall t' > t,\ \text{no new locks are acquired by } T\}$$

The **serializability order** of a 2PL schedule is given by ordering the lock points of all participating transactions in increasing chronological order.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | KTU Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C(L_i, L_j)$ | Compatibility function $\in \{0,1\}$ | 3-mark "draw the matrix" question |
| $LP(T_i)$ | Lock point of $T_i$ | Derive serializability order |
| $\#\text{Locks}_{\text{growth}}(T)$ | Number of locks acquired in growing phase | Verify 2PL compliance |
| $\#\text{Unlocks}_{\text{shrink}}(T)$ | Number of locks released in shrinking phase | Verify 2PL compliance |
| $\text{Lock escalation threshold}$ | Typically **\textasciitilde 5{,}000 row-locks} | Real-world tuning question |
| $P_{\text{deadlock}}$ | Probability of deadlock $\approx \frac{k(k-1)}{2N^2}$ where $k$ = locked items, $N$ = items in DB | Why deadlock detection is needed |

> [!NOTE]
> Avoid writing vertical bars in your answer-script tables. Use $\mid D_i \mid$ instead of $\mid D_i \mid$ when describing item cardinality.

## 2.5 Variants of 2PL (KTU Repeated Question)

KTU examiners routinely award 7 marks for distinguishing the four variants. Memorise the table below.

| Variant | When can $T$ write to unlocked items? | Can $T$ read after writing? | Prevents Cascading Abort? | Prevents Dirty Read? | Schedules Produced |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Basic 2PL** | Yes, immediately after lock-X is released | Yes | ✗ | ✗ | Conflict-serializable |
| **Conservative (Static) 2PL** | $T$ must pre-declare *all* locks **before** start | Yes (locks held till end) | ✓ | ✓ | Conflict-serializable, **deadlock-free** |
| **Strict 2PL** | Only after $T$ *commits* or *aborts* | Yes (S-locks held till end) | ✓ | ✓ | Recoverable + serializable |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | Only after $T$ *commits* or *aborts* | Only after commit | ✓ | ✓ | Recoverable + serializable, also ordered |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conservative 2PL** is deadlock-free but is **impractical** because transactions must know their entire read/write set in advance — usually impossible in ad-hoc SQL workloads. **Strict 2PL** is the *de facto* industrial standard (used in DB2, InnoDB).

## 2.6 Lock Conversion and the Lock-Upgrade Rule

A transaction that holds an S-lock on item $D$ and then issues an `upgrade(D)` to obtain an X-lock is performing **lock conversion**. The rule under 2PL:

$$\text{upgrade}(D) \text{ is permitted iff no other transaction holds an S-lock on } D$$

A blind *bulk* upgrade policy is a common source of **lock-upgrade deadlocks** — a guaranteed 7-mark question if asked.

## 2.7 Why 2PL May Still Fail — Deadlock and Starvation

> [!WARNING]
> **2PL guarantees conflict-serializability, but NOT freedom from deadlock or starvation.** KTU frequently tests this nuance.

- **Deadlock** arises under 2PL when two transactions hold locks the other needs, e.g., $T_1$ holds $A$, waits for $B$; $T_2$ holds $B$, waits for $A$.
- **Starvation** arises when a transaction waits indefinitely because lock requests keep getting preempted by higher-priority transactions.

**Detection vs. Prevention:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | KTU Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wait-Die (non-preemptive)** | Older transaction waits; younger is *aborted* (dies) | Avoids starvation; may abort unnecessarily |
| **Wound-Wait (preemptive)** | Older transaction *wounds* (rolls back) the younger; younger waits if older waits | Lower abort count; still no starvation |
| **Timeout-based** | Abort any transaction waiting > $\tau$ seconds | Simple but unfair |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OLTP banking systems** use **Strict 2PL with row-level granularity** to safely process millions of concurrent debits/credits.
- **Distributed 2PL (2PC + 2PL)** forms the concurrency substrate of distributed transaction systems like XA-compliant application servers.
- **Lock-free alternatives (MVCC)** in PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB bypass 2PL for read-heavy workloads, but 2PL remains the gold standard for **write-heavy** correctness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying 2PL Compliance and Deriving the Serial Order

### Problem Statement

Consider the following schedule $S$ of two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ on data items $A$ and $B$. Verify whether $S$ obeys 2PL, and if so, derive its conflict-serializable order.

| Time | $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | `lock-S(A)` | |
| 2 | `read(A)` | |
| 3 | `lock-X(B)` | |
| 4 | | `lock-S(A)` *(waits — $T_1$ already holds S, S+S compatible → granted)* |
| 5 | | `read(A)` |
| 6 | `write(B)` | |
| 7 | `unlock(A)` | |
| 8 | | `lock-X(B)` *(waits — $T_1$ still holds X)* |
| 9 | `unlock(B)` | |
| 10 | | `write(B)` |
| 11 | | `unlock(A)` |
| 12 | | `unlock(B)` |

### Step 1 — Identify the Lock Point of Each Transaction

- **$T_1$ acquires locks at times 1 and 3.** The last acquire is at time 3 → $LP(T_1) = 3$.
- **$T_2$ acquires locks at times 4, 8, and 10.** But wait — at time 7, $T_1$ unlocks $A$. Is $T_2$ still in its growing phase? At time 8, $T_2$ is requesting a *new* lock-X on $B$, which it did not previously hold. Since $T_1$ has already released a lock, $T_2$ has *entered* its shrinking phase only if it has itself released a lock. $T_2$ has not released any lock yet at time 8. So $T_2$ is still in its growing phase at time 8. Thus the last acquire is at time 10.

$$LP(T_1) = 3, \quad LP(T_2) = 10$$

### Step 2 — Verify 2PL Condition

- $T_1$: locks at 1, 3; unlock at 7. Since unlock(7) > last-acquire(3), $T_1$ satisfies 2PL. ✓
- $T_2$: locks at 4, 8, 10; no unlock before 10. The first unlock is at 11 > 10, so $T_2$ satisfies 2PL. ✓

### Step 3 — Derive the Equivalent Serial Order

By the **Lock-Point Theorem**, transactions are equivalent to a serial order matching their chronological lock points:

$$LP(T_1) = 3 < LP(T_2) = 10 \implies S \equiv T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \text{ (serial)}$$

$$\boxed{S \text{ is conflict-serializable, equivalent to } T_1 \prec T_2.}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Detecting a 2PL Violation

### Problem Statement

Schedule $S'$ of $T_3$ and $T_4$:

| Time | $T_3$ | $T_4$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | `lock-X(A)` | |
| 2 | `read(A)` | |
| 3 | `unlock(A)` | |
| 4 | | `lock-S(A)` |
| 5 | `lock-X(B)` | |
| 6 | `read(B)` | |
| 7 | `unlock(B)` | |

### Step-by-Step Verification

For $T_3$:

- Acquire: 1, 5
- First unlock: 3
- Last acquire: 5

Check: $5 > 3$? **Yes** — $T_3$ performed a lock acquisition *after* issuing an unlock.

$$\therefore T_3 \text{ violates 2PL at time 5 (lock-X(B) after unlock(A) at time 3).}$$

For $T_4$:

- $T_4$ issues one acquire at time 4, no unlock. ✓

### Consequence

Because $T_3$ violated 2PL, we cannot use the lock-point theorem to assert serializability. Construct the **precedence graph**:

- Edge $T_4 \rightarrow T_3$? Yes, $T_4$ reads $A$ (at time 4) *after* $T_3$ wrote $A$ (at time 2). Conflict: write-read on $A$.
- Edge $T_3 \rightarrow T_4$? No — $T_3$ and $T_4$ do not conflict on $B$ ($T_4$ never touches $B$).

Graph has no cycle → $S'$ is still conflict-serializable! **But this is incidental** — 2PL was violated, so the guarantee is lost. KTU would deduct marks if you claim 2PL is satisfied here.

> [!NOTE]
> 2PL is **sufficient** for conflict-serializability, not **necessary**. Some non-2PL schedules are also serializable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of 2PL Variants

Construct a $4 \times 5$ decision matrix a KTU student can reproduce from memory:

| Property | Basic 2PL | Conservative 2PL | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Conflict-serializable | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Recoverable | Not guaranteed | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Cascadeless | Not guaranteed | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Deadlock-free | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Requires pre-declaration | No | Yes | No | No |

**Logical chain for Strict 2PL dominance:**

$$\text{Strict 2PL} \Rightarrow \text{X-locks held until commit/abort} \Rightarrow \text{No dirty read} \Rightarrow \text{Recoverable} + \text{Cascadeless} \wedge \text{Serializable}.$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Lock-Point Serialization Theorem

**Claim:** *If every transaction in $S$ obeys 2PL, the precedence graph $P(S)$ is acyclic.*

**Proof outline (Board-style, 7 marks):**

1. Let $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ be a precedence edge, meaning $T_j$ reads/writes a data item last written/read by $T_i$.
2. For $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ to exist, $T_i$ must *release* a lock on some item $D$ before $T_j$ *acquires* a conflicting lock on $D$.
3. By 2PL, $T_i$'s release implies $T_i$ is in its shrinking phase → $LP(T_i)$ occurred earlier than the release.
4. Similarly, $T_j$'s acquire of the conflicting lock must occur during $T_j$'s growing phase → it occurs *before* $LP(T_j)$.
5. Combining (3) and (4): $LP(T_i) < LP(T_j)$.
6. Therefore, edges induce a strict total order on lock points, which is acyclic.

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, } P(S) \text{ is acyclic, and } S \text{ is conflict-serializable.}}$$

---

## 3.5 Deadlock Probability Derivation (Informal)

For a database with $N$ items, if each transaction randomly locks $k$ distinct items, the probability that two transactions share a common item (and hence are at risk of deadlock cycles) is:

$$P_{\text{conflict-pair}} \approx 1 - \frac{\binom{N-k}{k}}{\binom{N}{k}}$$

For small $k$ relative to $N$:

$$P_{\text{conflict-pair}} \approx \frac{k^2}{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This heuristic appears in the official KTU recommended text (Elmasri & Navathe, 7th Ed., Ch. 21) and is safe to reproduce in exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Two-Phase Locking Lifecycle (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Transaction Begins]) --> nodeGP{Growing Phase}
    nodeGP --> nodeLockS[Acquire Shared Lock S]
    nodeGP --> nodeLockX[Acquire Exclusive Lock X]
    nodeGP --> nodeUp[Lock Upgrade S to X]
    nodeLockS --> nodeLP{Lock Point Reached?}
    nodeLockX --> nodeLP
    nodeUp --> nodeLP
    nodeLP -->|No| nodeGP
    nodeLP -->|Yes - first unlock issued| nodeSP{Shrinking Phase}
    nodeSP --> nodeUnS[Release S Lock]
    nodeSP --> nodeUnX[Release X Lock]
    nodeUnS --> nodeCommit{All Locks Released?}
    nodeUnX --> nodeCommit
    nodeCommit -->|No| nodeSP
    nodeCommit -->|Yes| nodeEnd([Transaction Commits or Aborts])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Lock Point* is the instant of transition from the Growing Phase to the Shrinking Phase. It is a logical instant, not a clock tick.

## 4.2 Lock Manager Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transaction Layer]
        nodeT1[Transaction T1]
        nodeT2[Transaction T2]
        nodeT3[Transaction T3]
    end
    subgraph LM[Lock Manager Subsystem]
        nodeReq[Lock Request Queue]
        nodeComp[Compatibility Checker]
        nodeTab[Lock Table - Hash Map]
        nodeWait[Wait Queue per Data Item]
    end
    subgraph DD[Data Disk]
        nodeD1[Data Item A]
        nodeD2[Data Item B]
        nodeD3[Data Item C]
    end
    nodeT1 --> nodeReq
    nodeT2 --> nodeReq
    nodeT3 --> nodeReq
    nodeReq --> nodeComp
    nodeComp --> nodeTab
    nodeComp --> nodeWait
    nodeTab --> nodeD1
    nodeTab --> nodeD2
    nodeTab --> nodeD3
```

**Component responsibilities:**

- **Lock Request Queue:** FIFO buffer of all pending `lock-S` and `lock-X` requests.
- **Compatibility Checker:** Consults the compatibility matrix in O(1) time.
- **Lock Table:** Hash map `data-item → {mode, owner-list, wait-queue}`.
- **Wait Queue:** Per-item FIFO queue of blocked transactions; integrated with the **deadlock detector** which periodically builds a wait-for graph.

## 4.3 Wait-For Graph and Deadlock Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1((T1)) --holds A, waits for B--> nodeT2((T2))
    nodeT2((T2)) --holds B, waits for A--> nodeT1((T1))
    nodeT3((T3)) --holds C, no wait--> nodeDONE([Independent])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **cycle** in the wait-for graph (e.g., the edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ above) is the **necessary and sufficient condition** for a deadlock under 2PL. The deadlock detector runs the **Tarjan SCC algorithm** in $O(V+E)$ time.

## 4.4 Precedence Graph of a 2PL-Compliant Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1((T1 - LP=3)) --> nodeT2((T2 - LP=10))
    nodeT2((T2 - LP=10)) --> nodeT3((T3 - LP=15))
```

> [!NOTE]
> Edges in the precedence graph are induced by conflicts. Under 2PL, lock points induce a **topological order** — no back-edges — so the graph is a DAG.

## 4.5 Variant Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ{Does T declare all locks up front?}
    nodeQ -->|Yes| nodeC[Conservative 2PL - Deadlock Free]
    nodeQ -->|No| nodeQ2{Does T hold X-locks till commit?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeS[Strict 2PL - Industry Default]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ3{Does T hold ALL locks till commit?}
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeR[Rigorous 2PL]
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeB[Basic 2PL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define Two-Phase Locking. Identify its two phases and explain the role of the lock point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — key bullets):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** 2PL is a concurrency control protocol in which every transaction issues all its lock requests before issuing any unlock request, ensuring conflict-serializability.
- **Two phases (1 mark):**
  - *Growing Phase* — transaction may acquire locks, no unlocking.
  - *Shrinking Phase* — transaction may release locks, no new acquisitions.
- **Lock Point (1 mark):** The instant when the transaction acquires its final lock; the moment of phase transition. Lock points induce a serializability order: $T_i \prec T_j$ iff $LP(T_i) < LP(T_j)$.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Shared (S) and Exclusive (X) locks with a suitable compatibility matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **S Lock (1 mark):** Read-only lock. Multiple transactions may simultaneously hold S-locks on the same item.
- **X Lock (1 mark):** Read-write lock. Only one transaction can hold the X-lock; no other S or X lock is permitted concurrently.
- **Matrix (1 mark):**

| Held \ Requested | S | X |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | Yes | No |
| **X** | No | No |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing *Strict 2PL* with *Rigorous 2PL*. Strict = X-locks held to commit. Rigorous = *all* locks (S and X) held to commit.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting that 2PL does *not* prevent deadlocks — only Conservative 2PL does.
> - **Mistake 3:** Claiming 2PL guarantees *recoverability*. It does not. Basic 2PL may produce non-recoverable schedules.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mark the *lock point* in a schedule diagram — the lock point is the visual key to determining serializability order.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the Two-Phase Locking protocol in detail. With suitable examples, differentiate between Basic 2PL, Conservative 2PL, and Strict 2PL.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the following schedule $S$. Is it conflict-serializable? Is it 2PL-compliant? If not, suggest the minimum modification to make it 2PL-compliant.*

| Time | $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | `lock-S(A)` | |
| 2 | `read(A)` | |
| 3 | | `lock-S(A)` |
| 4 | | `read(A)` |
| 5 | `unlock(A)` | |
| 6 | `lock-X(B)` | |
| 7 | | `lock-X(B)` |
| 8 | `read(B)` | |
| 9 | `write(B)` | |
| 10 | `unlock(B)` | |
| 11 | | `unlock(A)` |
| 12 | | `read(B)` |
| 13 | | `write(B)` |
| 14 | | `unlock(B)` |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. 2PL Definition (2 marks):** Already covered in §1.1. Write the bipartite phase rule.

**2. Variants (5 marks):**

- **Basic 2PL** — Locks may be released as soon as the transaction has issued a write. **Drawback:** May produce cascading aborts and non-recoverable schedules. (1.5 marks)
- **Conservative 2PL** — Transaction acquires *all* locks at the start. No deadlock possible. **Drawback:** Impractical — requires a priori knowledge of the full read/write set. (1.5 marks)
- **Strict 2PL** — X-locks are held until commit/abort, S-locks may be released earlier. Cascadeless and recoverable. **Used in commercial systems.** (2 marks)

Provide a schedule diagram for each (sample illustrative schedules in §2.5 can be referenced).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify lock points (2 marks):**

- $T_1$: acquires locks at times 1, 6; first unlock at time 5. Since $6 > 5$, **$T_1$ has acquired a lock AFTER releasing one.**
- $T_2$: acquires locks at times 3, 7, 12, 13; first unlock at time 11. Last acquire is at 13, which is *after* unlock at 11.

**Both transactions violate 2PL.**

**Step 2 — Precedence Graph (2 marks):**

- $T_1$ writes $B$ at time 9; $T_2$ reads $B$ at time 12 and writes $B$ at time 13. So $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ on $B$. (1 mark)
- $T_1$ reads $A$ at time 2; $T_2$ reads $A$ at time 4. Read-read — **no conflict edge**. (1 mark)

$$\text{Graph: } T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \Rightarrow \text{acyclic, so } S \text{ is conflict-serializable.}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Modification for 2PL Compliance (3 marks):**

Move the `unlock(A)` of $T_1$ from time 5 to **after** time 6, and delay $T_2$'s `unlock(A)` to after its last acquire (time 13). In practice:

- $T_1$: defer `unlock(A)` until after `lock-X(B)`. New unlock-A at time $\geq 9$.
- $T_2$: defer all unlocks until time $\geq 14$.

After modification, $T_1$ last-acquires at 6, first-unlocks at $\geq 7$ → ✓. $T_2$ last-acquires at 13, first-unlocks at 14 → ✓.

**Final Answer:** *Schedule is conflict-serializable (order $T_1 \prec T_2$) but violates 2PL; defer all unlocks until after the last lock acquisition to restore compliance.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** *What is a lock compatibility matrix? Construct the matrix for shared and exclusive locks and explain how it is used by the lock manager.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the Wait-Die and Wound-Wait deadlock prevention schemes. Apply both schemes to the following scenario: $T_5$ (older) holds lock on $A$ and requests lock on $B$; $T_6$ (younger) holds lock on $B$ and requests lock on $A$. State which transaction is aborted in each scheme.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** A compatibility matrix $C$ is a $|M| \times |M|$ table where $M$ is the set of lock modes; entry $C(L_a, L_b) = 1$ if a transaction can hold $L_a$ on an item while another holds $L_b$, else 0.

**2. Construction (2 marks):** Draw the table from §2.2.

**3. Lock Manager Usage (3 marks):** When a transaction $T_j$ issues `lock-M(D)`, the lock manager:
- (i) Consults the lock table for current mode on $D$ (1 mark).
- (ii) Looks up $C(\text{current-mode}, M)$ in O(1) (1 mark).
- (iii) If 1, grants the lock; if 0, appends $T_j$ to the wait queue for $D$ and blocks $T_j$ (1 mark).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**1. Wait-Die Scheme (3 marks):** *Non-preemptive* — when a younger transaction requests a lock held by an older one, the younger transaction is *aborted* ("dies"). Older transactions never wait for younger ones.
- $T_5$ (older) requests $B$ held by $T_6$ (younger) → $T_5$ **waits**.
- $T_6$ (younger) requests $A$ held by $T_5$ (older) → $T_6$ **dies** (aborted).

**Result:** $T_6$ is rolled back, $T_5$ proceeds.

**2. Wound-Wait Scheme (3 marks):** *Preemptive* — when a younger transaction holds a lock requested by an older one, the younger is *wounded* (aborted). When an older transaction requests a lock held by a younger one, the older waits.
- $T_5$ (older) requests $B$ held by $T_6$ (younger) → $T_5$ waits, but $T_6$ is **wounded**.
- $T_6$ (younger) requests $A$ held by $T_5$ (older) → $T_6$ **waits**.

**Result:** $T_6$ is wounded and rolled back, $T_5$ proceeds.

**3. Comparative remark (1 mark):** Both schemes prevent starvation because transaction *age* is monotonically non-decreasing. Wait-Die tends to abort more transactions (younger victims); Wound-Wait aborts fewer but may waste more work already done by the younger transaction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield bullet points — reproduce in your last-minute revision sheet:**

- [ ] **2PL = Growing Phase + Shrinking Phase.** A transaction may *acquire* locks in the growing phase and *release* locks in the shrinking phase. Crossing is forbidden.
- [ ] **Lock Point** = the moment of phase transition; **serializability order** = order of lock points.
- [ ] **2PL guarantees conflict-serializability** (sufficient, not necessary).
- [ ] **2PL does NOT guarantee** freedom from **deadlock** or **starvation**.
- [ ] **Conservative 2PL** is the *only* deadlock-free variant — impractical in SQL because it requires pre-declaration of the full read/write set.
- [ ] **Strict 2PL** is the **industry standard** (DB2, InnoDB); it holds **X-locks** until commit/abort, eliminating cascading aborts.
- [ ] **Rigorous 2PL** holds **all** locks (S and X) until commit; even stricter.
- [ ] **Lock Compatibility:** S+S = Yes, S+X = No, X+X = No.
- [ ] **Lock-upgrade** is allowed only when no other transaction holds an S-lock; naive upgrades cause **deadlock cycles**.
- [ ] **Wait-Die:** older waits, younger dies. **Wound-Wait:** older wounds younger, older may wait for younger.
- [ ] **Deadlock detection** uses a **wait-for graph**; a cycle is necessary and sufficient for deadlock.
- [ ] **Lock granularity** — DB → Table → Page → Row → Tuple. Fine granularity ↑ concurrency ↓ lock-management overhead.
- [ ] **Two-phase locking rule** is a *theorem* (Eswaran, Gray, Lorie, Traiger — 1976), not just a heuristic. State this in 14-mark answers for examiner brownie points.
- [ ] **2PL is recoverable?** ❌ — only *Strict* and *Rigorous* 2PL are recoverable; Basic 2PL is not.
- [ ] **Phantom problem** is *not* solved by 2PL alone — needs **predicate locking** or **index locking**. (Frequent 3-mark trap question.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Recovery Management: Deferred Update, Immediate Update, and Shadow Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Database Recovery Management: Deferred Update, Immediate Update, and Shadow Paging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Database Recovery Management: Deferred Update, Immediate Update, and Shadow Paging

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Database Recovery?

**Database Recovery** is the process of restoring the database to a consistent state after a failure (transaction, system, or media). A *recovery manager* is a DBMS component that, using information recorded in the **system log**, ensures the **ACID** properties — especially **Atomicity** and **Durability** — are preserved even in the presence of crashes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Recovery techniques are classified into three families:
> **(i) Deferred Update**, **(ii) Immediate Update**, and **(iii) Shadow Paging**.
> All three are log/no-log based strategies evaluated against the cost metrics:
> **Ease of implementation, overhead, and recovery time.**

### 1.2 The Three Recovery Strategies — At a Glance

| Strategy | When is the DB updated? | Log Required? | REDO | UNDO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deferred Update** | After the transaction commits | Yes (REDO-only log) | Yes | No |
| **Immediate Update** | Before the transaction commits | Yes (UNDO/REDO log) | Yes | Yes |
| **Shadow Paging** | On a private page table (copy-on-commit) | Optional | No | No |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Ledger

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are a bank teller.
> - **Deferred Update** is like writing all the day's transactions on a *chit* first, and only after verifying the chit, you actually mark the **master passbook**. If the electricity goes out while writing the chit, the passbook is untouched — nothing to undo.
> - **Immediate Update** is like editing the master passbook *as customers arrive*. If the power fails mid-edit, you must trace your steps (UNDO) and then re-apply verified edits (REDO).
> - **Shadow Paging** is like keeping the *original passbook* on the desk and writing on a *photocopy*. When everything is correct, you replace the original with the photocopy in one atomic swap.

### 1.4 The Role of the System Log

The **System Log (or Journal)** is a sequential, append-only file stored on stable storage. Each log record contains:

$$
\text{Log Record} = \langle \text{LSN}, \, \text{TransactionID}, \, \text{Action}, \, \text{BeforeImage}, \, \text{AfterImage} \rangle
$$

where **LSN** = Log Sequence Number, an ever-increasing identifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** A safe DBMS guarantees that at least the **commit marker** of a transaction reaches stable storage before the transaction is declared successful — this is the **Durability** contract of **ACID**.

### 1.5 Failure Types the Recovery Manager Must Handle

1. **Transaction Failure** — logical error (bad input) or system error (deadlock victim).
2. **System Crash** — power failure, OS panic. *Main memory is lost, disk survives.*
3. **Disk Failure** — head crash, media damage. Requires **archival backups + log replay**.
4. **Catastrophe** — fire, flood. Requires **off-site redundancy** (RAID, mirrored sites).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Protocol

Before discussing the three strategies, we must fix one foundational rule used by *all log-based* techniques:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WAL Rule (Write-Ahead Logging):** Before any data item `X` is modified on disk, the **log record containing the new value of X** must already be written to **stable storage**.

This single rule is what makes crash recovery mathematically possible.

## 2.2 Strategy 1 — Deferred Update (NO-UNDO/REDO)

### 2.2.1 Core Idea
The database is **never updated on disk** until the transaction **commits**. All updates are accumulated in a *private transaction workspace* and a *REDO-only log*. The actual disk write is **deferred** to commit time.

### 2.2.2 Operation Steps
1. Transaction `Ti` begins → write `<Ti START>` to log.
2. For each write `W_item(X, new_value)`:
   - Append `<Ti, X, new_value>` to log (REDO entry).
   - **Do not** write X to the database on disk.
3. On commit:
   - Write `<Ti COMMIT>` to log.
   - Force-log the commit (WAL rule).
   - Now read the log and **REDO** all updates of `Ti` into the database.
4. On crash:
   - If `<Ti COMMIT>` is **not** in the log → do nothing (transaction was incomplete).
   - If `<Ti COMMIT>` **is** in the log → REDO all operations of `Ti` in log order.

### 2.2.3 Why "Deferred"?
Because the update of the actual database is **postponed (deferred)** until the transaction has reached its commit point. Until then, the on-disk database is unchanged.

### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** No UNDO needed (simpler recovery). The on-disk DB never holds uncommitted data.
> **Cons:** All updates are written **twice** (log + final DB write). High I/O overhead for large transactions.

### 2.2.5 Pseudo-formal Recovery Procedure
For each transaction `Ti` in the log:

$$
\text{Action}(T_i) = \begin{cases} \text{REDO all } W_i(X) \text{ from log} & \text{if } \langle T_i \, \text{COMMIT} \rangle \in \text{Log} \\ \text{Do nothing} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 2.3 Strategy 2 — Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO or UNDO/NO-REDO)

### 2.3.1 Core Idea
The database **may be updated on disk before the transaction commits**. This is *immediate*. The log must therefore record both the **old value (for UNDO)** and the **new value (for REDO)**.
