### 2.3.2 Operation Steps
1. `Ti` starts → write `<Ti START>` to log.
2. For each write `W_item(X, old, new)`:
   - Write `<Ti, X, old, new>` to log (WAL: log must hit stable storage first).
   - **Immediately** write the new value of `X` to the database on disk.
3. On commit → write `<Ti COMMIT>` to log (force-log).
4. On crash → run the **Recovery Algorithm**:
   - Scan log **backward** → perform **UNDO** of all uncommitted transactions.
   - Scan log **forward** → perform **REDO** of all committed transactions whose updates may not yet have reached disk.

### 2.3.3 The Two Variants of Immediate Update

| Variant | Log content on write | On crash of uncommitted | On crash of committed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UNDO/REDO** | Both old and new values | UNDO needed | REDO may be needed |
| **UNDO/NO-REDO** | Only old value (no REDO info) | UNDO needed | **Never needs REDO** (page was forced at commit) |

### 2.3.4 The ARIES Algorithm (Industry Standard)
Modern DBMSs (IBM DB2, Oracle) use **ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics)** — a refined **Write-Ahead Logging + STEAL + NO-FORCE** policy that combines UNDO and REDO and uses **Log Sequence Numbers (LSN)** plus a **Dirty Page Table (DPT)** and **Transaction Table (TT)** for efficient recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STEAL vs NO-STEAL:** *STEAL* means the buffer manager can write an updated page to disk even before the transaction commits. *NO-STEAL* disallows this.
> **FORCE vs NO-FORCE:** *FORCE* means all updated pages of a transaction are flushed at commit. *NO-FORCE* defers page writes.
> Deferred Update ≈ **NO-STEAL + FORCE**; Immediate Update ≈ **STEAL + NO-FORCE** (with ARIES).

### 2.3.5 Checkpoints
A **checkpoint** is a periodic synchronization point that limits recovery time. At a checkpoint the DBMS:
1. Suspends all writes.
2. Writes all committed in-memory buffers to disk.
3. Writes a `<CHECKPOINT [active transactions]>` record to the log.
4. Resumes normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Recovery only needs to examine log entries from the **last checkpoint onward**. This makes recovery time roughly $O(L_c)$ where $L_c$ is the log length since the last checkpoint.

## 2.4 Strategy 3 — Shadow Paging (No Log Required)

### 2.4.1 Core Idea
Shadow Paging treats the database as a collection of **fixed-size pages**. Each transaction is given a **private copy of the page table**; original pages are never overwritten — they become "shadow" pages.

### 2.4.2 Operation Steps
1. At start of `Ti`, the **current page table (PT)** is copied → this copy is the **shadow page table**.
2. When `Ti` updates page `p`:
   - A new page `p'` is allocated on disk.
   - The modified contents are written to `p'`.
   - The **private page table entry** for `p` is changed to point to `p'`.
   - Original page `p` remains untouched in the shadow.
3. On commit:
   - The pointer to the **current page table** in the master record is atomically switched from the shadow PT to `Ti`'s private PT.
   - This is a **single-pointer atomic write** — instantaneous.
4. On crash:
   - Recovery simply discards the private PT and reverts to the **shadow PT** (which is guaranteed to be a pre-commit consistent state).
   - **No UNDO, no REDO.**

### 2.4.3 Visual Analogy — Git Commit
> [!NOTE]
> Think of Shadow Paging as a poor man's **Git**: every commit is an atomic pointer swap to a snapshot. To "rollback" you just point HEAD back to the old snapshot. The disk is never overwritten in place.

### 2.4.4 Garbage Collection Problem
Old shadow pages become **garbage** (orphaned versions). A background **garbage collector** must reclaim them. If not cleaned, disk fills up with obsolete copies — this is the **"no-overwrite" tax**.

### 2.4.5 Advantages & Disadvantages
- **Pros:** No log → no UNDO, no REDO. Commit is a single atomic pointer write. Recovery is essentially free.
- **Cons:** Fragmentation, garbage collection overhead, and the page table itself becomes a single point of contention for very large databases.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table for KTU 2024 examinations.**

| # | Concept | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Log Record Size | $S_L$ | $S_L = \text{Fixed Header} + B_{old} + B_{new}$ | Bytes |
| 2 | Recovery Cost (Log) | $C_{log}$ | $C_{log} = n_{tx} \cdot (C_{undo} + C_{redo})$ | I/O ops |
| 3 | Recovery Cost (Shadow) | $C_{sp}$ | $C_{sp} = 1$ atomic pointer write | I/O ops |
| 4 | Checkpoint Interval | $T_c$ | $T_c = \sqrt{2 \cdot M_{flush} \cdot t_{crash}}$ (heuristic) | Seconds |
| 5 | ACID Atomicity Rule | — | $\forall T_i:\;\; T_i \text{ is } \textbf{all} \text{ or } \textbf{nothing}$ | Logical |
| 6 | WAL Rule | — | $\text{Log}(X_{new}) \to \text{Stable Storage} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Disk}(X_{new})$ allowed | Ordering |
| 7 | Deferred Update Policy | — | $\text{NO-STEAL} + \text{FORCE}$ | — |
| 8 | Immediate Update Policy | — | $\text{STEAL} + \text{NO-FORCE}$ | — |
| 9 | Shadow Paging Policy | — | $\text{NO-STEAL} + \text{NO-FORCE}$ (logical) | — |
| 10 | Average Recovery Time | $T_{rec}$ | $T_{rec} = \tfrac{L_c}{v_{disk}} \cdot (1 + \alpha_{redo})$ | Seconds |

**Notation Key:** $n_{tx}$ = number of in-flight transactions; $C_{undo}$, $C_{redo}$ = I/O cost of each; $M_{flush}$ = flush time; $t_{crash}$ = expected MTTF (Mean Time To Failure); $L_c$ = log length since last checkpoint; $v_{disk}$ = disk write throughput; $\alpha_{redo}$ = fraction of committed transactions needing REDO.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Banking & Finance:** Deferred Update is the conceptual ancestor of **two-phase commit (2PC)** protocols.
- **E-commerce / OLTP:** Immediate Update with ARIES — used by **Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB**.
- **Snapshot Filesystems (ZFS, Btrfs, WAFL):** These are direct descendants of **Shadow Paging** — they use copy-on-write and pointer-swap commits.
- **Distributed Version Control (Git, Mercurial):** Same atomic pointer-swap idea as Shadow Paging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — All Three Strategies on the Same Schedule

### 3.1.1 Common Transaction
Let there be a single transaction `T1` performing:
- $W_1(A, 100, 200)$ — write A from 100 to 200
- $W_1(B, 50, 150)$ — write B from 50 to 150
- `COMMIT`

The log records (in order) are:
- `<T1 START>`
- `<T1, A, 100, 200>`
- `<T1, B, 50, 150>`
- `<T1 COMMIT>`

We consider a **crash** at three different points and show how each strategy recovers.

### 3.1.2 Case 1 — Crash Occurs *Before* `<T1 COMMIT>`

#### Deferred Update Recovery

Since `<T1 COMMIT>` is **not** in the log, the recovery manager performs **NO action**. The database is unchanged (because DEFERRED never wrote to it). `T1` is simply not applied.

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Deferred)} = \{ A = 100, \; B = 50 \} \quad \text{(original values)}
$$

#### Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO) Recovery

The recovery manager scans the log **backwards**, finds `T1` did not commit, and **UNDO**s both writes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UNDO step 1: write-back } B \leftarrow \text{old value } 50 \\
\text{UNDO step 2: write-back } A \leftarrow \text{old value } 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Immediate)} = \{ A = 100, \; B = 50 \}
$$

#### Shadow Paging Recovery

The **master pointer** still points to the **shadow page table** (set at `T1` start). Recovery simply keeps the shadow PT. No log scan is required.

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Shadow)} = \{ A = 100, \; B = 50 \}
$$

### 3.1.3 Case 2 — Crash Occurs *After* `<T1 COMMIT>` but *Before* All DB Pages Flushed

#### Deferred Update Recovery

The log contains `<T1 COMMIT>`, so we **REDO** all writes in log order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REDO step 1: write } A \leftarrow 200 \\
\text{REDO step 2: write } B \leftarrow 150
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Deferred)} = \{ A = 200, \; B = 150 \}
$$

#### Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO) Recovery

`T1` is committed, so **no UNDO** is needed. We **REDO** any updates whose disk writes may not have completed:

$$
\text{REDO: write } A \leftarrow 200, \; B \leftarrow 150
$$

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Immediate)} = \{ A = 200, \; B = 150 \}
$$

#### Shadow Paging Recovery

The master pointer was **atomically swapped** to `T1`'s private page table at commit. The crash is irrelevant. The DB is already in the new consistent state.

$$
\text{DB after recovery (Shadow)} = \{ A = 200, \; B = 150 \}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Checkpoint Interval

Recovery time $T_{rec}$ is proportional to the log length $L$ that must be scanned:

$$
T_{rec} = k \cdot L
$$

where $k$ is a constant (time per log record). Each checkpoint costs $M$ time units (to flush buffers). If checkpoints occur every $T_c$ seconds, the **average recovery time** over a failure of mean inter-arrival $T_f$ is:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot T_c + \frac{M}{T_c} \cdot T_f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first term is the *expected* log length to scan (uniform-failure assumption: average scan length is $T_c / 2$); the second is the *amortized* checkpoint cost.

Minimize with respect to $T_c$:

$$
\frac{dT_{avg}}{dT_c} = \frac{k}{2} - \frac{M \cdot T_f}{T_c^2} = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad T_c^2 = \frac{2 \cdot M \cdot T_f}{k}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad T_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot M \cdot T_f}{k}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous "square-root rule"** for optimal checkpointing — frequently tested in KTU as a 3-mark short question. Substitute $M = M_{flush}$ and $T_f = t_{crash}$ (Mean Time To Failure).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Deferred Update | Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO) | Shadow Paging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Log needed?** | Yes (REDO only) | Yes (UNDO + REDO) | No |
| **At Commit, DB is** | Updated (after REDO) | Already updated (mostly) | Atomically swapped |
| **Recovery = UNDO?** | No | Yes (for uncommitted) | No |
| **Recovery = REDO?** | Yes (for committed) | Yes (for committed, if not forced) | No |
| **Garbage Collection?** | No | No | **Yes** (shadow pages) |
| **Fragmentation?** | None | None | **High** (old page versions) |
| **Recovery Time** | $O(n_{committed})$ | $O(n_{committed} + n_{aborted})$ | $O(1)$ (pointer reset) |
| **Used in** | Early systems, 2PC | ARIES, Oracle, InnoDB | ZFS, Btrfs, early System R |
| **Failure Mode Risk** | Lost updates if log truncated | Lost updates if WAL broken | Disk full from garbage |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Checkpoint Interval

**Given:** A DBMS has $M = 4$ seconds to flush buffers at a checkpoint. Mean Time To Failure $T_f = 3600$ seconds (1 hour). Scan constant $k = 0.01$ s/log-record.

**Find:** Optimal checkpoint interval.

$$
T_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot M \cdot T_f}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 3600}{0.01}}
$$

$$
T_c = \sqrt{\frac{28800}{0.01}} = \sqrt{2{,}880{,}000}
$$

$$
T_c \approx 1697.06 \text{ seconds} \approx 28.3 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Checkpointing too often wastes I/O; too rarely inflates recovery time. The **square-root optimum** balances these two costs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deferred Update — Activity Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Transaction T_i Starts]) --> A1[Write T_i START to Log]
    A1 --> A2[For each W_i X new]
    A2 --> A3[Append REDO entry T_i X new to Log]
    A3 --> A4{More writes?}
    A4 -- Yes --> A2
    A4 -- No --> A5{All checks passed?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Write T_i ABORT to Log]
    A6 --> A7([T_i Discarded])
    A5 -- Yes --> A8[Write T_i COMMIT to Log]
    A8 --> A9[Force Log to Stable Storage WAL]
    A9 --> A10[REDO all T_i writes from Log to DB]
    A10 --> A11([T_i Successfully Committed])
    A2 -.-> A12[Database NOT touched on disk]
    A12 -.-> A2
```

## 4.2 Immediate Update — Activity Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Transaction T_i Starts]) --> B1[Write T_i START to Log]
    B1 --> B2[For each W_i X old new]
    B2 --> B3[Append UNDO REDO entry to Log]
    B3 --> B4[Force log record to Stable Storage WAL]
    B4 --> B5[Write new value of X to DB immediately]
    B5 --> B6{More writes?}
    B6 -- Yes --> B2
    B6 -- No --> B7{Commit?}
    B7 -- No --> B8[Write T_i ABORT to Log]
    B8 --> B9([T_i Discarded])
    B7 -- Yes --> B10[Write T_i COMMIT to Log and Force]
    B10 --> B11([T_i Successfully Committed])
    B9 -.-> B12[On crash: UNDO backward scan]
    B11 -.-> B13[On crash: REDO forward scan if needed]
```

## 4.3 Shadow Paging — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stable_Storage
        M[Master Record Pointer]
        SPT[Shadow Page Table original]
        PPT[Private Page Table T_i]
        P1[Original Page p1]
        P2[Original Page p2]
        P3[Original Page p3]
        P1N[New Page p1 modified]
        P2N[New Page p2 modified]
    end
    M -- initially points to --> SPT
    M -- atomically swapped at COMMIT --> PPT
    SPT -- references --> P1
    SPT -- references --> P2
    SPT -- references --> P3
    PPT -- references --> P1N
    PPT -- references --> P2N
    PPT -- still references --> P3
```

## 4.4 Recovery Manager Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0([System Crash Detected]) --> C1[Locate last Checkpoint in Log]
    C1 --> C2{Recovery Strategy?}
    C2 -- Deferred Update --> C3[Scan log forward from checkpoint]
    C3 --> C4{Found T_i COMMIT?}
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[REDO all T_i writes in log order]
    C4 -- No --> C6[Ignore T_i no action]
    C2 -- Immediate Update --> C7[Phase 1: Scan backward UNDO uncommitted]
    C7 --> C8[Phase 2: Scan forward REDO committed]
    C8 --> C9[Database Restored]
    C2 -- Shadow Paging --> C10[Discard private page table]
    C10 --> C11[Keep shadow page table]
    C11 --> C12[Database Restored instantly]
    C5 --> C9
    C6 --> C9
    C9 --> C13([System Ready])
    C12 --> C13
```

## 4.5 WAL Protocol — Ordering Rule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Step 1 Log new value to stable storage] --> L2{Log flushed to disk}
    L2 -- Yes --> L3[Step 2 Write new value of data item to disk]
    L2 -- No --> L4[BLOCKED wait for I/O]
    L4 --> L1
    L3 --> L5([Disk write permitted])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Deferred Update and Immediate Update recovery techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deferred Update | Immediate Update |
|---|---|---|
| **DB modification time** | After commit | Before commit |
| **Log content** | REDO entries (new values) | UNDO + REDO entries (old & new) |
| **Recovery actions** | REDO only | UNDO + REDO |
| **Overhead** | Two writes (log + DB) | Lower (single disk write) |
| **Risk** | High I/O at commit | WAL rule must be enforced |

Deferred Update implements the **NO-STEAL + FORCE** policy, while Immediate Update implements **STEAL + NO-FORCE** (with WAL).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is Shadow Paging? Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Shadow Paging is a recovery technique where the database is organized as fixed-size pages, and a transaction is given a **private page table**. The original pages are never overwritten — they are maintained as *shadow pages*. On commit, a **master pointer** is atomically switched from the shadow page table to the private page table, achieving atomicity without a log.

- **Advantage:** No UNDO/REDO required; recovery is instant (one pointer reset).
- **Disadvantage:** Garbage collection overhead; high disk fragmentation from obsolete shadow pages.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **Deferred Update** recovery technique with a neat diagram. How does it handle system crashes for committed and uncommitted transactions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO)** recovery technique. State the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** protocol. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Deferred Update (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Deferred Update is a recovery strategy in which updates made by a transaction are *not* written to the database on disk until the transaction reaches its **commit point**. All modifications are first written to a **REDO-only log**.

**Step 2 — Procedure (3 marks):**
1. `<Ti START>` is written to the log.
2. For every write `W_i(X, new_value)`, a log record `<Ti, X, new_value>` is appended (REDO entry). The actual database is untouched.
3. On `COMMIT`, the `<Ti COMMIT>` record is force-written to stable storage (per WAL).
4. **All updates of `Ti` are then applied to the database** by reading the log in order (REDO phase).
5. On `ABORT` or crash before commit → **no action** is required because the database is unchanged.

**Step 3 — Crash handling (2 marks):**
- If `<Ti COMMIT>` is **present** in the log → REDO all of `Ti`'s updates in log order.
- If `<Ti COMMIT>` is **absent** → `Ti` is simply not applied; the database is in its pre-transaction state.

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Immediate Update + WAL (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** In Immediate Update, a transaction may write updated data values to the database *on disk* **before** it commits. The log must therefore capture both the old value (for UNDO) and the new value (for REDO).

**Step 2 — Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Write `<Ti START>` to log.
2. For each `W_i(X, old, new)`:
   - Write log record `<Ti, X, old, new>`.
   - **Force this log record to stable storage** (WAL rule).
   - Then write the new value of `X` to disk.
3. On commit, write `<Ti COMMIT>` to log and force it.
4. On crash → run recovery:
   - **Phase 1 (Redo pass — forward):** REDO all committed transactions whose updates may not be on disk.
   - **Phase 2 (Undo pass — backward):** UNDO all uncommitted transactions that wrote to disk.

**Step 3 — WAL Protocol (2 marks):**
> *"Before a data item X is modified on disk, the log record containing the new value of X must be written to stable storage."*
This guarantees that even if the system crashes, a valid UNDO/REDO record exists for every disk modification.

**Step 4 — Example (2 marks):**
Let `T1` execute: $W_1(A, 100, 200)$, $W_1(B, 50, 150)$, COMMIT.
- If crash occurs **after** $W_1(A)$ but **before** COMMIT → UNDO restores A=100, leaves B=50.
- If crash occurs **after** COMMIT → REDO ensures A=200, B=150 are on disk.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **Shadow Paging** in detail. Discuss its advantages and limitations. How does it differ from log-based recovery? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Deferred Update, Immediate Update, and Shadow Paging in terms of UNDO, REDO, log usage, recovery time, and storage overhead. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Shadow Paging (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** Shadow Paging avoids overwriting pages in place. Each transaction maintains a **private page table** that is a copy of the **current (shadow) page table** at the time the transaction started. Updated pages are written to *new* locations on disk, and the private page table is updated to point to these new pages.

**Step 2 — Procedure (2 marks):**
1. At `Ti` start → make a copy of the current page table as the **shadow PT**.
2. For each update to page `p` → allocate new page `p'`, write modified contents to `p'`, update the private PT entry to point to `p'`.
3. On `COMMIT` → atomically update the **master record** to point to `Ti`'s private PT. The shadow PT becomes obsolete.
4. On crash → the master record still points to the shadow PT → no UNDO/REDO needed.

**Step 3 — Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- No log required; recovery is essentially free.
- Commit is a single atomic pointer write — very fast.
- No UNDO/REDO logic to implement.

**Step 4 — Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Garbage collection** of old shadow pages is mandatory.
- **Fragmentation:** old page versions waste disk space.
- Page table can become a bottleneck for huge databases.

**Step 5 — Difference from log-based recovery (1 mark):**
Log-based recovery (Deferred/Immediate) uses an **append-only sequential log** to track every change. Shadow Paging uses **page-level versioning** and a **master pointer** instead. Log-based is preferred for fine-grained updates; Shadow Paging is preferred for snapshot/CoW filesystems (ZFS, Btrfs).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Deferred Update | Immediate Update | Shadow Paging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UNDO needed?** | No | Yes (for uncommitted) | No |
| **REDO needed?** | Yes (for committed) | Yes (committed, if not forced) | No |
| **Log type** | REDO-only | UNDO + REDO | None |
| **Recovery time** | $O(n_{committed})$ | $O(n_{committed} + n_{aborted})$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Storage overhead** | Log only | Log (largest) | Page versions |
| **Commit cost** | High (apply all updates) | Low | One pointer write |
| **Crash of uncommitted T** | No action | UNDO needed | No action |
| **Crash of committed T** | REDO | REDO if needed | No action |
| **Example system** | Early System R | ARIES, Oracle, InnoDB | ZFS, Btrfs, WAFL |

(Marks distributed: 1 mark for table header, 1 mark per row × 7 rows = 7 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the policies:** Students often write "Deferred Update uses UNDO/REDO" — **wrong!** Deferred Update is **NO-UNDO + REDO** (sometimes called REDO-only). Immediate Update (full form) is the one with **UNDO + REDO**.
> 2. **Forgetting WAL:** When explaining Immediate Update, **always** mention the Write-Ahead Logging rule. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 3. **Shadow Paging misconception:** Many students think Shadow Paging "uses a log" — it does **not**. Recovery is purely pointer-based.
> 4. **Missing diagrams:** A neat diagram (flowchart or page-table block) carries **1 mark** in long answers. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **No example:** Always illustrate with at least one transaction (e.g., $T_1: W_1(A); W_1(B); COMMIT$) to show what happens on crash.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Recovery Strategies:** Deferred Update, Immediate Update, Shadow Paging.
- **Deferred Update:** DB updated **only after commit**; REDO-only log; **NO-UNDO + REDO** policy (NO-STEAL + FORCE).
- **Immediate Update:** DB updated **before commit**; full UNDO+REDO log; **STEAL + NO-FORCE** policy; uses **WAL rule**.
- **Shadow Paging:** Uses **private + shadow page tables**; commit = atomic pointer swap; **no log, no UNDO, no REDO**.
- **WAL Rule:** Log → Stable Storage *before* Data → Disk.
- **Checkpoints:** Limit log-scan time. Optimal interval $T_c = \sqrt{2MT_f/k}$ (square-root rule).
- **ACID:** Recovery manager primarily guarantees **Atomicity** and **Durability**.
- **ARIES:** Industry-standard recovery algorithm (refined Immediate Update) — used in Oracle, DB2, InnoDB.
- **Real-world descendants:** ZFS/Btrfs = Shadow Paging; InnoDB WAL = Immediate Update; 2PC = Deferred Update.
- **Exam mantra:** "Deferred = commit last, Immediate = commit first, Shadow = swap pointer last."
- **Valuation tip:** Always end a 14-mark answer with a **comparison table** — examiners reward structured conclusions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to NoSQL Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to NoSQL Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NoSQL

> [!NOTE]
> **NoSQL (Not Only SQL)** is a class of database management systems that are designed to handle **unstructured**, **semi-structured**, and **polymorphic data** at massive scale, using flexible data models that depart from the rigid, tabular, row-column schema of traditional **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, NoSQL is best described as a **data-store paradigm** rather than a single product — it is a *family* of database engines engineered for distributed, horizontally scalable, schema-less data persistence.

The term "NoSQL" was originally coined by **Carlo Strozzi in 1998** to name his lightweight, open-source relational database that did not use SQL. However, the term was *re-purposed* in **2009 by Johan Oskarsson** during a meetup organized to discuss the rise of distributed, non-relational data stores like **BigTable** and **MongoDB**. In its modern sense, it stands for **"Not Only SQL"** — emphasizing *coexistence* with relational systems rather than *replacement* of them.

### 1.2 The Four Defining Properties of NoSQL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A database is classified as NoSQL only if it satisfies the majority of these four properties — these are the **sentinel checkpoints** examiners use:
>
> 1. **Schema-less / Flexible Schema** — The data model does not enforce a fixed structure; different records may have different fields.
> 2. **Horizontal Scalability (Scale-Out)** — Capacity is added by adding more commodity servers rather than upgrading a single machine (vertical scale-up).
> 3. **Distributed by Design** — Built-in replication and partitioning across nodes with no single point of failure.
> 4. **BASE Semantics over ACID** — Sacrifices strict transactional consistency for availability, partition tolerance, and performance.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library vs. Warehouse" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Filing Cabinet vs. Storage Garage**
>
> - An **RDBMS** is like a **filing cabinet** in a government office. Every drawer is labeled, every folder has a specific position, and the catalog (schema) must be updated every time a new file type is introduced. Excellent for structured reports, but slow when you suddenly need to store 10,000 photos.
>
> - A **NoSQL database** is like a **flexible storage garage**. You can throw in furniture, boxes, paintings, and bicycles — each in any shape or size — and the garage happily adjusts. You can also build a second garage next to it instantly when the first is full. Perfect for varied, evolving, high-volume data, but you lose strict organization.

### 1.4 Why NoSQL? — The Motivation

The shift to NoSQL was driven by the so-called **"Three V's"** of Big Data, defined by **Doug Laney (2001)**:

| The "V" | Definition | Engineering Pressure on RDBMS |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Terabytes to Petabytes of data | Single-server RDBMS cannot store it |
| **Velocity** | Millions of reads/writes per second | RDBMS locking and ACID transactions bottleneck |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Fixed schema rejects heterogeneous formats |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additional Pressure Points:**
> - **Cloud Computing** demands elastic, commodity-hardware clusters.
> - **Agile Development** requires rapid schema evolution per sprint.
> - **Web 2.0** applications (social media, IoT, gaming) generate relational-light, document-heavy data.
> - **Cost Economics** — NoSQL runs on cheap x86 servers; RDBMS needs high-end SAN storage and licensing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ACID vs BASE — The Philosophical Divide

> [!NOTE]
> **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is the **strict, pessimistic** contract governing **RDBMS** transactions. **BASE** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) is the **relaxed, optimistic** contract governing most **NoSQL** systems.

#### 2.1.1 ACID — The Relational Bedrock

- **Atomicity:** A transaction is an *all-or-nothing* unit. Partial completion is impossible.
- **Consistency:** A transaction moves the database from one *valid state* to another; all integrity constraints remain satisfied.
- **Isolation:** Concurrent transactions execute as if sequentially; intermediate states are invisible.
- **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects survive crashes, power loss, and failures.

#### 2.1.2 BASE — The NoSQL Counterpart

- **Basically Available:** The system *guarantees availability* — every request receives a response (may be a failure or stale data).
- **Soft State:** The state of the system *may change over time* even without input, due to eventual-consistency background propagation.
- **Eventually Consistent:** Given enough time *without new updates*, all replicas will converge to the same value.

### 2.2 The CAP Theorem — Brewer's Conjecture (Formalized 2002)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000; formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002):**
> In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most TWO** out of the following three properties — but never all three.

The three properties are:

- **Consistency (C):** Every read receives the *most recent write* or an error. (Linearizability)
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a *non-error response*, even if some nodes are down.
- **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite *arbitrary network message loss* between nodes.

#### 2.2.1 The Two Impossibility Proofs

**Proof 1 (CA is impossible under network partition):**
If a network partition occurs between two nodes N1 and N2, and a client writes to N1 while reading from N2, the system must either:
- Reject the read (sacrificing **Availability**), OR
- Return stale data (sacrificing **Consistency**).

**Proof 2 (CP is impossible under partition with availability demand):**
If the system is to remain available, N2 must answer. But without coordination with N1, it cannot guarantee the *latest* write — so consistency must be sacrificed, OR the write must be blocked (killing availability).

#### 2.2.2 CAP Categories for Real Systems

| Category | Sacrifices | Real-World Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **CA** (traditional RDBMS) | Partition Tolerance | PostgreSQL, MySQL (single-node) |
| **CP** | Availability | MongoDB (default), HBase, Redis Cluster |
| **AP** | Strong Consistency | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak |

> [!NOTE]
> **A Common Misconception Cleared by KTU Examiners:**
> CAP does NOT say a system is "two of three" *all the time*. The theorem applies **only during a network partition**. In normal operation, a system can offer all three — but under partition, the engineer must *choose* between C and A.

### 2.3 The Four Pillars of NoSQL Data Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the *classification* of a NoSQL database by its data model. Memorize the four types and their canonical use cases.

#### 2.3.1 Key-Value Stores

- **Structure:** A giant hash-map (or dictionary). Every item is a **key → value** pair.
- **Value can be:** anything — string, JSON blob, binary, image.
- **Lookup:** O(1) via the key.
- **Use Cases:** Session caching, shopping cart, user preferences, real-time bidding.
- **Examples:** Redis, Amazon DynamoDB, Memcached, Riak KV.

#### 2.3.2 Document Stores

- **Structure:** Stores **semi-structured documents**, typically JSON, BSON, or XML. Each document has its own structure.
- **Distinction from Key-Value:** The database *understands* the document's internal fields and can index/query them.
- **Use Cases:** Content management, product catalogs, IoT telemetry, mobile app backends.
- **Examples:** MongoDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB, Firebase Firestore.

#### 2.3.3 Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores

- **Structure:** Data is organized into **rows** and **column families** (groups of related columns). Unlike RDBMS, columns can vary row to row and be added on the fly.
- **Origin:** Inspired by Google **BigTable (2006)**.
- **Use Cases:** Time-series data, write-heavy OLAP, sensor logs, recommendation engines.
- **Examples:** Apache Cassandra, HBase, ScyllaDB.

#### 2.3.4 Graph Databases

- **Structure:** Stores **nodes** (entities), **edges** (relationships), and **properties** (key-value annotations on both).
- **Strength:** Traversing *n*-degree relationships in O(1) hops via *index-free adjacency*.
- **Use Cases:** Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, route planning.
- **Examples:** Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB, JanusGraph.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Memorize for 14-mark questions:**

| Property | RDBMS (SQL) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Tables with fixed schema | Schema-less: KV, Document, Column, Graph |
| Query Language | SQL (standardized) | Varies: MongoDB Query, CQL (Cassandra), GQL (Graph) |
| Scaling | **Vertical** (scale-up) | **Horizontal** (scale-out) |
| Transactions | Full **ACID** | **BASE** (eventual consistency) |
| Joins | Native, optimized | Mostly unsupported or expensive |
| CAP Standing | Typically CA | CP or AP |
| Consistency | Strong / immediate | Tunable / eventual |
| Best For | OLTP, complex reporting, financial systems | Big Data, real-time web, IoT, social |
| Open Source Examples | MySQL, PostgreSQL | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j |

#### CAP Theorem — Formal Property Definitions

| Property | Formal Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Consistency | $\forall \text{ read } r, \; r \text{ returns the value of the latest acknowledged write } w$ | $C$ |
| Availability | $\forall \text{ request } req \in R, \; \exists \text{ response } \in \{\text{success}, \text{failure}\}$ | $A$ |
| Partition Tolerance | System tolerates $\vert P \vert$ lost messages between subsets $S_1, S_2$ | $P$ |

**Brewer's Law (Compact Form):**
$$C \cap A \cap P = \emptyset \quad \text{under network partition}$$

#### Eventual Consistency Convergence Bound

For a system with $N$ replicas, given a quiescent period (no new writes), the maximum convergence time $T_{\text{converge}}$ to a consistent state is:

$$T_{\text{converge}} \leq T_{\text{prop}} \cdot d_{\max}$$

where $T_{\text{prop}}$ is the message propagation delay and $d_{\max}$ is the diameter of the replica cluster's communication graph.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam hall:**
> - **Netflix** uses **Cassandra (AP)** to store viewing history for 200M+ users across thousands of nodes.
> - **LinkedIn** uses **Espresso (custom KV/Document)** for member profiles.
> - **Google Search** uses **BigTable (CP)** for the web index — downtime would be catastrophic.
> - **Banks** still use **Oracle RDBMS (CA)** — they cannot tolerate stale account balances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why CAP Forces a Tradeoff (Constructive Proof)

We will mathematically demonstrate that under a network partition, **all three** of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance cannot be simultaneously satisfied.

**Step 1 — Define the system state.**

Let there be a distributed system of two nodes, $N_1$ and $N_2$, both holding a replica of the variable $x$. Initially:

$$x_{N_1} = x_{N_2} = x_0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Introduce a write.**

A client $C$ sends a write $w$ to $N_1$, setting $x = 1$. $N_1$ acknowledges the write:

$$x_{N_1} = 1, \quad x_{N_2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Introduce a network partition.**

A network partition $P$ now severs all communication between $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$\text{msgs}(N_1 \to N_2) = \emptyset, \quad \text{msgs}(N_2 \to N_1) = \emptyset$$

**Step 4 — Issue a read from $N_2$.**

A client $C'$ issues a read $r$ to $N_2$.

- If the system **must remain Consistent (C)**: $N_2$ must return $x = 1$. But $N_2$ has not received the write because of the partition. Returning $x = 0$ violates C; refusing the request violates **A (Availability)**.

- If the system **must remain Available (A)**: $N_2$ must answer. The only answer it can give is $x = 0$, which is stale — violating **C (Consistency)**.

**Step 5 — Conclude the impossibility.**

The two branches of Step 4 are exhaustive:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch 1 (C held)} &\implies A \text{ is violated} \\
\text{Branch 2 (A held)} &\implies C \text{ is violated} \\
\therefore \quad C \cap A \cap P &= \emptyset \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the formal derivation that Brewer's CAP theorem is correct.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a NoSQL Data Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners test the *reasoning* behind choosing a data model. Use this decision matrix to answer such questions:

| If your workload is… | And your access pattern is… | Then choose… | Because… |
|---|---|---|---|
| Session, cache, leaderboard | Point lookups by ID | **Key-Value** | O(1) retrieval, no query needs |
| Product catalog, CMS, user profiles | Field-level filters, nested data | **Document** | Native JSON, indexable fields |
| Time-series, IoT, event logs | Range scans by timestamp, write-heavy | **Column-Family** | Append-only column appends are fast |
| Social graph, fraud rings, recommendations | Multi-hop relationship traversal | **Graph** | Index-free adjacency, no expensive joins |
| Financial transactions | Strong ACID + complex joins | **RDBMS** | BASE semantics unacceptable for money |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Mapping RDBMS to Document Store

**Problem Statement:** Convert the following RDBMS schema into a MongoDB document model and justify.

**RDBMS Schema (Normalized, 3NF):**

```
Table: Customer (CustID, Name, Email, Phone)
Table: Order    (OrderID, CustID, OrderDate, Total)
Table: Item     (ItemID, OrderID, ProductName, Qty, Price)
```

**Step 1 — Identify the access pattern.**

Assume 95% of queries fetch a customer's *complete* profile (all orders + items) in one call. This is a classic **read-aggregate, not relational-join** workload.

**Step 2 — Choose the embed-vs-reference strategy.**

- **Embed** `orders` *inside* the customer document (denormalize).
- **Embed** `items` *inside* each order sub-document.

**Step 3 — Construct the document.**

```json
{
  "_id": "C001",
  "name": "Anjali S",
  "email": "anjali@ktu.ac.in",
  "phone": "+91-9876543210",
  "orders": [
    {
      "order_id": "O5001",
      "order_date": "2024-11-12",
      "total": 2499.00,
      "items": [
        { "product": "DBMS Textbook", "qty": 1, "price": 899 },
        { "product": "Laptop Stand", "qty": 1, "price": 1600 }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

**Step 4 — Justify the denormalization.**

$$\text{Reads}_{\text{aggregate}} = 1 \text{ (one document fetch)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad 3 \text{ (3-way join in RDBMS)}$$

For the *read-aggregate* workload, the NoSQL design wins on:

$$\text{Latency}_{\text{NoSQL}} \ll \text{Latency}_{\text{RDBMS}}$$

**Step 5 — Note the trade-off.**

Updating an item price now requires rewriting the entire customer document (instead of a single row in the `Item` table). This is acceptable because **updates are rare** and **reads are frequent** — the *read-write ratio* dictates the design.

$$\text{Decision Rule: } \frac{R}{W} \gg 1 \implies \text{Embed}, \quad \frac{R}{W} \approx 1 \implies \text{Reference (normalize)}$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define NoSQL. List any four characteristics of NoSQL databases.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** NoSQL (Not Only SQL) is a class of database management systems that provide a mechanism for storage and retrieval of data that is modeled in means *other than the tabular relations* used in relational databases. They are designed to handle large volumes of unstructured, semi-structured, and polymorphic data using flexible data models.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Schema Flexibility** — No predefined schema; each record can have its own structure.
2. **Horizontal Scalability** — Capacity increased by adding more nodes (scale-out), not upgrading existing ones.
3. **Distributed Architecture** — Data replicated and partitioned across commodity servers.
4. **BASE Semantics** — Eventually consistent rather than strictly ACID, prioritizing availability and partition tolerance.

---

**Q2. What is the CAP theorem? Why can't all three properties be guaranteed simultaneously?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem, 2000):** In any distributed data store, during a network partition, you can simultaneously provide at most **two** of the three guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.

**Why All Three Are Impossible:** When a network partition occurs between two nodes, one node holds the latest write while the other does not. If a client reads from the node without the latest write:
- Returning the **stale value** preserves Availability but violates Consistency.
- Refusing the request preserves Consistency but violates Availability.
- The partition itself cannot be "removed" in a real distributed system, so Partition Tolerance must always be assumed.

Hence, the system must sacrifice exactly one of (C, A) during a partition.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / Model Paper Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[Option A of Internal Choice]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the four major types of NoSQL databases with suitable examples and use cases. Compare them on data model, scalability, and consistency. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The four major types of NoSQL databases are:

**1. Key-Value Stores:**
- **Data Model:** Each item is stored as a key paired with a value (string, JSON, binary blob).
- **Examples:** Redis, Amazon DynamoDB, Memcached.
- **Use Case:** Session management, caching, real-time bidding systems, shopping carts.
- **Scalability:** Easily horizontally scaled by sharding on key hash.
- **Consistency:** Tunable — can be AP (eventual) or CP (strong).

**2. Document Stores:**
- **Data Model:** Stores documents (JSON, BSON, XML) where each document has its own structure.
- **Examples:** MongoDB, Couchbase, Firebase Firestore.
- **Use Case:** Content management, product catalogs, user profiles, mobile app backends.
- **Scalability:** Sharding by document key, with automatic rebalancing.
- **Consistency:** Configurable — strong (default in MongoDB) or eventual.

**3. Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores:**
- **Data Model:** Rows grouped into column families; columns can vary per row.
- **Examples:** Apache Cassandra, Apache HBase, ScyllaDB.
- **Use Case:** Time-series data, IoT sensor logs, write-heavy OLAP, recommendation engines.
- **Scalability:** Linear scalability with no single point of failure; AP-class systems.
- **Consistency:** Eventual by default (Cassandra uses tunable consistency levels).

**4. Graph Databases:**
- **Data Model:** Nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties on both.
- **Examples:** Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB.
- **Use Case:** Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, route planning, network/IT operations.
- **Scalability:** Specialized — uses index-free adjacency for fast traversal.
- **Consistency:** ACID for single-node writes; replication strategies vary.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Key-Value | Document | Column-Family | Graph |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Index | Key | Document ID | Row Key | Node/Edge ID |
| Query Capability | Key only | Field-level | Column-range | Pattern matching |
| Aggregation | None | Limited | Limited | Path queries |
| Best Fit | Cache | CMS | Logs | Relationships |

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss ACID and BASE properties. How does BASE differ from ACID, and when is BASE preferred over ACID? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**ACID Properties (RDBMS Standard):**

- **Atomicity:** A transaction is an indivisible unit — either all operations succeed or none are applied.
- **Consistency:** Transactions move the database from one valid state to another; all integrity constraints (keys, FKs, checks) are preserved.
- **Isolation:** Concurrent transactions execute as if they were serialized; intermediate results are hidden.
- **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects persist even in the face of system failures.

**BASE Properties (NoSQL Standard):**

- **Basically Available:** The system guarantees a response to every request (may be failure, may be stale data, but never a timeout).
- **Soft State:** The system state may change over time even without new input, because replicas may be propagating updates asynchronously.
- **Eventual Consistency:** Given a quiescent period with no new writes, all replicas will converge to the same value.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | ACID | BASE |
|---|---|---|
| Guarantee | Strong | Weak but sufficient |
| Latency | Higher (due to locking) | Lower (no global locks) |
| Availability | Reduced under contention | Maximized |
| Use Case | Financial, Inventory | Social media, IoT, Analytics |
| Coordination | Required (2PC) | Not required (gossip) |

**When BASE is Preferred:**

- **High Write Throughput:** When the system must accept millions of writes per second (e.g., IoT sensor data).
- **Global Distribution:** When users are spread across continents and partition tolerance is mandatory.
- **Read-Heavy Workloads:** When occasional staleness is acceptable in exchange for sub-millisecond response.
- **Big Data Analytics:** When aggregations over petabytes of data are more important than per-row accuracy.

**Example:** Amazon's shopping cart uses DynamoDB (BASE) so users can add items even when the network is unreliable, but bank balance lookups use Oracle (ACID) to ensure no double-spending.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Many students write "BASE means *no consistency*" — **WRONG**. BASE *guarantees* eventual consistency, not chaos. Marks are deducted for confusing BASE with "no consistency at all." Always state the **quiescent-period convergence** condition.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Option B of Internal Choice — Alternative]*

**Q. (a)** What is the CAP theorem? Prove that during a network partition, a distributed system cannot simultaneously provide Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**CAP Theorem Statement (Brewer, 2000):**
In a distributed data store, during a network partition, the system can guarantee at most **two** of the three properties: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.

**Definitions:**
- **Consistency (C):** Every read returns the value of the most recent successful write.
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a (non-error) response — without guarantee that it contains the most recent write.
- **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes.

**Proof (Constructive — Two-Node Scenario):**

Consider two nodes $N_1$ and $N_2$, each holding a replica of variable $x$.

**Initial State:**

$$x_{N_1} = x_{N_2} = 0$$

**Step 1:** A client writes $w: x := 1$ to $N_1$. $N_1$ acknowledges:

$$x_{N_1} = 1, \quad x_{N_2} = 0$$

**Step 2:** A network partition $P$ isolates $N_1$ from $N_2$:

$$\text{msgs}(N_1 \leftrightarrow N_2) = \emptyset$$

**Step 3:** A client reads $r$ from $N_2$.

**Case 1 — Preserve Consistency (C):**
$N_2$ cannot return $x = 0$ because that is stale. It must either:
- Refuse to respond → violates **Availability (A)**.
- Wait indefinitely for the partition to heal → violates **Availability (A)**.

**Case 2 — Preserve Availability (A):**
$N_2$ must respond. The only data it has is $x = 0$ (stale). Returning it violates **Consistency (C)**.

**Conclusion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Case 1} &\implies A \text{ violated} \\
\text{Case 2} &\implies C \text{ violated} \\
\therefore \quad C \cap A \cap P &= \emptyset \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Real-World Mapping:**

| Choice | Sacrifices | Systems |
|---|---|---|
| CA | P (only single-node) | Traditional RDBMS |
| CP | A | MongoDB, HBase, Redis (with strong consistency) |
| AP | C (strong) | Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak |

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the different types of NoSQL databases. For each type, give one real-world use case and one example product. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram:**

```
                       NoSQL Databases
        ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐
        │              │              │              │              │
   Key-Value       Document      Column-Family     Graph
     Stores         Stores          Stores        Databases
        │              │              │              │
   Redis          MongoDB        Cassandra         Neo4j
   DynamoDB       Couchbase      HBase             Neptune
   Memcached      Firestore      ScyllaDB          ArangoDB
```

**Type 1 — Key-Value Stores:**

- **Storage:** Hash-map style — `{ key → value }`. Value is opaque.
- **Real-World Use Case:** E-commerce **shopping cart** — cart-id maps to list of items.
- **Example Product:** **Redis** (with optional persistence via AOF/RDB).
- **Access Pattern:** Point lookups, O(1) time complexity.

**Type 2 — Document Stores:**

- **Storage:** JSON/BSON documents; schema-flexible per document.
- **Real-World Use Case:** **Product catalog** for an online store where each product has different attributes.
- **Example Product:** **MongoDB**.
- **Access Pattern:** Field-level filters, indexing, aggregation pipelines.

**Type 3 — Column-Family Stores:**

- **Storage:** Rows × Column-Families × Columns; sparse matrix.
- **Real-World Use Case:** **IoT sensor telemetry** — millions of sensors writing one column per reading.
- **Example Product:** **Apache Cassandra**.
- **Access Pattern:** Range scans on row key, high write throughput.

**Type 4 — Graph Databases:**

- **Storage:** Nodes, Edges, Properties; relationships are first-class citizens.
- **Real-World Use Case:** **Fraud detection** in banking — traverse relationships between accounts to find rings.
- **Example Product:** **Neo4j** (with Cypher query language).
- **Access Pattern:** Multi-hop traversals, pattern matching via GQL/Cypher.

**Summary Comparison:**

| Type | Storage Unit | Query Mechanism | Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Key-Value | (key, value) | GET, PUT | Redis | Session Cache |
| Document | JSON/BSON | MongoDB Query | MongoDB | CMS |
| Column-Family | Row + Column Family | CQL | Cassandra | Time-Series |
| Graph | Node, Edge | Cypher, GQL | Neo4j | Social Network |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "NoSQL = No SQL":** NoSQL means *Not Only SQL*, **not** "No SQL at all." Some NoSQL systems *do* support SQL-like query languages (e.g., Cassandra CQL, N1QL in Couchbase). Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Treating CAP as a "Pick 2 Forever" rule:** CAP applies **only during a partition**. In normal operation, all three can coexist. Writing "MongoDB is always CP" loses nuance and a mark.
> 3. **Forgetting eventual consistency's convergence condition:** State that convergence happens *after a quiescent period (no new writes)*, not at an arbitrary time.
> 4. **Mixing up Column-Family and Document stores:** Column-Family stores are *not* the same as Document stores. Documents are nested JSON; Column-Family stores have *rows* grouped into *families* of *columns*.
> 5. **Writing "BASE has no consistency":** This is factually wrong. BASE guarantees **eventual** consistency — the *strongest* form of weak consistency.
> 6. **Omitting the read-write ratio justification:** When asked to design a schema in MongoDB, *always* mention the read-write ratio (R/W) that drives the embed-vs-reference decision.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **NoSQL = Not Only SQL**, not "No SQL." Coined 2009, modern usage.
- **Motivated by the Three V's:** Volume, Velocity, Variety of Big Data.
- **Four Pillars of NoSQL Data Models:** Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph.
- **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability (RDBMS).
- **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent (NoSQL).
- **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000; proven Gilbert-Lynch, 2002):** Under partition, choose 2 of 3.
- **CAP applies ONLY during a partition** — not all the time.
- **CA systems:** Traditional RDBMS; **CP systems:** MongoDB, HBase; **AP systems:** Cassandra, DynamoDB.
- **Eventual consistency** = all replicas converge after a *quiescent period* with no new writes.
- **Horizontal scaling** = scale-out via commodity nodes; contrasts with RDBMS vertical scale-up.
- **Schema flexibility** is the *defining* feature of NoSQL.
- **Key-Value stores** are O(1) hash maps; best for caching/sessions. Example: Redis.
- **Document stores** store JSON/BSON; field-level queryable. Example: MongoDB.
- **Column-Family stores** excel at time-series and write-heavy loads. Example: Cassandra.
- **Graph stores** use *index-free adjacency* for fast traversals. Example: Neo4j.
- **Embed data** in documents when R/W ratio ≫ 1; **reference (normalize)** when ratio ≈ 1.
- **CAP tradeoff** is engineering-driven: choose based on whether you can tolerate stale data or downtime.
- **Formula to remember:** $C \cap A \cap P = \emptyset$ under network partition.
- **Use RDBMS for:** finance, inventory, ERP — anything needing strong ACID.
- **Use NoSQL for:** social media, IoT, real-time analytics, content management — high-scale, schema-flexible.
- **Real-world giants:** Netflix (Cassandra), LinkedIn (Espresso), Google (BigTable/HBase), Amazon (DynamoDB).
- **Exam mantra:** "ACID for correctness, BASE for scale."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of NoSQL Databases: CAP Theorem, BASE Properties, Use Cases, and Limitations of NoSQL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of NoSQL Databases: CAP Theorem, BASE Properties, Use Cases, and Limitations of NoSQL # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is NoSQL? **NoSQL (Not Only SQL)** is a class of database management systems that do not follow the traditional relational (tabular) schema-based architecture of RDBMS. It is designed for **distributed data stores** where massive scale, schema flexibility, and high availability are critical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** "NoSQL databases are non-relational, distributed, horizontally scalable, and schema-less data stores that sacrifice strict ACID guarantees in favor of performance, flexibility, and availability — typically modeled on the **CAP Theorem** and **BASE properties**." --- ## 1.2 The Four Major Types of NoSQL Databases ### 🔑 Type 1 — Key-Value Stores - Data is stored as a **collection of key → value pairs** (like a giant hash map). - The "value" is opaque to the database (a blob, JSON, or string). - **Examples:** *Amazon DynamoDB, Redis, Riak, Memcached*. ### 📄 Type 2 — Document Stores - Data is stored in **semi-structured documents**, typically JSON, BSON, or XML. - Each document is self-describing and can have its own schema. - **Examples:** *MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB*. ### 🏛️ Type 3 — Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores - Data is organized into **rows and dynamic columns grouped into "column families"**. - Optimized for reading/writing huge volumes of data across clusters. - **Examples:** *Apache Cassandra, HBase, Google Bigtable*. ### 🕸️ Type 4 — Graph Databases - Data is stored as **nodes (entities) and edges (relationships)** with properties. - Optimized for traversing complex relationship queries. - **Examples:** *Neo4j, JanusGraph, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB*. --- ## 1.3 CAP Theorem — Conceptual Analogy 🏪 > [!NOTE] > **The Coffee Shop Analogy (Imagined by Eric Brewer, 2000):** > Imagine a coffee shop chain with **3 branches in 3 cities**, all serving customers. The shop owner wants: > 1. Every branch shows the **same menu and prices** at all times (Consistency). > 2. Every customer who walks in is **served a coffee** even during a city-wide phone-line failure (Availability). > 3. Branches keep working **even if the network cable between cities is cut** (Partition Tolerance). > > In a **network partition** (cable cut), you can ONLY choose **two** of the three: > - Stop serving some customers (sacrifice **Availability**) → be Consistent and Partition-Tolerant (CP). > - Serve everyone but some may see the old menu (sacrifice **Consistency**) → be Available and Partition-Tolerant (AP). > - Be Consistent and Available only when there is NO network failure (CA — not realistic in distributed systems). ### Formal Definition of CAP (Brewer's Theorem) In any **distributed data store**, you can simultaneously provide **at most two** of the following three guarantees: | Property | Formal Meaning | Symbol | |---|---|---| | **Consistency** | Every read receives the most recent write or an error. | $C$ | | **Availability** | Every request receives a non-error response (even if stale). | $A$ | | **Partition Tolerance** | System continues operating despite arbitrary network message losses. | $P$ | The **mathematical constraint** is: $$ C + A + P \leq 2 $$ This means only **two of the three** can be guaranteed at any time during a network partition. --- ## 1.4 BASE Properties — Conceptual Analogy 🧱 > [!NOTE] > **The Group Project Analogy:** > In a college WhatsApp group project, members do not have a strict, synchronized, real-time document. Instead: > - The group is **Basically Available** — you can always read the doc, even if offline. > - It is in a **Soft state** — values may change over time without input (as others edit). > - It is **Eventually consistent** — within a few minutes, everyone's copy converges to the same version. > > This is the **BASE philosophy** — the *eventual* alternative to the rigid ACID model of RDBMS. ### BASE Acronym | Letter | Property | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **B** | Basically Available | System guarantees availability even in the face of partial failures. | | **A** | Soft State | State of the system may change over time, even without new input, due to eventual consistency. | | **E** | Eventually Consistent | System will become consistent over time, given enough time and no new updates. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** BASE is the **antithesis of ACID**. It trades **strong consistency** for **availability and partition tolerance**, making it ideal for large-scale, geographically distributed systems like social media platforms and IoT networks. --- # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of CAP Theorem ### 2.1.1 Consistency (C) - **Linearizability guarantee**: A read at time $t$ must return the value of the latest write that completed before $t$. - Requires that all replicas acknowledge a write before it is considered committed. - **Cost:** High latency because writes must propagate synchronously. - **Engineering Example:** Banking ledger systems (account balance must never be "stale"). ### 2.1.2 Availability (A) - **Every request gets a response**, but the response **may not contain the most recent write**. - The system remains responsive even when individual nodes fail. - **Cost:** Possible stale reads; clients may see different versions on different nodes. - **Engineering Example:** Social media news feeds, e-commerce product catalogs. ### 2.1.3 Partition Tolerance (P) - The system continues to operate despite **arbitrary message loss** between nodes (network split). - In a distributed system, partitions are **inevitable** — they will happen due to hardware failure, congestion, or link outages. - **Engineering Reality:** P is **non-negotiable** in real distributed systems. So the real choice is between **CP** and **AP**. ### 2.1.4 The Three Real-World Combinations | Combination | Behavior During Partition | Real-World Databases | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | **CA** (Consistency + Availability) | Cannot tolerate partitions; only works on a single node or LAN. | Traditional RDBMS: *PostgreSQL, MySQL* (single-node setup) | Sacrifices P; not truly distributed. | | **CP** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) | Rejects reads/writes on the minority partition to preserve consistency. | *MongoDB (default), HBase, Redis, BigTable* | Sacrifices A; system may return errors during partitions. | | **AP** (Availability + Partition Tolerance) | Continues serving requests on all nodes; resolves conflicts later. | *Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak* | Sacrifices C; clients may see stale data. | --- ## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of BASE Properties ### 2.2.1 Basically Available (B) - The system **always responds** to requests, even in the face of failures. - No downtime guarantee — partial degradation is acceptable. - Example: Amazon DynamoDB returns cached/old data if a replica is down. ### 2.2.2 Soft State (S) - The state of the system is **not guaranteed to be stable**. - Replicas may temporarily diverge because updates propagate asynchronously. - The system does not require input to "change state" — replication itself causes state transitions. - Example: In Cassandra, a row in one data center may differ from another for a few milliseconds. ### 2.2.3 Eventual Consistency (E) - **Given enough time and no new updates, all replicas converge to the same value.** - Mathematically: $\forall i, j \in \text{Replicas},\ \exists\ t\ \text{such that}\ \forall t' > t: V_i(t') = V_j(t')$ - Example: DNS systems — updates propagate globally within minutes/hours. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick Fact:** BASE is often called the *"anti-ACID"* because it does not provide the strict *Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability* properties of relational databases. --- ## 2.3 ACID vs BASE — Comparative Master Table | Property | ACID (RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL) | |---|---|---| | **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft State, Eventual Consistency | | **Consistency Model** | Strong / Immediate | Eventual | | **Availability** | May be sacrificed during failures | Always available (best effort) | | **Transaction Support** | Full multi-row ACID transactions | Limited or no multi-document transactions | | **Schema** | Rigid, predefined | Schema-less / dynamic | | **Scaling Strategy** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) | | **Best For** | Banking, ERP, OLTP | Big data, social media, IoT, real-time analytics | | **Examples** | Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j | | **CAP Position** | Closer to CA | Closer to AP or CP | --- ## 2.4 Detailed Classification of NoSQL Types | Type | Data Model | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Use Cases | Examples | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Key-Value** | Hash map: `key → blob` | Ultra-fast O(1) lookups, simple, highly scalable | No querying on values, no complex relations | Session storage, caching, shopping carts, leaderboards | Redis, DynamoDB, Riak, Memcached | | **Document** | JSON / BSON documents | Flexible schema, indexing, nested data | No JOINs (must do app-side), limited ACID | Content management, catalogs, mobile apps, IoT | MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase | | **Column-Family** | Rows with dynamic column families | Excellent write throughput, great for time-series | Limited query flexibility, complex data modeling | Time-series, logs, sensor data, analytics | Cassandra, HBase, Bigtable | | **Graph** | Nodes + Edges + Properties | Super-fast relationship traversal (constant-hop) | Not suitable for tabular data, partitioning is hard | Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs | Neo4j, JanusGraph, Neptune | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application | |---|---|---| | **CAP Constraint** | $C + A + P \leq 2$ | Pick any 2 of 3 guarantees in distributed systems. | | **Replication Factor** | $RF = N_{\text{replicas}}$ | Number of copies maintained for each data item. | | **Consistency Level (Quorum)** | $W + R > N$ | Where $W$ = write quorum, $R$ = read quorum, $N$ = replication factor; ensures strong read-after-write. | | **Eventual Consistency Bound** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} D(t) = 0$ | Difference $D(t)$ between replicas tends to zero as time progresses. | | **Partition Probability** | $P_p = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of at least one partition in $n$ nodes with per-link failure probability $p$. | | **Sharding Key Hash** | $H(k) \mod S$ | Routes key $k$ to one of $S$ shards for horizontal partitioning. | | **BASE Availability Bound** | $A_{BASE} \geq 1 - \epsilon$ | System availability is at least $1 - \epsilon$ for arbitrarily small $\epsilon > 0$. | --- # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrices ## 3.1 Mathematical Proof Outline: Why Only 2 of 3 in CAP? ### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Scenario Consider a distributed system with **two nodes**: Node A and Node B, connected by a network. A client writes $x = 1$ to Node A, and another client reads from Node B. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction **Step 1 — Define the three properties formally.** Let $W$ = a write operation that completes at time $t_w$ on node $N_w$. Let $R$ = a read operation that begins at time $t_r > t_w$ on node $N_r$. - **Consistency (C):** If $t_r > t_w$, then $R$ must return the value written by $W$. - **Availability (A):** If $N_r$ receives a request, it must respond (no timeout, no error). - **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system must function even if the network between $N_w$ and $N_r$ drops messages. **Step 2 — Assume all three properties hold simultaneously.** Suppose C, A, and P all hold. A partition occurs between A and B (network cable cut). **Step 3 — Analyze the read response.** The client now sends a read request to Node B. Node B is partitioned and **cannot contact Node A** to fetch the latest value of $x$. **Step 4 — Apply Consistency.** To satisfy C, Node B must return the most recent write (i.e., $x = 1$ from Node A). But it cannot, because the partition prevents communication. **Step 5 — Apply Availability.** To satisfy A, Node B must respond (not error out). The only way is to return a stale value or its own local copy. But this **violates C**. **Step 6 — Conclude the contradiction.** We have a logical contradiction: $$ C \land A \land P \implies \text{Contradiction} $$ Therefore, the system can guarantee **at most two** of the three properties: $$ C + A + P \leq 2 $$ **Step 7 — Real-world implication.** Since P (partition tolerance) is **inevitable** in any real distributed system (hardware fails, networks drop packets), the practical choice is: $$ P = 1 \implies C + A \leq 1 $$ You must sacrifice either **Consistency** (choose AP) or **Availability** (choose CP). --- ## 3.2 Quorum Derivation for Strong Consistency In a distributed system with $N$ replicas, define: - $W$ = number of replicas that must acknowledge a write. - $R$ = number of replicas contacted for a read. **Step 1 — Condition for read-your-own-writes consistency.** To guarantee that a read sees the latest write, the set of replicas contacted for the read must **overlap** with the set that acknowledged the write. **Step 2 — Overlap condition.** By the Pigeonhole Principle, the two sets overlap if and only if: $$ W + R > N $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $W$ and $R$.** For $N = 3$ (typical Cassandra default), choose $W = 2$, $R = 2$: $$ 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 \quad \checkmark $$ Strong consistency is guaranteed. **Step 4 — Alternative: $W = 1$, $R = 1$.** $$ 1 + 1 = 2 \not> 3 \quad \times $$ No guarantee of strong consistency — the read might hit a stale replica. **Step 5 — Generalization.** For maximum fault tolerance with strong consistency: - Set $W = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ and $R = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$. - This is called a **majority quorum** and ensures both consistency and survival of $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ node failures. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a NoSQL Database | Application Need | Required CAP Pick | Recommended NoSQL Type | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Banking / Financial ledger | CA (single-node RDBMS) | SQL RDBMS | Strict ACID, no distributed partitioning | | Social media news feed | AP | Wide-Column (Cassandra) | High write throughput, eventual consistency is acceptable | | Session / cache storage | AP (or CA in-memory) | Key-Value (Redis) | O(1) lookups, TTL-based expiry, ultra-low latency | | Product catalog with flexible attributes | CP or AP | Document (MongoDB) | Schema flexibility, secondary indexes, rich queries | | Fraud detection graph | CP | Graph (Neo4j) | Multi-hop relationship traversal in constant time | | IoT sensor time-series data | AP | Wide-Column (Cassandra / HBase) | Petabyte scale, write-heavy, time-window queries | | Knowledge graph (semantic web) | CP | Graph (Neptune / JanusGraph) | RDF triples, SPARQL queries | | Shopping cart with eventual consistency | AP | Key-Value (DynamoDB) | Always-available cart, conflicts resolved on merge | | Content Management System | CP | Document (CouchDB) | Document versioning, offline replication | | Real-time leaderboard (gaming) | AP | Key-Value (Redis Sorted Set) | In-memory, O(log N) updates, real-time ranking | --- ## 3.4 Trade-off Triad: Consistency vs Latency vs Availability | Design Choice | Read Latency | Write Latency | Consistency | Availability | |---|---|---|---|---| | Strong Consistency (CP) | High (must contact quorum) | High (must ack quorum) | Strong | Low (errors during partition) | | Eventual Consistency (AP) | Low (read from nearest) | Low (write to local node) | Weak (stale reads) | High (always responsive) | | Read-your-writes (compromise) | Medium (read from leader) | Medium | Causal | Medium | | Bounded staleness (compromise) | Low (async replicas) | Low | Bounded (e.g., $k$ versions or $t$ seconds) | High | --- # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ##
