[KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Model Answer:** BASE is an acronym that describes the consistency model adopted by most NoSQL databases. It is the alternative to the ACID model used in RDBMS. | Letter | Property | Explanation | |---|---|---| | **B** | Basically Available | The system guarantees availability, possibly with degraded performance, even when partial failures occur. | | **A** | Soft State | The state of the system may change over time without new input, due to asynchronous replication. | | **S** | Eventually Consistent | Given sufficient time without new updates, all replicas of a data item will converge to the same value. | Unlike ACID, BASE **sacrifices strong consistency** in favor of **availability and partition tolerance**, making it suitable for large-scale, distributed systems like Amazon DynamoDB and Apache Cassandra. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Note:** A 3-mark answer requires **all three expansions** plus **one example** of a BASE-compliant database. Avoid mixing up the letters. --- ### 📝 Question 2: List the Four Types of NoSQL Databases with One Example Each. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** The four major types of NoSQL databases are: 1. **Key-Value Stores** — Data is stored as `key → value` pairs. Example: *Redis*. 2. **Document Stores** — Data is stored as semi-structured documents (JSON/BSON). Example: *MongoDB*. 3. **Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores** — Data is organized into rows and dynamic column families. Example: *Apache Cassandra*. 4. **Graph Databases** — Data is stored as nodes, edges, and properties for relationship traversal. Example: *Neo4j*. > [!NOTE] > **Tip:** A neat 4-row table is the best way to score full marks. Adding a one-line "best use case" for each type earns extra credit. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### 📚 Question A: Explain the CAP Theorem in Detail with Real-World Examples. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024] #### **Part (a) — State and Explain the CAP Theorem. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Statement of CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem, 2000):** In any distributed data store, it is impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three of the following properties — at most **two out of three** can be satisfied at any given time. $$ C + A + P \leq 2 $$ **The Three Properties:** 1. **Consistency (C):** Every read receives the most recent write or an error. In a consistent system, all nodes see the same data at the same logical time. Formal guarantee = **linearizability**. 2. **Availability (A):** Every request (read or write) receives a non-error response, even if the response is not the most recent value. The system does not "go down" for any node. 3. **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes due to network failures. Since network failures are inevitable in distributed systems, **P is non-negotiable** in practice. **Why Only Two?** Assume a network partition splits the cluster into two halves. The minority half cannot communicate with the majority. To respond to a read, the minority must either: - Return **stale data** (sacrifices Consistency → AP system), or - **Refuse to respond** (sacrifices Availability → CP system). You cannot have both strong Consistency AND high Availability during a partition. --- #### **Part (b) — Discuss the Three Combinations (CA, CP, AP) with Examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Combination | Behavior | Examples | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | **CA (Consistency + Availability)** | Achieved only on a single-node or non-distributed system. No partition tolerance. | Traditional RDBMS: *PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle* (in single-node mode). | Cannot scale horizontally; not fault-tolerant to network splits. | | **CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)** | During a partition, the minority side rejects requests to preserve consistency. | *MongoDB (default config), HBase, Redis, Google BigTable, Apache ZooKeeper.* | Some users may see errors/timeouts during partitions, but data is always strongly consistent. | | **AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance)** | During a partition, all nodes continue to serve requests, accepting stale reads. Conflicts are resolved later (e.g., last-write-wins, vector clocks). | *Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, CouchDB, Riak, Amazon SimpleDB.* | Users always get a response, but may see slightly stale data. | **Real-World Engineering Choice:** | Application | CAP Pick | Justification | |---|---|---| | Banking ledger | CA / CP | Strong consistency is non-negotiable for monetary accuracy. | | Social media feed | AP | High availability and low latency are critical; users tolerate stale feeds. | | E-commerce cart | AP | Cart must always be writable; eventual consistency on merge is acceptable. | | DNS system | AP | Eventual consistency is fine; the world tolerates minutes of stale DNS. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do NOT** write "CAP means we can only choose one". The correct statement is "**we can choose two of three**". > - **Do NOT** confuse *Consistency in CAP* with *Consistency in ACID*. CAP Consistency = **linearizability** (every read sees latest write), while ACID Consistency = **no constraint violation**. > - Many students lose 2 marks by **failing to give a real-world example** for each of CA, CP, and AP. --- ### 📚 Question B: Explain the Types of NoSQL Databases with Their Use Cases and Limitations. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] #### **Part (a) — Explain Key-Value and Document Stores with Examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Key-Value Stores:** - **Data Model:** A simple hash map where every data item is a `(key, value)` pair. The database treats the value as an opaque blob. - **Schema:** Schema-less; the value can be a string, JSON, image, or any binary blob. - **Operations:** O(1) `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` by key. - **Examples:** *Amazon DynamoDB, Redis, Riak, Memcached, BerkeleyDB.* - **Use Cases:** - **Session storage** in web applications (store user login state with session ID as key). - **Caching layer** in front of a slow RDBMS. - **Shopping carts** in e-commerce sites. - **Real-time leaderboards** in gaming (using sorted sets in Redis). - **Limitations:** - **No querying on value** — you cannot ask "give me all users whose age > 25" because the DB has no idea what the value contains. - **No relationships** between keys (no foreign keys or JOINs). - **Limited secondary indexes** (only in advanced KV stores like Redis or DynamoDB). **2. Document Stores:** - **Data Model:** Collections of self-describing documents (JSON, BSON, XML). Each document can have a different structure. - **Schema:** Schema-less or schema-on-read. Fields can vary across documents. - **Operations:** Rich queries (filtering, sorting, aggregations), secondary indexes, and even joins via `$lookup` in MongoDB. - **Examples:** *MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB, RethinkDB.* - **Use Cases:** - **Content Management Systems (CMS)** where articles have different fields. - **Product catalogs** with varying attributes (e.g., clothing vs electronics). - **Mobile app backends** storing user profiles and posts. - **IoT data** with sensor-specific fields. - **Limitations:** - **No multi-document ACID transactions** (in older versions; MongoDB 4+ supports them with limitations). - **Document size limits** (e.g., 16 MB in MongoDB BSON). - **Data duplication** if not modeled carefully (denormalization is common). --- #### **Part (b) — Explain Column-Family and Graph Stores with Examples and List the Limitations of NoSQL. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **3. Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores:** - **Data Model:** Data is organized into **rows and column families**. Each row can have a different set of columns, and columns are grouped into families for efficient access. - **Schema:** Sparse, dynamic columns. Designed for write-heavy workloads. - **Operations:** Range scans on the primary key; secondary indexes are limited. - **Examples:** *Apache Cassandra, HBase, Google Bigtable, ScyllaDB.* - **Use Cases:** - **Time-series data** (sensor readings, logs, financial tick data). - **Write-heavy IoT platforms** ingesting millions of events per second. - **Messaging systems** storing conversation history. - **Recommendation engines** storing user-item interaction matrices. - **Limitations:** - **Complex data modeling** required upfront (CQL in Cassandra is not as flexible as SQL). - **Limited query flexibility** — you can only query on the primary key efficiently. - **Aggregations are slow** compared to dedicated analytics databases. **4. Graph Databases:** - **Data Model:** Data is stored as **nodes (entities)**, **edges (relationships)**, and **properties (key-value on nodes/edges)**. - **Schema:** Property graph model or RDF triple store. - **Operations:** Traversals (multi-hop queries), pattern matching (Cypher in Neo4j, Gremlin in JanusGraph). - **Examples:** *Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph, ArangoDB, OrientDB.* - **Use Cases:** - **Social networks** (friend-of-friend, mutual connections). - **Fraud detection** (find suspicious patterns in transaction graphs). - **Recommendation engines** ("users who bought X also bought Y"). - **Knowledge graphs** (Google Knowledge Graph, semantic web). - **Network and IT operations** (dependency mapping). - **Limitations:** - **Not suitable for tabular or document data** — not a general-purpose replacement. - **Sharding/partitioning is hard** because relationships cross nodes. - **Steep learning curve** for query languages (Cypher, Gremlin, SPARQL). --- **General Limitations of NoSQL Databases:** 1. **Lack of standardization** — No unified query language like SQL. 2. **Weaker consistency guarantees** (eventual consistency) — not suitable for financial transactions. 3. **Limited transaction support** — multi-row/multi-document ACID transactions are missing or limited. 4. **Immature tooling and reporting** — fewer BI/reporting tools compared to RDBMS. 5. **Open-source risk** — many NoSQL DBs depend on small communities; long-term support is uncertain. 6. **Schema flexibility is a double-edged sword** — easy to start, but can lead to data quality issues. 7. **No JOINs** — relationships must be handled in application code, increasing complexity. 8. **CAP trade-off** — must sacrifice either consistency or availability, which may not fit the use case. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B:** > - **Do NOT** write only the names of database types without explaining the **data model** in each. > - **Do NOT** omit **at least one real-world example** for each of the four types — examiners specifically look for this. > - **Do NOT** list NoSQL limitations vaguely. Use **specific keywords** like "lack of ACID", "no JOINs", "eventual consistency", "sharding complexity". > - A 14-mark question expects **clear sub-part division**, **tables**, and at least **one diagram** if possible. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - ✅ **NoSQL = "Not Only SQL"** — non-relational, distributed, schema-less, horizontally scalable. - ✅ **Four types:** **Key-Value** (Redis), **Document** (MongoDB), **Column-Family** (Cassandra), **Graph** (Neo4j). - ✅ **CAP Theorem** (Brewer, 2000): $C + A + P \leq 2$ — only **two of three** can be guaranteed. - ✅ **CA** = Traditional RDBMS (single-node); **CP** = MongoDB, HBase, Redis; **AP** = Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB. - ✅ **Partition Tolerance is non-negotiable** in real distributed systems → real choice is between **CP** and **AP**. - ✅ **BASE** = **B**asically **A**vailable, **S**oft state, **E**ventually consistent — the **anti-ACID** model. - ✅ **Eventual Consistency** = $\lim_{t \to \infty} D(t) = 0$ — replicas converge over time. - ✅ **Quorum formula:** $W + R > N$ guarantees strong read-after-write consistency. - ✅ **ACID** is for RDBMS (strong consistency); **BASE** is for NoSQL (eventual consistency). - ✅ **Key-Value** is best for caching and sessions; **Document** for flexible schemas; **Column-Family** for time-series; **Graph** for relationships. - ✅ **Limitations of NoSQL:** no JOINs, limited transactions, eventual consistency, lack of standardization, weak reporting/BI tools. - ✅ **Key mnemonics:** - **CA** = "Can't Always" partition (no P). - **CP** = "Consistent but Prone" to errors (sacrifices A). - **AP** = "Always Present" responses (sacrifices C). - ✅ **KTU board-exam buzzwords to use:** *horizontal scaling, schema-on-read, eventual consistency, quorum, partition tolerance, CAP trade-off, denormalization, ACID vs BASE*. ---

topics.title: NoSQL Architectural Patterns: Key-Value Stores, Graph Stores, Column Family Stores, and Document Stores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST402 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** NoSQL Architectural Patterns: Key-Value Stores, Graph Stores, Column Family Stores, and Document Stores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NoSQL

**NoSQL** (originally *"Not Only SQL"*) is a class of database management systems that intentionally relax the rigid, tabular, schema-bound relational model in order to achieve **horizontal scalability**, **high availability**, and **schema flexibility** on commodity hardware clusters. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes NoSQL systems into four canonical **architectural patterns** (sometimes called *data models*):

1. **Key-Value Stores**
2. **Document Stores**
3. **Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores**
4. **Graph Stores**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, Module 4 specifically expects students to *compare* these four patterns on the basis of **data model**, **querying capability**, **consistency model**, and **scalability mechanism**. Memorizing only one is not sufficient — examiners typically award marks for comparative tables.

### 1.2 The CAP Theorem Foundation

Every NoSQL discussion is rooted in **Brewer's CAP Theorem** (2000), which states that in any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two** out of the following three properties:

- **C** – **Consistency** (every read sees the latest committed write)
- **A** – **Availability** (every request receives a non-error response)
- **P** – Partition Tolerance (the system continues operating despite arbitrary network message loss)

> [!NOTE]
> Partition tolerance is **non-negotiable** in real distributed systems (network failures *will* happen). Hence, NoSQL systems effectively choose between **CP** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB in default config) and **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

#### 🔑 Key-Value Store — *The Hotel Locker System*
Imagine a hotel with **10,000 lockers**. Each guest receives a unique locker number (the **key**) and places their belongings inside (the **value**). The hotel concierge can instantly retrieve or replace a guest's items using only the locker number — no questions asked about *what* is inside, *how* it is shaped, or *why* it is stored. The hotel does not search inside lockers; it only matches keys.

$$\text{Key-Value Model} \equiv \{ \text{Key} : k \in \Sigma^* \} \mapsto \{ \text{Value} : v \in \text{Blob} \}$$

#### 📄 Document Store — *The Filing Cabinet of Self-Describing Folders*
Picture an office where every file folder is allowed to contain **different sub-sections** with **different fields**. One folder might hold `{Name, Address, Phone}`, while another holds `{Name, Email, SocialMediaHandles, Preferences}`. The cabinet doesn't enforce a fixed template — each folder describes itself using **JSON / BSON / XML**. You can search *inside* a folder using the field names.

#### 🏛️ Column-Family Store — *The Spreadsheet with Merged Columns*
Think of a giant Excel workbook where each **row** is identified by a primary key, but columns are **grouped into families** (e.g., `PersonalInfo`, `AddressHistory`, `OrderHistory`). Different rows may have *different* columns within the same family — sparsity is native. Reading is highly efficient because entire column families are stored together on disk.

#### 🕸️ Graph Store — *The Social Network Map*
Visualize Facebook: every person is a **node**, every friendship is an **edge**, and edges carry properties (e.g., "since 2019", "tagged in photo"). Traversing *"friends of friends who like Java"* requires following edges — exactly the kind of operation relational databases struggle with because of expensive multi-table JOINs.

### 1.4 Why NoSQL? — The Driving Forces

| Pressure on RDBMS | NoSQL Response |
|---|---|
| **Schema rigidity** | Schema-on-read flexibility |
| **Vertical scaling ceiling** | Horizontal scale-out on commodity nodes |
| **JOIN-heavy queries slow down** | Denormalized / locality-aware storage |
| **Unstructured / semi-structured data (JSON, logs, IoT)** | Native document or wide-column storage |
| **Global 24×7 availability required** | AP systems with eventual consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The **"Big Data"** thresholds commonly cited in literature are the **3 V's** — **Volume** (terabytes+), **Velocity** (millions of writes/sec), and **Variety** (structured + semi-structured + unstructured).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pattern 1 — Key-Value Stores

**Data Model:** An associative array (hash map) distributed across nodes.
**Key:** Typically a binary or string (e.g., UUID, URL).
**Value:** An **opaque BLOB** (the database does not interpret it).

**Operational Surface:**
- `PUT(key, value)` — insert/replace
- `GET(key)` — retrieve
- `DELETE(key)` — remove

**Scaling Mechanism:** **Consistent Hashing** distributes keys across N nodes using a hash ring of size $2^{32}$ or $2^{64}$.

$$\text{Node}(k) = \text{Successor}(\text{hash}(k)) \quad \text{on the ring}$$

**Canonical Examples:** *Redis, Amazon DynamoDB, Memcached, Riak, Aerospike.*

**Strengths:** Sub-millisecond latency, trivially partitionable, ideal for session/cache/leaderboard workloads.
**Weaknesses:** No range queries, no secondary indexes (in pure form), no multi-key transactions.

### 2.2 Pattern 2 — Document Stores

**Data Model:** Collections of **self-describing documents**, typically encoded in **JSON**, **BSON** (Binary JSON), or **XML**.
**Document** ≈ row in RDBMS, but with embedded nested structures.

**Structural Example (JSON):**

```json
{
  "student_id": "KTU2024CS001",
  "name": "Anjali R",
  "cgpa": 9.12,
  "skills": ["Python", "MongoDB", "React"],
  "address": {
    "city": "Kochi",
    "pin": 682021
  },
  "projects": [
    {"title": "Library Bot", "year": 2023},
    {"title": "Exam Predictor", "year": 2024}
  ]
}
```

**Operational Surface:** CRUD on documents, secondary indexes, aggregation pipelines.
**Canonical Examples:** *MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB, Firebase Firestore.*
**Strengths:** Natural mapping to OOP / web APIs, no JOINs needed for one-to-many (embed), flexible schema.
**Weaknesses:** Document size limits (BSON = **16 MB**), atomicity only at single-document level (multi-doc transactions are newer additions).

### 2.3 Pattern 3 — Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores

**Data Model:** A sparse, multi-dimensional map indexed by **row-key**, **column-family**, **column-qualifier**, and **timestamp**.

$$\text{Cell} = (\text{RowKey},\ \text{ColumnFamily}:\text{ColumnQualifier},\ \text{Value},\ \text{Timestamp})$$

**Conceptual Difference from RDBMS:**

| RDBMS | Column-Family Store |
|---|---|
| Table = set of rows with fixed schema | Column Family = set of rows with potentially different columns |
| Row = tuple | Row = sorted map of columns |
| Read entire row | Read specific column family — columnar locality |

**Operational Surface:** `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` on (row, column) coordinates; range scans over row-keys.
**Canonical Examples:** *Apache Cassandra, Apache HBase, Google Bigtable, ScyllaDB.*
**Strengths:** Petabyte-scale writes, tunable consistency (ONE/QUORUM/ALL), time-series friendly.
**Weaknesses:** No JOINs, no aggregation natively, query-by-non-row-key requires secondary indexes.

### 2.4 Pattern 4 — Graph Stores

**Data Model:** Two primitive structures — **Nodes** (vertices) and **Edges** (relationships). Both may carry **properties** (key-value pairs).

$$G = (V, E) \quad \text{where} \quad E \subseteq V \times V$$

**Traversal Power:** Native support for *index-free adjacency* — each node physically stores direct pointers to its neighbours, making graph traversal $O(1)$ per hop instead of $O(\log n)$ like a B-tree JOIN.

**Query Languages:** **Cypher** (Neo4j), **Gremlin** (Apache TinkerPop), **SPARQL** (RDF stores).

**Canonical Examples:** *Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB, OrientDB, JanusGraph, TigerGraph.*
**Strengths:** Path-finding, recommendation engines, fraud-detection rings, knowledge graphs.
**Weaknesses:** Poor for bulk analytics, sharding graphs is hard (cuts across partitions).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Comparison Sheet

| Aspect | Key-Value | Document | Column-Family | Graph |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Unit** | (key, blob) | JSON/BSON document | (row, col-family, col, ts) | (node, edge) with properties |
| **Schema** | None | Implicit / flexible | Dynamic columns | Property-based |
| **Query API** | GET/PUT by key | Rich query + aggreg. | CQL / range scans | Cypher / Gremlin / SPARQL |
| **Best Fit** | Cache, session, queue | User profiles, CMS, IoT | Time-series, telemetry, messaging | Social network, fraud, KG |
| **Weakness** | No secondary lookup | No cross-doc joins | No aggregation | Hard to shard |
| **Example** | Redis, DynamoDB | MongoDB, CouchDB | Cassandra, HBase | Neo4j, Neptune |
| **CAP default** | AP | CP / AP configurable | AP (tunable) | CA / CP typically |
| **Consistency** | Eventual | Tunable | Tunable (write/read quorum) | Strong (single-master) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *compare*, always draw a table with **at least 6 rows**. Examiners reward visual structure generously.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where Each Pattern Wins

- **E-commerce Cart / Session:** Key-Value → millisecond state retrieval.
- **Content Management / Catalog:** Document → flexible product attributes.
- **IoT Sensor Telemetry:** Column-Family → billions of (sensor_id, timestamp) writes.
- **Recommendation / Fraud Ring Detection:** Graph → multi-hop relationship traversal.

### 2.7 BASE vs ACID — The Consistency Trade-off

| Property | ACID (RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Acronym** | Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent |
| **Guarantee** | Strong, immediate | Eventual, convergent |
| **Latency** | Higher (locks, 2PC) | Lower (async replication) |
| **Use when** | Financial transactions | Social feeds, likes, views |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Consistent Hashing — The Mathematical Backbone of Key-Value Sharding

The naïve `hash(key) \mod N` approach causes a **massive re-shuffle** whenever a node is added or removed (only $\frac{1}{N}$ keys stay on their original node). Consistent hashing minimizes this disruption.

**Step 1 — Build the Hash Ring**

Treat the output space of a hash function $H$ as a circular ring modulo $2^m$ (typically $m = 32$).

$$\text{Ring} = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^m - 1\} \pmod{2^m}$$

**Step 2 — Map Nodes onto the Ring**

Each physical node $n_i$ is assigned one or more *virtual nodes* (vnodes) by hashing an identifier:

$$p_i = H(\text{"NODE"}\_\text{"} + i) \mod 2^m$$

**Step 3 — Map Keys onto the Ring**

Each key $k$ is mapped to a position:

$$p_k = H(k) \mod 2^m$$

**Step 4 — Owner Lookup (Clockwise Walk)**

The owning node for key $k$ is the **first node encountered** when walking clockwise from $p_k$:

$$\text{Owner}(k) = \min\{p_i \mid p_i \geq p_k\} \cup \min\{p_i\}$$

if no such node exists in the forward walk.

**Step 5 — Rebalancing Cost**

When a new node $n_{new}$ is added at position $p_{new}$, it takes ownership of only the keys in the arc $(p_{prev}, p_{new}]$. The fraction of keys remapped is approximately:

$$\frac{\Delta \text{Keys}}{\text{Total Keys}} \approx \frac{1}{N+1}$$

This is independent of the total keyspace size — a $K$-fold improvement over modular hashing.

---

### 3.2 Quorum Mathematics in Column-Family Stores (Cassandra)

Let $N$ = replication factor, $W$ = write quorum, $R$ = read quorum. Cassandra guarantees that the latest write is returned if:

$$W + R > N$$

**Analytical Derivation of "Strong Read" Condition**

A write is committed to $W$ replicas. A read contacts $R$ replicas and returns the value with the highest timestamp.

- *Probability of missing latest write* occurs when **all** $R$ contacted replicas are from the $(N-W)$ "stale" set.
- We need at least **one** of the $R$ replicas to be among the $W$ "fresh" set:

$$P(\text{strong read}) = 1 - \frac{\binom{N-W}{R}}{\binom{N}{R}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example**

Given $N = 3$, $W = 2$, $R = 2$:

$$W + R = 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 \quad \checkmark \text{ (strong consistency guaranteed)}$$

Now $N = 3$, $W = 2$, $R = 1$:

$$W + R = 3 \ngtr 3 \quad \text{(strong consistency NOT guaranteed)}$$

$$P(\text{strong read}) = 1 - \frac{\binom{1}{1}}{\binom{3}{1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%$$

---

### 3.3 Document Store — BSON Size Budget Derivation

MongoDB enforces a maximum document size of **16 MB**. For a collection of $D$ documents of average size $S$ bytes:

$$\text{Avg Document Size Limit} = \frac{2^{24} \text{ bytes}}{1} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes}$$

**Storage capacity for $n$ documents:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S_{\text{avg}} \leq n \cdot 16\,\text{MB}$$

If each document stores an array of $A$ sub-items, the safe upper bound is:

$$A_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{16\,\text{MB} - \text{overhead}}{\text{sizeof(subitem)}} \right\rfloor$$

---

### 3.4 Graph Traversal Complexity — RDBMS JOIN vs Graph Index-Free Adjacency

Consider finding *friends-of-friends-of-friends* (3-hop) on a network with $V$ vertices and average degree $\bar{d}$.

**In an RDBMS (using recursive JOINs):**

$$T_{\text{RDBMS}}(k\text{-hop}) \approx O\!\left(\bar{d}^{\,k} \cdot \log V\right)$$

The $\log V$ factor comes from B-tree index probes per JOIN.

**In a Native Graph Store (Index-Free Adjacency):**

$$T_{\text{Graph}}(k\text{-hop}) \approx O\!\left(\bar{d}^{\,k}\right)$$

Each hop is a single pointer dereference — independent of total graph size.

**Numerical Comparison**

Let $\bar{d} = 50$, $k = 3$, $V = 10^8$.

$$T_{\text{Graph}} = 50^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ pointer hops}$$

$$T_{\text{RDBMS}} = 50^3 \cdot \log_2(10^8) = 125{,}000 \cdot 26.57 \approx 3.32 \times 10^6$$

Speed-up factor:

$$\frac{T_{\text{RDBMS}}}{T_{\text{Graph}}} = \log_2 V \approx 26.57\times$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|
| Pure lookup by unique ID, no queries | **Key-Value** |
| Variable attributes per record, JSON-shaped | **Document** |
| Massive time-series / write-heavy | **Column-Family** |
| Multi-hop relationships, path queries | **Graph** |
| Mixed OLTP + analytics | **Document** with BI tools |
| Cache layer in front of RDBMS | **Key-Value (Redis)** |
| Social network features | **Graph + Document hybrid** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NoSQL Taxonomy — Master Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    NoSQL["NoSQL Database Systems"]
    NoSQL --> KV["Key Value Stores"]
    NoSQL --> DOC["Document Stores"]
    NoSQL --> CF["Column Family Stores"]

    KV --> KVex1["Redis"]
    KV --> KVex2["Amazon DynamoDB"]
    KV --> KVex3["Memcached"]

    DOC --> DOCex1["MongoDB"]

    DOC --> DOCex3["Amazon DocumentDB"]

    CF --> CFex1["Apache Cassandra"]
    CF --> CFex2["Apache HBase"]
    CF --> CFex3["Google Bigtable"]

    GR --> GRex1["Neo4j"]
    GR --> GRex2["Amazon Neptune"]
    GR --> GRex3["JanusGraph"]

    style NoSQL fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style KV fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723

```

### 4.2 CAP Theorem Positioning of Patterns

```mermaid
graph LR
    CAP["CAP Theorem Triangle"]
    C["C Consistency"]
    A["A Availability"]
    P["P Partition Tolerance"]

    CAP --- C
    CAP --- A
    CAP --- P

    CP["CP Systems"]
    AP["AP Systems"]
    CA["CA Systems rare"]

    CAP --> CP
    CAP --> AP
    CAP --> CA

    CP --> CPex["HBase MongoDB default Neo4j"]
    AP --> APex["Cassandra DynamoDB CouchDB"]
    CA --> CAex["Single node RDBMS only"]

    style CAP fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style CP fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style AP fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff
    style CA fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Consistent Hashing Ring — Key-Value Sharding

```mermaid
graph LR
    K1["Key K1 hash 0x1A"]:::keynode
    K2["Key K2 hash 0x4F"]:::keynode
    K3["Key K3 hash 0x9C"]:::keynode
    K4["Key K4 hash 0xE2"]:::keynode

    N1["Node N1 vnode 0x30"]:::nodenode
    N2["Node N2 vnode 0x77"]:::nodenode
    N3["Node N3 vnode 0xB5"]:::nodenode
    N4["Node N4 vnode 0x05"]:::nodenode

    K1 --> N1
    K2 --> N2
    K3 --> N3
    K4 --> N4

    classDef keynode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nodenode fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f3864,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Column-Family Logical Architecture (Bigtable Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RK["Row Key SENSOR 001"]
    RK --> CF1["Column Family Metadata"]
    RK --> CF2["Column Family Readings"]

    CF1 --> C1a["location Kakkanad"]
    CF1 --> C1b["installed 2024 01 15"]

    CF2 --> C2a["ts 1000 value 23.5"]
    CF2 --> C2b["ts 2000 value 24.1"]
    CF2 --> C2c["ts 3000 value 25.0"]

    style RK fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style CF1 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723
    style CF2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723
```

### 4.5 Graph Store — Social Network Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Anjali Node person KTU CS"]:::vertex
    B["Bharath Node person KTU EC"]:::vertex
    C["Chitra Node person KTU ME"]:::vertex
    D["Deepak Node person KTU CS"]:::vertex

    A ---|"FRIENDS since 2019"| B
    A ---|"CLASSMATE 2024"| D
    B ---|"FRIENDS since 2020"| C
    D ---|"FRIENDS since 2018"| C

    classDef vertex fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.6 Document Store — Embedded JSON Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["Document Student KTU2024CS001"]
    ROOT --> F1["Field name string"]
    ROOT --> F2["Field cgpa number"]
    ROOT --> F3["Field skills ARRAY"]
    ROOT --> F4["Field address OBJECT"]
    ROOT --> F5["Field projects ARRAY"]

    F3 --> S1["Python"]
    F3 --> S2["MongoDB"]
    F4 --> ADDR1["city Kochi"]
    F4 --> ADDR2["pin 682021"]
    F5 --> P1["proj Library Bot"]
    F5 --> P2["proj Exam Predictor"]

    style ROOT fill:#305496,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between SQL and NoSQL databases. List any four NoSQL data models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed, predefined | Dynamic, schema-on-read |
| Scaling | Vertical (bigger server) | Horizontal (more nodes) |
| Data model | Tables with rows/columns | Key-value, document, column, graph |
| Query language | Standardized SQL | Pattern-specific APIs (CQL, Cypher, etc.) |
| Transactions | Full ACID | Usually BASE / eventual consistency |

**Four NoSQL Data Models (1 mark):**
1. Key-Value Stores
2. Document Stores
3. Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores
4. Graph Stores

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the CAP theorem. Why is partition tolerance non-negotiable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CAP theorem**, proposed by Eric Brewer (2000), states that in any distributed data store, only **two of the three** properties — **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)** — can be guaranteed simultaneously.

**Why P is non-negotiable (2 marks):**
In any real distributed system deployed across multiple data centres or racks, **network failures are inevitable**. Packets get dropped, switches fail, links go down. If a system chooses *not* to be partition-tolerant, the moment a network partition occurs, the system must halt (sacrificing availability) or risk serving stale data (sacrificing consistency). Therefore, designers **must** tolerate partitions and choose between C and A.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the **Key-Value** and **Document Store** NoSQL data models. Compare their data structures, query capabilities, and use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Column-Family** data model with reference to Apache Cassandra. Discuss its data organization using row keys, column families, and timestamps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Key-Value and Document Store Models (7 marks)**

**Key-Value Store (3.5 marks):**
- Stores data as a collection of **(key, value)** pairs where the key is a unique identifier and the value is an opaque binary blob.
- The database **does not interpret** the value — it only retrieves/updates it by key.
- Operations: `GET(key)`, `PUT(key, value)`, `DELETE(key)`.
- **Example:** Amazon DynamoDB, Redis. Used for *session storage, caching, shopping carts*.
- **Strengths:** O(1) lookup, trivial horizontal sharding via consistent hashing.
- **Weaknesses:** No range queries, no multi-key operations.

**Document Store (3.5 marks):**
- Stores **semi-structured documents** (JSON / BSON / XML) where each document contains the data and its descriptive metadata.
- Documents in the same collection may have **different schemas**.
- Supports **rich queries**, secondary indexes, aggregation pipelines.
- **Example:** MongoDB document for a student:
  ```json
  {"sid":"KTU001","name":"Anjali","cgpa":9.1,"skills":["Python","ML"]}
  ```
- **Strengths:** Natural mapping to OOP and REST APIs, embedded sub-documents avoid JOINs.
- **Use cases:** Content management, product catalogs, user profiles, IoT.

**Comparison Table (embedded in answer):**

| Aspect | Key-Value | Document |
|---|---|---|
| Value format | Opaque blob | Self-describing JSON/BSON |
| Schema | None | Flexible, per-document |
| Querying | By key only | By any field, aggregation |
| Example | Redis | MongoDB |
| Best for | Cache, session | CMS, profile, catalog |

---

**(b) Column-Family / Wide-Column Model with Cassandra (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):**
The column-family model organises data as a **sparse, distributed multi-dimensional map**. Data is identified by a four-tuple:

$$(\text{RowKey},\ \text{ColumnFamily}:\text{ColumnQualifier},\ \text{Value},\ \text{TimeStamp})$$

**Apache Cassandra — Key Features (3 marks):**
1. **Partition Key** (first element of composite RowKey) determines the physical node.
2. **Clustering Keys** sort the rows within a partition.
3. **Tunable Consistency:** Every read/write can specify a quorum level (`ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`).
4. **CQL (Cassandra Query Language)** — SQL-like syntax but no JOINs.

**Example CQL Definition (1 mark):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
    sensor_id   text,
    reading_ts  timestamp,
    temperature double,
    PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, reading_ts)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (reading_ts DESC);
```

**Data Organisation (1 mark):**
Each row has column families (e.g., `metadata`, `readings`), and each column family holds many columns. Rows are physically partitioned by `sensor_id` and sorted by `reading_ts`, enabling efficient time-range scans.

#### ⭐ **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Graph Store** NoSQL data model. Discuss how it handles multi-hop queries using *index-free adjacency*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Consistent Hashing** as used in distributed key-value systems. Show with a derivation why adding/removing a node only affects $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of the keys. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Graph Store Data Model & Index-Free Adjacency (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A graph store models data as **vertices (nodes)** and **edges (relationships)**. Both nodes and edges may carry **properties** (key-value attributes) and **labels** (types).

$$G = (V, E),\quad E \subseteq V \times V$$

**Index-Free Adjacency (3 marks):**
Unlike relational databases that locate related rows via **index lookups + JOINs**, a native graph store gives each node **physical pointers** to its adjacent neighbours. When traversing an edge:

- Cost per hop = **O(1)** pointer dereference.
- Total cost for k-hop query = **O($d^k$)**, where $d$ is the average degree.

This is *independent* of total graph size — a property called **index-free adjacency** that makes graph databases scale where RDBMS collapse under recursive JOINs.

**Use Cases (2 marks):**
- Social network *friend-of-friend* queries
- Fraud detection (find ring of connected accounts)
- Recommendation engines
- Knowledge graphs (Google Knowledge Panel, Wikidata)

**Example Cypher query:**
```cypher
MATCH (a:Person {name:'Anjali'})-[:FRIEND*3]->(fof:Person)
WHERE fof.skill = 'MongoDB'
RETURN fof.name;
```

---

**(b) Consistent Hashing — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Setup (2 marks):**
Map both nodes and keys onto a circular ring of size $2^m$ (e.g., $2^{32}$) using a uniform hash function $H$. Each node is mapped to $v$ virtual nodes (vnodes) to balance load.

**Ownership Rule (1 mark):**
The node owning key $k$ is the **first node encountered walking clockwise** from $H(k)$.

**Derivation of $\frac{1}{N+1}$ Rebalancing Cost (4 marks):**

Assume $N$ existing nodes are uniformly distributed on the ring. Each node "owns" an arc of expected length $\frac{2^m}{N}$.

Now insert a new node. By uniformity, its position falls inside some arc of length $\frac{2^m}{N}$. It will split that arc into two arcs of expected total length still $\frac{2^m}{N}$, but it **steals the keys between its predecessor and itself**.

Probability that a given existing key now maps to the new node = length of stolen arc / total ring length:

$$P(\text{key remapped}) = \frac{\text{length of stolen arc}}{2^m} = \frac{2^m / N}{2^m \cdot (N+1)} \cdot N = \frac{1}{N+1}$$

Alternatively, simpler argument: when $N+1$ nodes share the ring uniformly, each owns $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of the keys. Adding a new node thus affects exactly $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of keys, regardless of total keyspace.

**Conclusion:** Consistent hashing ensures that node addition/removal causes **minimal key reshuffling** — a key requirement for elastic cloud-scale systems like Amazon DynamoDB and Apache Cassandra.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Column-Family" with "Columnar RDBMS"** — They are *not* the same. A column-family store has dynamic, sparse columns per row; a columnar RDBMS (e.g., Vertica) stores fixed-schema columns in columnar order on disk.
> 2. **Forgetting CAP is binary in practice** — Many students write *"all three"* are possible. Examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 3. **Not stating replication factor / quorum values** in Cassandra questions — always quote `N`, `W`, `R` and verify `W + R > N`.
> 4. **Mixing up BSON size limit** — It is **16 MB**, not 16 KB or 64 MB.
> 5. **Drawing NoSQL comparison as prose** — Examiners award 1–2 extra marks for a *well-structured comparative table* with at least 5 rows.
> 6. **Spelling "Quorum" as "Quorum/Quorant"** — verify spelling; minor but adds up across multiple answers.

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **NoSQL = "Not Only SQL"** — four canonical patterns: **Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph**.
- ✅ **CAP theorem**: in distributed systems, choose **CP** or **AP** (P is mandatory).
- ✅ **BASE** vs **ACID** — eventual vs strong consistency.
- ✅ **Key-Value**: opaque blob, O(1) lookup, *Redis, DynamoDB, Memcached*.
- ✅ **Document**: JSON/BSON, flexible schema, *MongoDB, CouchDB*.
- ✅ **Column-Family**: $(\text{rowkey}, \text{cf}:\text{col}, \text{value}, \text{ts})$, *Cassandra, HBase, Bigtable*.
- ✅ **Graph**: vertices + edges + properties, **index-free adjacency** gives O(1) per hop, *Neo4j, Neptune*.
- ✅ **Consistent Hashing** remaps only $\frac{1}{N+1}$ keys on node change.
- ✅ **Cassandra quorum rule**: $W + R > N$ for strong read.
- ✅ **BSON document size limit = 16 MB**.
- ✅ **Tunable consistency**: `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL` in Cassandra.
- ✅ **Index-free adjacency** distinguishes native graph stores from RDBMS.
- ✅ Always end comparative answers with a **comparison table** — examiners reward structure.
